parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game centers on exchanging red and green chips with asymmetric private values on red and a common value on green. Every player starts with 10 red and 10 green chips. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it is a natural medium of exchange and safe store of value. Red is privately valued: dog=$0.30, buffalo=$0.10, cat=$0.40. From an outside analyst perspective, cat values red the most, dog is intermediate, and buffalo values red the least; buffalo is also the relatively red-poor type in terms of marginal value and will be the most willing seller of red. However, in the game each player only knows their own values and infers the others’ preferences by observing which trades are proposed or accepted. Because green is common value and relatively high at $0.50 compared with most red valuations, all players will be wary of giving up green unless they believe they are getting red that is at least as valuable to them in expected terms. The main surplus opportunities lie in transferring red away from buffalo (who values it at $0.10) toward dog ($0.30) and especially cat ($0.40), in exchange for green flowing in the opposite direction. Strategic uncertainty arises because a proposer cannot choose which acceptor they trade with if both non-senders accept; instead, one is randomly chosen. This means a proposer must design offers that are acceptable to at least one plausible partner, but that do not accidentally give away favorable terms to a different acceptor with a higher or lower red value than anticipated. As the game proceeds, players update beliefs about others: a player who eagerly buys red with green likely values red highly; a player who tries hard to sell red for green is likely to have a low red value. Since there are only three rounds with one proposal per player per round, timing matters: players have limited chances to execute beneficial trades. Early offers tend to be more exploratory and conservative, while later offers can become more aggressive if someone is still far from their preferred portfolio, especially the high-red-value player (cat) trying to acquire red and the low-red-value player (buffalo) trying to unload it. The outside reconstruction below assumes individually rational behavior, simple heuristics based on own values, and gradual learning from observed accept/decline patterns, generating a plausible full path of offers and acceptances under these rules.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has observed any bargaining behavior yet, so each player is largely guessing about others’ red valuations. They do know that green is worth $0.50 to everyone, making it the benchmark currency. The likely pattern is tentative, small trades that test counterparties’ willingness to pay or sell. Cat, who values red most, will already be inclined to try to buy red; buffalo, who values red least, will be inclined to offer red for green; dog is in the middle and may explore either direction but will not want to give up green on very unfavorable terms. Because both non-senders respond simultaneously and only one is randomly selected if multiple accept, proposers will design offers they think at least one other might take without being too generous. This opening round should reveal some information about who is keen to buy or sell red, guiding behavior in later rounds.

By the start of round 1, some information has been revealed. Dog offered to pay 1 green per red and buffalo accepted, signaling that buffalo is relatively willing to give up red for green (consistent with a low red value). Buffalo then sold red to cat at a rate of 3 red for 2 green, which both increases buffalo’s green holdings and suggests buffalo is actively unloading red; cat’s acceptance at that price signals a relatively high red valuation. Cat’s own attempt to buy 3 red for 3 green was rejected by both others, showing that neither dog nor buffalo is willing to sell red at 1:1 green/red under current beliefs and holdings. Now dog has moderately more red and slightly fewer green; buffalo has fewer red and more green; cat has more red and fewer green. Given these patterns, dog and cat both know buffalo is the likely red seller. Cat, valuing red the most, will want to keep accumulating red but must preserve enough green to buy from buffalo. Dog, who has already made a profitable trade from buffalo, will be more cautious about overpaying and may also try to sell some red at an advantageous price if cat appears hungry for red. Buffalo will continue trying to trade red for green while not giving away too much after learning that cat is eager for red.

By round 2, trade patterns are clearer. Buffalo has repeatedly sold red for green, confirming a low red valuation and a goal of converting surplus red into green. Cat has consistently bought red, even at relatively aggressive prices, signaling the highest red valuation and a desire to accumulate red. Dog has both bought and sold red at different prices, suggesting a middling valuation and opportunistic trading: buying cheaply from buffalo and selling profitably to cat. Holdings have shifted so that cat now holds significantly more red and fewer green, buffalo holds fewer red and more green, and dog is somewhere in between. With only one proposal turn left for each player, this final round is about fine-tuning portfolios rather than large exploratory trades. Cat is near their target of a red-heavy portfolio and wants to preserve enough green value; buffalo may still want to dump a bit more red if there is a willing buyer; dog will look for one last positive-value trade but will avoid offers that are too generous given the short horizon. Some proposals will now be declined as players’ marginal gains from further rebalancing shrink and they become more selective.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip-bargaining game features one common-value chip (GREEN, always $0.50) and one privately valued chip (RED). Otter and Goose both value RED cheaply at $0.30, while Badger values RED highly at $0.80. All three value GREEN identically at $0.50. Because only each player knows their own private value for RED, the others must infer valuations from observed proposals and acceptances across turns. In equilibrium-like behavior, Badger, the high-value RED holder, wants to accumulate RED cheaply by exchanging GREEN (which is worth the same $0.50 to everyone) for RED from Otter and Goose (for whom RED is low-value). Otter and Goose are inclined to trade away RED at rates that give them more total dollar value (e.g., 1 RED → 1 GREEN is profitable for them: they lose $0.30 and gain $0.50), but they must also worry that if they offer too generous a rate, they reveal that RED is cheap for them and risk being exploited. Proposals that offer more GREEN per RED are more attractive to Otter and Goose, while Badger prefers trades that give as many RED as possible per GREEN paid. The uncertainty stems from each player’s lack of information about others’ RED valuation and from the random resolution when multiple players accept the same offer: the sender cannot choose which accepting partner they actually trade with. Over the three rounds, players experiment: early offers are more exploratory and coarser in terms of exchange rates, while later offers adjust to observed acceptance patterns. Badger is likely to become more aggressive in trying to buy RED as soon as they see any sign that others are willing to give up RED, while Otter and Goose will accept GREEN-for-RED offers that clearly increase their own payoff and decline trades where they give up too much value or suspect that the rate is unfair.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 RED and 10 GREEN and have no direct evidence of how others value RED. They only know that GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone and that RED lies somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00. This first round is exploratory: Otter, Badger, and Goose are likely to test simple, symmetric-looking trades involving small quantities. Otter and Goose, with low RED values, are willing to part with some RED for GREEN if the terms are at least even or slightly favorable. Badger, with a high RED value, prefers to accumulate RED and may already look for bargains. Because all responses are simultaneous and the realized partner is random when multiple players accept, senders must structure offers that are attractive enough to at least one counterpart but not so generous that they overpay regardless of who accepts.

After round 0, public history shows that Otter traded away 1 RED for 1 GREEN, and Goose traded away 2 RED for 1 GREEN to Badger. This suggests to observers that both Otter and Goose are at least somewhat willing to part with RED, and that Badger is an active buyer of RED. Badger’s consistent willingness to accept RED-for-GREEN trades hints that Badger’s valuation of RED is high. Otter and Goose may infer that RED could be more valuable to Badger than to themselves and become more cautious in selling at disadvantageous rates, especially the 2 RED for 1 GREEN rate Goose just accepted. At the same time, they still benefit from exchanging low-value RED for higher-value GREEN, just not at overly steep discounts. Badger, having successfully bought RED, may now try more aggressive terms to acquire additional RED while testing the limits of Otter’s and Goose’s willingness to sell.

By round 2, patterns are clearer: Badger has repeatedly accepted offers that give them RED in exchange for GREEN, while refusing to part with RED. Otter and Goose have mainly proposed and sometimes accepted 1 RED for 1 GREEN trades, and Goose previously refused the harsher 2 RED for 1 GREEN rate after seeing how much RED they gave up. The remaining horizon is short—only this final round—so players focus on concluding profitable trades rather than on signaling. Otter and Goose, as low-RED-value players, still benefit from swapping a few RED for GREEN at roughly even terms. Badger, now holding a large RED inventory and fewer GREEN chips, values additional RED only modestly more than before and is short on GREEN to pay with, so marginal trades must be especially favorable for Badger to proceed. This produces relatively cautious final offers: Otter and Goose stick to small, roughly one-for-one RED–GREEN exchanges, and Badger tests a tougher exchange rate that the others are no longer willing to accept.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red chips that have different private values to each player, while green chips have a common, known value of $0.50 to everyone. Each player starts with 10 red and 10 green chips. Otter values both colors at $0.50, so they see the chips as almost perfect substitutes. Peacock values red highly at $0.80 and green at $0.50, so they want to accumulate red even at a modest cost in green. Badger values red at only $0.20 and green at $0.50, so they are naturally inclined to sell red for green whenever they can do so at an exchange rate better than 0.4 red per green (from their private perspective). However, each player only knows their own values; they only know that others value red somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00 and green at $0.50. This uncertainty means they must infer others’ likely valuations from proposals and acceptances. For example, a player aggressively trying to buy red with green is plausibly someone who values red above $0.50, while someone eager to sell red for green is likely someone who values red below $0.50. Strategic tension arises because the proposer cannot choose which acceptor they trade with if multiple people accept; that reduces their ability to tailor trades to specific partners. Early in the game, players are likely to experiment with small, mildly favorable terms to probe others’ willingness to trade. As they observe what is accepted, they adjust expectations about others’ valuations. Because there are only three rounds and each player proposes once per round, there is limited time to exploit information, so they may accept any clearly profitable trade rather than waiting for a better offer. Given the true values, theory predicts peacock will try to buy red, badger will try to sell red for green, and otter will be relatively indifferent, making or accepting trades that yield small expected gains or strategic positioning. Over three rounds, we expect some red chips to flow from badger (low red value) toward peacock (high red value), often with otter occasionally participating when offers look at least slightly favorable at their symmetric valuation of both chip types.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no direct evidence about others’ valuations, so they are likely to make exploratory offers that test how attractive red-for-green trades are. Proposers may choose relatively modest, somewhat symmetric terms that are just slightly favorable from their own perspective, to maximize the chance someone accepts without obviously signaling desperation. Peacock is naturally inclined to experiment with buying red using green, while badger is inclined to test selling red for green. Otter, valuing both colors equally, will likely make a neutral or slightly advantageous offer to gather information.

By the start of round 1, players have seen that badger readily accepted selling red for green and that peacock was willing to give up green to gain red. This strongly suggests to observers that peacock values red relatively highly and badger values red relatively low, while otter appears more hesitant. With this emerging pattern, peacock can become more confident seeking red from badger, and badger can look to profit by repeatedly offloading red. Otter may now refine offers, perhaps targeting either side depending on which direction seems more profitable or information revealing.

In the final round, the pattern that badger is a consistent seller of red for green and peacock a consistent buyer is now clear from the history. Both will try to secure any remaining profitable trades before the game ends. Peacock may press for slightly more red per unit of green, confident that badger still benefits due to their low red value. Badger, facing no future rounds, will accept any trade that yields a gain in green relative to their low private valuation of red. Otter, with limited advantage and only one last proposal, may either attempt a modestly favorable trade with the most likely counterparty or simply sit on existing holdings if profitable terms seem unlikely.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player bargaining game with two chip types: GREEN (common value $0.50 for everyone) and RED (privately valued: $0.10 for cat-orange-7489 and camel-green-9744, $0.30 for buffalo-red-8335). Everyone starts with 10 GREEN and 10 RED. Because GREEN is more valuable than RED for all players, all agents prefer to hold more GREEN and fewer RED, but buffalo-red-8335 is relatively more willing to hold RED than the others. Strategic tension arises because proposals must involve offering one color and requesting the other, and the proposer cannot choose which responder, if multiple accept, will trade with them. Each player sees all past trades and chip holdings but does not know others’ private RED values, so they infer others’ valuations from which trades they accept or reject. Efficient surplus-creating trades generally move RED toward buffalo (who values it more) and GREEN toward cat or camel in exchange, but cat and camel are symmetric in values, which creates uncertainty for proposers: any trade attractive to one may also be attractive to the other, risking random partner selection. Over three rounds with one proposal per player per round, players will experiment early with modest, value-improving trades to learn about others’ RED valuations, then potentially escalate to larger, more directional trades once they infer that buffalo likely values RED more. Cat and camel will tend to offer RED and demand GREEN; buffalo will tend to offer GREEN to acquire undervalued RED, but must leave enough surplus for the counterpart given the public common-value GREEN. Bargaining power is limited by the short horizon (only three rounds), by the randomness when multiple players accept, and by the requirement that each trade must be individually value-increasing for both sides given their beliefs at the time.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 GREEN and 10 RED and have no information about others’ private RED values. Everyone knows GREEN is worth $0.50 to all, but only buffalo-red-8335 privately knows they value RED at $0.30, while cat and camel each value RED at $0.10. Early proposals are likely to be small, exploratory, and roughly value-neutral from the proposer’s perspective, to see who is willing to take the less-preferred color. Cat and camel will tentatively try to shed RED for GREEN; buffalo will test exchanging some GREEN for RED if the terms are favorable. Because responders act simultaneously and the realized partner is random if multiple accept, proposers face uncertainty about which counterpart they will actually trade with, so they typically choose terms that are just attractive enough to at least one expected responder while not being too generous.

After round 0, one trade has occurred: camel traded 2 RED to buffalo for 1 GREEN. Publicly, everyone sees that buffalo was willing to give up GREEN for RED at that rate, suggesting buffalo values RED more than GREEN relative to camel’s revealed preference. Cat observes that buffalo took RED while camel did not, so cat updates that buffalo likely has a higher RED value than either cat or camel. Camel now has slightly fewer RED and more GREEN; buffalo has more RED and less GREEN; cat’s holdings are unchanged. In round 1, cat and camel will try to exploit buffalo’s apparent willingness to hold RED by offering RED for GREEN on slightly more aggressive terms, while buffalo will attempt to acquire more RED from the player most willing to sell, now with better targeted proposals. However, cat and camel must still ensure that their offers give buffalo some surplus given a plausible RED value around $0.30, and buffalo must design trades that are tempting enough for at least one of them without overpaying in GREEN.

By the start of round 2, multiple trades have moved RED toward buffalo and GREEN toward cat and camel. The history shows that buffalo repeatedly accepted trades that increased their RED and decreased their GREEN, confirming for cat and camel that buffalo’s RED value is relatively high. Camel has also just accepted a trade with buffalo at a 3 RED for 2 GREEN rate, revealing camel’s willingness to part with additional RED at slightly better terms than previously offered. With only one final proposal each, players now focus on locking in remaining gains from trade. Cat, already having made a profitable trade with buffalo, is now more RED-poor and GREEN-rich and may be less eager to part with additional RED. Camel, having recently sold more RED, may offer a smaller or more balanced trade. Buffalo, who now holds a large RED stack and reduced GREEN, becomes more cautious: additional RED is still useful but marginally less so, and GREEN is relatively scarce, so buffalo may demand better terms or simply pass if no clearly profitable opportunity arises. The uncertainty about random partner selection still constrains offers that might be attractive to both cat and camel simultaneously, so proposers aim for terms likely to appeal to just one target counterpart.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game is driven by private values for red chips and common knowledge that green chips are always worth $0.50 to everyone. Each player starts with 10 red and 10 green. Dog values red at $0.90 (high), Peacock at $0.80 (medium), and Owl at $0.50 (low, equal to green). Because only own values are known, each player knows that green is common-value but is uncertain whether others value red more or less than they do. From an analyst perspective, Pareto-improving gains come from moving red chips from low-value holders (especially Owl) to higher-value holders (Dog and to a lesser extent Peacock) in exchange for green chips. Dog has the strongest incentive to accumulate red and should be most reluctant to give them up. Owl is roughly indifferent between red and green and is likely more willing to sell red for green, especially if they believe at least one opponent values red more. Peacock is in the middle and could profit both from buying red from Owl and sometimes selling red to Dog at attractive rates. Strategic uncertainty arises because anyone making an offer cannot tailor it to a specific partner: if both possible recipients accept, one is chosen at random. This pushes senders toward offers that are at least weakly profitable no matter which opponent accepts. Over three short rounds, players have limited time to learn about others’ values from observed behavior. Early offers are likely simple, favor moving red from Owl to the others. Later offers can adjust if, for example, Dog reveals a strong desire for red by accepting expensive terms. Because everyone wants to maximize own payoff and there is no final conversion constraint beyond chip value, the most likely pattern is: Dog tries to cheaply acquire red from Owl; Peacock sometimes competes to buy red from Owl; and both Dog and Peacock avoid selling many red unless they receive an unusually favorable amount of green. Given symmetric starting endowments and short horizon, players will likely make small, test-like trades each round rather than aggressive, all-in proposals.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has yet observed the others’ willingness to trade, so proposals will be exploratory but biased by their own valuations. Dog, who highly values red, is unlikely to sell red and will probably test whether anyone is willing to sell red cheaply for green. Peacock, with moderately high red value, has similar but slightly weaker incentives; Peacock may experiment with buying red from the apparently low-valuation counterpart, which is more likely to be Owl. Owl, who values red the same as green, is the best candidate to sell red if the price is attractive (more green per red). However, since Owl only knows their own valuation, they will initially probe the market to see how eager the others are to trade red. Because acceptance is simultaneous and random among acceptors, proposals that are profitable regardless of which opponent accepts are relatively safer. Early in Round 0, trades are modest-sized and aimed at discovering who most wants red.

After Round 0, the players have some information: Dog successfully bought red from Owl at 3 green for 2 red, signaling Dog’s willingness to pay more than one green per red. Owl learns that at least one opponent values red somewhat more than $0.50, and Dog confirms that Owl is prepared to sell red at a modest premium. Peacock’s initial attempt to buy red at similar terms was rejected, suggesting that the others either did not want to part with additional red at that price or were still cautious. Going into Round 1, Dog has slightly more red and fewer green, Owl has the opposite, and Peacock’s holdings are unchanged. Dog is now even more red-heavy and may still try to acquire red if the terms are favorable but must economize on green. Owl, now with more green and fewer red, might become more careful about further red sales or might selectively sell red only at better prices. Peacock has an incentive to both test Owl again as a red seller and watch whether Dog accepts relatively expensive trades, which would indicate very high red valuation.

By the start of Round 2, Dog has clearly signaled a relatively high valuation for red by repeatedly buying small amounts from Owl at prices slightly above 1 green per red. Owl has increasingly traded away red in exchange for green, consistent with Owl’s indifference between the colors and their willingness to take favorable ratios. Peacock has managed to buy some red from Owl as well, though at a slightly better rate for Peacock (1 green per red) than Dog obtained in Round 0. Given only one round left, all players know this is their last chance to adjust portfolios. Dog, now more green-constrained and red-heavy, will still accept very cheap red but is unwilling to overpay. Peacock is in a similar but milder position, preferring additional red if it can be obtained at or below 1 green per red. Owl, having learned that both opponents are willing buyers, has an incentive to set tougher selling terms (demand more green per red) or stop selling entirely if the price is not attractive. The final offers are likely to be small, conservative trades that preserve earlier gains rather than dramatic reversals.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features one common-value chip (GREEN at $0.50 for everyone) and one privately-valued chip (RED, ranging from clearly low to very high). All players start with 10 GREEN and 10 RED. Privately, ram values RED at $0.30 (low), alligator at $0.40 (medium), and butterfly at $1.00 (very high). Because GREEN is common-value, it serves as a natural unit of account: everyone is willing to trade GREEN 1-for-1 in value terms, but they disagree primarily about the value of RED. From the analyst’s perspective, trades that move RED from low-valuers (especially ram, somewhat alligator) to high-valuer butterfly in exchange for GREEN are efficiency-improving, but players don’t see the full value profile. Each proposer only knows their own RED value and sees others’ behavior. Ram and alligator know they like GREEN more than RED at the margin, so they are inclined to offer RED to buy GREEN. Butterfly knows RED is extremely valuable relative to GREEN, so is inclined to buy RED with GREEN when offers are favorable, and generally not to sell RED. The key uncertainty for each player is how others value RED, inferred only from observed acceptance/decline patterns. Given the symmetric, short horizon (3 rounds, 1 proposal each per round) and no side payments, rational proposals tend to be mildly aggressive: request more subjective value than you give, but not so extreme that no one ever accepts. We forecast that most executed trades will be RED → GREEN flows from ram and alligator to butterfly, with ram gradually becoming more willing to sell RED cheaply, and butterfly becoming more selective as her RED stock grows. Strategic risk comes from simultaneous acceptance: a proposer cannot target a specific recipient; if multiple opponents accept, a partner is randomly chosen. This makes precise price discrimination between the two opponents impossible and slightly discourages very generous offers that would be attractive to both simultaneously. Over the three rounds, we expect early exploratory proposals, then a settling into patterns where ram and alligator test butterfly’s appetite for RED, and butterfly proposes cautiously, often getting declined when she asks for RED too cheaply.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no behavioral information about others’ RED values, only the common range. Ram and alligator each know they value RED less than GREEN, but they do not know whether the two opponents are high RED valuers or not. Butterfly knows she values RED far more than GREEN, but doesn’t know that others are relatively low. We expect initial proposals to be exploratory: ram and alligator will likely offer RED for GREEN at terms that are somewhat favorable to themselves, to test whether anyone is willing to trade away GREEN. Butterfly will be interested in acquiring RED if the rate is better than 1 GREEN for 1 RED in her subjective terms. As proposer, butterfly is uncertain whether the others would sell RED cheaply, so she may experiment with a slightly demanding offer that asks for multiple RED in exchange for only a modest amount of GREEN. Acceptances in this round will heavily shape beliefs: if butterfly accepts a deal that is only modestly favorable to ram or alligator, they may infer that at least one opponent values RED higher than they do.

By the start of round 1, trade history has revealed some structure. Ram has successfully sold RED for GREEN to butterfly at a relatively RED-cheap rate (3 RED for 2 GREEN), and alligator has done similarly (2 RED for 2 GREEN). From these acceptances, ram and alligator infer that at least one opponent—most plausibly butterfly, given she was the unique accepter both times—seems relatively eager to buy RED with GREEN even at a small or zero gain in common-value terms, suggesting that player values RED more than they do. Butterfly, having accepted both offers, has accumulated RED and parted with some GREEN; she now has a strong incentive to be more selective, because marginal RED is slightly less critical once she already holds more than the others. Strategic tension arises: ram and alligator would like to continue selling RED to the high-valuer, but must balance the risk that their offers are too generous (giving away too much value) against the risk of no trade at all if they demand too much GREEN. Butterfly, in contrast, will likely accept only deals where she clearly gains in her own valuation—she is unlikely to keep paying nearly 1 GREEN per RED once she already holds an advantage. Butterfly’s own proposal this round is also informed by seeing others were willing sellers at modest prices; she may attempt to acquire more RED at a discount in terms of GREEN, but her earlier failed proposal (2 GREEN for 2 RED) suggests that ram and alligator are not willing to sell at parity when they are the ones being asked.

Entering the final round, the pattern is clearer. Several successful trades have shifted RED from ram to butterfly, and some from alligator to butterfly, while GREEN has flowed the other way. Ram now holds significantly more GREEN and fewer RED, and has learned that butterfly is willing to buy RED but has become more selective as the game progressed. Alligator has sold some RED but has also experienced at least one declined proposal, revealing that the high buyer’s willingness to pay in GREEN is limited. Butterfly holds a large RED stock and fewer GREEN chips, and knows the others will only sell RED when they receive enough GREEN to at least modestly beat their own valuations. With only one proposal each remaining, players face an endgame effect: there is no future reputation or learning to preserve. Ram is likely to make a somewhat conservative final offer that still modestly improves her payoff, attempting to sell remaining low-value RED for GREEN without being so demanding that butterfly declines. Alligator may try a last, slightly aggressive ask, hoping butterfly or even ram will bite, but is also aware of previous refusals. Butterfly, with much RED already, will be reluctant to overpay and may succeed only if she can induce a clearly profitable but still acceptable offer from a low-RED-valuer who wants a last improvement in GREEN holdings. However, simultaneous-response risk and the observed resistance to low-GREEN offers for RED make it likely butterfly’s final proposal is again declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: There are three players and two chip types. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 to everyone, and red chips are privately valued: dolphin=$0.40, ram=$0.20, iguana=$0.80. From an analyst perspective, iguana has very strong incentives to acquire red chips (most valuable to them), ram has the weakest incentive to keep reds (they value them least) and should be most willing to sell reds for greens, and dolphin is in the middle. Because everyone knows green is $0.50 but does not know others’ red values, players face uncertainty about how much others are willing to trade red for green. Proposers must choose one chip type to acquire (buy) and another to give up (sell), under the constraint of current holdings and not knowing others’ valuations. Recipients privately decide whether the offer is strictly beneficial in terms of their own values. If multiple players accept, the executed partner is random, so proposers cannot fully target the best counterpart. Over three rounds, players can update beliefs from observed acceptances/declines. We should expect attempts to move red chips from low-value holders (especially ram) toward higher-value holders (especially iguana), potentially paying with green. However, uncertainty about others’ red values and the risk of overpaying or being exploited can lead to cautious or rejected offers, especially early on. The forecast below assumes moderately rational, self-interested players who use simple value comparisons and are somewhat conservative early, becoming slightly more aggressive as they learn from responses.

rounds.round_explanation: All players start with 10 red and 10 green chips. No one has yet observed others’ behavior, so beliefs about others’ red values are diffuse. Given private values, dolphin sees red as slightly less valuable than green, ram sees red as clearly worse than green, and iguana sees red as more valuable than green. A reasonable early dynamic is that ram is inclined to trade away red for green, iguana would like to acquire red if the price is not too high, and dolphin may explore modest exchanges that are close to value-neutral for themselves but could reveal others’ willingness to trade.

After round 0, dolphin has traded one red for one green with ram, revealing that ram is at least somewhat willing to give up red for green on a close-to-even rate. Both dolphin and iguana observed that ram accepted a 1R-for-1G trade as recipient, while iguana and dolphin declined ram’s more aggressive attempt to sell 2 red for 1 green, and both declined iguana’s 1R-for-1G ask (which made red more expensive). This pattern suggests to everyone that red is relatively cheap from ram’s perspective and relatively expensive from iguana’s. Dolphin now has slightly more green and fewer red; ram has more red and fewer green but knows red is low value to them; iguana has unchanged holdings but a sense that others may undervalue red compared to themselves. Strategies are likely to shift toward more value-improving, but still cautious, offers targeting ram as a seller of red and possibly dolphin as a moderate counterparty.

Going into the final round, the executed trades have shifted red chips slightly toward the higher-value holder (iguana) and away from dolphin, while ram has repeatedly tried to sell red for green but found few takers at their posted terms. Dolphin and iguana now have some evidence that ram values red relatively low, while iguana’s previous attempts to acquire red at or above 1:1 suggest to others that iguana may value red highly. With only three turns remaining, players cannot rely on future bargaining to correct mistakes, so they are inclined to accept offers that deliver clear private-value gains and reject marginal or risky ones. Proposers, knowing this is the last chance, may push for clearly value-improving but slightly more assertive trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, three-round bargaining game with two chip types: GREEN (common value $0.50 to everyone) and RED (privately valued, but all players know the public range of red values). All start at 10 GREEN and 10 RED. The private values are: owl: GREEN $0.50, RED $0.30; iguana: GREEN $0.50, RED $0.10; whale: GREEN $0.50, RED $0.80. Ex ante, GREEN is a safe, known store of value at $0.50. RED is likely to be somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00, so each player knows that others might value RED more or less than they do but do not know exactly how much. Because all proposals must be one-for-one across colors (offer one color, request the other), and players cannot trade GREEN for GREEN or RED for RED, every proposal is implicitly a bet on relative red vs. green values. Privately, whale values RED the most and GREEN the least (relative to others), so whale is highly motivated to acquire RED and shed GREEN when possible. Iguana values RED the least and GREEN the most, so iguana is inclined to trade RED away for GREEN, but will worry that someone accepting an offer may be “signaling” that RED is actually valuable to them. Owl is intermediate, valuing RED more than iguana but less than whale, and might be somewhat flexible: willing to trade away some RED to more GREEN-leaning players if they offer generous terms. The main uncertainties: (1) how much each opponent values RED vs GREEN, (2) whether a generous offer induces multiple acceptances (creating lottery risk over counterparties), and (3) whether future rounds will allow better trades, making players reluctant to commit too early on thin margins. Over the three rounds, rational behavior with private information suggests small, mildly favorable trades early on to test others’ willingness to trade, followed by more targeted, value-extractive offers once some pattern of acceptance or rejection is observed. Whale will try to systematically turn GREEN into RED; iguana will try to reverse that; owl will look for slightly positive expected-value trades in either direction, balancing learning about others’ types against securing immediate gains.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric endowments (10 GREEN, 10 RED) but unknown relative red-values. No trade history exists, so everyone is uncertain about others’ valuations and risk attitudes. Owl moves first and is likely to make a conservative, mildly self-favoring offer that still looks attractive if others value GREEN strongly or RED weakly. Iguana, moving second, privately knows RED is relatively poor for them and will tentatively try converting RED to GREEN, but must balance that against the fear of giving too good a deal to someone who secretly loves RED. Whale, moving third, privately loves RED and will look for an early opportunity to stake out trades that convert GREEN to RED at favorable terms, potentially revealing their type if they accept aggressively generous red-for-green offers.

Going into round 1, there is now a small amount of public information. Owl’s first offer (1 RED for 2 GREEN) was quite demanding from others’ perspectives and was rejected by both, suggesting that neither opponent is desperate for RED at that implied exchange rate. Iguana’s subsequent offer (2 RED for 1 GREEN) was very generous to a red-lover and was accepted by whale, revealing that whale likely values RED relatively highly compared to GREEN. Iguana has reduced their RED holdings while increasing GREEN, consistent with their low private value for RED. Whale has increased RED and reduced GREEN, consistent with a high private value for RED. Owl still only directly knows their own values but can infer that whale probably likes RED more than iguana. This pattern sets the stage for more targeted bargaining: owl may now try to sell RED to whale but must offer terms that whale will still find acceptable given whale’s strong preference for RED. Iguana, having just strengthened their GREEN position, may probe for additional but less generous trades, while whale must avoid overpaying GREEN for RED given that others are now suspicious that whale is a red-enthusiast.

By round 2, the trading pattern is clear: iguana repeatedly sells RED for relatively little GREEN, whale repeatedly buys RED, and owl engages more cautiously, making only near-even trades. Publicly, both owl and iguana have strong evidence that whale consistently accepts trades where whale gives up GREEN to gain RED, even at mediocre or slightly unfavorable terms, revealing a strong preference for RED. Iguana, in contrast, has shown willingness to dump RED at aggressive discounts, signaling weak preference for RED. Owl sits in the middle and has so far made balanced trades that modestly improve their payoff. With this being the final round, there is no reason to hold back profitable trades: any remaining mutually beneficial swaps should be executed now. However, offer-makers must still calculate that the marginal gain from a trade outweighs the cost, and responders—especially whale, who already has rebalanced heavily into RED—must decide if the last trades are still worthwhile at the offered rates.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features a common-value chip (GREEN, always $0.50) and a privately valued chip (RED, either $0.20 or $0.60). Duck and Gorilla value red poorly ($0.20), while Kangaroo values red highly ($0.60). All players know green is worth $0.50 to everyone, but they are uncertain how others value red; they can only infer from observed proposals and acceptance behavior.

Incentives: Duck and Gorilla should be willing to give up red cheaply in exchange for green, because for them 1 GREEN ($0.50) is worth more than 1 RED ($0.20). Kangaroo, in contrast, prefers red over green: 1 RED ($0.60) exceeds 1 GREEN ($0.50). Thus, surplus-creating trades tend to move red chips from Duck/Gorilla to Kangaroo, and green chips from Kangaroo to Duck/Gorilla. However, each side tries to keep as much surplus as possible, leading to bargaining over the exact exchange rates (e.g., 1 RED for 1 GREEN vs. 2 GREEN, etc.).

Uncertainty and strategic behavior: Initially, no one knows who, if anyone, values red highly. Duck and Gorilla only know that for themselves red is low-value, and Kangaroo only knows that for herself red is high-value. Early trades can serve both as attempts to gain value and as signals of private red valuation: a player that is eager to buy red (especially at a premium) will look like a high-red-valuation type. Because multiple responders can accept and the partner is chosen randomly, proposers cannot tailor offers to a specific partner and must consider how attractive the deal is to all potential recipients. Over three rounds, players update their beliefs from observed trades and adjust their offers and acceptance thresholds accordingly. The most likely trajectory is that Kangaroo gradually accumulates red by offering green at terms favorable enough to convince at least one of the low-red players to part with red, while Duck and Gorilla focus on securing more green or at least not overpaying for red.

Given the short horizon (3 rounds, 3 turns per round), there is limited time to experiment. Players tend to start with cautious, slightly favorable offers to themselves. As they observe which offers get accepted, they refine beliefs: if Kangaroo reliably accepts red-for-green trades and proposes green-for-red trades, Duck and Gorilla infer that she values red more, and may try to extract more green per red in later rounds. Conversely, Kangaroo attempts to trade at ratios that remain just acceptable to at least one of the other two, without giving away all surplus. The resulting path is a mix of early conservative trades and gradually sharper terms as types become clearer and the game approaches its end.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 RED and 10 GREEN. No one yet knows others’ red valuations. Duck and Gorilla each privately know red is weak ($0.20), while Kangaroo privately knows red is strong ($0.60). We should expect tentative offers that test the market: low-red players tentatively try to turn some red into green, and Kangaroo looks for profitable ways to acquire red without immediately revealing how much she wants it.

Entering round 1, Duck has 9 RED / 11 GREEN, Gorilla 9 RED / 11 GREEN, and Kangaroo 12 RED / 8 GREEN. The two initial trades where Kangaroo accepted 1 RED for 1 GREEN suggest to Duck and Gorilla that Kangaroo may value red more than they do, but the evidence is still limited. Kangaroo, having cheaply acquired red, is ahead and now faces a trade-off: continue buying red aggressively (risking revealing her high valuation and forcing worse terms) or slow down and look for more favorable ratios. Duck and Gorilla may probe whether Kangaroo will accept slightly worse terms or may themselves avoid selling too much red too cheaply.

Before round 2, holdings are: Duck 8 RED / 12 GREEN, Gorilla 8 RED / 11 GREEN, Kangaroo 14 RED / 7 GREEN. Patterns are clearer: Kangaroo has consistently accepted 1-for-1 trades swapping GREEN for RED, strongly indicating a higher red valuation. Duck and Gorilla have each sold red several times at that rate, which is favorable for them given their low red value. With only one round left, players focus on locking in any remaining beneficial trades. Duck and Gorilla may still try to convert red to green on similar or slightly improved terms; Kangaroo is close to running out of green and must decide if further acquisitions of red are still worthwhile at end-of-game valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value chips identically: GREEN=$0.50 and RED=$0.80. That removes classic gains-from-trade based on valuation differences; any trade at non-1:1 rates destroys surplus for at least one party, and even 1:1 trades do not change anyone’s dollar value. However, players do not know others’ private valuations and only see public ranges for reds (wide) and a fixed value for greens. Each player knows green is common-value at $0.50, but they are uncertain whether others value red chips more or less than themselves within the public range. As a result, they may attempt trades that they believe are favorable given their private value for red, while worrying that better-informed opponents might only accept trades that are actually disadvantageous to the proposer. Strategic uncertainty centers on (1) whether others place a higher or lower value on reds, (2) whether others interpret a generous-looking trade as signaling low private red value, and (3) how aggressively to bid for reds versus preserving greens as a neutral, common-value asset. Because all three value chips the same, almost any non-1:1 red/green trade is actually a redistribution of surplus rather than an efficiency gain. Players may still propose such trades if they believe others’ red values are lower than their own or if they misinfer valuations from the wide public range. Over three rounds, we therefore expect a mix of cautious initial offers that hedge against exploitation, occasional slightly unbalanced trades that one side views as favorable, and some rejections where responders suspect the proposer is exploiting superior private information about red values. The exogenous proposer schedule gives duck-yellow-281 repeated initiative; if any player gradually infers that others value reds similarly, trading may slow as perceived arbitrage opportunities vanish, with players either refusing to move off close-to-1:1 rates or declining most trades entirely.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has symmetric holdings (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but only knows their own red value is $0.80 within the broad public range. A reasonable prior is that others might value red anywhere in that interval. Because $0.80 is toward the higher end of plausible values, each player suspects they may value reds more than at least some opponents and might try to acquire extra reds in exchange for greens. However, they also recognize that any trade too favorable to the proposer is unlikely to be accepted by a rational, similarly-informed opponent. In this first round, we expect exploratory offers: modestly unbalanced rates that test whether others reveal themselves as low or high red-valuers through their acceptance behavior. Rejections are likely when proposals look clearly disadvantageous given one’s own value for red.

After round 0, no trades have executed and holdings remain symmetric, but everyone has observed cautious or rejected offers. Duck’s first move tried to buy reds with a modestly unfavorable red price for the recipients (3 GREEN for 2 RED), signaling that duck privately values red highly relative to green. The universal rejection suggests others also place a relatively high value on reds. Dolphin’s 1:1 trade of 2 RED for 2 GREEN was rejected, likely because the responders—who value red more than green—see no gain in swapping equally. Swan’s 1:1 offer was similarly declined. Players now update their beliefs that others’ red values are not low; they infer that others are probably also near the top of the public value range. With reduced perceived arbitrage, proposals in round 1 are likely to be closer to break-even for the responder in an attempt to elicit at least one trade, but responders remain wary of even slightly unfavorable terms, as they increasingly suspect symmetric valuations.

Going into the final round, the only executed trade so far is dolphin’s purchase of 1 RED from Swan for 2 GREEN. Publicly, everyone sees Swan now holding an extra GREEN and one fewer RED, while Dolphin holds an extra RED and two fewer GREEN. Duck’s and Swan’s repeated rejections of 1:1 offers reinforce the inference that all three value reds at least as highly as duck and Dolphin do, so aggressive attempts to buy reds cheaply are unlikely to succeed. Swan’s acceptance of a slightly unfavorable trade (giving 1 RED for 2 GREEN, which is a value loss given their true valuations) could be interpreted by others as either a mistake under uncertainty or as evidence that Swan’s private value for reds might actually be lower than theirs. In this last round, duck and Dolphin may try one more time to exploit this perceived weakness in Swan’s red valuation, while Swan may either avoid further trades to prevent more loss or propose a balanced trade to rebalance holdings without obvious exploitation. However, with near-common knowledge that red is valuable, most non-1:1 offers remain unattractive, and additional trades are likely to be rejected unless a responder miscalculates or heavily discounts small value differences.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player, three-round bargaining game centers on exchanging red and green chips when all players know that green is worth exactly $0.50 to everyone, but red’s value is private and differs across players. Green is common-value and fairly high; red is potentially low or high and therefore more uncertain from others’ perspectives. The true private values are: pig: RED=$0.30 (low), bear: RED=$0.60 (moderate), hamster: RED=$1.00 (high). As an outside analyst, we know that hamster values red the most and pig the least, with bear in the middle. However, in the game, each player only knows their own red value and must infer others’ valuations from observed proposals and acceptances.

Because green is common-value, it serves as a natural medium of exchange and a clean reference point: any trade that gains green at the cost of red is attractive for a low-red-value player (like pig) and unattractive for a high-red-value player (like hamster), unless the rate of exchange is very favorable. That creates natural directional incentives: pig is inclined to sell red for green; hamster is inclined to buy red with green; bear is intermediate and can both buy and sell red depending on the rate. But the uncertainty about others’ red valuations means that proposers need to pick exchange rates that are attractive to at least one potential recipient while not giving away too much value from their own perspective.

Strategically, proposers must balance three forces: (1) value extraction—trying to gain more dollar value in expectation than they give up; (2) acceptability—setting terms generous enough that at least one other player is willing to accept; and (3) partner uncertainty—since multiple acceptances lead to a random partner, proposers cannot perfectly target the highest-value counterpart and must design trades that are acceptable to the likely counterpart type they want without being so generous that they lose surplus if a lower-valuation counterpart accepts instead. Responders decide based on a direct comparison of the value of chips they receive versus the value of chips they give up at their own private valuations, but also must anticipate that if the terms look too good, another player might also accept, reducing the expected gain via randomization.

Over three rounds, players observe public trade outcomes and chip holdings, updating inferred beliefs about each other’s red valuations. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be somewhat exploratory—testing what red-for-green exchange rates will be accepted. As information accumulates, later proposals can be more targeted, exploiting inferred high or low red values. Given the short horizon (only nine total proposal turns) and the fact that remaining chips at the end directly determine payoffs, players do not need to worry about future reputation beyond this single game; they instead focus on immediate expected value from each trade, subject to the evolving information revealed by earlier trades.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 red and 10 green chips and know only their own red value. Pig has low-value red and will be inclined to explore trades that convert red into green at moderate or even slightly unfavorable apparent rates, hoping someone values red more. Bear, with medium red value, will be cautiously interested in buying red if the price in green is low enough, or selling if someone oddly overpays for red. Hamster, with the highest red value, will be very interested in acquiring red if the green cost is modest. However, since no one yet knows others’ red valuations, the first few proposals are likely to be conservative tests around 1:1 or slightly biased exchange ratios between red and green, to see who bites.

By round 1, some information has been revealed. Pig’s initial willingness to sell 3 red for 2 green signals to others that pig probably values red relatively low. Hamster’s acceptance of that trade, plus bear’s later acceptance of an even 2-red-for-2-green swap, suggests that both hamster and bear see at least some value in acquiring red at roughly 1:1 rates with green. From hamster’s perspective, pig looks like a good future source of red at favorable terms, and bear looks like someone who is at least mildly red-hungry. From bear’s perspective, hamster’s earlier behavior hints at a reasonably high red valuation. Now proposers will try to refine terms to capture more surplus while still ensuring acceptances, especially between higher-red-valuing players and the lower-red-valuing pig.

Going into the final round, the history suggests that both bear and hamster are willing to buy red, especially when they can do so at roughly even or slightly favorable green-for-red rates, while pig seems consistently inclined to offload red but has started probing whether more aggressive prices (more red per green) might be acceptable. Players now understand more clearly that pig’s red valuation is relatively low and that at least one of bear or hamster is willing to pay over 1:1 in green for red. Since this is the last set of proposals, there is no need to preserve chips for future bargaining; each player focuses on immediate net gains in value. Risk of multi-acceptance still exists, but given only three players and specific histories, each proposer will try a final, slightly favorable trade that one counterpart is likely to accept while still leaving a margin of surplus.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game revolves around trading two chip types with different private values. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone, while red chips are privately valued: whale=$0.80, turtle=$0.20, swan=$0.10. This creates clear gains from trade: whale values reds most, turtle somewhat, and swan least; meanwhile, everyone values greens equally. Efficiently, red chips should migrate toward whale (and somewhat toward turtle) while green chips can be used as the currency to buy those reds. But players only know their own red values, and only see that green is worth the same to all. They must infer others’ valuations from the pattern of offers and acceptances. Because all players start with symmetric holdings (10 red, 10 green), differences in proposals signal how much someone wants or dislikes a chip type. Whale has the strongest incentive to acquire more reds, ideally paying in greens, but cannot reveal their exact red value; offering too generous a green-for-red rate would be unprofitable if others are low-valuers. Swan, who values reds very little, has the strongest incentive to sell red for green at relatively modest prices; however, swan does not know whale values red so highly, only that green is certainly safe at $0.50. Turtle is in the middle and may opportunistically buy or sell red depending on offered terms. Strategic tension arises because any trade must be simultaneously acceptable to at least one responder, and if both accept, the counterparty is chosen randomly, so proposers must design offers that are profitable regardless of which accepting player is matched. Over three short rounds with fixed proposer order, early offers act as both attempts at surplus creation and signals of preferences. Players balance current gains against the possibility that better offers might appear later, under uncertainty about others’ red valuations and about how aggressively each will try to exploit or reveal those valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no actual trade history exists, so players mainly test the waters and try to learn about others’ preferences. Whale, as the first proposer, is likely to float a relatively conservative green-for-red offer that is profitable given whale’s high red value but still potentially attractive to at least one partner if they value red weakly. Swan, knowing red is personally low-value, will tentatively try to offload some red for green, possibly at a steep red price in terms of green, not yet knowing whether someone else strongly values red. Turtle, with moderate red value, will react to whether early offers appear red-hungry (signaling a high red value player) or red-averse (signaling low valuations), and may lean more toward selling red early if the terms look favorable or remain cautious if the environment is unclear.

By the second round, players have observed that whale was willing to give up only 0.5 green per red (offering 1 green for 2 red), hinting at a relatively high red value, and that turtle was willing to sell red at that low green price. Swan attempted a one-for-one red-to-green swap that found no takers, suggesting that both whale and turtle view red as at least as valuable as green at par. Turtle then sold red 1:1 for green to swan, revealing more red-weakness than swan expected. Now whale’s incentive to keep acquiring red is even clearer, but both potential partners realize that someone must value red strongly. This creates more informed bargaining: swan should be more willing to sell red at modest green prices, and turtle may be more cautious about further red sales unless terms clearly beat green’s safe value.

Entering the final round, whale has accumulated a noticeable red surplus at relatively favorable prices, confirming to others that someone is red-hungry. Swan has shed several low-value red chips for green, moving toward a portfolio heavy in green, which is always safe. Turtle has sold some red but at varying rates, now more aware that whale is willing to pay nontrivial green-to-red ratios. With only one more proposal each, players have little incentive to hide preferences and focus instead on extracting final gains from trade: swan tries to offload remaining reds at any acceptable green rate; turtle is cautious about parting with more reds unless paid well; whale still seeks red but must balance profitability with the need to make an offer attractive enough that at least one partner will accept given their now-better-informed expectations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 to everyone. RED is privately valued: frog values RED at $0.60 (high), badger at $0.50 (medium), and hedgehog at $0.20 (low). Only each player knows their own valuation; they only know that others’ valuations lie in a broad common range. This creates potential gains from trade: hedgehog, who likely sees RED as relatively weak compared to GREEN, will often be willing to give up RED for GREEN, while frog in particular has strong incentives to acquire RED from others at a price that keeps some surplus. Badger is nearly indifferent between RED and GREEN in value but may still trade if they can marginally improve or hedge against mispricing. However, players also fear leaving money on the table: they do not know others’ exact valuations, so they are wary of trades that look too good for the other side. Since accepting decisions are simultaneous and any multiple acceptances are resolved by random selection, senders must anticipate both the probability of being accepted at all and the risk that their offer is so generous it is snapped up immediately by the ‘wrong’ counterparty. Over three rounds, each player proposes once per round, so there are nine total proposal opportunities. Early in the game, we expect exploratory offers that test others’ willingness to trade RED for GREEN. As history reveals who is eager to move RED away or hoard it, later proposals can better target the player most likely to accept at an advantageous rate. Because trades must involve offering one color and requesting the other, the core bargaining dimension is the RED:GREEN exchange ratio implicit in each proposal. Frog will try to acquire RED at a low GREEN price; hedgehog will try to sell RED for as much GREEN as possible; badger will often accept or propose near-even exchanges, leaning in whichever direction seems to yield a small edge. Not all turns will execute trades, as players may reject offers that appear to disadvantage them given their private valuations and limited time horizon.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, everyone starts with symmetric holdings but asymmetric private values. No one has yet observed others’ behavior, so proposals are likely to be conservative and somewhat exploratory, revealing hints about how much each player values RED relative to GREEN. Each player will try to gauge whether others are eager buyers or sellers of RED. Because there are many chips and three full rounds ahead, players can afford to decline marginal trades they suspect may be unfavorable, preferring to wait for clearer information later.

By the start of round 1, everyone has observed that hedgehog was willing to trade 1 RED for 2 GREEN and that badger accepted this, revealing hedgehog as relatively low-valuation for RED and badger as at least somewhat comfortable paying a premium in GREEN for RED. Frog’s even 1:1 offer for RED was earlier declined by both, so frog now knows others are not eager to surrender RED cheaply. This pushes frog toward offering slightly more GREEN per RED to induce trades, while hedgehog may continue trying to offload RED at favorable rates. Badger, having slightly increased their RED holdings and reduced GREEN, becomes more cautious about overpaying further but may still respond to attractive opportunities.

Entering the final round, patterns are clearer: hedgehog has repeatedly signaled willingness to sell RED for GREEN, and frog has successfully bought RED from hedgehog at a modest GREEN premium. Badger has shown selective willingness to pay for RED but is now more RED-heavy and GREEN-light than at the start, making further premium purchases less attractive. With no future rounds, players focus on locking in immediate gains instead of preserving bargaining position. Frog will still pursue profitable RED purchases, especially from hedgehog, while hedgehog attempts to convert remaining RED into GREEN at acceptable rates. Badger now mainly responds opportunistically, taking only clearly beneficial trades.
