parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game centers on exchanging red and green chips with asymmetric private values for red and a common known value for green ($0.50 each). Every player starts with 10 red and 10 green chips. Since green is known to be worth $0.50 to everyone, while each player privately values red chips differently (mouse: $0.30, unicorn: $0.20, tiger: $0.10), green is the high-value and focal medium of exchange, and red is relatively low value with cross-player gains from trade. Mouse is the highest red-valuer, unicorn intermediate, tiger the lowest. Efficiently, red chips should flow from tiger to unicorn to mouse and green chips should flow in the opposite direction, because group surplus rises when low-red-value agents sell red to higher-red-value agents in exchange for green. However, no player knows the others’ red values, only that red’s possible value is between $0.10 and $1.00. This uncertainty means every trade involves both value creation (when red moves to a higher valuer in exchange for green) and signaling/bargaining over the implied price of red in green. Proposers must offer trades that are privately profitable for themselves given their own valuations and that are at least weakly appealing for the unknown potential recipients. They must also consider that if more than one partner accepts, the recipient is chosen at random, so they cannot narrowly tailor offers to just one partner. Over three rounds, each player has three proposal turns, and all players observe previous trades and implied prices, which gradually reveal partial information about who likely values red more or less. Early proposals are likely to be exploratory and skewed toward the proposer, often declined when they look too one-sided. As the game progresses, players may converge on mutually beneficial red-for-green prices: low-red-value players (especially tiger) should try to sell red for green at low red:green ratios that are still attractive to higher-red-value players, while mouse should try to buy red with green at favorable prices. Unicorn is in the middle, sometimes acting as a bridge: willing to both buy red from tiger and sell it to mouse if the implied prices create arbitrage gains. Because there are only three rounds and three proposers per round, players face a tension between aggressive bargaining and securing sure but modest positive-value trades before the game ends. The most likely outcome is a handful of accepted trades in which mouse and unicorn gradually accumulate more red in exchange for some green, tiger sheds red for green, and some potential gains from trade remain unrealized due to limited time, signaling frictions, and the random resolution of multiple acceptances.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 red and 10 green, and no one has any information about others’ exact red values beyond the common range. Proposers in round 0 are likely to experiment with relatively conservative offers that test how others value red versus green. Tiger, with the lowest red value, is naturally inclined to sell red for green but must guess how generous to be to elicit acceptance. Mouse, who highly values red, is inclined to buy red with green but wants to avoid overpaying. Unicorn sits in the middle and may experiment in either direction. Because the game is just beginning and there is no price history, many early offers may be slightly too skewed toward the proposer and therefore declined, or else accepted only when they align with plausible cross-player gains from trade. Players will watch who accepts what to infer relative red valuations for later rounds.

After round 0, some information has been revealed through behavior. Tiger successfully sold red to unicorn at a ratio of 3 red for 2 green, suggesting tiger is quite willing to part with red and unicorn has at least moderate value for it. Mouse then bought red from unicorn at 2 red for 1 green, revealing mouse may value red more than unicorn does and that unicorn is willing to intermediate when margins are positive. By the start of round 1, tiger has slightly more green and fewer red, unicorn has rebalanced closer to its original bundle after acting as a middleman, and mouse holds a bit more red and slightly less green. Participants can infer a rough ordering of red values (mouse highest, tiger lowest, unicorn in between). New proposals are likely to refine trades along similar implied prices, with tiger trying to offload more red, mouse looking for further red purchases at reasonable rates, and unicorn focusing on trades that preserve or increase its total value while possibly arbitraging small price gaps.

By round 2, the pattern is clear: tiger repeatedly sells red for modest amounts of green, mouse repeatedly buys red with green at roughly 2 red for 1 green, and unicorn is cautious about selling red too cheaply after seeing mouse’s willingness to buy red. The trading history strongly suggests mouse has the highest red valuation, tiger the lowest, and unicorn intermediate. Because it is the final round, each proposer must decide whether to secure one last profitable trade or hold their remaining green and red. Tiger is still willing to convert low-value red into green if someone will pay, mouse still benefits from buying red at earlier-implied prices, and unicorn will accept only trades that clearly improve its payoff given the looming end of the game. The last-round nature reduces the value of signaling and increases the importance of immediate, individual surplus from each trade, leading to more straightforward, near-fair exchanges and fewer speculative or exploratory offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This is a three-player, three-round bargaining game with one efficient chip type (GREEN, common value $0.50 to everyone) and one privately valued chip type (RED) where values differ across players. Everyone starts with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. GREEN is symmetric and relatively valuable for all ($0.50 each). RED is cheap for parrot-blue-2249 ($0.10), highly valuable for fox-orange-371 ($0.70), and medium for bear-orange-314 ($0.50). Because only private values are known individually, players must infer others’ valuations from observed offers, asks, and acceptances.

Incentives: fox has the strongest incentive to accumulate RED and is willing to give up GREEN at a high RED:GREEN ratio that still leaves fox better off. Parrot should be most willing to dump RED cheaply to gain GREEN, because parrot’s RED is worth far less than their GREEN. Bear is roughly indifferent between RED and GREEN in value terms and may act as a more cautious or opportunistic trader depending on the inferred prices. Social surplus is created when RED moves from parrot (who values it least) to fox (who values it most) in exchange for GREEN, and potentially when bear trades with either side if the price is favorable.

Uncertainty: at the outset, each player knows only their own valuation and the public ranges, not the exact values of others. They will treat GREEN as obviously important, but RED’s value to others is uncertain. Parrot doesn’t know if others value RED more or less than $0.10, fox doesn’t know if the others value RED close to $0.70 or much less, and bear doesn’t know if others are above or below $0.50. Early-round proposals tend to be exploratory, revealing how eager others are to trade and at what rate. Because acceptances are private but outcomes and chip holdings are visible, later rounds will incorporate inferences from prior observed trades. Players are myopically self-interested and must also consider that multiple acceptances lead to a random partner: they may tailor offers that are attractive enough for at least one target without being too generous to all.

Over three rounds with one proposal per player per round, each player gets three chances to initiate trades. The likely pattern is: (1) exploratory but somewhat conservative proposals in round 0, (2) more targeted trades in round 1 as values are better inferred, and (3) final adjustments in round 2 where players attempt to lock in favorable chip mixes near the end of the game. Fox is expected to push hardest to acquire RED, parrot to convert RED into GREEN, and bear to selectively accept trades that generate clear value gains without giving away too many GREEN chips for underpriced RED or vice versa.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is the initial feeling-out phase. No one yet has evidence about others’ exact RED valuations, only priors based on the public range (RED could be anywhere from $0.10 to $1.00). Players often start with conservative, nearly value-safe proposals that would profit them even if the counterparty’s value is moderately similar. Fox is likely to test the waters by trying to buy RED with GREEN while still preserving surplus; parrot may try to offload small amounts of low-value RED for GREEN; bear will be cautious and may pass on early trades that are only marginally favorable or unclear.

After round 0, everyone sees that fox successfully bought RED from bear at a rate of 2 RED for 1 GREEN. This strongly suggests that fox values RED relatively highly, and at least bear thought that price was acceptable. Parrot learns that offering 1 RED for 1 GREEN was unattractive; that makes it clear that others do not see RED as so low-value that they will accept a 1:1 swap. Bear’s willingness to part with 2 RED for 1 GREEN reveals that bear is not dramatically more attached to RED than to GREEN. In round 1, players adjust: parrot may improve the concession on RED when buying GREEN, fox feels validated in pushing to acquire more RED, and bear becomes more selective about parting with RED at too low a price.

By round 2, much of the strategic uncertainty is reduced. Everyone has seen fox consistently trying to accumulate RED, parrot increasingly willing to dump RED for GREEN, and bear sometimes selling RED while remaining price sensitive. The end of the game is approaching, so players will try to finalize a chip portfolio close to their value optimum. Parrot, whose RED is low value, will likely continue to trade away RED when possible; fox, who strongly values RED, aims to grab any last RED at acceptable terms; and bear may either exploit any remaining mispricings or decline trades that are not clearly favorable given the imminent end of play.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features two chip types (RED, GREEN) with slightly heterogeneous private values for RED and common value for GREEN. Everyone starts symmetric in quantities (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but asymmetrically in values: monkey-pink-6874 (monkey) values RED at $0.80, buffalo-orange-6610 (buffalo) and goose-purple-9985 (goose) each value RED at $0.70, while all value GREEN at $0.50. Thus, monkey is the strongest RED-holder, while buffalo and goose are slightly less RED-oriented but still weakly prefer RED to GREEN. Because all players privately know only their own valuations, they begin with uncertainty about how much others favor RED versus GREEN within the announced public range. Viewed ex ante, a rational player will suspect that others also value RED at or above GREEN, but they cannot be sure who has the highest RED value. This creates a thin-margin bargaining environment: any red-for-green trade that is strictly profitable for both sides generally requires that the proposer suspects the counterparty values RED less and GREEN relatively more than they do. With these specific values, potential gains from trade are modest and mostly run from greener-to-redder players (if any player valued GREEN relatively more than RED). Because no such player exists here (RED is strictly more valuable than GREEN to all), nearly all cross-color trades are negative or zero surplus from at least one side’s perspective if they are not accompanied by favorable ratios. Still, players might experiment in early turns with small, nearly fair trades to probe others’ willingness to exchange chips and infer their valuations. Over three rounds of proposals, we would expect: (1) early, conservative offers near perceived indifference ratios, often involving 1–3 chips; (2) learning based on accept/decline patterns, gradually revealing that others also like RED and are reluctant to part with it; and (3) either a tapering of trade attempts or highly skewed proposals that go mostly declined. The randomization of the recipient when multiple accept introduces further uncertainty: a proposer cannot reliably target a particular counterparty, so they must design offers that are acceptable to at least one responder but robust to possibly going to the other. This tends to push offers closer to “generic” fair terms or to offers clearly favorable to all possible acceptors. Given the small valuation differences (0.80 vs 0.70 vs 0.50) and the public knowledge that RED tends to be valuable, the likely equilibrium pattern is low trade volume and few accepted offers unless someone significantly misjudges others’ values or intentionally makes slightly generous offers to induce trade. The following forecasted game assumes moderately cautious, value-conscious play with some initial experimentation and rapid learning that RED is generally prized by all three players.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has observed any prior behavior, so all three players are in pure uncertainty about others’ exact RED values, though they know the public range and that GREEN is fixed at $0.50. Monkey, with a relatively high RED value, is likely to be reluctant to part with RED unless getting GREEN in a favorable ratio, while buffalo and goose—each valuing RED at $0.70—also see RED as better than GREEN but might be slightly more open to parting with RED than monkey if they suspect some players may value RED less. Early proposals are likely to be small experiments, near perceived fairness, to test whether others strongly want RED or GREEN. Players will pay close attention to acceptances and rejections in this round to update beliefs about others’ preferences.

After round 0, no trades have executed: everyone remains at 10 RED and 10 GREEN. All three players have now seen that simple 1:1 or 2:2 RED-for-GREEN swaps are unattractive to others, revealing that both counterparts also likely value RED at least as much as GREEN. This pushes them to adjust expectations: each player infers that the others probably have a relatively high RED value, so profitable trades will require more skewed terms (offering more GREEN per RED when trying to acquire RED, or requesting more GREEN per RED when giving up RED). However, skewed trades that are good for the counterpart are often bad for the proposer given their own valuations. With limited bargaining rounds, players might test slightly asymmetric offers, but they are also wary of making strongly unprofitable trades just to induce an exchange. As a result, round 1 is likely to show cautious, slightly more lopsided proposals that still mostly get declined.

Going into round 2, one trade has executed: in round 1, goose traded away 3 RED for 2 GREEN with buffalo. This move suggests that goose may either undervalue RED relative to buffalo and monkey or is willing to sacrifice some value to rebalance holdings or experiment. From buffalo’s perspective, accepting that trade revealed that buffalo was happy to gain RED at a modest GREEN cost, signaling a fairly strong preference for RED. Monkey, who did not participate in that trade, updates beliefs that both buffalo and goose still see RED as valuable, but goose is perhaps somewhat more flexible. Current holdings are: monkey at 10 RED, 10 GREEN; buffalo at 13 RED, 8 GREEN; goose at 7 RED, 12 GREEN. With only one round left, players have limited time to improve payoffs. Monkey has a balanced but high-value RED position; buffalo is RED-heavy and GREEN-light; goose is RED-light and GREEN-heavy. Given all value RED more than GREEN, there is now even less mutual-surplus scope for trade: buffalo is unlikely to sell RED cheaply, goose is reluctant to give up scarce RED, and monkey needs strong terms to give up high-value RED. Round 2 thus features final attempts at modestly favorable exchanges, but given the now-clear pattern that everyone wants RED, these are forecast to be declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and green chips with privately known red-chip values and common-value green chips. Everyone publicly knows that green chips are worth $0.50 to all players, so green serves as a transparent reference asset. Red chips, however, are worth $0.30 to dog-blue-7632, $0.70 to cat-purple-384, and $0.50 to rabbit-blue-2391, but each participant initially only knows their own values. As they see proposals and accept/reject patterns, they may infer others’ valuations, especially who seems to value red more. Since there are only three rounds and each player gets three proposal turns, participants face a short horizon where immediate profitable trades can be more attractive than waiting for perfect information. The key inefficiencies arise because senders must offer trades that are (a) strictly profit-improving for at least one potential counterparty while (b) not obviously dominated by worse beliefs about others’ values, all under the constraint that any accepting partner could be chosen at random if multiple accept. The core strategic tension is around red chips: cat likely wants to accumulate red and is willing to part with green, while dog is inclined to sell red for green, and rabbit is in between. However, no one knows these exact relative valuations, so early offers tend to be conservative: modestly favorable to the sender but not so exploitative that they’re universally rejected. Over time, observed acceptances and refusals reveal that cat seems keen on acquiring red, dog is more willing to give red away, and rabbit behaves flexibly. This induces increasingly targeted offers: dog tries to sell red to cat or rabbit for green; cat tries to buy red from dog or rabbit using green; rabbit looks for small arbitrage-like gains. Uncertainty about who will accept a given deal, combined with the random selection among multiple acceptors, means proposers must avoid offers that could accidentally give surplus to the wrong counterpart (from their perspective). The resulting path is a sequence of small, plausible trades where each accepted deal strictly increases both parties’ monetary value given their own valuations, while rejections typically reflect offers that fail to give enough surplus to likely higher-value partners or are misaligned with inferred preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric endowments (10 red, 10 green) but asymmetric values for red. No one knows others’ red values but they know green is common-value at $0.50. This first round is about information gathering and setting baselines: dog, with low red value, is inclined to test trades that swap red for green; cat, who privately values red highly, will tentatively try to buy red; rabbit is in the middle and may experiment with both directions. Because beliefs about others’ valuations are still diffuse, offers are likely to be modest and simple—such as one color for the other in small quantities—designed to elicit accept/reject responses that reveal who seems to value red more. Any accepted trade here not only generates value but also serves as a signal about the recipient’s preferences.

By the second round, some structure has emerged. Dog has revealed a willingness to trade red away for green, signaling relatively low red value. Cat has signaled high demand for red by offering favorable red-for-green terms and dog has accepted, hinting to dog that cat likely values red more. Rabbit has accepted one modest red-for-green trade and rejected another, suggesting intermediate valuation and some selectivity. Given these observations, dog should now target cat more explicitly with offers that sell red for green at rates still personally profitable. Cat, having learned that dog is a good red source, will seek to buy more red from dog and might test rabbit with slightly less generous terms to see if rabbit also values red. Rabbit, seeing cat’s apparent appetite for red and dog’s seeming eagerness to sell, may probe opportunities that yield small surplus without giving up too much of either color. However, because acceptance is simultaneous and recipient selection is random when multiple accept, proposers must shape offers so the realistically expected accepting partner still leaves them better off.

Entering the final round, players have clearer but still imperfect beliefs. Dog likely sees that cat consistently buys red and rarely sells it, inferring that cat has a relatively high red value. Rabbit’s behavior suggests moderate red value: willing to trade when terms are good but not as aggressive as cat. With only one proposal turn left for each player, there is little value in holding chips beyond this round, so they focus on extracting final surplus. Dog is happy to offload remaining red against green if possible, especially to cat. Cat would like to acquire more red if the rate is favorable and might also shed any excess green that doesn’t convert into valuable red. Rabbit, knowing time is almost up, will only accept clearly profitable trades and is unlikely to make speculative offers. Offers now are more polarized: they either closely match inferred counterpart valuations (and get accepted) or are too demanding and are rejected, since there is no future benefit to signaling or reputation.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this 3-player, 2-chip-type bargaining game, all players start symmetrically in chip counts (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but differ slightly in how much they privately value RED. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone; RED is $0.50 for ram-pink-8623 and only $0.40 for squid-yellow-4597 and otter-pink-5988. This means ram is the high-value RED holder, while squid and otter perceive RED as slightly less attractive than GREEN. Since all chips already have positive value and players cannot convert between colors except via trade, the only strict surplus-creating trades (from the experimenter’s perspective) are those that shift RED chips from low-valuation players to the high-valuation player ram, in exchange for GREEN going the other direction. From each player’s subjective perspective, however, they don’t know exactly how others value RED; they only know the common range and that others might value RED somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00. Thus, ram is willing to give up GREEN for RED at moderately favorable rates; squid and otter, if they suspect others might value RED more than they do, may try to sell off RED for GREEN, but will want to avoid obviously dominated trades. Strategically, everyone knows that a proposal must appeal to at least one other player while not sacrificing too much of the proposer’s own value. The simultaneous-response rule plus random tie-breaking means proposers can’t pick their counterparty if both accept, so they try to craft offers that at least one target should like, without being so generous that both rivals eagerly jump in. Over three short rounds with fixed proposal order (ram, then squid, then otter each round), players have limited time to learn about others’ values from observed acceptances and rejections. The likely dynamic is that early offers are exploratory and somewhat conservative: modest 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 swaps with slight edges to the proposer, testing others’ willingness to trade RED for GREEN. As rejections accumulate, proposers may shade slightly more generous to induce at least one acceptance before the game ends, especially if they see opportunities to reallocate RED away from low-valuation holders toward higher-valuation holders. Because the valuation gap here is small ($0.10 per RED between ram and the others), many marginal trades are nearly break-even in subjective terms, so the game is likely to see few trades and mostly cautious proposals whose acceptances signal something about private RED valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have identical holdings (10 RED, 10 GREEN) and no information about others’ RED valuations beyond the common range. Strategic pressure is low because there are still three full rounds ahead. Proposals this round are likely to be exploratory: small swaps that slightly favor the proposer while testing whether others are inclined to trade RED for GREEN or vice versa. Ram, as the first mover, may attempt to acquire RED using GREEN at an advantageous rate if they suspect others value RED less. Squid and otter, who themselves value RED slightly less than GREEN on a per-dollar comparison, may experiment with offers that unload some RED for GREEN without being too generous, trying to see how much appetite there is for RED among the others.

Going into round 1, players have observed that squid and otter completed a symmetric 2-for-2 RED-for-GREEN trade, revealing that at least otter is comfortable swapping RED for GREEN one-for-one, consistent with a relatively low RED valuation. Squid was also willing to propose that swap, suggesting they, too, do not value RED above GREEN. Ram infers that both others likely value RED at or below GREEN and sees an opportunity to acquire RED at fair or slightly favorable rates. Squid now has more RED and fewer GREEN; otter has fewer RED and more GREEN. This asymmetry can shape proposals: squid may want to rebalance back toward GREEN, and otter might hesitate to give up further GREEN without clear gain. Ram, observing the revealed willingness to trade RED for GREEN, will attempt a modest but more targeted offer to pick up RED from one of the low-valuation players.

By round 2, the final round, trade history has made the relative RED valuations fairly clear: ram persistently tries to acquire RED via GREEN, and both squid and otter have at times been willing to give up RED for GREEN at or near 1:1. After ram’s successful 3-for-3 swap with squid in round 1, ram now holds the highest RED stack and fewer GREEN, while squid has moved toward more GREEN and fewer RED, and otter remains relatively GREEN-rich but cautious. With only three turns left in the game, each player faces a last chance to adjust their portfolio. Ram may attempt one more value-neutral or slightly unfavorable-looking 1:1 swap to pull in extra RED, banking on others’ low RED valuations. Squid, having already signaled low RED value, might still trade if they see no downside, while otter, with substantial GREEN, will be wary of trading away GREEN without a clear subjective gain. The short horizon and modest valuation differences make further trades unlikely unless framed as essentially risk-free from the responder’s point of view.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. RED is privately valued: camel-pink-767 loves RED the most ($1.00), butterfly-yellow-2139 has moderate value ($0.60), and alligator-orange-9612 values RED the least ($0.30). All players know the public range for RED (0.10–1.00) and know GREEN is fixed at $0.50, but each only knows their own exact RED value.

This structure creates a natural incentive for surplus-generating trades: alligator-orange-9612 should be relatively willing to give up RED (low private value) for GREEN (high common value), while camel-pink-767 should be willing to pay a premium in GREEN to acquire more RED. Butterfly-yellow-2139 sits in the middle and might trade with either side depending on terms. However, because of incomplete information, players cannot perfectly tailor offers. They must infer others’ RED valuations from the public range and from observed behavior.

Strategically, each proposer must choose one chip color to give and one to receive, asking for a positive quantity of one and offering a positive quantity of the other, without exceeding current holdings. Responsive players decide whether to accept based purely on their own valuation and any inferences about how desperate or informed the proposer might be. When multiple responders accept, a random recipient is chosen, so proposals might be designed to appeal primarily to one target while still being marginally acceptable to another.

Over three rounds, with each player proposing once per round, the likely pattern is: early conservative, somewhat exploratory offers (to test whether others will trade RED for GREEN and at what implicit price), followed by more targeted proposals once some information has been revealed through acceptances or refusals. Camel is likely to attempt to buy RED using GREEN; Alligator is likely to sell RED for GREEN when offered a favorable rate; Butterfly will sometimes sell RED to Camel and sometimes buy RED from Alligator if the price is attractive. Because only a few moves are available, not all Pareto-improving trades will be realized, and some offers that would on paper create surplus may be declined if they look too unfavorable from the responder’s private perspective or if responders suspect the proposer is exploiting them.

The forecast below assumes moderately rational, myopic, but value-sensitive behavior: players roughly compare the value of what they give up to what they receive at their own valuations, accept clear individual gains, reject clear losses, and make proposals that seem likely to be accepted by at least one counterpart, without perfectly optimizing under uncertainty.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold symmetric portfolios: 10 RED and 10 GREEN. No trade history exists yet, so beliefs about others’ RED values are based only on the common range. Camel, with the highest RED valuation, has a strong incentive to attempt to acquire RED by paying in GREEN, but must avoid offering too generous a rate. Butterfly may try to profit by selling some RED to someone who appears to value it highly or by buying cheap RED if offered by a low-valuation player. Alligator, with very low RED value, is likely to accept trades that convert RED into GREEN at most reasonable rates and may also propose such conversions when in the proposer role. This first round is thus largely exploratory: players test market terms to see who is willing to trade and at what implicit exchange rates between RED and GREEN.

Entering round 1, players have already learned something from round 0 behavior. Camel successfully bought RED from Alligator at 1:1 (GREEN for RED), revealing that at least one counterpart is willing to trade RED away at that rate. Later, Alligator offered 3 RED for 2 GREEN, and Butterfly accepted, indicating that Butterfly is willing to acquire RED when it is relatively cheap. Portfolios have diverged: Camel now holds more RED and fewer GREEN, Alligator has more GREEN and fewer RED, and Butterfly has modestly increased RED and reduced GREEN. This pattern suggests a value hierarchy: Camel values RED very highly, Butterfly somewhat, Alligator the least. In round 1, Camel can try again to purchase RED, possibly at a slightly more aggressive rate. Butterfly may attempt to flip some RED to Camel at a profit or acquire GREEN back. Alligator, having gained GREEN and lost RED, may still be willing to sell RED but at somewhat tougher terms after seeing willing buyers on both sides.

By round 2, trade history strongly reinforces the ordering of RED valuations: Camel consistently buys RED; Alligator consistently sells RED for GREEN; Butterfly switches sides depending on the rate, sometimes buying cheap RED and sometimes selling when the terms favor GREEN. Portfolios now reflect these roles: Camel is relatively RED-heavy and GREEN-poor, Alligator is GREEN-rich and RED-poor, and Butterfly is in between. With only one round left, players are likely to make more aggressive, value-driven proposals to lock in gains. Camel might accept slightly worse terms if additional RED is still very profitable at her valuation. Alligator will likely continue unloading remaining RED when the GREEN payment looks favorable. Butterfly, seeing clear demand from Camel and supply from Alligator, may attempt intermediary trades if capacities allow, but with only three final proposals, each offer tends to be simple and aimed at obvious surplus: Camel buying from Alligator, Alligator selling to either partner, and Butterfly trading opportunistically if a profitable price emerges.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game centers on trading red and green chips with private values. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Red chips are worth $0.30 to owl-blue-3422 and $0.60 to both dolphin-red-8512 and iguana-yellow-5193. All begin with 10 red and 10 green. This creates clear, though privately known, comparative advantages: owl is the low-value holder of red and thus has a strong incentive to sell red in exchange for green when it can get more than $0.30 of value per red, while dolphin and iguana are high-value red holders and gain from buying red from owl at any effective price between $0.30 and $0.60, and from each other only if they can pay less than $0.60 in value per red. However, each player only knows their own valuations, so they must infer others’ values from observed offers and acceptances. Early in the game, offers are likely to be exploratory and somewhat generous to tempt at least one acceptance and learn about others’ preferences. Because multiple acceptances trigger random partner selection, proposers cannot target a specific counterpart and must design offers that are robustly beneficial with either potential recipient. As trading history accumulates, players refine beliefs about others: owl learns that others likely value red more if they eagerly buy red; dolphin and iguana learn about owl’s red value from owl’s willingness to trade it away, and may infer each other’s valuations from how aggressively they bid for red or green. The finite three-round horizon and one proposal per player per round create some urgency but also encourage gradual experimentation. By the final round, proposers are likely to push harder for surplus extraction, proposing trades closer to their inferred acceptance thresholds. Across the nine turns, we should expect several mutually beneficial trades of red from owl to at least one of the high-red-value players, plus occasional failed offers where proposers misjudge the minimum surplus needed for acceptance or are too ambitious in the final stages.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has seen any behavior yet, and each player only knows their own values. Owl suspects red might be more valuable to others (common in these tasks) and will likely test selling red for green at a moderately favorable rate to both sides. Dolphin and iguana, each valuing red more than green, suspect others might value red less and will tentatively try to buy red if they can do so at an attractive rate, but without being sure whether owl or the other high-red-value player will accept. Offers will tend to be symmetric and simple (small integer quantities) to probe acceptance ranges and start revealing who is relatively high or low on each color.

After round 0, history suggests that owl is willing to part with red at modestly favorable rates, while both dolphin and iguana refused to sell large amounts of green for red, hinting that they may also value red relatively highly or at least are cautious about overpaying. Owl updates that red is likely more valuable to at least dolphin, and probably iguana as well, given their reluctance to sell green for red at the tested terms. In round 1, owl will refine its selling terms, perhaps improving the price it gets for red while still leaving positive surplus to induce acceptance. Dolphin and iguana, having seen owl’s willingness to sell, are more confident that buying red from owl can be profitable and may concentrate offers toward owl rather than each other, where surplus is thinner. Failed offers in this round will tend to be those that overreach based on still-rough beliefs.

By round 2, the players have clearer signals: owl has repeatedly sold red and bought green, revealing low red value; dolphin and iguana both have shown willingness to buy red from owl at moderate rates but remain reluctant to trade between themselves at thin margins. Each knows this is the final round, so proposers will attempt to secure one last beneficial trade, often pushing terms closer to the edge of expected acceptability. Owl will try to sell remaining red at a relatively favorable price in green, while dolphin and iguana aim to pick up any additional red from owl at attractive terms or, failing that, might make last-ditch offers to each other, though those are less likely to be accepted. Because beliefs are sharper, some moderately generous offers are now anticipated and accepted quickly, while more aggressive final attempts may be turned down as too one-sided.
