parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game centers on trading red and green chips with privately known red values and common-value green chips. Each player starts with 10 red and 10 green chips. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so green is a natural medium of exchange and provides an anchor: anyone giving up green wants at least $0.50 of expected value per chip. Red values differ: turtle=$1.00, elephant=$0.60, pig=$0.90. From an analyst perspective, turtle has the strongest incentive to accumulate red (high personal value and likely believes others value red less on average), elephant has the weakest incentive to hold red and is relatively more willing to trade red away for green, and pig is intermediate. However, each player only sees their own red value plus the common prior range for others, so they face uncertainty about others’ exact valuations and therefore about how attractive any given red↔green rate will look. Bargaining dynamics are driven by simultaneous, private accept/decline decisions and randomization among multiple accepters, which means proposers cannot pick their partner and must price trades so that at least one counterparty strictly benefits in expected value while also not being so generous that both counterparts eagerly accept (if that randomness is undesirable). Over only three rounds, each with one proposal per player, players will tend to favor simple, clearly profitable trades: elephant tries to offload red for green at rates where red is cheap to them but may still look attractive to high-red-value counterparts; turtle and pig try to pick off these advantageous offers while avoiding overpaying green for red. Early turns are exploratory and reveal some information about willingness to trade at particular terms; later turns react to these signals and to the evolving chip holdings. Given the short horizon and lack of penalties for failed offers, many proposals will be somewhat aggressive (favorable to the proposer), with a mix of declined and accepted trades as players probe the others’ valuations within the plausible range implied by the common prior about chip values.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 red and 10 green chips and know only their own red value and the common green value. The strategic tension is between information gathering and immediate surplus extraction. Turtle, with the highest red value, will likely try to buy red for green at an aggressive rate that is still plausibly acceptable to at least one counterpart. Elephant, knowing red is relatively low value to them, is inclined to sell red for green but must guess how much others value red. Pig sits in the middle and is willing to trade in either direction if a clear gain emerges. Players are particularly uncertain about others’ red valuations, so early offers serve as both profit opportunities and signals of how the proposer perceives the red/green tradeoff.

By the start of round 1, trade history has revealed that elephant was willing to trade away red for green on fairly generous terms (selling 3 red for 2 green), suggesting elephant’s red valuation is relatively low. Turtle successfully acquired red at break-even green terms for elephant (from turtle’s perspective this is profitable, given their higher red value), and pig took a favorable red-for-green deal from elephant. Both turtle and pig now infer that elephant is a likely future source of cheap red and that pig and turtle both value red more than elephant does. This creates a more competitive environment for any future offers involving elephant’s red. With some information revealed, proposers will refine their offers: elephant may continue to offload red but at slightly improved terms for themselves, while turtle and pig try to position offers that exploit perceived valuation gaps without prompting both counterparts to accept.

Going into the final round, patterns are fairly clear: elephant consistently trades away red for green, indicating a low private valuation of red; pig and turtle are net acquirers of red, with turtle seemingly the most aggressive in pursuing red. Everyone now expects future red-for-green offers from elephant to be attractive to both turtle and pig, so any such offers may elicit multiple acceptances, introducing random partner selection risk. In this last round, players face a finite horizon: there is no value in saving attractive trades for later, so they are willing to propose their best remaining opportunities. Turtle aims to buy red cheaply one more time but may push terms too far and be declined. Elephant likely sets terms closer to their own indifference point, still making red-for-green trades that remain acceptable to at least one higher-red-value counterpart. Pig tries to exploit remaining surplus but must be wary of overpaying green for marginal red gains in the final moves.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so 1 GREEN is always worth $0.50 to every player. RED is privately valued and heterogeneous: kangaroo values RED at $0.10 (very low), fox at $0.70 (very high), and gorilla at $0.40 (moderate). This creates asymmetric incentives: fox strongly wants to accumulate RED from either partner; gorilla is also a net buyer of RED from kangaroo but could be ambivalent trading with fox; kangaroo is a natural seller of RED in exchange for GREEN. However, no one initially knows others’ RED values; they only see their own, the public GREEN value, and the shared ranges. Proposals thus serve a dual role: they try to earn surplus and also signal how much a player may value RED. Because trades can be accepted by more than one partner and then randomly assigned, proposers must design deals that are profitable against any possible counterparty while recognizing they cannot pick who accepts.

From an analyst’s perspective, on-value, mutually beneficial trades look like kangaroo selling RED to either fox or gorilla for GREEN at terms between $0.10 and the counterpart’s private value. For fox, paying up to ~1 GREEN per RED could be profitable in pure value terms (0.50 cost vs 0.70 gain), while gorilla can safely pay up to 0.8 GREEN per RED (0.40 vs 0.50). But because players do not know this structure, they are likely to start with conservative, mildly favorable offers and adjust slightly based on which deals get accepted. In a short 3-round game, there is limited time to explore, so players will tend to favor straightforward, small-quantity offers that clearly improve their own payoffs given any plausible opponent value. We therefore expect: (1) kangaroo to test selling small quantities of RED for GREEN; (2) fox to push more aggressively to acquire RED, perhaps from kangaroo first but leaving offers just good enough that gorilla might consider them; (3) gorilla to play a middle role, sometimes buying RED from kangaroo and occasionally selling or swapping if offered good enough terms by fox. Some profitable offers will nevertheless be declined because recipients worry the other side is exploiting private information. The overall dynamic will be a few small trades that increase fox’s RED holdings, shift some GREEN to kangaroo and/or gorilla, and leave some surplus unrealized due to caution and the random-recipient rule.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players still hold their initial 10 RED and 10 GREEN and are highly uncertain about others’ RED values. This first round is where players test the waters with simple, small trades. Kangaroo is likely to probe whether others will pay for RED, since RED is low-value for them. Fox, with the highest private RED value, will want to start accumulating RED but must avoid signaling too extreme a preference that might trigger adverse pricing later. Gorilla, with a mid-range RED value, will compare any incoming RED-for-GREEN offers to their own valuation and may be cautious until they see how aggressive fox and kangaroo are. Because this is the very first round, offers may be conservative: modest quantities and not too lopsided terms, to ensure that any acceptance is immediately profitable even under pessimistic beliefs about counterparties’ values.

After round 0, a couple of small RED–GREEN trades have occurred. Kangaroo has discovered that at least gorilla is willing to pay 1 GREEN for 1 RED, which is highly profitable for kangaroo (trading a $0.10 chip for a $0.50 chip). Gorilla finds this acceptable because they gain 1 RED worth $0.40 at a cost of a $0.50 GREEN, a small expected loss in pure value terms but potentially justified early on as a belief that RED might be more valuable or for experimentation. Fox’s initial attempt to buy RED at 1 GREEN for 1 RED was refused by both others, which gives fox information: that price may look too favorable to fox, or partners might suspect fox values RED highly. Everyone now has a bit more structure to their beliefs: kangaroo sees strong demand for RED at 1:1, fox sees resistance to that same price coming from someone else, and gorilla has signaled some willingness to buy and sell at parity. In round 1, players adjust. Kangaroo can confidently continue selling RED, perhaps targeting fox or gorilla with similar or slightly improved deals. Fox may either slightly sweeten their offers or aim trades more carefully based on who has recently been willing to move RED. Gorilla, sitting in the middle, may try to arbitrage a bit—buying RED cheaply from kangaroo and trying to test more aggressive sales to fox—or may just keep accepting profitable RED purchases from kangaroo if offered again.

By round 2, clear patterns have emerged. Kangaroo has repeatedly profited by selling RED for GREEN and now holds substantially more GREEN and fewer RED. Fox has managed to pick up extra RED from kangaroo at a 1:1 GREEN price, a very favorable deal given fox’s high RED valuation. Gorilla has both bought and attempted to sell RED at parity, discovering that selling RED for 1 GREEN is not attractive to others, likely because everyone values GREEN at $0.50 and at least one partner (fox) seems to value RED strongly. With only one round left, players are less concerned with long-run signaling and more focused on immediate value gains. Kangaroo remains happy to dump any remaining surplus RED if they can get 1 GREEN per RED; fox is especially eager for more RED, but other players may resist over-generous terms that seem too beneficial to fox given prior refusals. Gorilla might look for one last chance either to shed some surplus RED if overstocked, or to opportunistically buy if kangaroo prices stay low. However, time pressure and more refined beliefs can make players more selective, and some mutually beneficial trades might still be left on the table if they appear too risky or exploitable.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging red and green chips when only greens have a common, known value ($0.50 each). Each player privately values red chips differently: Tiger values red very low ($0.10), Unicorn values red very high ($0.80), and Bat values red at the same as green ($0.50). All know that everyone values green at $0.50, but do not know others’ red values. The core efficiency opportunity is to move red chips away from Tiger (who values them least) toward Unicorn (who values them most), with Bat roughly indifferent between red and green. However, proposals must trade one chip color for the other at discrete quantities, and acceptance is simultaneous, with random partner selection if more than one accepts. This randomness pushes proposers to structure trades that are attractive to at most one plausible counterparty (to avoid unwanted partners) or at least make them indifferent among who accepts. Early in the game, players face substantial uncertainty about others’ private values, so they probe with modest offers that are safe given worst-case valuations. Over time, observed acceptances or rejections update beliefs: for example, if Unicorn aggressively bids for red, others might infer that Unicorn values red highly; if Tiger is quick to exchange red for green, others can infer Tiger’s red is low. Because there are only three rounds and each player has one turn per round, players are under time pressure to secure profitable trades without revealing so much that they lose surplus. We should expect: (1) Tiger trying to turn low-value red into green at rates near or above 1 red for 1 green, while avoiding giving too good a deal when others might not value red much; (2) Unicorn attempting to acquire red by offering green, ideally exploiting any player who undervalues red, while experimenting to learn who bites; and (3) Bat acting cautiously, often near 1:1 trades or slightly favorable to themselves, since their red and green valuations are equal. The game will likely feature a couple of successful trades that start moving red away from Tiger and perhaps from Bat toward Unicorn, mixed with declined offers that are too lopsided or misaligned with the hidden valuations and emerging beliefs.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 red and 10 green chips. Players only know their own red value, so they have to infer others’ valuations from early behavior. It is round 0 and there is plenty of time left, so offers will likely be exploratory and relatively conservative, designed to be safe in value terms while testing who seems eager to trade red for green or vice versa. Tiger, who undervalues red, is inclined to sell red for green. Unicorn, who strongly values red, will try to buy red without giving away too much green. Bat, being indifferent between red and green, will tend to accept modestly favorable trades but not big losses. Early accept/reject decisions will serve as signals about whether red is more or less valuable to each counterpart.

By the start of round 1, everyone has seen Tiger accept receiving fewer reds for greens and Unicorn accept 1:1 and 2-red-for-1-green trades. This suggests Tiger is comfortable dumping red and that Unicorn values red enough to part with green in a slightly unfavorable green-for-red rate. Tiger now suspects that at least one partner, likely Unicorn, values red more than $0.50, and Unicorn infers that Tiger’s red value is probably low. Bat has not traded and remains opaque. With two rounds left, Tiger may push harder to trade away red for green at rates favorable to themselves. Unicorn might offer deals aimed at Tiger again, but must consider that Tiger now knows Unicorn wants red. Bat may start probing lightly, but will avoid clearly losing trades given their equal valuations.

Entering the final round, substantial information has been revealed: Tiger has repeatedly been willing to give up red, Unicorn has kept buying red even when terms are slightly unfavorable in green, and Bat has become a net seller of red and buyer of green at fair or slightly favorable terms. Everyone now views Unicorn as the high-red-value player, Tiger as the low-red-value player, and Bat as roughly indifferent. With only three turns left, players focus on last-chance improvements to their portfolios. Tiger, likely holding relatively few greens now, may try a final trade to pick up green if any red remains. Unicorn would love to grab more red but must offer terms that Tiger or Bat still find acceptable despite their learning. Bat may only accept clearly favorable or at least breakeven trades, guarding against last-round exploitation. Rejection thresholds tighten as there is no future signaling benefit from making or accepting speculative offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player 2-chip bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values on the red chips and a flat, common value on greens. Everyone knows that each green chip is worth exactly $0.50 to all players, but they are unsure how much others value red chips beyond a loose public range. In fact, buffalo-red-6989 privately values reds the most ($0.90), dog-orange-384 is intermediate ($0.50), and cat-pink-8143 values them the least ($0.40). This creates a natural incentive for buffalo to try to acquire red chips and to part with green chips if that helps. Dog is roughly indifferent in value terms between colors, while cat is actually better off, in pure value terms, holding green rather than red, but does not know that others have systematically higher red values. Because proposals must trade different colors and trades execute whenever at least one responder accepts (with random partner selection if multiple accept), proposers cannot perfectly target their favorite partner; they must design offers that are attractive enough to at least one counterpart while accounting for the risk that both might accept and a less-preferred partner is randomly chosen. Early in the game, uncertainty about others’ red values makes players more cautious: dog is likely to test moderate exchange rates that seem “fair” relative to the public range, buffalo is likely to push more aggressive red-for-green trades, and cat, not knowing others value red more than they do, may initially over- or under-sell reds. As trades are accepted or rejected, everyone infers something about others’ preferences—especially about how much they seem to like or dislike red chips. Those inference dynamics interact with inventory constraints: as one player accumulates more of a color, it changes both their marginal willingness to trade and how appealing their offers look. Over three rounds, each player has three proposals. Buffalo’s high red value and willingness to give up green tends to dominate the surplus creation opportunities: trades where buffalo acquires reds from cat or dog in exchange for greens will tend to be accepted whenever they offer at least weakly positive gains for the counterpart. Dog, having symmetric values, often behaves as a liquidity provider and can profit modestly by taking favorable rates in either direction. Cat is the one best positioned to sell reds for greens, but because they do not know they are the lowest red-valuer, they may be conservative and require at least a slight perceived gain in chip count or value to trade. By the final round, any history of accepted trades and visible chip distributions will have given some coarse signals: if buffalo repeatedly tries to buy red at slightly generous terms, others will infer buffalo likes red more; if cat is willing to part with red cheaply, the others infer cat’s red value is low. The resulting equilibrium-like path in this forecast has buffalo steadily building a red-heavy portfolio at the expense of green, dog making small, roughly break-even trades early and tightening later, and cat eventually recognizing that trading away red for green is at least harmless and often beneficial, leading to more acceptance of mildly red-for-green offers in the later rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, each player holds 10 red and 10 green chips. No one has any direct information about others’ private red values beyond the public range. Players accordingly treat both colors as roughly comparable in value, perhaps with slightly more caution around red because its value range is wider. Dog moves first and is likely to test a conservative, nearly value-neutral trade to gather information. Buffalo, who loves reds, will try early to buy red with green, but cannot be too aggressive yet because they do not know whether others are desperate to keep red. Cat, who actually values red the least, does not yet know that others might be systematically higher on red, so they start somewhat conservatively, not offering very lopsided trades against their own interest. The first round thus features mild, near-parity offers and at most a modest reallocation of red toward buffalo.

By the start of round 1, one trade has occurred: buffalo has paid 3 green for 2 red from cat. Everyone observes the new holdings and infers that at least one of them was willing to trade at a slightly red-favoring rate. Cat knows they gained extra green and lost some red; buffalo knows they profit substantially in value terms, reinforcing buffalo’s desire to continue acquiring red. Dog, having not traded yet, sees that buffalo is willing to give up multiple greens for red and updates that buffalo might value red relatively highly. This pushes dog to demand better terms if they consider selling red later. Cat, having accepted a generous offer and still not fully appreciating how low their red value is in comparison, becomes somewhat more open to parting with red for green at similar or slightly less favorable rates. Round 1 proposals are therefore somewhat more targeted: buffalo becomes more assertive in buying red, dog experiments with a modestly advantageous rate, and cat tries to replicate their earlier favorable pattern but is more cautious after seeing no one accepted their own initial small trade.

By round 2, buffalo has executed two trades with cat and now holds a noticeably red-heavy, green-light portfolio, while cat holds significantly more green and fewer red. Dog still has their original endowment and now strongly suspects that buffalo places a higher value on red than they do, and that cat is at least somewhat willing to sell red for green. Cat infers that their prior trades were not harmful and may even have been good, making them more comfortable continuing to move red for green if terms look reasonable. Buffalo wants still more red but must be careful: as their green stock dwindles, each green given up is more painful, and the others—especially dog—will demand more favorable rates to part with red. In this final round, dog tries one more modest probe that does not shift much because others now need strong incentives. Buffalo makes one last, slightly generous offer to cat to convert remaining green into high-value red. Cat, now heavily shifted toward green and less concerned about end-of-game rebalancing, accepts one last red-for-green trade at a rate that appears acceptable given their beliefs. The final outcome features buffalo with a large red surplus, cat with a large green surplus, and dog relatively unchanged, reflecting the underlying value asymmetries revealed through prior play.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value chips identically: GREEN=$0.50 and RED=$0.40, with 10 of each color initially. Because values are common across players, there is no potential for mutually beneficial trade: any chip that leaves a player reduces their value by exactly the same amount it increases their partner’s value. Rational, value-maximizing players who understand this will be reluctant to trade at all, and any observed trade would likely come from misperception, experimentation, or an attempt to exploit others’ possible misunderstanding. Strategic uncertainty arises from not knowing whether others have the same valuations: each player only knows their own values and the public range for each chip type. Given the narrow public range on GREEN (exactly $0.50) but wider range on RED (from $0.10 to $1.00), each player may suspect that some others value RED much more or much less than they do, and might probe with offers that look favorable if the partner has a lower RED value or a higher GREEN value. However, with symmetric valuations in fact, everyone’s best reply is to decline any exchange that reduces the total dollar value of their bundle. This leads to a forecast where nearly all trades are declined, with proposals mostly exploratory or feigned attempts to gain slightly better-valued chips without overpaying, but ending in no executed trades as players compare what they give and receive using their own valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. They know GREEN is exactly $0.50 from the public range, but RED could vary across players in principle. Each player knows their own RED=$0.40, but not the others’. Strategically, this first round is where players might experiment to test whether others have different RED values—for example, offering trades that would be attractive only to someone who values RED differently. However, because every player in fact values RED below GREEN, any trade that swaps GREEN for RED at par or worse is unattractive, while trades that give away RED for GREEN are also unattractive if they do not increase total value. Thus, we forecast proposals that look like probing offers but that others will reject after computing their implied value change.

After a full first round with no accepted trades, players update their beliefs that others’ values are likely similar to their own, or at least that no obviously favorable arbitrage is available. With symmetric true values, the best-response logic to observed refusals is to continue rejecting any offer that does not strictly increase one’s own total chip value. Proposers may still generate offers, either to test for mistakes, to see if someone is especially eager to change their composition for non-monetary reasons, or simply because the game incentivizes making some proposal each turn. These offers are likely to cluster around near-even exchanges that would be weakly or strictly value-decreasing for rational receivers, leading again to universal rejections.

By round 2, with six consecutive declines and no change to anyone’s holdings, players have strong evidence that others are evaluating trades similarly. With common-value GREEN and likely similar RED valuations inferred from behavior, any non-exploitative trade is at best value-neutral in chips and often value-negative once players factor in risk about unknown valuations. Consequently, the last round is predicted to feature either symbolic or extremely conservative proposals, with receivers sticking to a strict rule of rejecting any trade that does not strictly raise their own payoff. Given fully symmetric true values, that rule implies no accepted trades in the final round either.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 to everyone. RED is privately valued: badger=$0.20, peacock=$1.00, otter=$0.40. From an analyst’s perspective, total value is maximized by moving RED from badger (lowest value) and otter (middle value) toward peacock (highest value), while letting badger and otter receive GREEN or value-equivalent concessions in return. Because players only know their own values, they infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. Peacock likely treats RED as highly valuable and is willing to give up GREEN for RED on favorable terms. Badger likely wants to trade away RED for GREEN whenever they can get more than their own $0.20 per-RED value, while otter is intermediate: they value RED but may sell it if the GREEN payment is generous. Strategic uncertainty comes from not knowing if others also like RED a lot. Each proposer must also anticipate that if both potential recipients accept, the partner is chosen randomly, so offers attractive to multiple opponents create randomness in outcomes. Early in the game, players experiment with relatively modest trades to learn about others’ willingness to part with or acquire RED; later proposals become more targeted and generous when they seek to close remaining mutually beneficial trades before the game ends at round 2.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has symmetric holdings but different private values for RED. No one yet knows how much others value RED versus GREEN, so the first round is mainly exploratory. Proposers will tend to make modest, slightly self-favoring offers that still look appealing under many possible opponent valuations. Responses will convey the first signals: a player who eagerly trades away RED for little GREEN may be revealing low RED value, while a player who aggressively accumulates RED or refuses to sell it even for favorable GREEN may be revealing high RED value.

After round 0, some information has effectively been revealed. Peacock’s attempt to buy RED for GREEN and otter’s acceptance signal that peacock likely values RED more than GREEN and that otter is at least somewhat willing to part with RED if paid enough GREEN. Badger’s failed attempt to sell RED for relatively generous GREEN suggests others are not yet desperate for RED at that rate. In round 1, players adjust: peacock may push harder to accumulate RED from both opponents; otter becomes more selective about selling additional RED; badger may try slightly better terms or pivot to buying RED if they suspect others undervalue it.

By the final round, players understand that peacock is actively trying to accumulate RED, while badger is very willing to sell RED, and otter is more cautious after one profitable early sale. With only three turns remaining, there is pressure to close any obviously beneficial trades. Peacock may now offer more generous GREEN payments per RED to secure additional units, while badger tries to dump remaining RED as long as they get clearly positive value in GREEN. Otter may engage only if the price is clearly favorable, given their moderate private value for RED and the looming end of the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player bargaining game with two chip colors: RED (privately valued, heterogeneous across players) and GREEN (common-value). Each player starts with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. The private values are: buffalo-red-8335 values RED at $0.90 and GREEN at $0.50; camel-green-9744 values RED at $0.80 and GREEN at $0.50; cat-orange-7489 values RED at $0.70 and GREEN at $0.50. From an outside observer’s perspective, RED is strictly more valuable than GREEN for all players, but buffalo-red-8335 has the strongest preference for RED and cat-orange-7489 has the weakest. Efficient trades move RED from cat-orange-7489 (who values RED least) toward buffalo-red-8335 (who values it most) in exchange for GREEN flowing in the opposite direction, but players do not know others’ exact values and must infer them from proposals and acceptances. Because proposals must be one-color-for-another and the recipient is randomly selected if multiple players accept, proposers must offer deals that are good enough to elicit at least one acceptance but not so generous that both others jump in when the proposer really prefers a specific partner. Over just three rounds (nine proposal turns), there is pressure to extract favorable terms quickly rather than wait for perfect information. Early moves are exploratory and reveal rough preferences (e.g., who seems RED-hungry or GREEN-hungry). By midgame, players likely recognize that buffalo-red-8335 is a strong RED buyer and cat-orange-7489 is more willing to part with RED if compensated in GREEN. camel-green-9744 is in the middle and can arbitrage between the two if they can trade RED cheaply with cat-orange-7489 and sell it at a premium to buffalo-red-8335. Uncertainty remains about the exact valuation gaps, so players shade offers toward their own advantage and accept only trades that clearly increase their own chip value, while factoring in the risk that a generous seeming proposal might attract both potential partners and go to the less-preferred one by random tie-breaking.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 opens with symmetric endowments and complete uncertainty about others’ private RED values. All players know that GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone, but they only know their own RED value. buffalo-red-8335, with the highest value for RED, is likely to try to buy RED cheaply using GREEN, probing how eager others are to trade. camel-green-9744, with a midrange RED value, will experiment with offers that either buy RED from whoever values it least or sell RED at a high price if they sense strong RED demand. cat-orange-7489, who privately has the weakest taste for RED, may already suspect they can profit by exchanging RED away for an attractive quantity of GREEN, but in this first round they are still gauging how aggressive the others are. Because any accepted trade immediately alters holdings and future bargaining power, each player balances information-gathering with direct value gains, and overtly lopsided offers are likely to be declined as everyone is still fresh and not under deadline pressure.

By the start of round 1, only one trade has occurred: cat-orange-7489 sold 2 RED to buffalo-red-8335 for 2 GREEN. Publicly, everyone sees buffalo-red-8335 now holds more RED and fewer GREEN, while cat-orange-7489 has fewer RED and more GREEN. This pattern suggests to observers that buffalo-red-8335 is relatively RED-hungry and that cat-orange-7489 is comparatively willing to part with RED. buffalo-red-8335 infers that at least one other player is willing to trade RED at a roughly 1:1 RED-for-GREEN rate and may try to improve those terms slightly in their favor without scaring off all acceptances. camel-green-9744, having had an initial offer rejected, now faces evidence that their earlier price (trading away RED) was unattractive; they may either sweeten terms to move RED out or flip strategy and attempt to accumulate RED more cheaply. cat-orange-7489, having profited modestly from the first deal, will look for further beneficial swaps but is now more cautious about giving away too much RED since buffalo-red-8335’s willingness to buy signals that RED might be quite valuable to them.

Going into the final round, several patterns are now visible: buffalo-red-8335 has repeatedly tried to buy RED with GREEN, reinforcing the perception that they value RED relatively highly. cat-orange-7489 has already sold multiple RED for GREEN but has also started to moderate, suggesting that while they are the weakest RED holder, they do not want to deplete their RED entirely at poor terms. camel-green-9744 has both sold and bought RED in small quantities, taking on an intermediate, somewhat arbitrage-like role. With only one proposal turn left for each player, there is time pressure: any profitable trade must be made now or not at all. This pushes players to propose deals that are slightly more generous than before to elicit quick acceptance, while still yielding them a positive gain based on their private valuations. At the same time, because any final trade will lock in the end-of-game distribution, players may reject offers that are only marginally beneficial or that risk leaving them with an unbalanced portfolio they subjectively dislike.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value green chips equally at $0.50, but differ on red: peacock values red at $0.40, owl at $0.30, and dog at $0.20. Greens are thus strictly more valuable than reds for everyone, and peacock values red the most while dog values it the least. Because everyone starts with 10 red and 10 green and there is no discounting across rounds, the main incentive is to convert low-value red into high-value green. However, trades must involve giving up one color to receive the other, and each player only knows their own red value. This uncertainty makes it hard to pinpoint exact profitable exchange rates: peacock does not know if the others value red as much as they do, and similarly for owl and dog. Rational but imperfectly informed players are likely to explore exchange ratios that give partners at least weak gains given their own valuations, hoping that asymmetric red values exist. Over three rounds, players can cautiously adjust offers in light of observed acceptances and rejections, but each proposal is bilateral with random partner selection if multiple accept. This randomness discourages extremely tailored deals and encourages offers that at least one counterpart might plausibly accept. Given the modest differences in red value, we expect small exchanges with ratios near 1:1 or 2:1 in green-for-red or red-for-green to test the waters. More red-rich, red-poor valuations (like dog) may be willing to trade away red for green at somewhat disadvantageous rates because green is always high and safe, while peacock will be relatively more eager to accumulate red if it can be secured without giving up too much green. Still, since all know that green is the common valuable chip, no one is likely to give up many greens cheaply, so most trades will be modest, contested, and some attempts will be declined as players probe for mutually beneficial rates under value uncertainty.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 red and 10 green and is unsure how others value red. Peacock, with the highest red valuation, is inclined to seek red in exchange for green but must balance that against the common understanding that green is reliably valuable. Owl sits in the middle on red value, and dog has the lowest red valuation and thus the strongest incentive to turn red into green if the exchange rate is acceptable. In this first round, offers are likely to be exploratory: modest quantities and near-balanced rates that let proposers test whether others are willing to trade red for green or vice versa without revealing too much about their private red values.

After round 0, there has been one successful trade: owl gave 3 red to dog in exchange for 2 green. Publicly, everyone observes owl now holding more green and fewer red, and dog holding more red and fewer green, signaling that at least one player was willing to trade away red for green at a rate where red might be perceived as somewhat less valuable than green. This provides soft evidence that red is not overly precious to some players, which may embolden peacock to try again to acquire red or encourage others to propose further red-for-green exchanges. However, the rejection of dog’s aggressive 3-red-for-1-green offer also shows that there are limits to how disadvantageous a rate others will accept. Round 1 proposals will likely adjust slightly: peacock may improve terms for counterparts, owl might cautiously exploit dog’s apparent willingness to trade red, and dog may moderate its earlier, too-steep ask.

By the final round, a pattern has emerged: multiple trades have involved players giving up red to secure green. Publicly, this reveals a shared willingness to convert red into green at modestly unfavorable red-to-green ratios, suggesting that green is broadly regarded as safer or more valuable. Peacock has repeatedly accepted red in exchange for green, hinting (to careful observers) that peacock may have a relatively high red valuation. Owl appears somewhat flexible, both giving and receiving red, while dog has made strong attempts to trade red away. With only one round left and limited remaining time to exploit value differences, players are likely to make slightly bolder offers, trying to lock in beneficial exchanges while remaining within bounds that others might plausibly accept given what has been learned so far.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game revolves around trading red chips, whose values differ across players, in exchange for green chips, which are common-value at $0.50 each. Everyone starts with 10 red and 10 green chips. Privately, ram values red at $0.40, alligator at $0.20, and butterfly at $0.50, while all value green at $0.50. Socially efficient trades move red chips away from alligator (who values them least) and toward butterfly (who values them most), ideally using green chips as payment. However, each player only knows their own values and must infer others’ values from observed proposals and acceptances. This creates uncertainty about how much others are willing to trade and at what terms. Ram has a relatively balanced valuation (red 0.40 vs green 0.50), so they are mildly inclined to acquire green but can still value red significantly. Alligator has a strong reason to sell red for green at even modestly favorable terms, but may not realize just how low their red valuation is relative to others. Butterfly is the strongest holder of red (0.50 vs green 0.50), and is nearly indifferent in value terms; butterfly will only agree to trades that do not reduce total value, and may even aim for a small surplus. Because acceptance is simultaneous and, when multiple players accept, the final partner is chosen at random, proposers must design offers that are attractive to at least one counterparty while not giving away too much surplus. Over the three rounds, players are likely to move some red from alligator to butterfly and possibly from ram to butterfly, with green flowing the other way, but bargaining power and incomplete information will temper large or extreme trades. Early rounds will feature more exploratory offers; later rounds will incorporate learnings from which deals were accepted or declined, with players updating beliefs about relative valuations and risk of overpaying or missing profitable trades.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has yet observed others’ behavior, so each player is experimenting with simple, relatively small trades to probe others’ valuations. Ram is likely to test the market by offering red for green at terms that slightly favor them if others treat red as roughly comparable to green. Alligator, who privately values red much less than green, will tentatively try to sell red for green but may not immediately reveal how eager they are to dump red. Butterfly, believing red and green are equal in value, will be cautious about exchanging chips and will tend to decline offers that clearly reduce their value or are not obviously beneficial. With full chip endowments and no prior history, declined offers are common in this round as players calibrate expectations.

By round 1, some information has been revealed. Alligator successfully traded away 3 red for 2 green to ram, signaling to observers that alligator is at least somewhat willing to part with red, and that ram is at least mildly willing to acquire red at that price. Ram now has more red and fewer green, and may become more selective about giving up green. Alligator, with slightly fewer red and more green, may continue trying to sell red but might adjust terms after seeing ram’s previous acceptance and butterfly’s caution. Butterfly, having declined earlier trades and not having traded yet, has given a signal of being value-protective. Offers in this round may be a bit more targeted: ram could experiment with smaller or more favorable terms to keep trading with alligator, while both ram and alligator are unlikely to offer very generous deals to butterfly unless they believe butterfly values red highly. Declines are still likely when terms look unbalanced under each player’s beliefs.

Going into the final round, patterns are clearer. Alligator has repeatedly demonstrated a willingness to trade away red for green, which both ram and butterfly can interpret as red being relatively low-valued for alligator. Ram has both bought red from and sold red to alligator, suggesting a moderate valuation and sensitivity to price. Butterfly finally accepted a trade in round 1 that gained green at the cost of extra red, implicitly signaling a relatively high valuation for red compared with green, or at least comfort holding more red. In this last round, players know there are no future opportunities, so they focus on any remaining positive-value trades given their beliefs. Ram may try one more moderate offer to convert red to green if feasible. Alligator will look for a last chance to turn low-value red into higher-value green but must avoid terms that others now recognize as too generous. Butterfly, now holding more red, will only accept trades that do not obviously worsen their payoff. Final trades are likely to be small and selective, as each player avoids moves that are clearly dominated given what they have learned.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging red and green chips that have common public quantity information but privately known monetary values for red. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so they serve as a natural benchmark asset. Red chips are low value for iguana ($0.10) but relatively high for ram and dolphin ($0.50), creating asymmetric incentives and scope for mutually beneficial trade if players infer each other’s valuations from proposals and responses. Iguana is strongly motivated to trade away red chips for green whenever possible, because each red she gives up loses only $0.10 while each green she receives gains $0.50. Ram and dolphin see red as just as valuable as green on a per-chip basis, so they are roughly indifferent between holding red or green in purely monetary terms; their bargaining strategy is more about avoiding bad deals and possibly trying to exploit counterparties who reveal low valuation for red. However, no player sees others’ true values, so they must infer them from behavior (e.g., steep red-for-green offers suggest the proposer values red less). Proposals are constrained to be simple bilateral trades of one color for another, with uncertain matching if more than one responder accepts. Because each turn can be accepted by either of two responders and ties are broken randomly, proposers must anticipate that any attractive deal to one high-value partner may also be attractive to the other, diluting their ability to target a specific counterparty. Public history of trades and holdings over three rounds means players can react to observed patterns, but the short horizon and symmetric proposal rights reduce the scope for complex reputational play. The likely outcome is that iguana offers relatively generous red-for-green exchanges to entice at least one partner, while ram and dolphin are cautious about giving up green too cheaply and may sometimes prefer to sit out trades that could advantage a competitor more than themselves, despite being roughly value-neutral in monetary terms. The game’s uncertainty comes from hidden red valuations, ambiguous interpretation of offers, and the random selection among multiple acceptors, all of which make precise surplus division and partner selection noisy rather than fully controlled.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game all players hold 10 red and 10 green chips. No one knows others’ red values yet, so this round is primarily about signaling and initial exploration. Iguana, who privately has low-value red, has strong incentives to initiate trades that exchange red for green; ram and dolphin, who value red and green equally at $0.50, have weaker directional incentives and will be more focused on not making obviously disadvantageous trades or giving too much information away. Because responders act simultaneously and tie-breaking is random, each proposer faces uncertainty about which partner they might end up trading with, which discourages finely targeted proposals and encourages somewhat symmetric or broadly acceptable deals.

By round 1, the history of offers and any executed trades in round 0 gives players a bit more information. Iguana’s willingness to trade away more red than green (3 red for 2 green) suggests to others that iguana may value red relatively low, though ram and dolphin do not know whether this is due to true valuation or strategic bluffing. Ram’s neutral 1:1 red-for-green attempt in round 0, when declined, may make ram suspect that others either do not want to reshuffle colors yet or that they are wary of making trades that seem neutral in value. Now, iguana has fewer red and more green than initially, ram has more red and fewer green, and dolphin is unchanged. Given these holdings and emerging beliefs, iguana still wants to offload red for green but must consider that ram already holds extra red and may be less eager to take on more without compensation. Dolphin remains a potential trading partner for iguana, though uncertainty about dolphin’s red valuation and attitude toward color balance persists. Each proposer must now consider not just chip values, but also how trades shift relative advantages going into the final round.

Going into the final round, iguana has shifted a substantial amount of red into green through successful trades, revealing a consistent pattern of undervaluing red relative to green. Ram and dolphin have together absorbed iguana’s discarded red in exchange for green, and both now hold fewer green and more red than at the start. At this stage, further trades have diminishing strategic value: ram and dolphin are roughly monetarily indifferent between red and green, but may resist additional trades that further worsen their green balance relative to competitors or that appear to overly favor iguana. Iguana, meanwhile, has limited remaining red to trade, so any final proposals are likely to be either small tweaks or somewhat aggressive attempts that others are reluctant to accept. With no future rounds, there is no reputational or dynamic benefit to compromising on marginal exchanges, so players are more likely to decline modest or one-sided offers and lock in their current payoffs.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so trading GREEN among players never creates or destroys value; it is mainly a bargaining currency. RED values differ sharply: duck values RED at $0.10 (low), gorilla at $0.20 (medium), and kangaroo at $0.80 (high). Socially efficient trades would move RED from duck (lowest value) to kangaroo (highest value), potentially via gorilla, using GREEN as payment. However, each player privately knows only their own values, so they infer others’ valuations from conservative ranges and observed behavior. Duck has the strongest incentive to sell RED for GREEN because RED is almost worthless to them. Kangaroo has the strongest incentive to buy RED, but they will try to do so at a favorable price in terms of GREEN. Gorilla, with moderate RED valuation, is in the middle and may both buy RED cheaply and sell it later or hold it if trades are advantageous. Uncertainty about others’ valuations makes players cautious: proposals that look too lopsided risk being declined; generous-looking offers might hide private information. Because there are only three rounds and each player proposes once per round in fixed order, early moves can be exploratory—probing acceptance thresholds—while later moves adjust to the observed willingness to trade. The random tie-breaking when multiple players accept creates an additional layer of risk: a sender cannot target a specific partner if both are willing, so proposals that appeal to only one likely counterparty are strategically safer when a sender wants a particular direction of trade. Overall, the likely pattern is duck offering RED for GREEN at modest prices, kangaroo trying to acquire RED without giving up too much GREEN, and gorilla opportunistically trading based on perceived mispricing in others’ offers. Some offers will be declined due to mismatch between the proposer’s expectations and the recipients’ private valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the beginning of round 0, everyone has symmetric chip holdings (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but asymmetric private values. Players only know the public value ranges and their own exact values, so they are uncertain about exactly how much others value RED versus GREEN. This first round is where they test the waters. Duck, with very low RED value, is naturally inclined to try selling RED for GREEN. Kangaroo, with very high RED value, is inclined to buy RED but will not reveal maximum willingness to pay immediately. Gorilla is in between and might experiment with a moderate exchange rate. Because no prior trades have occurred, all players rely mainly on their own values and the plausible ranges, so proposals are likely to be conservative and simple: single-color-for-single-color trades with small quantities to probe others’ thresholds.

By the start of round 1, the trade history reveals that duck was willing to swap 1 RED for 1 GREEN, and kangaroo accepted that and later successfully bought RED from duck at a 1:1 rate for 2 chips as well. Gorilla’s attempt at a symmetric 1 RED for 1 GREEN trade was rejected by both others, giving some signal: duck may see limited upside in paying RED (which they value low) to gain GREEN (which is more valuable), and kangaroo may suspect that exchanging at parity with gorilla does not improve their position compared to trading with duck. Current holdings reflect that RED has flowed from duck to kangaroo, with duck holding more GREEN and fewer RED, and kangaroo holding more RED and fewer GREEN; gorilla’s holdings remain at the initial levels. Strategically, duck has weakened ability to sell large amounts of RED because their RED stock is lower. Kangaroo now has a strong RED position but slightly constrained GREEN, making further RED purchases more costly in terms of their remaining GREEN. Gorilla, seeing that duck and kangaroo already traded at 1:1, might either attempt slightly better terms or sit out unprofitable trades. Players also start inferring that kangaroo likely values RED more than duck, based on acceptance patterns, though exact values remain unknown.

Going into round 2, the pattern is clear: duck has repeatedly been willing to trade away RED at relatively cheap terms for GREEN, while kangaroo has actively tried to acquire RED but now faces diminishing GREEN reserves. Gorilla has only one successful trade so far—from duck—and their attempts to trade at 1:1 have been declined, signaling that others expect at least slightly better terms than simple parity. Holdings have shifted so that duck is relatively GREEN-rich and RED-poor, kangaroo is RED-rich and GREEN-poor, and gorilla sits in the middle with a somewhat improved RED stock from duck’s last sale. This final round offers each player just one remaining proposal, so moves are more likely to be either opportunistic grabs (e.g., kangaroo trying to pick up a bit more RED if cheap enough) or last-ditch sales (duck trying to offload remaining low-value RED). Because any trade must be attractive given the tightened constraints and inferred preferences, and because there is no future beyond this round, players will reject offers that do not provide immediate, clear gains in terms of their own chip values.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, multi-round bargaining game over two chip colors: RED and GREEN. All players begin with 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, while RED is privately valued: duck-yellow-281 (Duck) values RED at $1.00, swan-blue-7512 (Swan) at $0.70, and dolphin-blue-4630 (Dolphin) at $0.20. Publicly, players know that RED is somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00 and GREEN is exactly $0.50, but they do not know the exact RED values of others. This creates asymmetric incentives: Duck has the strongest motive to buy RED and sell GREEN; Dolphin has the strongest motive to sell RED and buy GREEN; Swan is in the middle, preferring RED to GREEN but not as strongly as Duck. Because all trades require offering one color to buy another, the natural efficiency pattern is RED flowing from Dolphin to Swan and especially to Duck, with GREEN flowing the other way. However, uncertainty about others' RED values makes players cautious about offering deals that might be too generous or that might signal exploitable preferences. Strategic issues include: (1) Balancing immediate gains from trade against the option value of waiting for better offers in later rounds; (2) Managing the risk that a proposal attractive to more than one counterparty will be randomly assigned, depriving the proposer of their preferred trading partner; (3) Learning from observed acceptances and rejections to infer relative valuations and adjust later proposals; and (4) The fact that each player only proposes once per round, so failed proposals are costly in opportunity terms. Given the valuations, we expect Duck to aggressively seek RED, often at the expense of GREEN, Swan to seek modest RED gains while guarding against being exploited by a possible high-RED-value rival, and Dolphin to opportunistically dump RED for GREEN or for seemingly favorable terms when possible, while reacting cautiously to highly asymmetric trades that might indicate that the proposer values RED much more than Dolphin does. Over three rounds, players will likely move from exploratory and modest trades toward more targeted and larger exchanges once they infer who most wants which color, but they remain constrained by risk of overpaying and by the randomness of partner assignment when multiple players accept.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold symmetric endowments (10 RED, 10 GREEN) and have only their own valuations. No trade history exists, so proposals are partly exploratory: Swan and Duck, both valuing RED above GREEN, are inclined to acquire RED while offering GREEN; Dolphin, who values RED the least, is naturally inclined to sell RED for GREEN if the terms look favorable. However, because no one knows others’ RED values, each proposer must test the waters with deals that are plausibly acceptable across a range of possible valuations and that do not reveal too much about their own type. Responders face the tradeoff between grabbing seemingly good terms early and waiting in case later proposals are even better.

By the start of round 1, the history reveals that Dolphin twice accepted trades selling RED for GREEN at fairly even or mildly unfavorable RED terms, revealing Dolphin’s relatively low RED valuation. Swan and Duck also see that Dolphin is a willing liquidity provider of RED. Duck’s generous 3 RED for 4 GREEN proposal was accepted, suggesting to observers that Duck values RED highly, while Swan’s earlier 2-for-2 RED-for-GREEN swap looks modest and more consistent with intermediate valuation. This pushes the game toward a pattern where Dolphin specializes in selling RED, Duck in buying RED more aggressively, and Swan in opportunistically trading when terms are clearly favorable. Proposals in this round are shaped by updated beliefs: Duck should feel emboldened to offer slightly harsher terms to Dolphin, while Swan may be more cautious about offering attractive terms that could be snapped up by the high-RED-value Duck if the structure allowed, though here proposals are directed to the field and acceptance is random among accepters.

Entering round 2, the pattern is clear: Dolphin has repeatedly sold RED for GREEN at terms that are favorable to buyers; Duck has consistently bought RED and paid premium GREEN prices relative to Dolphin’s low RED value; and Swan has only successfully traded once and has watched Duck steadily accumulate RED. Swan can infer that Duck likely values RED more highly, while Dolphin’s repeated willingness to sell confirms a low RED value. With only one proposal left per player, there is pressure to capture remaining gains from trade. Duck still wants RED and has plenty of GREEN; Dolphin is close to an efficient portfolio and will accept only obviously beneficial trades; Swan faces a tough environment, as any generous buy-RED proposals are more likely to benefit Duck when roles reverse. Given their remaining holdings and inferred types, we expect modest, possibly conservative proposals from Swan and Dolphin and one more assertive acquisition attempt from Duck, with some offers being declined as remaining marginal gains shrink.
