parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with symmetric endowments (10 RED, 10 GREEN), but asymmetric private values for RED. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so GREEN-GREEN trades are pointless and GREEN is a natural numeraire. Bear and Pig value RED at $0.60, slightly above GREEN, while Hamster values RED at only $0.10. From an analyst perspective, the efficient pattern is for Hamster to sell RED to Bear or Pig for GREEN, since Hamster gives up low-value RED for higher-value GREEN, and Bear/Pig do the opposite. However, players know only their own RED value; they rationally infer that others also see GREEN at $0.50 and RED somewhere in [$0.10, $1.00], so they face uncertainty about whether others value RED more or less than they do. This creates two main strategic tensions: (1) Signaling and inference: early offers reveal partial information. A player aggressively buying RED with GREEN hints that they value RED above $0.50; a player who readily sells RED for GREEN hints the opposite. (2) Competition and random recipient risk: when a proposer makes a good deal for any counterparty, multiple responders might accept; the realized trade partner is then chosen randomly, so proposers cannot target the precise best partner. This reduces how finely they can tailor offers and discourages extremely generous deals that could be grabbed by the "wrong" responder. Over the three rounds, we should expect Bear and Pig to probe whether there is a low-RED-value seller (which there is: Hamster), while Hamster experiments with selling RED for GREEN if those proposals appear attractive. As the game proceeds, observed acceptances and rejections sharpen beliefs: Bear and Pig become more confident that Hamster is a likely low-value RED holder, and Hamster learns that Bear and Pig are relatively high-value RED holders. This pushes trades toward the efficient pattern of RED flowing from Hamster to Bear/Pig, with GREEN flowing the other way, though uncertainty and the three-player random-recipient rule prevent perfectly efficient or fully revealing outcomes.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone holds 10 RED and 10 GREEN. No one has yet learned about others’ valuations beyond the common knowledge that GREEN is worth $0.50 to all. Bear and Pig know they prefer RED slightly to GREEN, but cannot tell whether the others also value RED above or below $0.50. Hamster knows RED is much worse than GREEN but is unsure whether the others are high- or low-RED types. Strategically, Round 0 is about probing: Bear and Pig are likely to test modest RED-for-GREEN exchanges to see who bites, while Hamster will be cautious about revealing that RED is almost worthless to them, but still inclined to accept favorable RED-for-GREEN trades. Because of the random-recipient rule, proposers face the risk that any attractive offer can be accepted by either potential partner, so they spread the surplus thinly enough that they still profit even if either counterparty accepts.

Going into Round 1, the only successful trade so far is Pig buying 3 RED from Hamster for 2 GREEN. Publicly, players see that Hamster was willing to give up RED in exchange for GREEN at fairly favorable terms for Pig. Bear can infer that Hamster likely values RED relatively low compared to GREEN, while Pig interprets Hamster’s acceptance as confirmation that Pig’s own moderately high RED valuation is not shared by at least one other player. Hamster has learned that Pig is keen to acquire RED. The failed offers—Bear’s attempt to buy GREEN with RED and Hamster’s attempt to buy GREEN with RED—indicate that no one is so desperate for RED as to give up multiple GREEN per RED. In Round 1, Bear and Pig should update by trying to acquire more RED from Hamster at slightly more generous terms, while Hamster may test whether Bear also values RED highly, but must reconcile that both Bear and Pig might now compete for Hamster’s RED, complicating who will accept which offers under the random-recipient rule.

By Round 2, substantial information has been revealed through patterns of acceptance. Hamster has repeatedly sold RED for GREEN, and both Bear and Pig have shown willingness to acquire RED from Hamster, suggesting to all parties that Hamster’s RED value is low while Bear’s and Pig’s are higher. Publicly, everyone now sees a directional flow: RED migrating from Hamster toward Bear and Pig, GREEN flowing back. With only one round left, the focus shifts from exploration to exploitation—players aim to lock in any remaining profitable trades that align with their now-sharper beliefs. Bear, currently with a relatively RED-heavy portfolio, is close to satiation and will only accept clearly favorable terms. Pig may attempt one last purchase of RED from Hamster, but must account for random recipient risk if Bear might also accept. Hamster, seeing continuing demand for RED and having very low private value for it, is inclined to propose one final sale of remaining RED at terms still attractive to Bear or Pig but not so generous as to risk leaving surplus on the table.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features one low-value red holder (turtle) and two high-value red holders (whale and swan). Green chips are common-value at $0.50 to everyone, while red chips are worth $0.10 to turtle and $0.90 to whale and swan. Everyone knows the public ranges ($0.10–$1.00) for red and exactly $0.50 for green, but only knows their own private value for red. Thus, turtle knows red is relatively weak for them and that others might value red more, while whale and swan know red is very valuable to them and suspect others may value it less. From an analyst’s perspective, there is strong surplus potential when turtle trades away red (low value) in exchange for green, especially if the counterparty is a high-red-valuing player. However, the rules introduce uncertainty: when multiple players accept a proposal, the partner is chosen at random. This means proposers cannot perfectly target their preferred trading partner and must weigh the expected value of possible counterparties. Incentive-wise, turtle wants to sell red and buy green at any effective exchange rate where they receive at least $0.10 of value per red given up (ideally more); whale and swan want to acquire red using green, but not at such a high green-per-red price that they sacrifice more than $0.90 in green value to gain $0.90 in red value. There is also learning over rounds: observed acceptances and rejections give noisy signals about others’ red values. Given symmetry between whale and swan, we expect similar behaviors from them and competition for good deals with turtle. Proposers may start with modest but favorable offers, then adjust as they learn what is acceptable and as holdings change. Random resolution of multi-accept trades can also create competition: for attractive offers, both high-red players may accept and hope to be randomly chosen. Across three rounds, we expect most efficient trades to involve turtle selling red to a high-red player in exchange for green, though not necessarily at fully efficient prices due to incomplete information and the random partner selection rule.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold 10 red and 10 green chips. Turtle knows their red is weak ($0.10) relative to $0.50 green, so they are motivated to sell red for green. Whale and swan both have high red values ($0.90) and common green value ($0.50), so they want to buy red with green if the exchange rate is favorable. However, no one knows others’ exact red values; they only know that red could be anywhere from $0.10 to $1.00. Early in round 0, players test the waters with relatively conservative trades to infer how others respond. Because both whale and swan have the same underlying preferences but do not know this, they may compete for good deals with turtle. The random resolution of multi-acceptance means that when a deal is obviously good for both whale and swan, they might both accept and rely on chance to get the trade. We therefore expect: (1) turtle proposes a red-for-green trade that is good for turtle but still possibly attractive to at least one high-red player; (2) whale and swan each propose red-for-green trades that favor themselves, likely offering less green per red than turtle would like, and they adjust after seeing turtle’s initial outcome.

By the start of round 1, the only executed trade has been whale’s sale of 1 green for 2 red to swan. Current holdings: turtle still has 10 red, 10 green; whale has 8 red, 9 green; swan has 12 red, 11 green. Players update their beliefs: turtle’s initial offer of 3 red for 2 green (which both others rejected) suggests that at least one counterpart demands better terms to give up green, pointing to relatively high red values among at least one opponent. Whale saw that swan was willing to buy red at a rate of 0.5 green per red, consistent with swan valuing red at least somewhat more than green. Swan saw that whale was willing to give up green for red at 0.5 green per red as well. This mutual acceptance of the 2 red for 1 green deal (when whale proposed it) between high-red players offered them a neutral-value trade; they may interpret that as a safe, non-revealing way to lightly rebalance holdings. Turtle, having seen that more favorable-for-others trades are possible, may improve their offer slightly to increase the chance of acceptance. Whale and swan, knowing they both like red, may now seek to trade with turtle rather than with each other, since trading among themselves is only marginally beneficial at equal rates.

At the start of round 2, after round 1 trades, chip holdings are: turtle has 10 red and 10 green (they traded 2 red for 1 green with whale, then 2 red for 1 green back with whale, netting out to original holdings); whale has 8 red and 9 green; swan has 12 red and 11 green. The history reveals that turtle is willing to trade red at roughly 2 red for 1 green, while whale is repeatedly willing to trade at 2 red for 1 green in both directions. This suggests to the others that both turtle and whale have relatively closer valuations for red and green than the extremes might allow, or are cautious and prefer incremental trades. Swan’s repeated attempts to buy red at 2 red for 1 green and seeing low acceptance from turtle reinforce that turtle demands at least 0.5 green per red. In this final round, players know there is no future opportunity to adjust holdings, so the focus is on any obviously profitable last trades. Turtle should eagerly accept any offer giving at least 0.2 green per red (given personal valuations), but will only see the proposals from whale and swan. Whale and swan, recognizing their own high red valuation and that turtle has not accepted trades worse than 2 red for 1 green, may settle on that price as a focal point. However, because whale and swan already hold many red chips and have not successfully extracted surplus from turtle beyond neutral deals, they might be less motivated to continue trading unless a clearly beneficial last-moment deal appears.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN has a known common value of $0.50 to everyone, so any trade that destroys GREEN value is very salient. RED values are private and asymmetric: frog values RED highly ($0.70), badger moderately ($0.60), and hedgehog weakly ($0.20). Socially efficient trades move RED away from hedgehog toward frog or badger, and move GREEN in the opposite direction as payment. Because GREEN is symmetric, bargaining power is driven by how cheaply each player is willing to give up RED. Hedgehog is intrinsically a RED seller and GREEN buyer, frog is a strong RED buyer and GREEN seller, and badger sits in between. However, each only knows their own RED value; they must infer the others’ valuations from which offers get accepted. Uncertainty pushes them toward conservative, slightly profitable trades that they expect at least one counterparty to accept, but they also avoid trades that create obvious regrets if the unseen alternative partner might have been better. The random recipient rule when multiple players accept encourages proposers to design offers that are attractive to at least one likely counterparty but not so generous that both jump in, or else they risk ending up trading with the less preferred partner. Over three rounds, they will tentatively explore the RED-for-GREEN exchange rate, with frog typically pushing hardest to accumulate RED, hedgehog exploring sales of RED for GREEN, and badger opportunistically accepting favorable rates while being cautious not to overpay in GREEN. Early trades convey information about who is hungry for RED, and later rounds adjust to these perceived patterns, but the short horizon (3 rounds) limits how far they drift from simple, nearly myopic value-improving deals.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, no one has seen anyone else’s behavior, so they only know that RED values lie somewhere in the public range. Each player is likely to begin with relatively modest, one-shot profitable trades that would be acceptable to at least one counterparty with a medium RED value. Given hedgehog is a low RED valuer, they are inclined to test the market by offering RED for GREEN at a slightly favorable rate. Frog, as a high RED valuer, will try to buy RED with GREEN but not at a rate so generous that even a high-RED opponent would accept. Badger, with intermediate RED value, will primarily accept when they see clearly positive expected value and will be tentative proposing very skewed trades without feedback.

By round 1, some information has been revealed: frog accepted hedgehog’s 1 RED for 1 GREEN offer, signaling frog’s RED valuation is likely above $0.50, and badger accepted frog’s 2 RED for 1 GREEN, suggesting badger’s RED value is at least moderately high. Hedgehog infers that their RED is relatively undervalued by themselves compared to at least frog, so they are willing to keep selling RED, perhaps at a slightly worse rate for buyers. Frog, seeing strong demand for RED (both hedgehog selling and badger buying), may test more aggressive terms to accumulate RED using fewer GREEN. Badger, having just paid 0.5 GREEN per RED, now tries to either arbitrage (sell some RED at a better rate) or to acquire more RED at roughly similar or better rates, depending on how frog and hedgehog respond.

Going into the final round, all players have seen that frog is very eager to acquire RED, hedgehog is consistently happy to sell RED, and badger is somewhat flexible, sometimes buying and sometimes selling depending on the rate. With only one proposal left for each player, they focus on extracting last incremental gains. Hedgehog aims to unload remaining relatively low-value RED for GREEN at any still-acceptable price. Frog, already holding a lot of RED, will still trade if they can get very favorable terms but may avoid offers that are now clearly unattractive to hedgehog or badger. Badger, seeing the horizon end, will consider small, nearly risk-free arbitrage-like trades but decline anything that does not give a clear immediate surplus in their private values.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values on red chips and a common, known value for green chips. Everyone knows each green chip is worth $0.50 to all players. However, red chips are worth $0.30 to mouse-blue-9609 (Mouse), $1.00 to tiger-red-2074 (Tiger), and $0.80 to unicorn-red-4526 (Unicorn). All start with 10 red and 10 green chips. Because green is common-value, all players treat it like a stable, transparent currency. Red, in contrast, is privately valued, so each player only sees their own red value; they must infer others’ valuations from observed trade proposals and acceptances. From an analyst’s perspective, Tiger values red far more than green (2:1), Unicorn moderately prefers red to green (1.6:1), and Mouse actually prefers green to red (0.5 vs 0.3), so efficient trade would move red chips from Mouse (low red value) to Tiger and Unicorn (high red value) in exchange for green moving the other way. Strategic tension arises because players cannot choose which responder they will trade with when multiple accept; this pushes them to construct offers that are especially attractive to their most likely high-value counterpart, but perhaps only modestly attractive to others. Additionally, given just three rounds and one proposal per player per round, proposers must balance early exploratory offers (to learn or signal) against direct surplus-extracting offers that immediately improve their own position. Mouse will likely try to sell red for green at moderately generous terms, Tiger will try to accumulate red with aggressive but not too revealing offers, and Unicorn will balance between acquiring red and not overpaying with green. Many borderline trades will be declined due to uncertainty: players know green’s exact common value, but they must guess how much others value red, and they risk either leaving money on the table or making a bad deal if they misjudge others’ willingness to trade.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players have symmetric holdings (10 red, 10 green each) but different private values for red. Mouse sees red as relatively weak, so it is natural for Mouse to experiment with trading away red for green on favorable terms. Tiger and Unicorn both privately value red highly, but they do not yet know that others have different valuations; they only know that green is common-valued. In this first round, offers are likely to be somewhat exploratory: Mouse may test whether others are willing to give up green for red, Tiger may tentatively offer green to get red without being too generous, and Unicorn may adopt a mid-range stance—willing to gain red at a decent rate but wary of overpaying. Because everyone still has full endowments and no trade history, the probability of decline is high if offers appear lopsided from any responder’s own perspective.

Going into Round 1, the only executed trade so far is Tiger giving 3 green to Unicorn for 2 red. Publicly, all players see that Tiger was willing to part with multiple green chips for relatively few red chips, and Unicorn accepted that. This suggests, from Mouse’s and Unicorn’s perspectives, that Tiger may value red quite a bit—or at least more than green—and from Tiger’s perspective, that Unicorn also sees some decent value in red. Unicorn’s green holdings are now slightly lower while red holdings increased; Tiger’s green holdings decreased and red increased. Mouse has unchanged holdings after its first declined attempt. In this round, Mouse may react by sweetening terms when offering red for green, guessing that some others value red more than she does. Tiger, having successfully acquired red, might now offer trades that are slightly less generous, pressing their apparent advantage. Unicorn, having just paid a moderate amount of green for red, may become choosier, trying not to erode green holdings too far while still open to favorable opportunities.

By Round 2, Tiger has repeatedly signaled a willingness to exchange green for red, and Unicorn has repeatedly accepted trades that give up green for red at roughly even or slightly unfavorable apparent terms from green’s perspective. Mouse, after finally executing a trade selling red for green in Round 1, has increased green and reduced red holdings, consistent with its low private valuation of red. Publicly, the pattern is that Tiger and Unicorn are both shifting green into red, while Mouse is moving in the opposite direction. This makes it increasingly clear that Mouse probably values red less, while Tiger and Unicorn value red more. In this final round, proposers are aware that time is ending and will likely propose more aggressive, value-extracting offers: Mouse could continue to unload remaining red for green, but may face resistance since Tiger and Unicorn have already paid a lot of green; Tiger and Unicorn, in turn, might make last attempts to gain red, especially from Mouse, but Mouse will only accept if the green compensation is clearly beneficial given its low red valuation. Acceptance thresholds tighten as each player tries to lock in a final improvement without taking on a clearly losing trade.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player, three-round bargaining game, everyone starts with 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN has a common known value of $0.50 for all players, so GREEN is always worth $0.50 regardless of who holds it. RED, however, is privately valued: parrot and bear each value RED at $0.20, while fox values RED much more at $0.90. Each player knows their own values but only has a public range for others, so they must infer how others value RED from observed proposals and responses. From an analyst’s perspective, efficient trade moves RED away from parrot and bear toward fox, especially if fox can compensate them with GREEN, which is more valuable to them than RED. Fox wants to accumulate as much RED as possible without giving away more value in GREEN than they gain. Parrot and bear would like to offload RED at a price above $0.20 in terms of GREEN, but they face uncertainty: they do not know exactly how much fox values RED, nor do they know how much the other low-RED-value player is willing to undercut them. Because acceptance decisions are simultaneous and the recipient is randomly chosen if multiple players accept, senders must think about whether they want both potential recipients to find a deal attractive; they cannot target a specific partner. Early in the game, parrot and bear are likely to test the market with exploratory offers of RED for GREEN that are mildly favorable to themselves but not so generous that fox’s acceptance is guaranteed. Fox, who likely recognizes that some others may value RED less, may start with somewhat aggressive demands for RED in exchange for relatively few GREEN, hoping that at least one of the low-RED-value players will bite. As the game progresses and players update beliefs from observed acceptances and rejections, trades can become more tailored: if fox suspects that both parrot and bear are low-value-RED types, fox may push for very favorable RED-for-GREEN ratios. However, with only three rounds (nine total proposals) and no communication other than observable offers and accept/reject behavior, learning is limited. Some mutually beneficial trades may fail because proposers misjudge the minimum GREEN price that parrot or bear require to part with RED. The game is a mix of value-extraction by fox, strategic shading by parrot and bear, and incomplete information that keeps all players somewhat cautious. The forecast below assumes risk-neutral, myopic but somewhat strategic behavior, with players picking reasonable, simple heuristics rather than solving the game perfectly.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have symmetric holdings (10 RED, 10 GREEN), but very different private values for RED. No one has yet observed any behavior, so proposals are likely to be relatively exploratory. Parrot and bear are both low-RED-value players and would like to test whether they can profitably convert RED into GREEN. Fox, who values RED highly, wants to start accumulating RED at a good price, ideally paying with as few GREEN as possible. Because no one has revealed their type, early trades should be moderately conservative: offers are likely to be just good enough that they might be accepted by either possible counterparty, but not so generous that the proposer obviously overpays.

Going into round 1, the history shows that parrot’s initial attempt to sell RED for GREEN was rejected by both others, and fox successfully bought 3 RED from bear for just 1 GREEN. Bear’s acceptance reveals that at least one other player is likely low-value on RED, and fox now holds more RED and slightly fewer GREEN. Parrot, seeing bear’s willingness to trade RED cheaply, infers that bear may also undervalue RED and that fox is eager to buy RED at discount prices. Bear learns that fox is happy to buy RED but might have driven too hard a bargain the first time. Asymmetric information is somewhat reduced: it is now more plausible that both parrot and bear are low-RED types and fox is high-RED. This makes fox more confident in pushing for additional RED at favorable rates, while parrot and bear are more aware that they may have left surplus on the table and might try to negotiate slightly better terms without scaring fox away.

By the start of round 2, multiple accepted trades have confirmed the pattern: fox consistently buys RED from parrot and bear at cheap rates (2–3 RED per 1 GREEN), and both parrot and bear are repeatedly willing to sell RED for modest increases in GREEN. This strongly indicates to all players that fox is a high-RED-value type and that parrot and bear are low-RED-value types, with GREEN being the stable, high-value store for everyone. The remaining scope for gain lies in transferring more RED from parrot and bear to fox, as long as there is enough GREEN remaining with fox to pay for them. Parrot and bear may attempt to raise their RED price slightly, while fox may try to squeeze out one last favorable deal. With only one round left and limited remaining GREEN in fox’s hands, players are more willing to accept any trade that yields a small but clear expected gain, even if it is not perfectly priced.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player bargaining game with two chip types, RED and GREEN, and three rounds of alternating proposals. Each player starts with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, but RED is privately valued and asymmetric: goose values RED highly at $0.90 (and GREEN at $0.50), buffalo values both colors equally at $0.50, and monkey values RED very poorly at $0.10 (and GREEN at $0.50). From an analyst’s perspective, efficient reallocation would move RED chips from monkey to goose, and possibly from buffalo to goose, in exchange for GREEN (or nothing if bargaining power allowed) because that raises total surplus: goose converts low-value RED from monkey into high-value RED, and monkey converts low-value RED into valuable GREEN. However, players see only their own values. Goose knows RED is more valuable than GREEN for themselves and will generally try to acquire RED and shed GREEN at favorable rates. Monkey knows RED is worse than GREEN for themselves and will be willing to dump RED for GREEN at modest terms. Buffalo is value-symmetric and is likely indifferent in pure value terms, but may still trade to exploit perceived mispricings or to test others’ willingness to pay. Strategic uncertainty arises because no one knows others’ private values or whether a given preference pattern is common. The bargaining dynamic will center on discovering which direction trades are attractive and who is willing to trade RED for GREEN at what rate. Goose will push for RED cheaply, monkey will seek GREEN for their RED, and buffalo may oscillate or position in the middle, occasionally acting as an intermediary. Random selection among multiple acceptors adds another layer of risk: a proposer can’t perfectly target a specific partner if more than one is willing. Over three rounds, proposals will likely become more tailored—early turns probing for interest, later turns exploiting observed acceptance patterns—while always constrained by the proposer’s current holdings and by the requirement that each proposal involves offering one color and requesting a different one.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 RED and 10 GREEN. No one has yet observed others’ behavior, so proposals are exploratory. Goose, who values RED more than GREEN, is motivated to signal an interest in acquiring RED or in offloading GREEN, but must guess what others might accept. Monkey, with very low RED value, should be inclined to turn RED into GREEN if offered. Buffalo is nearly value-neutral and may accept trades that seem slightly favorable or that reveal information about others’ preferences. Because this is the first round, offers are likely conservative and symmetric, testing whether other players will move RED for GREEN at modest rates. No one yet knows that monkey particularly dislikes RED and goose particularly loves it, so efficient RED reallocation has to be discovered through trial trades.

By Round 1, some structure has emerged. Goose successfully traded 3 GREEN for 2 RED with monkey, modestly improving goose’s bundle and strongly improving monkey’s; then monkey traded 3 RED for 2 GREEN with buffalo, which again suited monkey’s preferences and left buffalo with relatively more RED. The net effect is that RED has started to move away from monkey, accumulating with goose and buffalo. Observationally, both goose and buffalo have seen that monkey is willing to give up RED for GREEN at fairly generous terms, hinting that monkey values RED relatively little. Monkey has also observed that both others are willing to acquire RED if they can do so at reasonable exchange rates. In this middle round, goose may push harder to acquire RED, perhaps from buffalo, while monkey continues to look for ways to shift any remaining RED into GREEN. Buffalo, who now holds extra RED, may start to suspect that RED is somewhat valuable to others and demand better terms when parting with it.

Entering Round 2, goose has consistently used GREEN to accumulate RED, monkey has used RED to gain GREEN, and buffalo has been somewhat cautious, occasionally trading but rejecting deals that seem too unfavorable. The public history now suggests that goose likes RED, monkey is eager to divest RED, and buffalo is roughly indifferent but increasingly aware that others attach different values. With only one round left, proposals become more aggressive: players have no reason to hold back surplus-improving trades, and they face a last opportunity to adjust portfolios. Goose will try to secure any remaining attractive RED-for-GREEN deals, likely from buffalo, while monkey, if still holding some RED, might attempt one more push to offload it. Buffalo becomes more selective, knowing both counterparts appear willing to move value in predictable directions and trying to avoid being exploited in the final trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game revolves around exchanging red and green chips, where green chips have the same known value ($0.50) for everyone, but red chips are privately valued and heterogeneous: otter and squid value red highly ($0.90 and $0.80), while ram values red very little ($0.10). Because players only know their own values, they must infer others’ valuations from proposed trades and acceptance behavior. Ram is structurally a natural seller of red and buyer of green (since red is worth much less than green to ram), while otter and squid are natural buyers of red and sellers of green. However, since everyone sees the same public range for red ([$0.10, $1.00]), each player can plausibly believe that others might have either high or low red values. This creates uncertainty: otter and squid don’t initially know that ram is desperate to unload red, and ram doesn’t know that otter and squid really want red. Early in the game, proposals will likely be somewhat conservative, testing the waters of what others accept. Over time, trades that reveal a pattern of one player consistently offering red for green (or vice versa) will help others infer relative valuations. Strategic tension comes from (a) extracting surplus from beneficial red-green trades, (b) not revealing too much private information too quickly, and (c) the three-player structure: when a player makes an attractive offer, two potential counterparties may compete to accept, and the proposer cannot choose which one they end up trading with. This means proposers often must design trades that are acceptable to at least one likely counterparty, while anticipating that the realized partner is determined randomly if more than one accepts. Across three short rounds with perfect public history, we should see movement of red chips from ram to the high-red-valuers (otter and squid) in exchange for green, with some missed opportunities and declined offers as players probe each other’s willingness to trade under uncertainty about exact valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players still have their initial endowments (10 red, 10 green). None know the others’ exact red values, so the primary goal is exploratory: propose trades that are plausibly beneficial given the public range but that also test whether others are relatively red-loving or red-averse. Ram is the only player for whom green is clearly better than red, so ram is especially motivated to initiate red-for-green trades. Otter and squid, if they notice an opportunity, may already start attempting to buy red using green, but they must balance aggressive offers against the risk that others do not value chips as they expect. Since players move sequentially this round, each proposal and response also serves as a signal for the next proposers about possible valuations.

By the start of round 1, one substantial trade has occurred: ram has sold a block of red to squid for green. Publicly, everyone now sees that ram was willing to give up many reds for relatively few greens, and that squid was willing to absorb those reds, hinting that ram likely has a low red value while squid likely has a higher red value relative to green. Otter learns that squid is a strong candidate to want red and that ram is likely eager to trade red away. Squid, having just purchased red from ram, may now be more selective, while ram may seek to continue offloading red for green if possible. Otter and squid also start forming expectations about each other’s and ram’s willingness to take on or shed red, making targeted offers likelier.

Entering round 2, the history reveals a consistent pattern: ram has repeatedly offered red in exchange for green and successfully traded away a significant number of reds, while otter and squid have selectively bought red with green. This pattern clarifies relative valuations: ram is almost certainly the lowest red valuer, and both otter and squid appear to value red more than green. Remaining red chips are now more concentrated in otter and squid’s hands, and ram’s green holdings are higher than at the start. With only one round left, players have limited time to capture any remaining surplus. Otter and squid might still attempt to buy small additional quantities of red from ram at favorable terms, while ram will only accept trades that improve its green share without giving up too many valuable greens it has already acquired. However, given prior trades, feasible win-win opportunities are more limited, so some proposals may be declined if they do not strictly benefit both sides given their now-inferred valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game involves trading two chip types: GREEN chips, which are known by all to be worth $0.50 each, and RED chips, which have private, player-specific values. Camel privately values RED chips highly at $0.80, while Butterfly and Alligator both value RED at only $0.20. All three value GREEN at $0.50. Because the common public range for RED spans $0.10–$1.00, each player knows there may be substantial heterogeneity in others’ RED values but does not know the exact numbers. From an analyst’s perspective, Camel is the high-RED-valuing player; Butterfly and Alligator are symmetric low-RED-valuing players. Rationally, we expect Camel to try to acquire RED chips and give up GREEN chips, because Camel’s marginal value of RED (0.80) exceeds that of GREEN (0.50). For Butterfly and Alligator, the reverse holds: they should be inclined to trade away RED for GREEN if the terms are favorable, because they value GREEN (0.50) more than RED (0.20). However, players must simultaneously worry about adverse selection and competition. Butterfly and Alligator, not knowing Camel’s true RED value, may suspect that a player aggressively buying RED has a high private value and thus may become cautious about selling too cheaply. Likewise, Camel faces the risk that if a trade is attractive for both low-RED players, multiple acceptances could occur and the realized partner is chosen at random, making it hard to target only one counterparty or to exploit asymmetries between them. Over the three rounds, each player gets three proposal turns in a fixed order. Early in the game, players are likely to probe the market with exploratory offers that signal or test others’ valuations. As the game proceeds and some information is revealed through observed acceptances or rejections, offers may become more tailored and efficient, with Camel increasingly trying to consolidate RED holdings and at least one of the low-RED players accepting trades that convert their relatively low-value RED into higher-value GREEN. The finite horizon of three rounds creates pressure for players to secure surplus before the game ends, but uncertainty about others’ values and the randomization when multiple players accept can still leave some potential gains unrealized.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips and know only their own private RED value. Camel privately knows RED is very valuable (0.80) and GREEN is less valuable (0.50), so should prefer to buy RED and sell GREEN. Butterfly and Alligator see symmetric private values (RED 0.20, GREEN 0.50), and therefore should be inclined to trade away RED for GREEN, but may initially test the market rather than immediately concede favorable terms to others. Because no one has yet observed any trading behavior, uncertainty about others’ private values is highest in this round. Proposals are likely to be somewhat exploratory and may not immediately clear all possible gains from trade.

At the start of round 1, the only executed trade so far is Butterfly’s sale of 3 RED for 2 GREEN to Alligator. Publicly, this indicates that at least one low-RED-valuing player is willing to trade away RED at favorable terms for GREEN; Alligator’s acceptance reveals that Alligator is comfortable acquiring additional GREEN at the cost of RED, consistent with valuing GREEN relatively strongly. Camel has seen that Butterfly and Alligator are willing to move RED and GREEN but has not yet found a partner for a RED-for-GREEN swap. Camel’s first proposal having been rejected at a 1:1 ratio may push Camel to improve terms slightly or to target one of the low-RED players more aggressively. Butterfly, having successfully reduced their RED holdings, may become somewhat less eager to sell more RED cheaply, while Alligator, now holding more GREEN and fewer RED, may look to rebalance only if terms are clearly favorable.

By round 2, several trades have been proposed and a few have executed, revealing patterns. It is now clear in public history that Butterfly has repeatedly offered to sell RED for GREEN and that Alligator has sometimes accepted such deals, indicating both of them place relatively higher value on GREEN than on RED. Camel has succeeded in obtaining some RED from Butterfly by offering a generous GREEN payment, which suggests to Butterfly that Camel may value RED more than they do. With only one round left and three turns remaining, players face a closing window to extract surplus. Camel, having already improved its RED holdings, may attempt one more favorable trade if possible but could also become selective, accepting only clearly advantageous terms. Butterfly and Alligator, recognizing that no further opportunities will arise after this round, may propose more aggressive or lopsided trades in their favor, though such proposals are less likely to be accepted if they deviate too far from perceived fair ratios established earlier in the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players begin with identical chip endowments (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but have different private values for RED. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 to everyone, so any trade of GREEN for GREEN is pointless. RED is low-value for owl ($0.20), high-value for iguana ($0.70), and intermediate for dolphin ($0.60). Because players only know their own values, each must infer others’ valuations from observed offers and acceptances.

The pure surplus possibilities arise from reallocating RED from owl (who values it least) toward iguana and dolphin, in exchange for GREEN which everyone values the same. Socially efficient trades move RED away from owl, but privately each player only cares about their own gain. A trade is privately appealing if the chips received are worth more (at that player’s own values) than the chips given up.

Incentives:
- Owl wants to give up RED and receive GREEN: exchanging 1 RED (worth $0.20) for 1 GREEN (worth $0.50) gains $0.30 per unit. Owl will try to sell RED relatively cheaply in terms of GREEN to attract a counterparty but still secure a gain. Owl is unlikely to ever buy RED at the expense of GREEN, since RED is strictly worse.
- Iguana highly values RED. They want to accumulate RED while giving up GREEN: trading 1 GREEN ($0.50) for 1 RED ($0.70) yields $0.20 per unit. Iguana will tend to offer GREEN for RED, but cannot be sure whether others also value RED highly; too generous a price might be accepted by both opponents, creating competition risk where a random partner is chosen.
- Dolphin also values RED more than GREEN, but slightly less than iguana does. Dolphin has an incentive structure similar to iguana’s—willing to give GREEN to get RED—but at a slightly narrower gain margin. Dolphin may be more cautious if offered unfavorable green-for-red ratios.

Uncertainty and signaling: Early proposals reveal how the proposers internally rank RED vs GREEN: someone trying to buy RED with GREEN implies RED likely has higher private value for them. As proposals and acceptances play out, others can infer who is a strong demander of RED and adjust pricing accordingly. However, with only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, there is limited time to fully learn valuations or to punish uncooperative behavior.

Strategically, players face a race against time: waiting for a better price may mean missing profitable trades altogether. Because multiple acceptances lead to random partner selection, a proposer cannot precisely target a preferred counterpart; they must design offers that are acceptable but not too attractive to both potential partners if they want to shape who is likely to accept. Across the three rounds, we therefore expect: (1) initial exploratory offers that test others’ willingness to trade RED vs GREEN, (2) mid-game adjustments as valuations are partially inferred, and (3) final attempts by iguana and dolphin to scoop remaining cheap RED from owl, if revealed, or conservative offers if uncertainty remains too high.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, players know only their own valuations. Owl knows RED is weak and GREEN is strong, so is inclined to sell RED for GREEN if possible. Iguana and dolphin both internally prefer RED over GREEN and will seek to buy RED, but they do not yet know that owl is especially keen to dump RED. Round 0 proposals are therefore exploratory: each player tests basic tradeoffs to gauge how others value RED versus GREEN.

After round 0, some structure is revealed. Iguana has signaled strong demand for RED by accepting owl’s offer, and owl has demonstrated willingness to sell RED below par in GREEN terms. Dolphin observes that iguana bought RED first, suggesting iguana might value RED even more than dolphin does. Now iguana wants to keep buying RED but must avoid giving too favorable a price, while owl seeks to continue offloading RED. Dolphin has an incentive to compete for RED but may be wary of overpaying, especially having just seen owl reject a 1:1 swap when dolphin proposed it.

By the final round, owl has largely converted RED into GREEN and views any remaining RED as low-value inventory. Iguana and dolphin each hold more RED than at the start and have learned that owl is a cheap source of RED when still holding it. With only one proposal each remaining, players focus on quick, clearly beneficial trades. Iguana wants to secure any last profitable RED from owl, while dolphin may still seek marginal gains but faces tighter opportunities after iguana’s aggressive prior buying.
