parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, three-round bargaining game with two chip types: GREEN (common-value $0.50 for everyone) and RED (privately valued). All start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN chips. Players know their own valuations but not the others', only that RED is worth between $0.10 and $1.00. Here, privately, RED is actually lowest for whale-orange-8655 ($0.30), medium for zebra-pink-5323 ($0.60), and highest for swan-blue-3036 ($1.00). GREEN is common-value at $0.50 to all. Efficiently, RED should flow toward swan and away from whale, and GREEN can flow the opposite way if needed to compensate. However, players cannot condition on exact others’ values; they must infer from a plausible prior and from observed behavior. Each proposal involves offering one color and requesting the other, so trades are always RED-for-GREEN swaps. Given the true valuations, any trade that moves RED from whale to zebra or swan in exchange for GREEN is surplus creating, with the largest surplus when RED goes to swan. But behavioral frictions matter: players may demand too much GREEN for their RED, or misjudge how much others value RED, leading to rejections. Because acceptances are simultaneous and recipients are randomly chosen if multiple accept, proposers cannot perfectly target a partner; they must consider both possible counterparties’ incentives. Over only three rounds, players have limited time to learn from others’ behavior, so they are likely to make simple, myopic trades that look immediately profitable given conservative guesses about others’ valuations. In this forecast, we assume moderately sophisticated but risk-averse behavior: each player offers trades that are clearly personally profitable even if the counterparty values RED only moderately higher than $0.50, and they accept trades that give them clear expected surplus. This generates a pattern where RED gradually moves from whale to zebra and swan, with GREEN flowing back, but not to the fully efficient extreme because of bargaining uncertainty and the random-recipient constraint.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players have symmetric holdings (10 RED, 10 GREEN) but asymmetric private values. No one has yet observed any behavior, so they rely on priors and simple heuristics. Whale, having relatively low RED value, is inclined to sell RED for GREEN at rates that look profitable versus a conservative guess of others’ RED values. Zebra has medium RED value and is willing to buy RED if the RED price is clearly below $0.60. Swan, with the highest RED value, is the most eager buyer but also cannot be sure others undervalue RED as much as he does. Proposals in this round serve a dual role: they move chips and also reveal some coarse information about how the proposer internally prices RED versus GREEN.

By round 1, some information has been revealed through behavior. Whale has twice shown willingness to exchange RED for relatively cheap GREEN, signaling that whale probably values RED less than the others. Swan has accepted a bargain purchase of RED and offered a break-even or slightly generous RED price to whale, suggesting that Swan values RED at least as much as, or more than, GREEN. Zebra has declined a 1:1 RED-for-GREEN trade from their own perspective as sender (they chose not to sell RED cheaply) and did not accept Swan’s early trade with whale, hinting that Zebra places RED above $0.50 but is more cautious. Holdings are now asymmetric: whale has fewer RED and more GREEN; swan has more RED and fewer GREEN; zebra remains at the original 10/10. Players now condition on these updated holdings: whale can continue to sell RED at modest discounts, swan wants to acquire remaining cheap RED, and zebra may start to participate if a clearly favorable rate appears.

Going into the final round, players have seen several trades at or near a 2 RED for 1 GREEN type of price when whale sells, and some 1:1 or 2:2 offers from others. This history suggests to everyone that whale’s RED value is relatively low, while both zebra and swan seem to value RED at least as much as GREEN. Holdings have evolved so that whale has offloaded some RED to both zebra and swan in exchange for GREEN, zebra has begun to accumulate RED while keeping a balanced position, and swan has already improved their RED holdings as well. With only one proposal left per player, incentives shift toward opportunistic, myopic gains rather than signaling: each proposer looks for a clearly profitable, likely-to-be-accepted trade. However, because all three now hold somewhat more RED than at the start (in expectation for zebra and swan) and GREEN is still valuable, there is less slack to make obviously Pareto-improving trades. Some proposals are declined because they offer insufficient surplus given better knowledge of others’ likely RED valuations and the lack of future bargaining rounds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value green chips identically at $0.50 each, but they differ in red-chip value: pig-blue-1277 and elephant-yellow-5788 value red at $0.40, while turtle-yellow-3106 values red much lower at $0.10. Everyone starts with 10 red and 10 green chips. Because green is common-value and known to be at least as valuable as $0.50 to everyone, and red is clearly in the $0.10–$1.00 public range, the safe inference for all players is that green is at least as good as red for everyone, and especially good relative to red for themselves. This creates a strong incentive for pig and elephant (high-red players) to try to acquire red from turtle, who is the most likely to part with red cheaply, while turtle tries to turn their relatively low-value red into higher-value green. However, nobody knows that turtle’s red is especially low and pig/elephant’s red is moderately high; each only knows their own type and that others’ red values could be anywhere in the public range. Strategic uncertainty arises because (1) proposers don’t know how much surplus a trade offers the counterparty, so setting terms too aggressively may lead to rejection, and (2) both non-senders respond simultaneously, with tie-breaking random, so a proposer cannot precisely target a single partner. Over three rounds, the early turns tend to be exploratory, revealing rough willingness to trade and hinting at others’ valuations. As profitable trades occur, holdings shift, altering who has bargaining power and what terms are credible. Rational players try to execute trades that increase their own value while anticipating that others will only accept if the deal is at least weakly beneficial given their private valuations and expectations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric holdings (10 red, 10 green) but asymmetric red values privately. Each expects green to be at least as valuable as red for everyone, making green a natural asking currency. Pig and elephant, with higher red values, are inclined to buy red cheaply if possible, while turtle wants to sell red for green. However, because players only know their own valuations, early proposals tend to be conservative and exploratory, testing demand for red versus green and observing who is willing to trade at what terms.

After round 0, pig has gained red and given up some green, turtle has upgraded some low-value red into green, and elephant’s holdings remain unchanged. The accepted 3-red-for-2-green trade suggests to observers that turtle is relatively willing to part with red, and that pig values red reasonably highly. Elephant infers that pig is a serious competitor for turtle’s red and that turtle’s red might be cheap. Pig now has more red and fewer green, shifting pig toward wanting green back but only at favorable terms. Turtle, now holding more green and fewer red, will be more reluctant to sell green but may still offload residual red at a profit. Proposals in this round respond to these updated holdings and inferred types, but players still must respect that others’ valuations are not fully revealed.

By the final round, both pig and elephant have successfully purchased some low-value red from turtle at ratios that are modestly favorable to turtle ex post (since turtle’s red is worth only $0.10 each) but still profitable for the buyers given their $0.40 red value. Turtle has converted multiple low-value red chips into higher-value green, improving their payoff. Pig and elephant now both face diminishing returns from further red purchases because their green balances are lower and additional red is less transformative. With only one proposing opportunity left for each, players balance the chance of a last marginally profitable trade against the risk that their offers are rejected due to others’ now-stronger outside options and reduced need to trade.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value green chips identically at $0.50 (common knowledge range matches the true value), but they differ in how much they privately value red chips: kangaroo-red-2064 values red the most ($1.00), gorilla-red-579 is intermediate ($0.90), and fox-green-431 values red the least ($0.60). In value terms, all players should want to move from green into red, since each gives up $0.50 for at least $0.60 in expected value. However, because everyone privately believes red is relatively valuable to them, each is also reluctant to part with red cheaply. From an observer’s perspective, there is very little scope for truly mutually beneficial trades: any trade that moves red from kangaroo (1.00) or gorilla (0.90) to fox (0.60) destroys total surplus, and trades swapping red among kangaroo and gorilla just move value from one to the other without increasing group surplus. The only efficiency-enhancing pattern would be red flowing from fox to kangaroo or gorilla, but even fox still values red higher than green, so she is unlikely to part with red at a 1:1 or worse red-for-green rate. Because players only know their own values, they face uncertainty about others’ valuations of red and may try to infer them from observed proposals and acceptances. Strategically, the highest-value player for red (kangaroo) has the strongest incentive to acquire red even at apparently generous green-for-red offers, while fox, as the lowest-value red holder, has the weakest willingness to overpay for red and is the most likely to test trades that slightly favor herself in red-for-green terms. Gorilla is in the middle and may accept trades that give more red in exchange for green if they look at least break-even in her private values. Simultaneous responses and random tie-breaking create a risk for proposers when both potential partners could profit: a proposer can’t target the best partner, so she must design offers that work reasonably well regardless of which responder is chosen. Over three short rounds with each player proposing once per round, players may start with cautious or exploratory offers, then adjust in later rounds based on who seems willing to exchange red for green and at what apparent rates. Given the narrow surplus available and common high demand for red, many offers that superficially transfer red for green will be declined as too unfavorable to at least one side, making accepted trades relatively sparse and often modest in size.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 red and 10 green chips. No one has seen any behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ red values are diffuse but anchored by the public range that red is somewhere between $0.10 and $1.00. Players commonly know that green is exactly $0.50, so any trade is mentally benchmarked against $0.50 per green. This first round is likely to feature exploratory offers: proposers will test whether others are willing to exchange red and green at rates that are slightly favorable to themselves. Because fox privately places the lowest value on red, she may still try to cheaply accumulate red if she believes others value red even less. Kangaroo, as the highest red valuer, will bias toward acquiring more red, possibly offering generous green-for-red ratios early to ensure acceptance. Gorilla may behave somewhat similarly to kangaroo but with slightly less aggression, probing what trades can pass without giving away too much in expected value.

Going into round 1, the only executed trade has moved some red from gorilla to kangaroo at a 1:1 red-green rate. This reveals that gorilla was willing to part with red for green on those terms, and that kangaroo strongly wants red. Observers infer that kangaroo likely has a high valuation for red, while gorilla’s willingness to trade suggests her red value may not be at the very top of the possible range. Fox, having seen her own even trade declined, learns that others are not eager to trade red at parity with green when they are on the selling side. Strategically, kangaroo is now richer in red and poorer in green, but still with the highest red value, so she continues to seek red if it is cheap enough while guarding her remaining green carefully. Gorilla may become more selective after seeing her own offer to buy red declined and having already sold some red. Fox, still holding her original mix, might adjust by offering a slightly more favorable deal to potential red sellers or by attempting to sell a little red if she thinks others overvalue it.

By round 2, some pattern is clear: kangaroo has repeatedly accepted deals that give her more red, revealing a very high private valuation for red. Gorilla has both sold and bought red in response to terms that look privately favorable, indicating an intermediate valuation. Fox, after managing to sell red for green in round 1, recognizes that others seem willing to pay relatively generously for red and so becomes reluctant to buy it back except at very good terms. Everyone now has partial information: they know that kangaroo is the most aggressive red accumulator, gorilla is flexible, and fox is cautious about red. The final round typically sees more targeted offers: fox will try to sell red only if she can extract multiple green per red; kangaroo will offer to buy red only when the green cost is not too high; gorilla may test one last time whether she can profitably adjust her mix of red and green. With only three proposal opportunities left (one per player), the urgency to lock in any profitable trade increases, but so does the selectiveness about offers that might worsen one’s final portfolio.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game features two chip types: GREEN (common value $0.50 each) and RED (privately valued, heterogeneous across players). All players start with 10 RED and 10 GREEN. The private valuations are: tiger values RED at $0.80, bat at $0.90, and unicorn at $0.60; all value GREEN at $0.50. Because RED is more valuable than GREEN for all players, no one has an obvious incentive to trade away RED for GREEN at any rate that another player would accept, unless they misestimate others’ RED values or infer that someone else undervalues RED. From an analyst perspective, bat is the highest-value RED holder, then tiger, then unicorn; efficient surplus gains would involve RED flowing toward bat and tiger and GREEN flowing toward unicorn. However, since players only know their own private RED value and see only chip quantities and trade history (not valuations), they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be exploratory and conservative (small quantities, ratios near 1:1), as players avoid selling off too much RED. Because there is no direct reason to trade GREEN (identical values) and RED is strictly more valuable to all players than GREEN, most mutually beneficial trades will be very thin or fail if others also value RED highly. The main pattern we forecast is that senders will attempt to buy RED with GREEN at slightly favorable rates for themselves, while receivers will often reject because they also consider RED strong. Occasional accepted trades may occur when unicorn is willing to sell some RED if offered enough GREEN (as unicorn has the lowest RED value), and bat, with the highest RED value, has the strongest reason to accumulate RED. As trade history accumulates, players may update beliefs: rejections suggest others also value RED highly; acceptances, especially of RED-for-GREEN swaps, reveal relatively lower RED valuation by the seller. Because there are only three rounds and each player gets only one proposal per round, the scope for learning is limited; many proposals remain tentative, and several will be declined. Overall, we expect modest reallocation of RED from unicorn to bat (and sometimes to tiger) in exchange for GREEN, with most attempted trades failing due to overlapping high valuations and randomization risk when multiple parties might accept.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, no one has information about others’ private RED values beyond the shared belief that RED could plausibly lie between $0.10 and $1.00. All see symmetric initial chip quantities, so there is no inventory-based asymmetry. Bat, tiger, and unicorn each know they personally value RED more than GREEN, but do not know whether that is true for others. Round 0 is therefore mainly exploratory: senders will likely try small trades that test whether others are willing to part with RED for GREEN or vice versa. Because any accepted trade immediately changes holdings and is observed by all, even a single acceptance can shift beliefs about who values what. Players also face the risk that if they propose a “good deal” for a specific target partner, a different player might accept instead and be randomly selected as the actual recipient. This encourages cautious proposals that aren’t too generous to any potential counterparty.

After round 0, two key inferences are available. First, unicorn accepted 2 GREEN for 1 RED twice, revealing that unicorn values RED relatively less than the others or is at least willing to part with it at a 2:1 GREEN:RED rate. Second, both tiger and bat showed willingness to sacrifice GREEN for RED at that same 2:1 rate, indicating they both value RED strongly. Tiger and bat, seeing unicorn’s behavior, may now try again to buy RED from unicorn, perhaps at similar or slightly improved rates. Unicorn, having given up 2 RED already, might grow more cautious about further RED sales or try to reverse the flow by offering GREEN for RED. However, unicorn also realizes that both tiger and bat appear eager RED buyers, suggesting unicorn can hold out for at least 2 GREEN per RED, and maybe more. Competition and randomization risk matter: if either tiger or bat proposes too generous an offer to lure unicorn specifically, the other might also accept, risking an overly costly trade with the “wrong” partner.

By round 2, a pattern is clear to all: unicorn repeatedly accepts selling 1 RED for 2 GREEN, and both tiger and bat repeatedly buy RED at that price. This strongly suggests to tiger and bat that unicorn’s RED valuation is lower than theirs, roughly consistent with a threshold between their own values and unicorn’s; they now view unicorn as a reliable source of RED at 2 GREEN per RED. Unicorn, conversely, sees that both tiger and bat persist in paying that rate, confirming they value RED highly. Yet unicorn may also worry about having sold “too much” RED; with only a few rounds left, unicorn might become more conservative, limiting further RED sales or even trying to rebalance by buying back RED at a better rate. However, given the established 2:1 norm and the short remaining horizon, it is likely that at least one more 2 GREEN-for-1 RED trade with unicorn will be attempted and accepted. As inventories shift, tiger and bat’s willingness to pay might remain steady because each additional unit of RED still yields positive surplus at their valuations, and they do not observe each other’s exact values. Unicorn may attempt a small reverse trade (selling GREEN for RED), but the others now clearly understand that RED is jointly prized and will be reluctant to give it up for GREEN at any rate near 1:1.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip bargaining game has one common-value chip (GREEN at $0.50 for everyone) and two privately valued chip types (RED, BLUE). Bear strongly values BLUE ($1.00) and moderately values RED ($0.60); Buffalo moderately values BLUE ($0.70) and almost does not value RED ($0.10); Goose values RED and BLUE very little ($0.10 each). Everyone values GREEN identically at $0.50. From an omniscient perspective, the high-surplus trades move BLUE chips toward Bear and Buffalo (especially Bear) and move RED chips away from Buffalo and Goose toward Bear (since only Bear gives RED more than trivial value). However, since each player only knows their own private values, they must infer others' valuations from observed behavior. Buffalo and Bear know they both like BLUE more than RED, but they do not know the exact numbers or that Goose barely values colored chips. As the repeated proposal schedule always starts with Buffalo, then Bear, then Goose in each round, earlier turns are structurally more valuable for testing the market and building inventory, while later turns let players respond strategically to visible history. Buffalo should try to trade away low-value RED for GREEN or BLUE, but will be cautious about giving up BLUE unless a large surplus is clear. Bear should aggressively accumulate BLUE and some RED, using GREEN (and possibly excess RED) to pay, because Bear converts BLUE to the highest value. Goose, with low private values on RED and BLUE and no informational advantage, is best off not giving up GREEN cheaply and using proposals mainly to offload RED/BLUE when they can get at least GREEN or slight gains in value; but Goose may underestimate how much the others value BLUE and RED, especially Bear’s strong BLUE preference. The randomness when multiple players accept a proposal creates additional strategic uncertainty: a proposer cannot target a specific partner, so offers must be attractive enough that at least one player accepts, but not so generous that the proposer loses surplus no matter who accepts. Over three rounds, we can expect initial exploratory trades in Round 0, slightly more informed deal-making in Round 1 as players update beliefs, and more selective, value-focused trades in Round 2 as chip inventories and visible patterns of acceptance reveal rough relative preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, each player holds 10 of each chip color and only knows their own private values. No one yet has evidence about others’ valuations. Buffalo is likely to test whether anyone will pay GREEN for Buffalo’s low-value RED or BLUE, Bear is motivated to acquire BLUE while preserving enough GREEN, and Goose is inclined to hold onto GREEN and see what others are willing to offer for RED or BLUE. Early proposals in this round are exploratory: players float simple one-color-for-one-color trades to probe demand without risking large inventory shifts.

After Round 0, inventory and observed acceptances reveal some structure. Buffalo now holds slightly fewer BLUE and more GREEN, having accepted Bear’s BLUE-for-GREEN deal, which tentatively signals that Buffalo values BLUE at least moderately and is willing to trade GREEN for it. Bear’s successful acquisition of BLUE, paying in GREEN, signals a high BLUE valuation and a willingness to part with some GREEN. Goose’s declined proposal suggests others were not eager to give up GREEN for a 1:1 RED swap. In this second round, Buffalo and Bear refine their attempts: Buffalo may try to trade away RED or extra BLUE for GREEN at more favorable rates, and Bear may experiment with acquiring RED cheaply and offloading remaining less-valued chips. Goose remains cautious, recognizing that getting GREEN in exchange for RED or BLUE may require more generous terms.

Heading into the final round, the history shows that Bear has paid GREEN both for BLUE and for RED, strongly hinting that Bear values colored chips, especially BLUE, more than GREEN. Buffalo has only once traded, giving up GREEN to obtain BLUE, confirming moderate to high BLUE valuation and relatively lower concern about small changes in GREEN. Goose has managed to trade RED for GREEN but has failed to convince others to part with GREEN for either RED or BLUE at 1:1 or at modestly better rates, suggesting Goose still has little market power in colored chips. With limited turns left, Buffalo and Bear look for final, high-margin trades that move chips toward their highest private values while protecting their remaining GREEN. Goose may take one last shot at exchanging low-value RED or BLUE for GREEN, but the others have learned to be selective. The final proposals are more conservative in quantity but sharper in which colors they target, aiming for clear private-value gains with minimal risk.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, whose values differ privately across players, while green chips are common-value and thus uninteresting to trade purely for value differences. All players value red chips at $1.00 and green at $0.50, but they differ on blue: otter values blue at $0.40, peacock at $0.50, and cat at $0.70. Red is equally valuable to everyone, so there is no pure gains-from-trade motive in red-for-red exchanges; instead, red will function as a high-value currency that players may try to swap for low-value chips they personally dislike or to entice others into giving up more-preferred chips. Blue is where real gains from trade lie: cat values blue the most and is willing to pay with red or green to accumulate blue; otter values blue the least and has the strongest incentive to trade blue away for red or, secondarily, green; peacock sits in the middle and may trade blue depending on whether terms are sufficiently favorable in red. Because players know only their own values, they must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances. For example, generous offers of blue for red signal that blue might be low-value to the sender or high-value to the recipient; reluctance to give up blue reveals the opposite. Strategic uncertainty arises from simultaneous responses and random recipient selection when multiple players accept: proposers cannot target a specific partner, so they must design offers that are attractive on average to at least one responder without being so generous that they overpay. Early in the game, players will probe with mildly favorable trades to learn about others’ blue valuations and willingness to move red; later rounds will likely feature more targeted exchanges, especially otter trying to offload blue for red, and cat trying to buy blue with red or perhaps green. Peacock’s intermediate blue value makes them a flexible trader, sometimes selling blue to cat at a premium in red, and sometimes buying blue cheaply from otter. Green, being common-value, will be used sparingly as a sweetener or low-stakes probe rather than a main object of trade. Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), we expect moderate but not complete reallocation: cat will end with more blue and fewer red, otter with fewer blue and more red, and peacock’s portfolio will reflect opportunistic trades that happened to be accepted under uncertainty about others’ true values.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players are starting with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) and no information about others’ private values beyond the public ranges. They likely believe red is highly valued by everyone, green is fixed and modest, and blue could vary. The first moves will tend to be cautious, small trades that slightly favor the responder to encourage acceptance and generate information. As proposer order is otter, then peacock, then cat, otter has the first opportunity to signal priorities by which chip they try to shed or acquire. Peacock, moving second, can react to otter’s revealed tendencies, and cat, moving last, observes two proposals before acting and may already suspect that blue is relatively more important to them than to others, though they do not yet know specific values.

Entering round 1, one trade has occurred: otter swapped 2 blue for 1 red with cat. Observers can infer that otter is willing to give up blue at a discount for red, hinting that blue may be relatively low value to them, while cat is willing to pay red for blue, suggesting blue is relatively high value to cat. Peacock attempted a similar blue-for-red trade but found no takers, perhaps because the terms looked less attractive given new suspicions about others’ valuations. Cat’s failed attempt to buy blue with green indicates others do not see green as a sufficiently attractive payment compared to red. With this early information, otter is likely to continue offloading blue for red, cat to continue trying to buy blue (perhaps sweetening offers), and peacock to be more careful with blue and red, possibly experimenting with green or adjusting exchange rates.

By round 2, the history shows a pattern: otter repeatedly sells blue for red and has accepted receiving blue at relatively favorable rates when peacock offered, suggesting otter just wants to convert lower-valued blue into higher-valued red whenever advantageous. Cat has consistently tried to accumulate blue even at the cost of red, revealing a strong preference for blue. Peacock has been in the middle, sometimes selling blue to cat for red and once buying blue from otter, effectively arbitraging between them when terms look good. With only three proposal turns left (now peacock, then otter, then cat), players will aim for final reallocations that exploit these revealed preferences without overpaying. Proposers will design offers that are clearly attractive to at least one other player, given observed behavior, and responders will be more selective, accepting only trades that strictly increase their total monetary value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 of each chip color. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so green chips are straightforward: they are neither especially good nor bad relative to others without knowing opponents’ private values. The strategic uncertainty comes from red and blue chips, which have different private values for each player. Badger values red and blue highly ($0.80 each), camel values them moderately ($0.60 red, $0.40 blue), and squid values blue moderately and red poorly ($0.60 blue, $0.20 red). From an analyst perspective, there are clear gains from trade: squid should like to get rid of red in exchange for blue or possibly green; badger should want to accumulate red and blue from squid or camel if prices are favorable; camel is intermediate and can both arbitrage and respond to perceived mispricings. However, during play, each only knows their own valuations and must infer others’ based on observed offers and acceptances. Proposers must choose a single color to request (buy) and a different color to give up (sell), and any accepting responder receives the deal at the same terms. Because acceptance is simultaneous and the realized partner is random if multiple accept, proposers cannot target a specific counterparty. That encourages them to post terms that are acceptable to at least one other player, but not so generous that they overpay given their own valuations. Over three rounds, each player gets exactly three proposal turns. Early in the game, proposals may serve partially as information-gathering: relatively conservative, mildly advantageous terms that might or might not be accepted, revealing who has comparative advantages. As the final round approaches, players face deadline pressure and may accept somewhat worse deals to convert disliked chips into more valuable ones before the game ends. Given these priors, a likely pattern is that squid will try to trade away red, badger will try to acquire both red and blue at favorable rates, and camel will be somewhat selective, sometimes serving as a middle player trading into their better-valued red while unloading lower-valued blue when possible. The realized sequence below reflects one plausible complete playthrough under these incentives and uncertainties.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color. No public information about valuations has yet been revealed, so proposals are likely to be conservative and roughly balanced in nominal chip counts, while still favoring the proposer’s own preferences. Badger, who values both red and blue highly, will likely try to acquire more of at least one of those colors. Camel may test the waters by trying to reshape their mix toward red (which they value more than blue) or by offloading green if they suspect others might value it more. Squid, who dislikes red relative to blue and green, will be inclined to sell red for blue or green. In this first round, each player’s proposal can also signal something about how they internally rank the colors, which will shape how others respond in later rounds.

After Round 0, the public history shows that camel and squid willingly traded red and blue at 1:1, suggesting both are at least somewhat flexible between those colors. Squid then traded away red for green at 1:1, while camel accepted, which hints that squid may particularly dislike red, and camel may be comfortable accumulating red even at the expense of green. Badger has not yet traded, so their preferences remain opaque to others. Now, with updated holdings and some weak signals about opponents’ priorities, proposals in Round 1 can be more targeted. Badger might start revealing a preference for red or blue by what they try to buy; camel might look to unload surplus red or rebalance green; squid, now with very little red, might focus on marginal improvements between blue and green or small speculative trades.

By the start of Round 2, some structure is clear from the history: squid has systematically moved out of red into green and a bit more blue, while camel has accepted trades that increase red at the expense of both blue and green, and badger has finally traded to convert green into red. This pattern suggests to observers that red is relatively more valued by camel (and possibly badger) and less valued by squid. With only one final proposal opportunity each, players now face a deadline: any remaining misallocations must be corrected now or not at all. Badger, already having improved their mix of high-value red and blue, may only accept clearly favorable offers. Camel might attempt one more rebalancing move but must be careful not to give up too much blue or green for marginal red gains. Squid, holding mostly liked colors (blue and green) and few disliked red, has less pressing need to trade and will be choosy, leading to either a final modest mutually beneficial trade or a string of conservative offers that get declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common known value for green chips ($0.50 each). Each player starts with 10 of each chip. Dog values red highly ($1.00) and blue low ($0.20); Butterfly values blue highly ($0.90) and red moderately ($0.60); Ram values both red and blue low ($0.40 and $0.10, respectively). Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it acts as a natural reference point and medium of exchange. Dog has the strongest incentive to accumulate red (and to a lesser extent green) by selling blue. Butterfly wants to accumulate blue and is willing to give up some red and possibly green. Ram has relatively weak preferences over red and blue but still likes green more than either; Ram is thus a likely seller of red and blue in exchange for green. However, each player only knows their own payoffs; they infer others’ values from the structure of offers and responses over time. Because only one accepting responder is chosen at random when multiple accept, proposers must design offers that are attractive but not so generous that they frequently trigger multi-acceptor randomness that can hand gains to unintended partners. Over the three rounds, we should expect (1) early, somewhat exploratory offers that test how willing others are to trade green for red/blue; (2) mid-game reactions that build on observed acceptances or refusals, with Dog and Butterfly increasingly positioning to swap Dog’s blue for Butterfly’s red, and (3) late-game attempts by Dog and Butterfly to finalize surplus-enhancing trades, possibly using Ram opportunistically when Ram’s low valuations make Ram willing to accept modestly favorable rates. Most declined trades will be ones that ask a responder to give up high-value chips (from their perspective) for insufficient compensation, while accepted trades will typically move chips from lower-value holders to higher-value holders, especially blue toward Butterfly and red toward Dog, with green serving as the balancing chip.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no direct evidence of others’ private values, so they are likely to make relatively conservative, symmetric-looking offers anchored around the known value of green chips. Dog will tentatively try to convert blue into red or green, Butterfly will probe for blue at a moderate price, and Ram will experiment with modest green-for-colored-chip trades. Responses this round reflect baseline caution: players will avoid giving up chips they personally value highly unless the rate looks clearly favorable relative to the common $0.50 green benchmark.

By the second round, Ram has revealed a willingness to exchange green for blue at roughly even terms (GREEN 2 for BLUE 2), signaling that Ram does not value blue as highly as the average participant might. Dog and Butterfly can infer that Ram is relatively indifferent about blue and perhaps also about red, strengthening their belief that Ram might accept modestly favorable rates involving those colors. Dog now has slightly fewer blue chips and the same number of red and green; Butterfly has slightly more blue and fewer green; Ram has more green and fewer blue. This pattern encourages Dog and Butterfly to use green as a payment vehicle and to tailor proposals more specifically to exploit perceived weaknesses in Ram’s valuations while still trying to avoid trades that are obviously bad for their unknown counterpart’s incentives.

Entering the final round, Dog has diversified strongly into red and green by selling blue; Butterfly has gained blue but given up green; Ram has oscillated between blue and green and has realized that Dog is quite willing to buy red and green with blue, while Butterfly is very eager to acquire blue. The remaining opportunities lie primarily in moving remaining surplus cheap-to-holder chips into the hands of those who value them more. However, with only three turns left, players become more selective and may reject offers that do not yield clearly positive expected gains, especially when random matching among multiple accepters could undermine a carefully targeted trade. Final offers therefore aim to be just appealing enough that exactly one likely responder finds them acceptable. Dog will try to offload any remaining surplus blue in exchange for green or red, Butterfly will push once more for blue at a discount, and Ram will attempt a last small Pareto-improving exchange if the rates look safe given previous behavior.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, whose values differ privately across players, while green chips have a common, fixed value of $0.50 for everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each chip color and wants to maximize their own total dollar value, not joint surplus. Because players only know their own red/blue values, they must infer others’ valuations from revealed behavior (what they offer and what they are willing to accept). Public ranges (red/blue between $0.10 and $1.00) tell them these chips can be highly valuable to some players, but not how valuable to each specific counterpart. This creates uncertainty and strategic experimentation: early offers both test the market and signal (truthfully or not) how much a player seems to value certain colors. In this particular configuration, lion highly values blue and somewhat values red (BLUE=1.00, RED=0.40), iguana highly values red and somewhat values blue (RED=0.80, BLUE=0.40), and alligator values both red and blue but less than at least one counterpart (BLUE=0.60, RED=0.30). Green is worth $0.50 to everybody, so it functions like a stable “currency” for side-payments. The core efficiency logic is: send red chips toward iguana, blue chips toward lion, and treat green as neutral, with alligator often playing a middle role who can profit from trading away red to iguana and blue to lion while collecting green or somewhat less-valued colors in return. However, because players do not know that structure, they will probe with smaller, relatively safe trades that seem favorable given their own values, while trying to avoid obviously dominated deals. Accepted trades will be those that give each party a clear value gain relative to their private valuations, and rejections will occur when a trade seems too risky or reveals that the proposer is demanding too much surplus. Over three rounds, 9 total proposal turns offer enough time for some learning and pattern recognition, but not enough for full convergence to perfect efficiency, so we expect modest, somewhat conservative trades anchored around green as a stable chip, and more aggressive red/blue swaps later as players infer who values what.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold symmetric endowments (10 of each color) and have no direct evidence of others’ private values. They know only the public ranges and their own valuations. Proposers in this first round are likely to make small, information-gathering offers that look favorable from their own perspective, using green as a safe medium because its value is known and common. Alligator, seeing red as their lowest-valued chip and green as slightly more valuable, may tentatively try to acquire more green or blue by parting with red. Lion, who values blue most and red moderately, will be inclined to try to accumulate blue while spending green or some red. Iguana, with the highest value on red, will be keen to acquire red, potentially by giving up blue or some green. Because no one has yet signaled clear willingness to trade at specific rates, offers that ask for too many high-value chips in exchange for too few low-value chips will likely be declined, whereas clearly favorable swaps where the recipient gains obvious private surplus will be accepted. This round serves primarily to set initial beliefs about others’ preferences.

By round 1, the early trades in round 0 have revealed partial information: iguana’s willingness to trade away green for red, and lion’s willingness to give red for blue, suggest that iguana values red relatively highly and lion values blue strongly. Alligator, having successfully moved some low-valued red for green, learns that someone is prepared to pay more than they privately value red. This encourages more targeted proposals: alligator can try to function as a middle trader, shifting red toward iguana and blue toward lion in exchange for green or less-valued chips. Lion, after one mutually beneficial red–blue trade, has evidence that iguana also wants red and is willing to part with blue; lion can attempt similar or slightly more demanding rates. Iguana, now with a clearer sense that lion likes blue and at least one counterpart is willing to give up red, may push for additional red accumulation, potentially using blue or green as payment. However, with more structure inferred, counterparties also become more selective, rejecting trades that allocate too little of the shared surplus. As a result, some more aggressive proposals are likely to be declined, while moderately generous, clearly win–win offers continue to be accepted.

Entering the final round, trading history has clarified the broad pattern: iguana persistently seeks red and is willing to trade away blue and occasionally green; lion repeatedly tries to gain blue and is prepared to spend red and occasionally green; alligator’s early success trading red for green signals relatively low red value and comfort using green as a medium. With only three proposal turns left, players must balance experimentation with immediate gain. There is little time to recoup losses from a bad trade, so riskier or marginal offers are less attractive to accept. Proposers may either offer slightly generous terms to secure one last sure improvement or attempt more aggressive splits of surplus, which risk rejection but could yield high private gains if accepted. Because random partner selection among multiple acceptors can dilute control, proposers might structure offers that clearly favor their most likely counterpart (the agent whose values best align with the proposed direction of trade). Still, final-round caution and prior learning combine to produce a mix: at least one clear, mutually beneficial swap exploiting the known red–blue comparative advantages, and at least one declined proposal that pushes too hard on terms or asks for a color the recipients privately value highly.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Each player begins with 10 of each color. Whale and Duck both value red chips very highly at $1.00 each, while Dolphin values red less at $0.60. In contrast, Dolphin values blue at $0.80, higher than Whale’s $0.40 and Duck’s $0.60. All players value green at $0.50. Because players know only their own valuations, they must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances. Key incentives: (1) Players want to trade away chips they privately value less (e.g., Whale wants to trade away blue; Dolphin wants to trade away red; no one wants to trade away too much green because its value is known and symmetric). (2) They want to acquire chips that are relatively more valuable to them, ideally from players who appear to value them less. (3) Because multiple acceptances lead to a random partner, senders cannot precisely target one counterpart, which encourages offers that could plausibly be attractive to more than one player while still being profitable. Over three rounds (nine total turns), we expect some early “probing” offers that reveal information about valuations, followed by more targeted trades if patterns emerge. Given the short horizon, players may be moderately aggressive in demanding surplus, but still need to avoid obviously one-sided offers that no rational agent with similar value ranges would accept. The likely emergent pattern: Whale and Duck, both valuing red at $1, will be relatively unwilling to give up red cheaply; Dolphin will test swapping red for blue or green. Whale may try to offload blue to either partner in exchange for red or green, while Duck is in between—happy to acquire red, somewhat favorable toward blue, and neutral on green. We forecast a game in which only trades that clearly improve expected private value for both sides are accepted; some attempts will be declined as players learn what others are likely to accept.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players start symmetric in chip counts but asymmetric in valuations. No trade history exists yet, so proposals are informative tests of others’ preferences. As first mover, Whale is likely to start with a conservative and slightly favorable offer, probing whether anyone is willing to give up a chip that Whale suspects might be less valuable to them. Dolphin and Duck, moving later in the round, will update based on Whale’s initial offer and any acceptances or rejections. Since there are still two full rounds ahead, players may be willing to reject mildly unfavorable offers and wait for better opportunities, but they will also want to start learning how much others seem to value each color.

By round 1, a single accepted trade has already shifted holdings and revealed information. Dolphin accepted Whale’s initial offer of 2 blue for 2 green, signaling that Dolphin is willing to trade away blue at a rate that is only marginally worse, given her high blue value, to acquire green with known value. This suggests to others that Dolphin values blue relatively highly but may still accept trades when the downside is small. Whale now holds more blue and less green, consistent with Whale’s low private value for blue and neutral stance on green. Duck declined both early offers, indicating more selectivity or uncertainty. In this round, Whale is likely to leverage the extra blue to try to obtain red—especially from Dolphin, whose earlier attempt to trade red away suggests a lower private value for red. Dolphin will likely refine her strategy, offering trades that better exploit her high blue value, while Duck may start probing with offers involving colors that seem in higher demand.

Entering the final round, the trade history reveals strong patterns: Whale has been systematically converting less-valued blue into higher-valued red and some green, Dolphin has been willing to give up red when compensated with blue or green, and Duck has remained conservative, declining all proposals so far. Holdings and revealed preferences imply that Whale and Dolphin have already captured substantial gains from trade, while Duck risks ending with the initial bundle and no surplus from bargaining. With only three turns left, there is pressure to make deals that are clearly beneficial, but also to avoid a final mistake that destroys value. Whale, now rich in high-value red, will be reluctant to give it up cheaply; Dolphin may still seek marginal improvements using blue, and Duck might finally accept a modestly favorable offer if it meaningfully increases red or preserves blue. The short horizon encourages concrete, straightforward trades with obvious mutual benefit rather than complex or highly speculative offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features asymmetric private values over red and blue chips, with green chips having a known common value of $0.50 for everyone. Each player begins with 10 chips of each color, but values them differently:
- Turtle (turtle-yellow-8674): strongly prefers BLUE ($1.00) slightly over RED ($0.90), both over GREEN ($0.50).
- Gorilla (gorilla-purple-9767): RED ($1.00) is highest, then BLUE ($0.70), then GREEN ($0.50).
- Zebra (zebra-blue-1097): values are relatively low and flat: RED ($0.60), BLUE ($0.50), GREEN ($0.50).

From an analyst’s perspective, efficient surplus-creating trades would move RED chips toward Gorilla and Turtle (who value them more than Zebra), and BLUE chips toward Turtle (who values them most). GREEN chips are neutral—no private informational edge—but may be used as a relatively safe medium of exchange when players are unsure about others’ red/blue valuations.

However, each player knows only their own private red/blue valuations. Early in the game, proposals will therefore serve two roles: (1) try to improve the proposer’s bundle, and (2) implicitly test what others might value by observing who accepts or declines. Because only one round of proposals per player happens per round, and there are only three rounds total, players have limited time to experiment and learn. They must balance information gathering against the risk of making offers that are too generous or too stingy.

In equilibrium-like reasoning, each proposer will offer trades where, given their own valuations, they gain clear value if accepted and suspect they are not giving away something that others might value dramatically more. Thus, players will often offer slightly unfavorable exchanges from the responder’s perspective, hoping that at least one counterpart either has lower valuations for the chip they are giving up or misestimates the trade’s attractiveness. When multiple players could benefit from a proposal, uncertainty arises because responders know that accepting competes with the other responder; with random recipient selection among accepters, each responder’s expected gain from accepting is multiplied by a probability of actually being chosen.

Because Gorilla values RED the highest and Turtle values BLUE the highest, the natural surplus-creating pattern is RED flowing to Gorilla and BLUE flowing to Turtle, often at the expense of Zebra, who starts with the same quantities but lower valuations. Nonetheless, Zebra may occasionally accept slightly bad trades if they mistakenly infer others’ values or overestimate the chance of a more favorable future offer. Over the three rounds, we should see proposals that become somewhat more targeted—especially after early accepted trades reveal partial hints about who values which colors more—though with so few turns and private values, substantial uncertainty and cautious bargaining are likely to persist until the end of the game.

rounds.round_explanation:
In the first round, players know nothing about others’ private red/blue values, only that values lie in a relatively wide $0.10–$1.00 range for red and blue. Proposers are likely to make conservative, surplus-seeking offers that test others’ willingness to trade without giving away too much. Because everyone starts with symmetric holdings (10 of each color), no one is constrained by inventory yet, and initial offers will often use green as the ‘safe’ payment medium or small offers of red/blue to probe where the others’ interests lie. Rejections will be common as everyone feels out the environment.

Turtle moves first and may try to cheaply acquire blue (which they value highly) or red, paying with green. Gorilla, as a high-red player, will likely seek red in exchange for green or blue. Zebra, with flatter values, might either experiment or stay quite conservative, accepting only clearly value-improving trades. This round mainly sets informational baselines for later turns.

By the start of round 1, the only executed trade is Gorilla’s purchase of 2 RED from Zebra for 2 GREEN. Publicly, everyone sees Gorilla’s strong revealed demand for RED and Zebra’s willingness to give up RED for GREEN at a 1:1 rate. This suggests to the others that Gorilla likely values RED more than GREEN, and that Zebra either values RED similarly to GREEN or was at least indifferent at that rate.

Turtle now has a hint that RED may be valuable to Gorilla and that Zebra may be a softer source of RED. Gorilla, having succeeded once with a green-for-red offer, may either push a similar structure again or adjust terms knowing future offers might need to be slightly more generous. Zebra has just sacrificed some RED, slightly reducing their RED inventory; Zebra might become more cautious, realizing that they may have underpriced RED if others are demanding it. Proposals in this round are likely to be more strategically targeted, especially toward RED for Gorilla or BLUE for Turtle, but still somewhat conservative given ongoing uncertainty.

Going into the final round, the accepted trades so far have shifted value toward the higher-valuing players but only modestly. Gorilla has already acquired additional RED from Zebra, and Turtle has obtained some RED from Zebra as well. Publicly, Zebra looks like a consistent net seller of RED in exchange for GREEN, potentially signaling weaker preference for RED. Gorilla’s unsuccessful blue-for-red attempt in round 1 also suggests that, at least at a 1:1 rate, neither Turtle nor Zebra wants to part with more RED for BLUE.

With only three turns left total, each proposer must decide whether to push for a last value-improving trade or avoid risky offers that could be either exploited or simply ignored. Turtle may try to pick up extra BLUE or RED if they can do so by paying with GREEN, but counterparts will be wary of symmetrical 1:1 trades that seem too obviously favorable for the proposer. Gorilla, whose priorities are clearly oriented toward RED, might attempt one more strong bid for RED, perhaps swapping away BLUE or GREEN. Zebra, with somewhat lower and flatter valuations, may either seek to correct earlier trades (e.g., rebalancing chip types) or simply refrain from accepting offers that do not clearly increase their own payoff. Overall, the final round is likely to feature at most one more accepted trade, with others declining as players avoid late-stage exploitation.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip endowments but asymmetric private values on red and blue chips. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 to everyone, so they serve as a natural benchmark and ‘safe’ currency. From an analyst’s perspective, blue is highly valuable for zebra ($1.00) and moderately valuable for cat ($0.60), but relatively weak for iguana ($0.30). Red is most valuable for cat ($0.90), slightly less for iguana ($0.80), and relatively weak for zebra ($0.40). Efficient surplus comes from moving red chips toward cat and iguana and moving blue chips toward zebra (and somewhat toward cat) away from iguana. However, players only know their own values. Early in the game, each will experiment with simple one-color-for-one-color trades or small ratios that ‘feel fair’ given their own values, without fully understanding the others’ valuations. The simultaneous, private accept/decline plus random tie-breaking over multiple acceptors makes partnership choice noisy: proposers can’t target a specific partner with certainty and must consider that any attractive offer could be captured by an unintended recipient. As rounds progress, the public trade history can partially reveal who seems eager to give up or acquire specific colors, but with only three rounds, learning is limited. Players will tend to demand modest expected gains per trade (based on their private values), avoid giving up what they privately perceive as their most valuable chips unless compensated in value, and may occasionally miss high-surplus trades due to misinference or strategic caution. Cat, holding the highest red value, will be reluctant to part with red cheaply and especially interested in trading away blue for red or green. Iguana, who values both red and blue less than cat but values red more than zebra does, has incentives to sell blue to zebra or cat while acquiring red, possibly using green as a balancing medium. Zebra, with the highest blue value and lowest red value, is naturally inclined to trade away red and green to accumulate blue. Given the short game, we expect a few moderate, ‘safe’ trades that roughly respect each player’s local value ratios, leaving significant unrealized surplus on the table.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, everyone has 10 chips of each color and no information about others’ private values. Players are likely to make conservative, symmetric-seeming offers that feel slightly advantageous given their own valuations, using green as a neutral medium. Because no one yet has a clear reputation or observed pattern, acceptance decisions are driven mostly by immediate private-value comparisons. Proposers also face uncertainty about which opponent—if any—will accept, so they may frame offers that look modestly appealing to either potential partner, without finely tailoring to a specific counterpart.

After round 0, some information has leaked: iguana was willing to trade away green for blue, then blue for red, and zebra was willing to accept blue for red. This suggests to observers that zebra likes blue more than red, and iguana doesn’t mind shedding blue when red is on offer. Cat has revealed a willingness to give up blue for green, which might signal that blue is relatively less important than either green or red. Players now tentatively update: zebra likely has strong demand for blue, iguana values red somewhat, and cat is at least not desperate for blue. Proposals may become slightly more targeted, particularly with blue-for-red or blue-for-green structures, yet uncertainty remains high and risk of random partner selection still shapes how broad or narrow offers are framed.

By the final round, players have seen repeated patterns: zebra repeatedly accepts opportunities to gain blue and shed red or green; iguana has a consistent willingness to part with blue and sometimes green to gain red or blue; cat has traded away several blue chips in exchange for red or green, hinting that red is highly prized and blue is comparatively dispensable. Still, no one knows exact marginal values, and time is short, so players are inclined to propose trades that create clear private gains with minimal downside risk. Because this is the last round, there is no value in signaling or experimentation beyond immediate payoff, so offers and responses are driven almost purely by current marginal valuations and simple heuristics about which colors each counterpart tends to like or dislike.
