parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a symmetric-information-looking but actually private-value 3-player bargaining game with simple linear values. All players value green chips the same ($0.50), but differ slightly on red and blue. Bear values red and blue at $0.80, Peacock values red at $0.80 and blue at $0.70, and Swan values blue at $0.80 but red only at $0.50. All start with 10 of each color. Because green is common value and only $0.50, it is strictly dominated in value relative to each player’s favorite color (red for Bear and Peacock, blue for Bear and Swan). That makes green a natural “currency” to pay with when buying more valuable colors. Bear privately wants to accumulate both red and blue; Peacock wants to accumulate red and is relatively okay parting with blue or green; Swan wants to accumulate blue and is relatively happy to trade away red and green. Players only know their own chip values and must infer others’ preferences from proposals and acceptances. Early in the game players experiment with simple, mildly generous trades using green as payment to discover who likes which colors. Once a pattern of acceptances emerges (Swan showing willingness to sell red and green for blue, Peacock showing willingness to sell blue/green for red, Bear revealing strong interest in both red/blue), the bargaining settles into relatively efficient two-way and sometimes competitive three-way trades. Uncertainty appears in whether simultaneous acceptances occur, in how generous one must be to induce acceptance from either of two potential partners, and in learning others’ private values from very limited interaction over just three rounds. Given the short horizon (three rounds × three turns), players are likely to offer reasonably fair, slightly favorable-to-self exchanges that clearly improve the counterpart’s payoff based on plausible value ranges, rather than trying to squeeze out every cent of surplus. Over time, Bear tends to emerge with more red and blue, Peacock with more red and fewer blue, and Swan with more blue and fewer red/green, while green circulates as the main payment chip.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has 10 of each color and no information about others’ private values beyond the public ranges. Green’s known low value makes it a natural currency: offering green to buy red or blue is likely to be attractive. The first round is mainly exploratory. Each proposer wants to test whether at least one of the two others will take a simple, obviously beneficial trade, while keeping terms slightly in their own favor. They also want to avoid trades that could reveal too much about just how much they value a color, so they avoid extreme offers. We should expect small quantity trades that move green toward players who are more willing to hold it temporarily, and move red/blue toward players who are more likely to value them more highly.

By the start of round 1, some patterns are revealed: Bear has willingly paid green for red and blue and also accepted red-for-blue trades, suggesting Bear values both red and blue relatively highly and is okay giving away green. Peacock has sold red for green and bought blue for green, hinting at a strong preference for red but some interest in blue. Swan has sold blue for green and red for blue, implying blue is relatively valuable to Swan, while green and red are more expendable. Everyone now has slightly shifted holdings and some tentative beliefs about who likes what. In this middle round, players fine-tune: Bear tries to buy more of high-valued chips from whoever seems most willing to sell, Swan continues to upgrade red/green into blue when possible, and Peacock tries to rebuild or maintain a strong red position while unloading less-valued chips. Offers become a bit more targeted, and each proposer aims for trades that are clearly beneficial for at least one other player, to maximize acceptance probabilities.

The final round is the last chance to adjust portfolios. Everyone now has clearer signals: Bear consistently buys red and blue with green, Peacock tends to accumulate red and is open to giving up blue and green, and Swan tries to finish with a large blue pile while offloading red and green. Because there is no future beyond this game, concerns about signaling disappear; proposers purely maximize immediate expected value. They target obviously welfare-improving trades based on the emerging pattern: Peacock is a good counterpart when trading red against blue/green, and Swan is a good counterpart when trading blue against red/green. Bear can often profit by triangulating between them, buying undervalued colors from one and selling to the other. In practice, last-round offers focus on converting less-valued holdings (especially green, and for Swan also red) into each player’s preferred color, producing relatively efficient specialization: Bear with strong red/blue, Peacock with red-heavy holdings, and Swan with blue-heavy holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with identical chip endowments (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but have different private values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. From an analyst’s perspective, BLUE is globally high-value, especially for camel-red-6481 ($0.80) and monkey-yellow-524 ($0.90), and still decent for gorilla-yellow-5964 ($0.30). RED is moderately valuable for camel ($0.50), modest for gorilla ($0.30), and low for monkey ($0.20). Since players only know their own valuations, each must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and accept/reject behavior. Likely bargaining dynamics: (1) gorilla, with relatively symmetric values on RED and BLUE, can profit by arbitraging between camel’s and monkey’s stronger preferences for BLUE and camel’s stronger preference for RED, but gorilla doesn’t know exactly who values what. (2) camel will tend to seek BLUE (high private value) and be more willing to spend RED or possibly some GREEN to get it, but will be cautious about overpaying in terms of GREEN (known to be worth $0.50 to all). (3) monkey strongly favors BLUE and is least attached to RED; this makes monkey a natural seller of RED and buyer of BLUE, but monkey must ensure not to give away too many chips for insufficient expected value. Strategic uncertainty centers on how aggressively each player tries to signal or conceal their values via early proposals, how they interpret others’ acceptances/rejections, and how they balance extracting favorable trades now against keeping options open for later rounds. With only three rounds and three turns per round, players must act under time pressure, often settling for moderately favorable trades rather than waiting for perfect ones. The random selection among multiple accepters also creates risk for proposers: they cannot perfectly target the counterpart who would yield them the best informational or strategic advantage, which makes simple, obviously-profitable-to-the-recipient trades more attractive than finely tuned, partner-specific offers.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has yet observed any behavior, so each player’s beliefs about others’ valuations are largely based on the common prior that RED and BLUE are in the $0.10–$1.00 range and GREEN is fixed at $0.50. Proposals are likely to be exploratory, aiming to test whether others are eager to buy or sell particular colors. Gorilla moves first and may try a small-value but information-rich trade using GREEN (whose value is known) to see how others react. Camel, moving second, will likely experiment with trades that acquire BLUE or shed RED, probing how costly it is to buy BLUE. Monkey, moving third, may respond to what has been learned so far, possibly positioning as a willing seller of RED or reluctant seller of BLUE, depending on the offers seen.

By the start of round 1, players have seen some evidence: camel accepted trading GREEN for BLUE, revealing strong BLUE demand; camel’s own BLUE-for-RED attempt was rejected, suggesting others don’t see that as attractive at 1:1; and monkey successfully traded RED for GREEN with gorilla, indicating gorilla is relatively willing to part with RED to acquire GREEN. Players update: gorilla now suspects camel and monkey both place high value on BLUE; camel infers that neither gorilla nor monkey find RED-for-BLUE at even terms attractive; monkey infers that gorilla values GREEN at least as much as RED. In this round, gorilla can try to sell BLUE dear or buy GREEN cheaply; camel will keep seeking BLUE but may adjust the price; monkey may sell more RED or cautiously try small BLUE trades, but avoid offering too many BLUE chips at low terms.

Heading into the final round, behavioral patterns are clearer. Camel has twice paid GREEN or accepted trades that reduce GREEN for BLUE, strongly signaling a high value for BLUE despite GREEN’s known $0.50 value. Monkey has both sold RED for GREEN and bought BLUE for GREEN, signaling RED is relatively cheap and BLUE relatively dear. Gorilla’s behavior shows a willingness to exchange RED and GREEN but reluctance to sell BLUE cheaply. With only three proposals left, each player faces a last chance to slightly improve their bundle. Camel, now first in this round, is motivated to secure one last BLUE chip if possible. Gorilla, moving second, may try to exploit camel’s or monkey’s revealed BLUE hunger by offering a modest but positive surplus trade. Monkey, last, chooses among residual small improvements, but faces the risk that others’ chip holdings and remaining willingness to trade are limited, so may either passively propose a conservative trade or aim for one more marginally beneficial GREEN-for-RED or BLUE-for-RED swap.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued red/blue chips. All players know greens are safe and symmetric, while red/blue values are uncertain: each player knows only their own valuation and the public range ($0.10–$1.00).

Given their own valuations:
- Alligator (A): RED $0.70 (high), BLUE $0.30 (low), GREEN $0.50.
- Otter (O): RED $0.30 (low), BLUE $0.50 (high), GREEN $0.50.
- Tiger (T): RED $0.60 (high), BLUE $0.20 (low), GREEN $0.50.

Privately, each will want to accumulate more of their high-value color and dump their low-value color while not giving up too much value. But they must design trades that look plausibly beneficial to at least one unknown valuation type among their two potential partners. The public range and the symmetry of the game create uncertainty: for example, when A offers blue for red, A knows blue is low for them, but O or T might see blue as high; likewise, A infers that someone may value red or blue more or less but cannot pinpoint who or by how much.

Strategically, all three rounds will likely feature:
1) Early, relatively conservative trades using green as a safe bridge, because everyone knows green’s value and risk is minimal (a player will often be willing to give up slightly more green chips to attract a trade that brings in a chip color they suspect might be higher value).
2) Gradual revelation via action: who is eager to acquire red vs. blue, at what implied exchange rates, and with greens used as a benchmark for fair value. If one player consistently buys red, others infer that red might be high for them; similarly for blue.
3) Competition risk: any proposal is broadcast to both potential partners, and if both accept, the partner is chosen at random. So a sender cannot perfectly target the preferred counterparty, and both responders must decide whether to compete for a possibly favorable trade while knowing they might not be selected.
4) Short horizon: with only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, the game is short. Players are unlikely to invest heavily in signaling and instead try to secure positive-value trades whenever they see a sufficiently good deal, especially in later rounds when there are fewer remaining opportunities.

Given the private values, efficient patterns (from the analyst’s perspective) would involve moving red chips toward Alligator and Tiger (who value red highly) and blue chips toward Otter (who values blue highly), often financed with green chips. However, because the players only see their own payoffs, we should expect imperfectly efficient trades: players will sometimes trade at rates that look suboptimal from the outside but are still privately beneficial under their beliefs. Over the three rounds, the likely trajectory is: cautious, exploratory trades in round 0; somewhat more aggressive, value-seeking trades once some preferences are inferred in round 1; and more opportunistic, endgame trades in round 2, with players using their remaining low-value chips and some greens to grab whatever high-value chips they can still get.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has 10 of each chip. No trades have occurred, so beliefs about others’ red/blue values are very diffuse within the public range. Proposers will likely use green (known $0.50) as a safe anchor and construct offers that are clearly positive for themselves under any plausible belief, yet not so extreme that responders always reject them. Each player tests the water: Alligator probes demand for red versus green, Otter explores whether someone is willing to pay for blue with green, and Tiger tries to turn low-value blue into higher-value red using green as a sweetener. With no prior history, responders are cautious but willing to accept clearly favorable or nearly fair trades in order to start moving their portfolios toward higher-value colors.

By round 1, the first three trades have already shifted holdings and conveyed information. From the outside, we see: (1) Otter accepted Alligator’s offer of 1 RED for 2 GREEN, suggesting Otter is not extremely averse to red or may value extra chips over green; (2) Tiger accepted Otter’s trade of 3 GREEN for 2 BLUE, implying Tiger may not like blue much but is willing to take it if the green payment is generous; (3) Alligator accepted Tiger’s trade of 4 BLUE for 2 RED, signaling Alligator clearly values red over blue. In-game, the players only directly see who traded what, but each infers tendencies: Alligator seems red-loving, Otter appears open to red, and Tiger is willing to part with blue at the right price. Beliefs sharpen: offering red to Alligator is probably costly for the proposer, and giving blue to Otter might be expensive if Otter likes blue. In this round, proposers adjust: Alligator now tries to buy more red by offloading blue; Otter focuses more on acquiring blue while paying with red; Tiger uses green and blue to pull in extra red. Responders weigh these offers against updated expectations about their own and others’ valuations and the remaining two rounds.

Round 2 is the final opportunity for trades. By now, players have clearer behavioral evidence: Alligator repeatedly trades away blue to gain red and green; Otter increasingly trades away red to gain blue; Tiger is willing to part with blue and sometimes green but also acquires red. Each infers that red is probably valuable to Alligator and Tiger, blue is likely valuable to Otter, and green is everyone’s safe fallback. With only three proposal turns left in the entire game, players become more opportunistic and less concerned about future signaling. They try to convert their lowest-value colors into their highest-value colors, even at somewhat unfavorable apparent exchange rates, as long as the trade still increases their total expected value. Game dynamics now emphasize endgame rebalancing: Alligator uses remaining blue and perhaps green to chase red; Otter uses remaining red and some green to secure blue; Tiger uses blue (and some green) to top up red if possible. Responders accept proposals that give them clear value gains and decline those that would leave them with too many low-value chips in the final portfolio.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips, with green chips common-valued at $0.50 to everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Because green is transparently valuable to all, it serves as a natural medium of exchange and a benchmark for what is a “good” or “bad” deal. The asymmetries are in red and blue: Dolphin values blue highly and red weakly (BLUE=1.00, RED=0.20), Frog values red highly and blue weakly (RED=1.00, BLUE=0.30), and Pig has moderately valued red and blue (both 0.40). This creates strong gains-from-trade opportunities: Dolphin wants to acquire blue and dump red; Frog wants to acquire red and dump blue; Pig is intermediate and can either act as a middleman or opportunistically accept favorable trades with either side. However, the players only know their own valuations, so they infer others’ values from the structure of offers and acceptances. Strategic uncertainty comes from (1) not knowing rivals’ private values, (2) the random resolution when multiple players accept an offer, and (3) the finite three-round horizon that limits how long they can wait for better terms. Early in the game, players are likely to make cautious, relatively small trades that test counterparties’ willingness to exchange particular colors, using green as a safe numeraire. As they see who accepts or rejects which packages, they update their beliefs about who values red vs blue. Because a proposer cannot choose which accepting player they trade with, they will often design offers that are clearly unattractive to at least one rival in order to target a specific counterparty. By the final round, the pressure to capture any remaining surplus encourages more generous, immediately acceptable offers that lock in sure gains rather than speculative signaling. Overall, we expect (i) Dolphin to propose trades that exchange red for blue or green, (ii) Frog to propose trades that exchange blue or green for red, and (iii) Pig to arbitrage between them when it can profitably realign its portfolio, especially by picking up red from Dolphin and passing red to Frog, while unloading blue to Dolphin or Frog depending on observables price-like terms.

rounds.round_explanation: The first round occurs with symmetric endowments and no prior history, so players mainly probe each other’s preferences. Proposers are cautious: they want positive expected value but must consider that an overly generous deal may reveal exploitable information, while an overly exploitative deal will be declined and waste their turn. Because everyone knows green is worth $0.50 to all, early offers commonly use green as the payment chip, priced roughly against how much the proposer personally values red or blue. Dolphin will likely test whether anyone is willing to part with blue or green in exchange for its low-valued red. Frog will likely test demand for its low-valued blue in exchange for its high-valued red. Pig, with intermediate preferences, will watch carefully, possibly making a small arbitrage-motivated move if it sees a clear mispricing relative to its own valuations.

After round 0, a few inferences are possible. Dolphin successfully traded red for blue with Pig, revealing Dolphin’s willingness to dump red and Pig’s willingness to hold more red and less blue at those terms. Frog’s attempt to give up blue for red was rejected by both, suggesting its relative price for red was too aggressive or that others do not value red as highly as Frog does. Pig then swapped blue for green with Dolphin, which both sides found profitable given their valuations; to observers, this hints that Dolphin values blue above green and that Pig is only modestly attached to blue. Going into round 1, players update: Dolphin now has relatively more blue and less red and green; Pig has more red and green and less blue; Frog still has its original bundle. Proposers now aim at more targeted trades, using the new holdings and prior accept/reject patterns to better tailor offers: Dolphin may try modest red-for-green or blue-for-green probes, Frog may soften its earlier price for red, and Pig will consider whether it can exploit newly created imbalances in holdings.

By round 2, the final round, players face a closing window to realize gains from trade. Several patterns have emerged from previous acceptances: Dolphin reliably takes blue and green in exchange for red at terms that reflect its low value for red, Pig has shown a willingness to absorb red and give up blue and sometimes green at moderate rates, and Frog’s strong desire for red is clearer after it accepted a sizable red-for-green trade. The histories also let each player guess approximate relative prices others are using internally. With only one proposal left per player, they are more likely to make clearly profitable, nearly “fair” offers that have a high probability of immediate acceptance. Risky signaling or waiting for a slightly better bargain is dominated by locking in sure improvements. Thus, we expect small- to medium-sized exchanges that move each player closer to their privately optimal mix—Dolphin tilting further toward blue/green away from red, Frog capturing extra red if possible while not overpaying, and Pig fine-tuning its middleman position by shedding low-valued chips relative to what it can still obtain from others this late in the game.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
Each player begins with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. RED and BLUE are privately valued: iguana-blue-1059 (iguana) values BLUE highly ($0.80) and RED weakly ($0.20); owl-green-168 (owl) values RED highly ($0.70) and BLUE weakly ($0.30); squid-purple-9957 (squid) is more moderate (BLUE $0.50, RED $0.10). All know GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone and that RED/BLUE are somewhere in [0.10, 1.00], but each only knows their own exact numbers.

Incentives: iguana wants to convert RED into BLUE or GREEN; owl wants to convert BLUE into RED or GREEN; squid wants to shed RED first and convert into BLUE or GREEN, but at milder rates. Because GREEN is symmetric and reasonably valuable, it will often appear as the medium of trade, especially early on when players do not yet know each other's valuations. RED and BLUE will be revealed gradually via proposals and acceptances: aggressive attempts to buy a color or willingness to sell it cheaply signal high or low private value.

Strategically, each proposer wants to construct trades that are (i) strictly profitable for themselves given their own valuations and (ii) plausibly profitable for at least one other player, given only coarse beliefs about others' valuations. Since multiple acceptances trigger random partner selection, proposals that are attractive to more than one counterpart are risky for the proposer if counterpart payoffs differ; however, with symmetric starting endowments and only 3 rounds, players are likely to focus on finding any beneficial trade rather than finely targeting a single partner.

Uncertainty limits how extreme the trades will be. Players typically avoid very lopsided deals unless they are nearly sure about others' values; instead, they propose deals where their own expected gain is clear while leaving enough value on the table that someone else is also likely to gain. Over the three rounds, we should see early exploratory offers, some mild updating about who values what, and then slightly sharper, value-targeting trades in the final round, particularly around iguana trying to acquire BLUE, owl trying to acquire RED, and squid getting rid of cheap RED while picking up BLUE or GREEN.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each chip and very limited information about others' RED/BLUE valuations. Proposers are likely to make conservative, symmetric-looking trades that exploit their own clear gains while still plausibly benefiting someone else. GREEN, with its common value, is a natural benchmark for what a 'fair' trade looks like. This round should feature exploratory offers: each player tests whether others are willing to trade away colors that they themselves value relatively less.

After round 0, iguana and owl have completed a trade: iguana gained some BLUE and gave up some RED; owl gained valuable RED and gave up weak BLUE. Both should be more confident that BLUE is relatively more valuable to iguana and RED is relatively more valuable to owl. Squid observed this pattern and infers that RED is attractive to owl and BLUE to iguana. Proposals in this round are more targeted: iguana tries further to accumulate BLUE or at least shed low-value RED; owl doubles down on gathering RED or GREEN; squid seeks to unload low-value RED while avoiding very unfavorable BLUE-for-RED terms that seem to favor owl or iguana too much.

Going into the final round, each player has observed consistent patterns: iguana repeatedly accepts trades that yield BLUE or move out of RED; owl repeatedly seeks RED and is willing to give up BLUE and some GREEN; squid has shown willingness to trade away RED for BLUE or GREEN but remains cautious about making trades that seem too favorable to owl’s known RED preference. With only one proposal remaining for each player, they attempt last, somewhat more aggressive rebalancing moves, still constrained by uncertainty about the exact valuations of others. Each trade now weighs the marginal value of one more chip of a color against the chance that no one accepts if the terms are too one-sided.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips, plus a common-value green chip. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so any voluntary trade involving green must at least weakly improve both sides’ private valuations of red/blue compared to $0.50. Gorilla-purple-8501 has relatively low blue value ($0.10) and moderate red ($0.40). Llama-pink-7039 and whale-orange-533 share identical, higher values: blue $0.90, red $0.60. This creates a natural pattern: gorilla is a low-value holder of blue, so they are likely to sell blue to llama/whale when possible; llama and whale strongly value blue and somewhat value red, and might trade with gorilla when the rate is favorable. However, none of them knows the others’ valuations, so early rounds are partially exploratory: players test what terms get accepted and infer relative valuations. Because multiple acceptances lead to random partner selection, proposals that are attractive to both llama and whale should be phrased so that either partner delivering the same chip mix is acceptable to the proposer. As the game is only three rounds with one proposal per player per round, incentives are to secure clearly profitable trades early rather than hold out for perfect information. Yet each player is cautious not to overpay in their own units: they compare the marginal value they give up with what they gain, knowing only their own values. Llama and whale might compete implicitly for cheap blue from gorilla but cannot undercut each other directly; instead, they decide whether to accept gorilla’s offers. By the final round, players have limited time, so they may accept modestly profitable trades they would have rejected earlier, especially if no prior deals occurred.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, no one has information about others’ valuations. Gorilla knows their own low blue valuation and may suspect that others might value blue differently, making blue a natural thing to sell. Llama and whale, having symmetric high blue valuations, see both red and blue as profitable compared to green, but don’t know gorilla’s valuations. In this first round, proposals are likely to be conservative and exploratory: small quantities and near-value-neutral exchanges from each proposer’s perspective, with at most one clear side gaining surplus once private values are considered. Because all start with symmetric chip counts, no one is liquidity-constrained yet, so choices reflect pure value considerations and learning motives.

By round 1, one trade has occurred: gorilla traded away 2 blue for 1 red to llama. Gorilla’s acceptance reveals that blue is relatively cheap for them compared to red; llama’s acceptance suggests blue is fairly valuable to them. Whale observed this, inferring that at least one other player values blue more than red and that gorilla may be a willing blue seller. Llama now holds more blue and fewer red, making them slightly more willing to sell red if they can restock blue or green at a good rate. Players are still cautious but have some evidence of relative valuations. Proposals this round may be slightly more aggressive in extracting surplus, especially from gorilla as the apparent low-blue-value player, but everyone still guards against overpaying given the short horizon.

By the final round, gorilla has repeatedly sold blue for red, signaling very low blue value, while llama and whale have not executed further trades, indicating they are not desperate for marginal improvements at unfavorable prices. Everyone now knows the game ends after these last three proposals, so they may accept slightly weaker but still positive deals, but they will not accept trades that clearly lower their private value. Llama and whale understand that gorilla will part with blue cheaply but only for red at roughly a 2:1 blue-to-red ratio, and gorilla sees that at least one other player values blue enough to accept those terms. Proposers now either try one last time to exploit these revealed preferences with modestly profitable offers or refrain from overly unbalanced proposals they expect to be declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player bargaining game with privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green. All start with 10 of each color. Tiger values both red and blue much more than the others (especially blue), while duck and lion have more modest and closer valuations. Because players only know their own values, they must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances. Efficient trades move red/blue from low-value holders (duck, lion) to the high-value holder (tiger), possibly in exchange for green (which is equally valued by all) or for the other color chip that the recipient values less. Duck and lion face strategic uncertainty: tiger’s strong interest in blue/red will emerge only through behavior, but neither knows exact numbers. The simultaneous accept rule plus random tie-breaking creates competition to accept good offers quickly but also risk that a favorable counterparty is not chosen if multiple players accept. Over three rounds, each player has three proposal turns, but the window is short: they must quickly test the market, update beliefs about others’ values, and then refine proposals. Early offers are more exploratory (probing who likes which chips) and later offers are more targeted, reflecting inferred high-value directions (especially toward tiger’s strong blue/red preferences). Players aim to extract surplus in their trade direction: sellers of valuable chips want more units in return; buyers want favorable exchange rates, while managing the risk that an offer is either too generous (both others accept) or too stingy (no one accepts).

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, everyone still has symmetric endowments (10 of each color) and only their own valuations. The main strategic objective is information gathering: use small, safe trades to see who is willing to trade which color. Duck and lion do not yet know that tiger values blue and red very highly; tiger suspects others value these less but not by how much. Proposals are likely to involve green, because it has a known common value and is a natural medium of exchange. Each proposer has an incentive to make slightly favorable offers that are still plausible enough to be accepted, while watching who bites to infer relative valuations.

By round 1, some information has been revealed. Duck has shown a willingness to trade away green for red and to sell blue to tiger for green, indicating moderate values on red and relatively low value on blue. Tiger’s eagerness to exchange multiple green for blue signals that tiger values blue highly. Lion, whose initial probing offer for blue against green was rejected, learns that simply swapping green for blue at equal ratios is not attractive to others at that stage. Now, players refine their strategies: tiger tries to accumulate more blue and red from low-value holders, duck may continue to arbitrage between colors using green, and lion must decide whether to engage with tiger at slightly better terms or stay out if trades look unfavorable.

Going into the final round, patterns are clear: tiger is aggressively accumulating blue and red, paying with green; duck and lion are both willing to give up blue for green, and duck has also sold red when the terms are good enough. All players now have much sharper beliefs about relative valuations. With only three proposal turns left, there is urgency to lock in remaining surplus. Duck tries to trade away remaining low-value blue (or surplus green) for red or green at favorable ratios; lion, holding relatively low-value blue, looks for one last beneficial exchange; tiger continues to seek red and blue, but must balance the marginal value of additional chips against the cost in green, which still has positive value. Proposals tend to be more one-sided in favor of the proposer, but still within a range that at least one counterpart might accept, given their private values and current holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game centers on trading privately valued red and blue chips against a common-value green chip, with uncertainty arising because each player only knows their own private values. All begin symmetric in quantity (10 of each color) but asymmetric in valuations: Bear and Goose both value red highly ($1.00) and blue relatively low ($0.30 and $0.20), while Ram values blue highly ($1.00) and red somewhat less ($0.80). Green is worth $0.50 to everyone. From an analyst’s perspective, efficient surplus-creating trades move red chips toward Bear and Goose and blue chips toward Ram, potentially using green as a medium of exchange or as a safe-value item. However, players do not know one another’s exact values, only that each color falls in a public range, so early moves will often be exploratory and somewhat conservative, trying to feel out who values what by seeing which offers get accepted. Bear, with the strongest preference for red and moderate disdain for blue, has a clear incentive to shed blue in exchange for red or green, especially if counterparties appear to like blue. Goose similarly prefers red over blue but values blue even less, so Goose is also inclined to dump blue for red or green. Ram is the natural counterparty for blue, strongly preferring to acquire blue while parting with some red or possibly green if the terms are favorable. Across the three rounds (nine total proposal turns), players will likely start with modest offers involving single-chip trades or balanced swaps that guarantee a gain given their own values, even if the counterparty has symmetric valuations, because they cannot rely on favorable private information. Strategic uncertainty shows up in (1) setting exchange rates (e.g., how many low-value blue to offer for one high-value red without being rejected), (2) the risk that multiple respondents accept a good deal, leading to a random partner rather than a chosen one, and (3) the fact that each accepted trade changes holdings, which then changes future bargaining leverage and opportunities. Given the short horizon (only three rounds), players have limited scope to signal and then exploit information; instead, they are likely to propose locally advantageous, low-risk trades that they expect at least one other to accept on plausible valuations. As the game proceeds, observed acceptances and rejections update players’ beliefs about others’ values, so later rounds may see more targeted trades (e.g., Bear and Goose pushing blue to Ram, Ram offering red to Bear or Goose) if early responses reveal who is relatively eager to move particular colors. Still, the finite horizon and random recipient selection when multiple acceptances occur will keep proposals somewhat coarse and self-protective, rather than fully efficient or finely tuned.

From a surplus standpoint, the analyst can identify clear mutually beneficial opportunities: Bear and Goose are willing to give up blue chips they value at $0.30 or $0.20 for additional red chips they value at $1.00 or for green worth $0.50, while Ram can happily trade away red (worth $0.80) or even some green ($0.50) in order to gain blue ($1.00). Realized play here is forecasted to partially exploit these complementarities without fully saturating them, because of incomplete information, limited time, and noisy acceptance incentives (players might accept a slightly unfavorable trade if they misestimate others’ values or if they hope to use new holdings to improve future trades). The transcript below reconstructs one plausible full game path consistent with rational but boundedly cautious trading under these incentives and uncertainties.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, everyone still holds 10 of each chip color, and nobody has concrete evidence about the others’ specific values. Proposals are likely to be small and information-seeking, designed to be modestly profitable given the sender’s own payoffs while still plausibly acceptable if others share similar or slightly different valuations. Bear moves first and has a strong preference for red over blue, but cannot yet identify which opponent most values blue. Goose, moving second, also prefers red to blue and might test the waters with a red-for-blue or red-for-green offer. Ram, moving last, knows they value blue the most and wants to acquire blue, so Ram will likely propose trading away some red or green to obtain blue. Given everyone’s limited information, round-0 trades are expected to be relatively conservative and symmetric (like 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 offers) rather than extreme or strongly exploiting the full value differences.

After round 0, there has been one executed trade: Ram successfully acquired one blue from Bear in exchange for one green. Publicly, everyone now sees that Bear was willing to give up blue for green at a 1:1 rate and that Ram was willing to pay green for blue, suggesting that Ram might value blue more than green and that Bear may not view blue as especially precious. Goose observes this and can infer that at least one opponent is actively trading blue for non-blue at even terms. Holdings now are: Bear (BLUE 9, GREEN 11, RED 10), Goose (10 of each), Ram (BLUE 11, GREEN 9, RED 10). In this midgame round, Bear and Goose are both slightly more confident that Ram is a natural buyer of blue, and Ram knows Bear is a willing blue seller at modest prices. However, the information is still coarse, and each player must worry that overly generous offers will be quickly snapped up, possibly by the ‘wrong’ partner due to randomization when multiple players accept. This encourages slightly more targeted, but still cautious, proposals.

Entering the final round, there have been two successful trades: Bear traded one blue for one green with Ram in round 0, and then two blue for one red with Ram in round 1. Current holdings are: Bear (BLUE 7, GREEN 11, RED 11), Goose (BLUE 10, GREEN 10, RED 10), Ram (BLUE 13, GREEN 9, RED 9). Publicly, it is now clear that Ram consistently accepts trades that increase blue holdings even at the cost of green and red, while Bear is regularly offloading blue to get either green or red. Goose has not participated in any trade yet, suggesting either more conservative thresholds for acceptance or valuations not yet well-matched by observed offers. With only one proposal turn left per player, they must consider that there is no future beyond this round: trades are now purely about immediate payoff, without any signaling value. Bear may try one more blue-for-red or blue-for-green trade, likely pitched to be attractive to Ram; Goose may make a final attempt to gain red at the expense of blue; and Ram, already heavily invested in blue, might still be willing to trade some red or green for blue if the price is favorable, but will be more cautious not to overpay in terms of their own private values.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips, with green chips having a common and publicly known value of $0.50 each. All players start with 10 of each chip color, and each round every player can make one proposal where they offer some quantity of one chip color in exchange for a different color. The twist is that acceptance decisions are simultaneous and, if more than one player accepts, the partner is randomly chosen, which introduces strategic risk when making broadly attractive offers.

From an analyst’s perspective, the incentives are clear: octopus-green-4361 values both red and blue at $1.00 each, making them a high-value aggregator of non-green chips. lion-blue-472 values blue at $0.70 and both red and green at $0.50, while ram-purple-2824 values blue at $0.70, green at $0.50, and red at only $0.20. Efficient surplus-creating trades tend to move red chips from ram-purple-2824 (who values them least) to octopus-green-4361 (who values them most), often in exchange for green (which is worth $0.50 to everyone) or possibly blue. Similarly, blue chips are especially valuable in the hands of octopus-green-4361, slightly more so than for the others, though the spread is smaller.

However, the players don’t know these comparative values. Each player only knows their own valuations and can only infer others’ values from observed trade patterns. This generates uncertainty: a player offering to buy red with green or blue must guess whether others will find the terms attractive. Because the game has a short, fixed horizon of 3 rounds (with one proposal per player per round), players face a tension between trying to learn about others’ values through exploratory offers and locking in immediate gains. The random tie-break when multiple players accept adds further uncertainty: a proposer may want to make a deal that only one target is likely to accept, avoiding the risk of an unintended trading partner capturing the surplus.

Given the schedule, lion-blue-472 acts first in every round and may initially probe with conservative offers that try to cheaply acquire blue (which they privately value highest) or green (safe, common-value) without making their preferences too obvious. octopus-green-4361, as the high-value buyer of both red and blue, is strongly motivated to accumulate those chips whenever possible, especially from ram-purple-2824, who is likely to sell red more cheaply due to their low private value. ram-purple-2824, recognizing that red is not very valuable to them, has an incentive to accept trades that turn red into either green or blue at terms that look good from their perspective, while being more reluctant to part with blue. Over the three rounds, we expect a few mutually beneficial trades that reallocate some red toward octopus-green-4361 and some blue toward them as well, while lion-blue-472 and ram-purple-2824 try to exploit their own perceived good deals without overpaying, all under incomplete information and the risk that attractive offers draw multiple competing acceptances.

The following forecast reconstructs one plausible full-play path consistent with these incentives and uncertainties: some exploratory offers early, at least one surplus-creating red-for-green trade between octopus-green-4361 and ram-purple-2824, and subsequent rounds where players adjust their strategies based on the observed history and remaining holdings.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has observed any prior behavior, so proposals are likely to be exploratory and relatively conservative. lion-blue-472 moves first and may test the market for green or blue without giving away too much value, trying to see who is willing to trade and at what apparent terms. octopus-green-4361, as a high-value buyer of both red and blue, will likely already be inclined to ask for red or blue in exchange for green, because green’s value is safely known at $0.50 to everyone and is a natural medium of exchange. ram-purple-2824, realizing that their red is relatively weak, is primed to accept trades that upgrade red into green or blue if the terms look favorable from their private perspective. This first round sets the tone: who is willing to sell red, who is eager to buy it, and whether generous offers attract multiple acceptances.

Going into round 1, players have seen that lion-blue-472 was unwilling to swap green for blue at 1:1, that octopus-green-4361 successfully bought red from ram-purple-2824 using green, and that ram-purple-2824 tried but failed to use red to buy blue. This history suggests to everyone that ram-purple-2824 may be relatively eager to sell red, and that octopus-green-4361 is an active buyer of red, valuing it at least as much as green. lion-blue-472 might infer that red is in demand and re-evaluate their own willingness to part with or acquire red. octopus-green-4361, bolstered by a successful trade, is likely to continue pushing for more red aggregation. ram-purple-2824 may try again to turn red into more valuable chips but will be wary of offering terms that look too stingy to others, given the previous rejection.

By round 2, the pattern is clear: octopus-green-4361 has repeatedly and successfully purchased red from ram-purple-2824 using green, while lion-blue-472’s attempts to trade have been consistently declined, and ram-purple-2824’s own proposals to sell red for blue or green have also failed. Players now know that octopus-green-4361 is a strong demander of red and is willing to part with green, while ram-purple-2824 can be induced to sell red at terms that at least look fair from their viewpoint. The final round leaves limited time, so players have an incentive to secure any remaining profitable trades rather than experiment. lion-blue-472 may make one last attempt to structure a simple, safe-looking swap involving green, though others might still decline if they see better value in their current holdings. octopus-green-4361 is likely to press for more red if any is left with ram-purple-2824, again using green as currency. ram-purple-2824 may either make a final attempt to offload red or, having seen repeated rejections of their own offers, decide that only particularly attractive terms to others will work—and might instead simply hold their chips if no clearly good trade is available.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features two privately valued chip types (RED and BLUE) plus a common-value GREEN chip. Each player starts with 10 of each color and knows only their own red/blue values. All know that green is $0.50 to everyone and that others’ red/blue values lie somewhere in a public range, but not the exact numbers. From the analyst’s perspective, cat-yellow-2069 and duck-green-2458 are high-value buyers of both RED and BLUE ($1.00 or $0.70 each), while buffalo-purple-5232 has relatively low values ($0.60 RED, $0.10 BLUE). Buffalo is in the best position to profit by selling BLUE (worth almost nothing to them) and possibly some RED to the others; cat and duck benefit from buying RED and BLUE when the price (in green or less-valued chips) is favorable relative to their private valuations. However, the players must infer each other’s values from behavior, so early proposals will likely be cautious, somewhat exploratory, and framed in simple one-color-for-one-color exchanges. With only three rounds and three turns per round, proposers face a tension between testing the waters and quickly locking in sure gains. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposals that are obviously favorable to both non-senders risk attracting both as accepters, leaving the proposer unable to choose the better counterparty. This encourages terms that are modestly profitable but not so generous that both rivals eagerly accept. Over the game, we should see buffalo trying to offload blue and occasionally trade for green or red, while cat and duck selectively accept deals that give them high-value chips for green and, later, possibly trade between themselves if relative values suggest mutually beneficial swaps. Declines in early rounds reflect uncertainty and screening: players avoid trades that might reveal they are too eager or that may be slightly negative given their private values. As they update from observed proposals and acceptances, later rounds may feature slightly sharper, more value-exploitative deals, especially where buffalo sells blue to whichever high-blue-value buyer is more responsive at a price above $0.10 but below that buyer’s value.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has symmetric holdings (10 of each color) but asymmetric information about valuations. Buffalo knows their own unusually low BLUE value, so it is natural for them to begin by trying to sell BLUE for either RED or GREEN at a rate suggesting BLUE might be valuable. Cat and duck, each valuing both RED and BLUE highly, will be cautious in accepting any trade that gives up green (guaranteed $0.50) for a chip whose value they think might not be dramatically higher. Early turns thus serve as price discovery: buffalo tests how much they can get for blue, and cat/duck use buffalo’s initial pricing to learn whether buffalo sees blue as valuable or cheap. Because there is no prior trade history yet, proposals and responses lean conservative, and many offers are likely to be declined if they look even slightly unfavorable under plausible beliefs.

Going into round 1, buffalo has slightly fewer BLUE and more GREEN after having sold blue to duck; duck now has proof that buffalo is willing to part with blue at a discount, hinting that buffalo’s blue value is low. Cat learned that duck was willing to buy blue when cat was not, suggesting duck might also value blue appreciably. Both cat and duck, however, turned down green-for-blue or green-for-red deals on their turns in round 0, signaling that they are not desperate to swap away green at even rates. In this round, buffalo will likely continue monetizing blue or possibly red, but might soften terms or aim the offer more clearly toward the high-blue player. Cat, seeing that blue is contested, might try to buy blue from buffalo more cheaply or to test buffalo’s willingness to part with red. Duck, having already profited from buying blue at a low implicit price, may now test whether cat or buffalo will trade green for red at slightly improved rates.

By round 2, buffalo has repeatedly sold BLUE for GREEN, accumulating green while shedding a chip they value little. Both cat and duck have demonstrated willingness at different times to pay roughly one green per two blue from buffalo, revealing that they see blue as clearly worth more than $0.50 but not infinitely so; buffalo infers that at least one or both rivals value blue fairly highly. With only one round left, there is limited time to experiment: players will now look for final, sure-profit trades. Buffalo can still profitably sell remaining blue, but both buyers know that buffalo’s blue value is low and may demand slightly better terms. Cat, who values both red and blue at $1.00, would like to buy red or blue using green if terms are better than 1:1 in value, but must guess how much buffalo and duck value red. Duck similarly wants to accumulate more red and maybe more blue, but may be content if further proposals are not obviously advantageous. The final offers are therefore more targeted: buffalo tries one more blue-for-green trade; cat makes a relatively aggressive attempt to buy red from duck at a price that might just be acceptable if duck is uncertain about cat’s valuation; duck ends with a conservative attempt at red-for-blue or red-for-green that is acceptable only if the counterparty’s red value is low enough.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with symmetric chip holdings (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but asymmetric private values for red and blue. Green is common-value at $0.50 to everyone, so green behaves like money or a safe numeraire. Butterfly values both RED ($1.00) and BLUE ($0.90) highly; Squid also values both highly (RED $0.90, BLUE $0.80); Kangaroo values RED ($0.40) more than BLUE ($0.10), and is much poorer in terms of value per chip than the other two. From an observer perspective, the key source of gains from trade is that Kangaroo’s BLUE is almost worthless to Kangaroo but quite valuable to both Butterfly and Squid, while Kangaroo’s valuation for RED is still lower than theirs. Rationally, Kangaroo should be willing to trade away BLUE for GREEN or for small amounts of RED; Butterfly and Squid should aggressively try to acquire BLUE from Kangaroo at favorable terms. However, each player only knows their own payoffs and sees only quantities and colors, so they must infer others’ valuations from observed offers and acceptances. Early in the game, proposals will likely be exploratory and conservative, using GREEN as a focal medium of exchange. As history accumulates, players infer that others value red/blue fairly highly (because they resist giving them up cheaply), leading to tightened margins and more selective acceptance. Strategic uncertainty arises because proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors; if both outside players accept, the game randomly picks a recipient. This makes it risky to propose deals that are good with one partner but bad with another. The proposer schedule is fixed (Butterfly, then Kangaroo, then Squid in each round), so later proposers in each round can condition on the evolving chip distribution and inferred valuations. Across three rounds, we expect at least one substantial trade of BLUE from Kangaroo to a high-value player, some red/green rebalancing, and possibly a failed attempt by a high-value player to squeeze favorable terms that others decline.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has 10 of each color. No one yet has revealed anything about their valuations. Proposals in this round are likely to be simple and symmetric-looking, often framed in terms of GREEN as a neutral chip. Butterfly, as first mover, may tentatively try to acquire GREEN or BLUE while giving up a bit of RED, testing how eager others are to trade. Kangaroo, as the low-value player (from the analyst’s perspective), could profitably trade away BLUE for GREEN or RED, but they don’t yet realize how much others might value BLUE, so they may offer modest, seemingly fair trades. Squid, moving last, can observe who was willing to part with which color and may already infer that RED and BLUE are valuable to others if offers to give them up are stingy or declined. Expect at least one declined proposal as players calibrate what constitutes an acceptable rate of exchange.

After round 0, the only executed trade was Kangaroo giving 4 BLUE to Squid for 2 RED. Publicly, this reveals that Kangaroo is willing to part with BLUE more readily than RED, and that Squid is willing to give up RED to get BLUE. Butterfly learns that at least one of the others values BLUE enough to pay in RED, and that Kangaroo’s BLUE may be relatively cheap. The chip holdings now are: Butterfly (10R,10B,10G), Kangaroo (12R,6B,10G), Squid (8R,14B,10G). With this new distribution, Butterfly’s proposals in round 1 can try to exploit Kangaroo’s apparent willingness to sell BLUE, perhaps using GREEN as a safe currency. Kangaroo, now richer in RED and poorer in BLUE, might still try to dump more BLUE or even sell some RED for GREEN if they think GREEN is equally valuable to everyone. Squid, as a strong holder of BLUE, may want to rebalance into GREEN or even buy more RED if terms are favorable, but will be cautious about offering too generous rates now that everyone has seen BLUE traded for RED.

By the start of round 2, several trades have shifted chip holdings and revealed patterns: Kangaroo has twice been willing to trade away BLUE and then to swap RED for GREEN, and has also accepted a GREEN-for-RED deal from Squid. This publicly suggests that Kangaroo may value GREEN at least as much as RED and is quite willing to use BLUE as a source of gains. Butterfly has only executed one trade so far, buying BLUE from Kangaroo for GREEN, which hints that Butterfly sees BLUE as at least as attractive as GREEN. Squid has signaled a strong preference for BLUE over RED (buying BLUE with RED) but later also traded RED for GREEN, showing some willingness to rebalance. Current holdings going into round 2 (given the prior trades) are roughly: Butterfly richer in BLUE and poorer in GREEN; Kangaroo richer in RED and GREEN but poorer in BLUE; Squid richer in BLUE and with moderate RED and GREEN. With only one round left, players may become more aggressive, trying to execute clearly positive-value trades rather than exploratory ones. Butterfly, as first mover, might again target BLUE or RED from Kangaroo using GREEN; Kangaroo might try one last time to swap relatively low-valued BLUE for GREEN or RED; Squid may attempt a small rebalancing move but will decline any trade that obviously worsens their high-valued holdings. Some proposals are likely to be declined because others now have firmer beliefs about relative values and are more selective.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All players value green chips equally at $0.50, so green serves mostly as a neutral medium rather than a strong target. The key heterogeneity is in red and blue: bat values red and blue at $0.80 each, fox values blue highly at $0.90 and red poorly at $0.40, and frog values blue highest at $1.00 and red moderately at $0.50. This creates clear surplus opportunities when red moves from fox to bat or frog, and when blue moves from bat or fox to frog. However, each player only knows their own values and only the common range for others, so they face uncertainty about how much the others are willing to pay or accept. Early in the game, players are likely to test the market with small, slightly favorable trades to learn who wants what. Because acceptance is simultaneous and random among acceptors, senders must design offers that are attractive but not so generous that they worry both opponents will jump in. Over three rounds, each player has three chances to propose, and trades update holdings immediately, so later proposals respond to the emerging pattern of who seems eager to buy or sell each color. Bargaining tensions revolve around (1) extracting surplus from fox’s relatively low red valuation, (2) trading blue toward frog and away from fox and bat, and (3) avoiding trades that give away too much blue or red in exchange for common-value green. With limited time (three rounds) and incomplete information, we should expect modest, incremental trades rather than perfectly efficient reallocation, with at least one or two beneficial red- or blue-based exchanges involving frog as a key blue buyer and fox as a red seller.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each color and have no direct evidence of others’ valuations. Proposers will likely make small, exploratory offers that are mildly favorable to themselves but not extreme, aiming to discover whether others are hungry for particular colors. Because blue is likely perceived as attractive within the public range, early offers may cautiously sell or buy blue and red against green, using green as a safe counter-chip. Players will want to avoid very lopsided trades that might accidentally heavily favor an opponent with unknown special values.

After round 0, everyone has seen two declined attempts involving blue and one accepted trade where frog bought red from fox for green. This suggests to all that (a) fox was willing to give up red for green at a roughly even rate, and (b) frog is interested in red. Bat learns that modest blue-for-green and red-for-blue swaps did not immediately attract takers. Players update their beliefs: fox may suspect others value red more than she does, and frog infers fox is a relatively cheap red seller. In this round, proposers are more willing to target known counterparties: bat may try to buy red or sell green; fox may again move red toward frog; and frog, owning extra red and still high-value blue preferences, may tentatively offer blue for red to improve her portfolio further.

By the final round, some structure has emerged. Fox has repeatedly traded away red and gained green and some blue, reinforcing the perception that she discounts red relative to others and values blue reasonably. Frog has acquired both red and blue over time but failed to sell blue for green in the last attempt, hinting that others do not see green as especially attractive. Bat has bought red from fox using green, indicating bat’s relatively strong red valuation and modest willingness to part with green. With only one proposal left per player, they now focus on final, value-improving tweaks—likely small, one- or two-chip trades that fit the revealed pattern: fox as a residual red seller, frog as a strong blue buyer, and bat as a balanced red–blue holder willing to use green to fine-tune their mix.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with identical chip holdings (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN), but they value chips differently. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 each for everyone, so it is a safe medium of exchange and a natural benchmark. The private values that matter are on RED and BLUE. Dog values RED and GREEN equally at $0.50, and BLUE lower at $0.30. Parrot and Turtle both value BLUE at $1.00 and RED at $0.80 and $0.60 respectively, making BLUE their most valuable chip and RED their second-best. This creates strong, but asymmetric, gains from trade: Dog wants to dump BLUE (low value) in exchange for RED or GREEN; Parrot/Turtle will happily take BLUE if the price in RED or GREEN is favorable enough. Because players only know their own values, they face uncertainty about others’ relative rankings of RED vs GREEN vs BLUE, though the common public range suggests BLUE and RED might be worth up to $1.00. This makes them cautious about offering too generous terms. Efficient surplus would typically involve Dog giving BLUE to Parrot or Turtle in exchange for RED or sometimes GREEN, allowing BLUE to move toward those who value it highly. Strategic tension arises because any proposal that leaves obvious profit to the responder might be interpreted as a sign the proposer is desperate or mispricing, and because multiple acceptors trigger a random recipient. Early in the game, players experiment with safe, modest-imbalance trades that appear mutually beneficial under many plausible valuations, then update beliefs from what is accepted or rejected. Over the three rounds, we should see Dog try to trade away BLUE and accumulate RED or GREEN; Parrot and Turtle both compete indirectly for Dog’s BLUE while also sometimes probing each other’s valuations on RED vs GREEN. Uncertainty about others’ values and the randomization among multiple acceptors makes players shade offers toward being just good enough to attract one partner without being so generous that both rivals always accept.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, no one has any information beyond their own values and the public ranges. Players are likely to make exploratory, conservative offers that would be appealing under many possible valuations. Dog, who values BLUE least, has a strong incentive to offer BLUE in exchange for RED or GREEN. Parrot and Turtle know BLUE could be very valuable to others (and is very valuable to themselves), but they do not yet know how Dog ranks chips. Initial trades will focus on small quantities, testing counterparties’ willingness to give up RED or GREEN for BLUE and revealing some information through acceptances or rejections.

By the start of round 1, Parrot has already bought some BLUE from Dog, revealing that Parrot values BLUE at least as much as the offered price in RED. Dog infers that BLUE is probably relatively valuable to Parrot, confirming Dog’s strategy of offloading BLUE. The rejection of Parrot’s RED-for-GREEN offer suggests that others do not view GREEN as obviously superior to RED, and Turtle’s failed BLUE-for-RED trade indicates that neither Dog nor Parrot is eager to give up RED for BLUE at parity. This pushes proposals toward slightly more generous BLUE-for-RED terms when targeting Dog and more cautious RED-for-GREEN trades. Players now refine offers to exploit perceived advantages: Dog continues dumping BLUE; Parrot and Turtle look for chances to cheaply acquire BLUE from Dog without overpaying; everyone remains wary of weakening their portfolios in terms of value per chip.

Entering the final round, Dog has successfully converted several low-value BLUE chips into higher-value RED, increasing both Dog’s total value and revealing to Parrot and Turtle that Dog is consistently willing to trade away BLUE at a discount. Parrot and Turtle have learned that competitors are careful about giving up GREEN, and that RED is in demand from Dog. With only one proposal left for each player, incentives shift toward opportunistic, clearly beneficial trades rather than long-term positioning. Dog continues to seek any last chances to offload remaining BLUE for RED or GREEN. Parrot and Turtle weigh whether they can still profitably buy BLUE or consolidate into their favorite colors, while avoiding trades that obviously reduce their own valuations given the now-short horizon.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 chips of each color and know only their own private values: Badger values BLUE highly (0.90) and RED moderately (0.60); Peacock values RED very highly (1.00) and BLUE very little (0.10); Unicorn values BLUE the most (1.00) and RED the least (0.40). GREEN is common-value (0.50) for everyone, so it functions as a stable medium of exchange and a natural reference point. Each player wants to convert less-valued colors into more-valued ones for themselves, but they also know that others’ values are somewhere in the public range, not exactly what they are. That uncertainty means they must infer others’ preferences indirectly from observed trades, while remaining wary of giving away too much surplus. Badger will tend to acquire BLUE when cheap and be relatively willing to trade away some RED if the price is good; Peacock will be strongly motivated to acquire RED and dump BLUE; Unicorn will aggressively protect BLUE and be more willing to let go of RED. However, because offers must specify both the color demanded and the color offered, and acceptance decisions are simultaneous among the two potential recipients with random tie-breaking, proposers must design trades that are attractive enough to at least one counterpart while not overpaying if both accept. Over the three rounds, players experiment early with relatively safe, GREEN-anchored offers and then adjust based on revealed behavior and updated beliefs, but they never fully know the others’ exact valuations. The likely result is partial but not fully efficient reallocation of RED and BLUE: Peacock improves her RED holdings somewhat, Unicorn protects most of his BLUE, and Badger opportunistically picks up or sheds chips where small, low-risk gains are available.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players have symmetric chip endowments but asymmetric and privately known values. No one has yet seen any trade behavior, so beliefs about others’ preferences are diffuse within the public ranges. Given the uncertainty and three full rounds ahead, players in Round 0 are likely to make conservative, GREEN-anchored offers that plausibly appeal to at least one counterpart across many possible valuations. They will avoid very lopsided offers that might reveal too much or risk immediate rejection. This round serves both as an attempt at small gains and as an information-gathering phase: who is willing to give up which colors, and at what terms.

By the start of Round 1, the initial trades have revealed useful information. Badger has signaled a willingness to pay more than GREEN value for BLUE, making it plausible that Badger values BLUE relatively highly. Peacock has shown strong willingness to trade away BLUE for RED at a generous rate, signaling low BLUE and high RED value. Unicorn’s declined offer suggests caution about exchanging RED for merely one GREEN, hinting that RED might still be somewhat valuable to him or that he is generally cautious. With this partial information, players now refine their offers: Peacock will lean harder into monetizing BLUE for RED, Unicorn will focus on exchanging unwanted RED for GREEN at more favorable terms or targeting Peacock’s BLUE if cheap, and Badger will look for mid-range, relatively safe improvements without overcommitting.

Going into the final round, players have more structured beliefs: Peacock is clearly desperate for RED and happy to offload BLUE; Unicorn highly values BLUE and is gradually converting low-value RED into GREEN; Badger appears flexible, often acting as a middleman when a trade looks slightly favorable in own-value terms. With only three turns left and no future rounds to exploit information, this stage is about extracting final marginal gains. Players may offer somewhat more generous trades than earlier to ensure acceptance, but they still avoid dominated moves that clearly reduce their own payoff. The bargaining becomes more tactical: proposers tailor offers to the most likely high-value counterpart (e.g., Peacock for RED, Unicorn for BLUE) while considering that the other player can also accept and be randomly selected, so prices must still be acceptable to either potential recipient or rely on sharp asymmetry in acceptance behavior.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips but common value for green chips. Everyone knows that green is worth $0.50 to all, but each player knows only their own payoffs for red and blue. From our vantage point, blue is heterogeneous: owl-purple-3001 values blue highly ($0.70), mouse-green-4201 moderately ($0.40), and dog-orange-6603 low ($0.30). Red is medium value for dog and owl ($0.50) and slightly lower for mouse ($0.40). Because players only know their own payoffs, they understand that others might value colors differently but do not know the exact pattern. This creates classic bargaining uncertainty: a proposer must guess what might be attractive to each potential partner while protecting their own surplus. We should expect players to use green as a neutral, safe currency because its value is publicly known and identical across players, facilitating risk-free trades when reshuffling red and blue between differently valuing players. High-value holders of blue (especially owl) are reluctant to part with blue unless compensated heavily, whereas low-value holders of blue (especially dog) are more willing to trade away blue in return for mixes of green or red. Because when multiple players accept, the recipient is chosen randomly, proposers have an incentive to construct offers that are clearly better for exactly one target (to avoid unintended partners), or at least equally acceptable to all if they do not mind randomization. Over three rounds, each player gets three proposal opportunities and can observe previous trades. Early in the game, bargaining is more exploratory: players try relatively conservative trades to probe what might be acceptable. As the game progresses, they have more information about others’ potential valuations from what trades were accepted or rejected, but they still face uncertainty and must consider the random recipient rule. Since there is no explicit time discounting or final-endgame bonus, but only three rounds total, players may slightly relax their standards in later turns if they have not gained value yet, increasing the chance of accepting positive but modest-surplus offers. Overall, the likely dynamic is that dog and mouse try to trade low-value blues for green or red, while owl tries either to accumulate more blue or at least avoid losing too much blue while using green as payment where necessary. Efficient gains should involve blue flowing toward owl and away from dog, with compensation in green or red, but incomplete information and random matching mean realized trades may only partially achieve that efficient reallocation.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but different private payoffs for red and blue. No public trade history exists yet, so players must rely on priors: they know that others may have higher or lower values for each color, but not how much. Given no prior signals, first-round offers are likely to be cautious and use green as the payment medium because its value is common knowledge. Dog, the first proposer, is a low-value holder of blue but a relatively strong holder of red. Mouse and owl will evaluate offers primarily by ensuring they obtain chips they plausibly value at least as much as what they give up, with special caution regarding blue since they do not know how much the proposer values it. Because of the random-recipient rule when multiple players accept, proposers may try to design offers that are attractive to exactly one plausible partner, or at least clearly negative for one player, to control expected counterparties indirectly.

After round 0, no trades have been executed, so holdings remain at 10 of each chip for all players. However, players have observed that relatively balanced, green-for-red or green-for-blue swaps were rejected. This signals that others are somewhat conservative and may believe these near-even trades do not generate enough surplus or may not improve their private values. Proposers in round 1 have slight evidence that they must offer more favorable terms to entice acceptance. Dog knows internally that blue is his weakest chip and that someone might like blue more, so he may start experimenting with selling blue. Mouse and owl see that green has not yet moved and might still be used to offset trades in red and blue, but they will be wary of giving up chips they privately value highly (for owl, blue; for dog and owl, red) without clear advantage. Because nothing has moved yet and there are only three rounds, some players might start to relax their demands slightly, but not dramatically, preferring trades with clear private gain.

Entering the final round, some informative trades have occurred. From round 1: dog traded away 2 blue for 1 red to owl, and then accepted 1 blue in exchange for 1 green from mouse. Publicly, others see that dog is willing to part with blue at seemingly generous terms and that owl was willing to acquire blue even when giving up red, suggesting owl may value blue at least comparably to red. Mouse also showed some willingness to trade green for blue at a 1:1 rate. After these moves, holdings (from the observer’s perspective) are: dog: RED 11, BLUE 9, GREEN 9; mouse: RED 10, BLUE 9, GREEN 11; owl: RED 9, BLUE 12, GREEN 10. Dog has slightly more red and less green; owl has accumulated blue but lost some red; mouse has a bit more green and a bit less blue. In this last round, players have only one remaining proposal each. They now know that blue is at least moderately demanded (both owl and mouse have accepted blue trades), and green is clearly used as a trading currency. Proposers may be more willing to accept modest but safe gains because there are no future opportunities. Still, without precise knowledge of others’ valuations, offers will remain somewhat conservative and may not fully achieve the theoretically optimal reallocation of blue to owl. Each player now trades off the marginal value of extra chips against the risk of unfavorable swaps, and given the short horizon, some beneficial trades will still be left on the table.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin symmetrically with 10 chips of each color and very similar private values. Reds are the most valuable chip for everyone (Alligator and Buffalo: $1.00, Rabbit: $0.90), blues are somewhat less valuable (Alligator and Buffalo: $0.70, Rabbit: $0.60), and greens are common-value at $0.50 to everyone. Because valuations are close and red dominates blue and green for all players, the main incentive is to trade away green or, secondarily, blue chips in order to acquire more red chips. However, any proposed trade must leave some surplus for the counterparty relative to their unknown private values, and proposers do not know that everyone else values colors almost identically. This uncertainty about others’ values, coupled with the inability to choose which accepting partner they get when multiple players accept, pushes players toward modest, seemingly fair ratios like 1 red for 2 greens or 1 red for 2 blues early on. Players will be reluctant to give up reds cheaply, so initial offers may be exploratory and often declined if they appear one-sided or if a responder anticipates better later opportunities. Over three rounds, with each player proposing once per round, we can expect early cautious trades that test the demand for red versus green and blue, followed by slightly more aggressive or targeted offers once some beliefs about others’ preferences are updated from observed acceptances and rejections. Since everyone privately prefers red and is indifferent on greens in the same way, mutually beneficial trades exist but are thin: they mostly come from small hedging moves (e.g., Rabbit giving up slightly undervalued red or blue for a bundle of green when facing a more red-hungry counterpart) or from players misestimating others’ valuations and accepting offers that yield only small or even negative surplus. The finite three-round horizon also encourages players to accept marginally profitable trades late in the game, since there is no reason to preserve chips for future bargaining once the final round is reached.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, players have no information about others’ private values beyond the public ranges, so first proposals are likely to be cautious and symmetric-looking. Because all three value red most, the most natural first moves are offers that try to buy red using less-valued chips (green or blue) at ratios that still might look fair to a responder. Responders must weigh accepting a modestly profitable trade now against waiting for potentially better bargains in later rounds. Given symmetric priors and no trade history yet, the default attitude is slightly conservative: players tend to reject very unequal offers, accept clearly favorable ones, and sometimes accept roughly fair-looking deals in order to gain a small edge or reveal little information.

By the second round, players have observed that red is in demand: Alligator successfully bought a red for greens from Buffalo, and Rabbit successfully bought a red from Alligator using blue.
