This suggests to everyone that others are indeed willing to give up red if compensated by a couple of lower-valued chips, and that red is consistently viewed as comparatively scarce and valuable. Alligator and Rabbit now each have slightly more red, and Buffalo has slightly fewer. Buffalo may try to rebuild red holdings or exploit blue/green differences, while Alligator and Rabbit may probe whether others will still sell red or whether they can get favorable terms using their now somewhat skewed portfolios. Responders, having seen these previous trades, become a bit more price-sensitive about red, rejecting deals that give up red at too low a premium.

The final round removes any incentive to hold onto chips for future bargaining; each player now wants to lock in any last positive-surplus deals. Histories reveal that 2-for-1 trades involving red have sometimes been accepted, especially where the responder views the offered chips as not much worse. At this stage, players holding relatively more red may try to sell at slightly better terms, while those with fewer reds may attempt to buy even at thin margins. However, because valuations are quite similar and everyone has learned that red is consistently prized, offers that require giving up red for only a small gain are more likely to be rejected, while opportunities to upgrade from green or blue into red can be attractive if the price is right. Final trades in this round are thus likely to be marginal improvements, reflecting a mix of last-minute opportunism and cautious pricing based on the limited information revealed so far.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, which have different private values for each player, while green chips are common-value and thus a natural numeraire. All players start with 10 of each color. Goose values red highly (0.90) relative to blue (0.50) and green (0.50), so goose is strongly motivated to accumulate red and is reluctant to give it up unless compensated by many blue/green chips. Bear has flat values (0.50) for all colors, making bear relatively indifferent across colors and more willing to facilitate trades if they gain total chip value. Buffalo values red (0.80) more than blue (0.20) and green (0.50), so buffalo also prefers red over blue and is especially willing to unload blue when compensated. Because each player only knows their own payoffs, they must infer others’ values from observed offers and acceptances. Goose and buffalo are both high-red types but do not know that ex ante; they will probe by offering small amounts of their less-valued chips (blue/green) to try to buy red. Bear’s equal values mean bear can profit as a middle player if they receive favorable terms trading with either side. Strategic uncertainty revolves around who values what: early trades serve as signals of preferences and rationality. Strong bilateral gains exist when red moves from bear to goose or buffalo and blue moves from buffalo to goose/bear, but misaligned beliefs and fear of being exploited may limit extreme trades. Over three rounds (nine total turns), we’re likely to see exploratory offers early, followed by slightly more targeted proposals once players infer that red is often defended and blue is often offered. Because acceptances are simultaneous and matching is random when multiple accept, proposers will typically design offers that are attractive to at least one plausible counterparty while not so good that both jump on them, to reduce lottery risk. However, with only three rounds and limited history, many proposals remain modest and some mutually beneficial trades fail because the proposer underestimates others’ willingness to trade or mis-prices the terms.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, each player has 10 of each chip and no information about others’ private values beyond the public ranges. Players use these first proposals to probe relative preferences. Goose, who privately values red the most, is inclined to test the market by trying to buy red at favorable rates using blue or green. Bear, being value-flat, may try low-risk offers that gain a bit of value but also reveal which colors others defend. Buffalo, who dislikes blue the most and likes red, is inclined to offer blue and demand red. Early in this round, offers are exploratory and may be too greedy to succeed, but the responses start to reveal who is willing to part with which colors.

By the start of round 1, one trade has executed: buffalo bought red from bear for blue, leaving buffalo with more red and fewer blue, and bear with more blue and fewer red. Goose has seen that bear was willing to part with red at a moderate price, and that buffalo was willing to pay with blue, suggesting buffalo values red more than blue. Bear infers that buffalo dislikes holding blue and likes red, and may expect similar preferences from goose but is less certain. Given this, goose may try again to buy red but faces competition from buffalo and must be wary of overpaying given that red is already scarce at bear. Bear, now holding relatively more blue, may seek to rebalance by selling blue to whichever party values it more, attempting small value-gaining trades consistent with their flat valuations. Buffalo, having successfully shifted some blue into red, might either push for additional red or look to sell off remaining blue at rates that still increase their payoff, possibly targeting goose if goose appears more hesitant.

Entering round 2, a second trade has executed: bear has traded some blue to goose for green. Bear now has fewer red than at the start (due to selling to buffalo) and somewhat more blue and green overall, consistent with modest value gains from their perspective. Goose has given up some green for blue, which is roughly value-neutral for them but provides information about bear’s flexibility. Buffalo has clearly signaled a willingness to trade blue away for red but faced resistance on the last attempt, suggesting that marginal red is now tightly held and more expensive. In this final round, each player recognizes there is little time left. Goose may make one last attempt to buy red, but must offer increasingly generous terms; given limited remaining red at bear and no visible willingness from buffalo to sell red, goose may instead try for minor value-improving trades involving blue/green. Bear, having already booked some gains, may avoid risky trades that could lower their value, instead proposing conservative offers that only slightly improve their portfolio. Buffalo, low on blue and high on red, may recognize that additional red is hard to obtain and that selling red is painful; they may test whether a generous blue-plus-green or green-heavy offer materializes, but absent that, they will likely protect their red holdings and only propose or accept trades that clearly increase their payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric endowments (10 red, 10 blue, 10 green) but have different private values for red and blue chips, while green chips have a common known value. Peacock-red-3668 values red chips especially highly (0.80) and blue at 0.50; cat-orange-2462 values red and green equally (0.50) and blue the least (0.30); otter-blue-7516 prefers red (0.70) over green (0.50) and blue (0.20). This creates classic double-sided gains from trade: peacock and otter are natural red buyers and blue sellers; cat is a natural blue buyer relative to otter and a moderate red buyer relative to blue, but less intense than peacock and otter. Because green is common value, trades involving green only reshuffle risk or timing rather than create private-value surplus, so most value-creating trade will tend to focus on red/blue swaps. However, participants only know their own values, so they must infer others’ preferences from the offers they see. Players are incentivized to shade offers in their favor while still making them attractive enough to be accepted, under uncertainty about others’ valuations and willingness to compete for the same deal. The random recipient rule when multiple players accept makes it risky to design highly favorable offers that might attract several takers, because the sender cannot select the best partner. Over the three rounds, each player has three proposal opportunities and multiple response opportunities, but there is no requirement or guarantee that mutually beneficial trades will occur; players might reject modest gains if they hope for better later, or misinterpret others’ value signals, leading to declined trades and persistent inefficiencies.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, everyone holds the symmetric 10–10–10 bundle and lacks information about others’ red/blue valuations. The first mover, peacock, knows they value red highly and blue moderately, so they will likely avoid giving away red cheaply and be more willing to part with blue. Cat, moving second, knows they value blue weakly and may try to sell blue for red or green, but they are unsure how much counterparties value blue. Otter, moving last, knows they also like red strongly and blue weakly, so they are a natural co-competitor for red and potential supplier of blue. In this first round, offers serve partly as screening and signaling: players test counterparties’ willingness to trade red versus blue and see whether anyone bites on relatively sender-favorable prices. Given the early stage and high uncertainty, at least one trade is plausible but not guaranteed; conservative responses are likely if offers look too one-sided.

After round 0, cat has revealed a willingness to trade green for blue by accepting peacock’s slightly favorable blue-for-green offer, signaling that cat is not strongly attached to blue and is comfortable giving up green at close to even value. Peacock has shown that they are willing to shed some blue, while otter’s rejected attempt to buy red with blue signals that at least one counterpart (likely peacock) does not find blue sufficiently attractive at that rate. Now cat moves first, having learned that both others were reluctant to take blue earlier; cat will likely be more cautious about using blue as their only carrot. Peacock, moving second, has slightly more green and less blue and may now probe for opportunities to trade surplus green or blue for red, but must avoid offers that are so favorable to counterparties that both might accept and invoke the random partner risk. Otter, last again, has seen limited interest in blue and no successful red trades, so they may either sweeten their blue-for-red terms or explore using green instead, all under continuing uncertainty about exactly how others rank the colors.

By the final round, some information has been revealed through accepted and rejected trades. Cat appears repeatedly willing to give up green and blue, suggesting they are flexible and perhaps uncertain about their marginal values. Peacock has signaled a persistent interest in red and a tolerance for paying with green, while being cautious about paying too much with red or accepting blue-heavy deals. Otter has shown demand for red and willingness to pay with blue and green; they have just sold some blue for green to cat, slightly improving their own portfolio toward greener, higher-value chips. With only three proposal turns left, there is limited time to realize additional gains. Players may modestly soften their terms to increase the probability of at least one more trade, but uncertainty about others’ exact valuations and the random-recipient rule still discourage overly generous offers that might attract both counterparts simultaneously. Final offers are likely to be slightly more competitive than earlier ones but still conservative, with one or two marginally beneficial deals at most.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players begin with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but different private values for red and blue; green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Badger highly values both red and blue at $1.00 each and is indifferent over trading between them except for information/strategic effects. Squid strongly prefers blue ($1.00) and sees red as almost worthless ($0.10) while valuing green at the common $0.50. Camel has intermediate values: blue $0.80, red $0.40, and green $0.50, so green is its single best chip, and both red and blue are somewhat valuable but strictly worse than green. From an omniscient perspective, surplus is created when red chips move away from squid (where they are worth $0.10) toward badger (where they are worth $1.00) or, to a lesser extent, toward camel ($0.40). Similarly, blue chips are most valuable in the hands of squid or badger ($1.00 each), and least valuable for camel ($0.80). Green chips are equally valuable to all.

However, each agent only knows its own values and can only infer others’ preferences from the common ranges and observed behavior. Squid has a strong incentive to convert red into green or blue, likely offering seemingly generous red-for-green or red-for-blue trades that are still profitable given squid’s very low value for red. Badger has a strong incentive to acquire both red and blue but will be reluctant to give up too much green unless trades are clearly favorable in its own valuation; badger may also attempt red-for-blue or blue-for-red swaps if it infers that someone undervalues one color. Camel, which values green most, will typically resist giving up green unless compensated by a large number of reds or blues; it may be willing to sell red to squid for green (since green dominates red for camel) and could be tempted to acquire blue if the price is good, given blue’s $0.80 value.

Because recipients are chosen randomly when multiple players accept, proposers cannot directly target a specific partner; they must design offers that look attractive to at least one likely counterparty but not so attractive that a more desirable trade partner is crowded out by competition. Simultaneous, private responses mean that each responder must anticipate how others might react and whether competition will dilute their chance of winning the deal. Over three rounds, with each player proposing once per round, we expect early offers to be somewhat exploratory, revealing information about relative valuations. Later proposals are likely to exploit perceived mispricings, especially in shifting red away from squid and blue away from camel while using green as a primary medium of exchange or a reference asset. Some mutually profitable trades will be missed due to strategic uncertainty and the risk of overpaying or losing a good deal to a rival when multiple acceptances trigger random recipient selection.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players still hold their initial 10-10-10 portfolios and have minimal information about others’ private values. Proposals are likely to be simple and to use green as a baseline, since its value is common knowledge. Squid is especially eager to turn low-value red into green or blue, badger wants to pick up extra red and blue but may be cautious about giving away green, and camel wants to preserve green while testing prices on red and blue. Trades that look lopsided in terms of chip counts can still be privately attractive because players differ in how they value each color. Responses will be conservative: each responder will check whether the trade improves their own payoff given their private values, without yet having a strong model of others’ preferences.

After round 0, squid has shed some of its low-value red in exchange for green, and badger now holds more red but less green. This hints to the others that squid may value red relatively little and that badger is comfortable holding red—suggesting red might be quite valuable to badger. Camel’s declined attempt to buy green with red suggests camel values green more than red, or at least isn’t willing to overpay. In round 1, squid will likely keep trying to offload remaining red or acquire more blue, badger may look for ways to re-balance by turning excess red into blue or green at favorable rates, and camel will look for any undervalued opportunities to sell red or blue for green. Each player updates their beliefs slightly from prior offers: squid infers that badger likes red enough to accept 5R for 2G, and both badger and squid infer that camel is very protective of green.

Entering the final round, squid has converted a chunk of its least-valued red into green, but its attempt to buy blue in round 1 was rebuffed, suggesting that others view blue as relatively valuable. Badger now holds a large stock of red and has just traded some of it for blue with camel, confirming that badger is happy to pay red for blue and that camel is willing to sell blue at a premium over red. Camel’s acceptance of 3R for 2B reveals that camel views blue as more valuable than red but still not so valuable as to hold it at any cost; it is actively arbitraging between its holdings of red and blue. In this last round, each player is aware that there are no future opportunities, so they focus on any immediate, safe gains: squid may try a slightly more generous or diversified offer to entice an acceptance, badger will try to refine its portfolio if it sees a clear edge, and camel may look for one final trade, especially if it can acquire more green or improve its red–blue mix. Strategic experimentation gives way to more one-shot, value-driven decisions, and some offers that would have been rejected earlier might now be accepted if they give even a small sure gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 to everyone) and privately valued red and blue chips. Ram holds the strongest preferences (RED $0.90, BLUE $0.70), Butterfly is intermediate (RED $0.70, BLUE $0.50), and Dog has very weak preferences for red and blue (RED $0.20, BLUE $0.10). Everyone knows the common green value and that red/blue are somewhere between $0.10–$1.00 but not each other’s exact values. Because Dog values red and blue so little, Dog is naturally positioned as a “chip broker” who may sell red/blue away in exchange for green, while Ram most wants to accumulate red and blue, and Butterfly is also inclined to accumulate but is less aggressive than Ram. However, players do not know this exact configuration, so they must infer others’ values from observed offers and acceptances.

Key incentives: Ram is strongly motivated to turn green into red/blue, especially red, as long as the price per chip is favorable. Butterfly also wants cheap red/blue but will not usually sell green cheaply unless getting high-value chips in return. Dog, given very low own values for red and blue, is willing in principle to trade them away for green (which is worth relatively more to Dog) if the terms look good enough from Dog’s limited perspective. Strategic uncertainty comes from the random selection of the recipient when multiple players accept and from incomplete information: each offer both signals and conceals valuations. Early in the game, players likely probe with modest, somewhat conservative offers, and as history unfolds they update beliefs and adjust prices.

Because there are only three rounds and each player gets exactly one proposal per round, time is short. Ram leads every round, so Ram can push a consistent strategy of trying to buy high-value red/blue from Dog or Butterfly with green, adjusting aggressiveness based on who seemed more eager to trade in previous turns. Dog, moving second each round, responds to Ram’s pricing and may experiment with offers that convert red/blue into green, especially if Ram has revealed a strong willingness to pay. Butterfly, always last, sees the most history in each round and can choose between opportunistic acceptance (exploiting Dog’s or Ram’s mispricing) and cautious rejection when prices seem bad or too revealing. The random tie-breaking when multiple acceptances occur discourages finely targeted exploitation and can make players shade offers somewhat in favor of the side they expect to be the more eager partner.

Overall, we expect green chips to flow toward Dog (who values them relatively more than red/blue) and red/blue chips to flow toward Ram and Butterfly, especially Ram. Serious surplus gains arise when Dog parts with red/blue at prices that look acceptable from Dog’s perspective but are very favorable to Ram or Butterfly. However, because of limited time and uncertainty, not all such potential trades will occur, and some offers that would be efficient given true values may still be declined if they appear risky or reveal too much.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 begins with completely untested beliefs. Ram moves first without knowing whether Dog or Butterfly is the more willing seller or buyer for each chip type. A natural early tactic for Ram is to test demand for green by offering to sell some green to see who bites and at what “price.” Dog then gets a turn to see if Ram’s move suggests strong interest in red or blue and may experiment by trying to trade into more green. Butterfly, going last, views both prior offers and uses them to infer whether either opponent is desperate for certain colors, responding more cautiously at this early stage.

After Round 0, Ram has successfully bought red from Dog using green and has revealed a clear willingness to trade green for red at a fairly generous rate from Ram’s perspective. Dog now knows Ram values red more than green, but does not know Ram’s exact valuations or Butterfly’s stance. Dog’s own failed attempt to buy green with multiple red suggests the price Dog offered was unattractive to both others, hinting that Dog may be undervaluing red relative to them. Butterfly has shown caution by declining to sell green for blue at a 1:1 rate, signaling that Butterfly likely values green at least as much as, or more than, blue. In this round, Ram can attempt to further exploit Dog’s apparent undervaluation of red and Butterfly’s reluctance to trade away green, potentially shifting some blue chips as well.

Going into the final round, Ram has repeatedly succeeded in acquiring Dog’s red at a roughly 1:1 rate with green, while none of Dog’s attempts to convert red or blue back into green have worked. This pattern suggests to Dog that the others put relatively high value on green and that the terms Dog has offered are not generous enough to entice them. Butterfly has remained conservative, refusing to trade green for blue or red at parity, indicating that Butterfly either values green at least as highly or is wary of revealing information in the final turn. In this last round, Ram has one more chance to extract value, likely again targeting Dog as the more flexible counterparty. Dog may make one last attempt to rebalance, but facing two cautious partners and only one remaining proposal, Dog is unlikely to find a mutually agreeable, clearly profitable trade. Butterfly, with the very last move in the game, can only propose one final offer and is unlikely to move far from a now-profitable, reasonably balanced portfolio without a very clear gain, which is hard to guarantee under uncertainty.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but asymmetric private values for red and blue chips. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so green functions as a natural benchmark and a focal medium of exchange. Iguana and Alligator both value blue highly ($0.70) and red more than Lion does, while Lion values both red and blue only $0.20. This makes Lion relatively indifferent between red and blue and comparatively more reliant on green for value, while Iguana and Alligator are more eager to accumulate blue and at least moderately value red. However, each player knows only their own value structure, so they must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and responses. The main strategic tension is between offering terms generous enough to be accepted under uncertainty about others’ values, versus protecting one’s own surplus—especially because green is obviously valuable to all, yet privately, blue and red might be valued very differently by counterparties. Because multiple acceptances trigger random partner selection, proposers cannot safely target a single counterpart; they must design proposals that are either acceptable from more than one perspective (risking randomness) or sufficiently tailored that at most one player finds them attractive. Over the three-round horizon, there is limited time to correct mispricings, so early trades can lock in advantageous positions or reveal information. The likely pattern is that Lion tries to trade away blue and red for green or for combinations that roughly preserve value under Lion’s own valuations, while Iguana and Alligator compete implicitly for cheap blue and, to a lesser extent, for red, each trying to buy undervalued chips without overpaying in green. Observed acceptances and refusals gradually reveal who values which colors, but given the short game, players will often rely on conservative, plausibly fair-looking exchange rates anchored near the public ranges for red and blue and the known $0.50 value of green.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have 10 of each chip and no direct evidence of others’ valuations beyond the public ranges and the known common value of green. Lion, with low valuations for red and blue, is naturally inclined to trade those away for green or for perceived gains in overall value, but must guess how much counterparties will pay. Iguana and Alligator both value blue relatively highly and may look for opportunities to acquire blue or red from a potentially less interested Lion. However, they also do not want to overpay in green, which they know everyone values at $0.50. Thus, early proposals are likely to be exploratory, with relatively modest quantities and exchange ratios that seem plausibly acceptable given the public ranges, while players watch who accepts what to infer preferences.

After round 0, the only executed trade was Lion’s sale of blue to Iguana for green, which suggests to Iguana that Lion undervalues blue relative to green and might continue to part with blue (or red) at favorable rates. Alligator learns that Iguana is willing to pay green for blue and that Lion is willing to give up blue, hinting at a potential future arbitrage, but Alligator has yet to trade. Lion now holds relatively more green and fewer blue chips. With some information revealed, Lion might test trading away red as well, while Iguana and Alligator try to structure offers that reflect their high value for blue but avoid overpaying in green, aware that Lion’s revealed behavior shows comparative indifference toward blue.

Going into the final round, some structure has emerged. Lion has converted both blue and red into additional green, consistent with low valuations for colored chips and a preference for the safe, known $0.50 value of green. Iguana and Alligator have each used trades to adjust their portfolios toward more blue and some red, reflecting their higher valuations for these colors. With only three proposal opportunities remaining, players are unlikely to attempt large, risky rebalancing trades and instead may look for small last-minute gains that seem clearly beneficial under their own valuations and at least plausibly attractive to others based on observed history. Proposals now tend to be more targeted and conservative, as the cost of a declined final offer is simply foregone surplus with no chance to compensate later.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color) but asymmetric in values for red and blue. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Whale values red the most (RED=$0.70), dolphin values red and green equally (RED=$0.50, GREEN=$0.50), and duck values red and blue relatively poorly (RED=$0.20, BLUE=$0.20). Blue is worth more to whale ($0.30) than to dolphin or duck ($0.20). Because players only know their own values, they must infer others’ priorities from observed offers and acceptances. In equilibrium-like play, trades that move red from duck to whale or dolphin, and blue from whale to others, can increase total surplus. However, players face uncertainty about whether a given offer is attractive to one or both potential recipients, and acceptors face uncertainty about counterpart selection when multiple players accept. Early in the game, proposers will experiment with small, value-safe exchanges of green (a known common-value currency) for red or blue to learn others’ preferences. As history accumulates, offers will more clearly target the high-red-value whale as a buyer of red and the lower-blue-value players as sellers of blue, using green as a balancing chip. Because there are only three rounds (nine proposals total) and no future penalties, players are likely to seek positive, low-risk gains each time they propose and to accept any trade that yields a clear value increase given their current portfolio and expectations about being chosen when multiple acceptances are possible.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have 10 of each color and no information about others’ private values beyond the public green value. Proposers in this round are likely to use green as a safe numeraire to test how others value red and blue. With no prior trade history, each proposer must balance making an offer that is personally profitable under any possible partner against the risk that both partners accept and counterpart selection becomes random. Small, conservative trades that slightly improve the proposer’s portfolio and are plausibly attractive to at least one counterparty are most likely.

After round 0, some information has been revealed. Duck showed willingness to trade away red and blue for green, signaling relatively low private values for red and blue. Whale demonstrated a willingness to give up green for blue, suggesting blue is more valuable to whale than green. Dolphin traded green for red and then accepted green for blue, indicating roughly similar valuations for red, green, and blue or at least a desire to rebalance. With updated holdings and inferred preferences, proposers can now more deliberately target trades: duck as a seller of red/blue, whale as a buyer of red and somewhat of blue, and dolphin as a flexible counterpart who may respond to small improvements. Green continues to serve as a safe, common-value medium to fine-tune portfolios.

By the final round, players have seen multiple instances of duck willingly trading away red and blue, whale acquiring red and some blue, and dolphin engaging in modest rebalancing trades. Beliefs are now more precise: whale likely has the strongest desire for red, duck is relatively indifferent to red and blue compared with green, and dolphin is roughly balanced across colors. With no future rounds, each proposal in round 2 aims at a last improvement in portfolio value. Proposers will focus on clearly beneficial trades with known counterpart patterns: whale as a buyer of red (from duck), duck as a seller of red and blue in exchange for green, and dolphin willing to adjust holdings when the value difference is small but positive. Riskier, multi-unit offers are less likely than simple 1-for-1 trades that still move chips toward higher-value holders.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value GREEN chips equally at $0.50, so green is a common-value asset and serves as a natural focal numeraire. The private uncertainty is about the relative values of RED and BLUE across players. Zebra privately values RED highly ($0.80) and BLUE moderately ($0.30); Gorilla values BLUE more than RED ($0.40 vs. $0.20); Turtle strongly prefers BLUE and almost ignores RED ($0.70 vs. $0.10). Efficient trades tend to move RED chips toward Zebra, BLUE chips toward Turtle (and somewhat Gorilla), and away from players who value them less, often using GREEN as a balancing payment. However, each player only knows their own payoffs, so they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. This creates bargaining frictions: proposers will initially make conservative offers that preserve their own surplus, then adjust as they learn who is keen to trade for which color. The random resolution of multiple acceptances weakens targeted side-dealing, so proposers try to post offers attractive to at least one counterpart but not so generous that both eagerly accept. Over three short rounds, players face a tradeoff between experimenting to learn others’ values and locking in sure gains. Given the short horizon and simultaneous responses, we expect modest, relatively symmetric trades that are obviously beneficial given a player’s own values, especially trades swapping low-value colors for higher-value ones, with GREEN occasionally used to sweeten offers or as a safe store of value when a player distrusts counterpart valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 of each chip. No one has yet revealed information, so proposers rely mainly on their own values and the public range. They know others might have different RED/BLUE values within $0.10–$1.00, and that GREEN is safely worth $0.50 to all. In this first round, offers are likely exploratory but conservative: players test whether others are eager to acquire the color they personally dislike, using GREEN or their least-valued color as payment. Rejections here are especially informative about counterpart valuations, shaping expectations for later rounds.

After round 0 trades, chip holdings and inferred preferences have shifted. Gorilla has shown a willingness to pay GREEN for BLUE and to dump RED cheaply, suggesting BLUE is relatively valuable and RED relatively weak for them. Turtle signaled that RED is low value by trading many RED away for BLUE. Zebra, who treated BLUE as expendable for GREEN, appears to value GREEN or RED more. With these signals, players now try to refine positions: Gorilla and Turtle both want BLUE but must be careful not to massively overpay each other; Zebra wants to accumulate RED cheaply while offloading BLUE when possible. Proposers balance learning against preserving surplus, using GREEN sparingly to avoid overcommitting to uncertain counterpart valuations.

Going into the final round, there is limited time to exploit what has been learned. Zebra has already improved their RED position and reduced BLUE somewhat; Gorilla and Turtle have both revealed strong BLUE demand and weak RED valuation. The remaining gains come mostly from small, targeted trades that push RED further toward Zebra and BLUE toward Gorilla or Turtle, constrained by random recipient assignment in case multiple players accept. With no future rounds, players no longer need to preserve reputation or information for later bargaining: they focus purely on immediate value gains and are more willing to post sharper, less exploratory offers, while rejecting any trade that looks even slightly negative based on their now-updated beliefs.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so trades involving only GREEN against itself are pointless. RED and BLUE are privately valued and create gains from trade: cat-green-4185 likes BLUE the most ($0.70) and is indifferent between RED and GREEN ($0.50 each); iguana-yellow-3262 values RED the most ($0.80), then GREEN ($0.50), and dislikes BLUE ($0.30); zebra-pink-2457 likes BLUE the most ($0.80), then GREEN ($0.50), and dislikes RED ($0.40). Privately, each player only knows their own payoff table and can only infer others’ values from observed proposals and acceptances. Efficient reallocation would move BLUE chips toward cat and zebra, and RED chips toward iguana, with GREEN serving as a neutral medium or fallback at $0.50. Strategic uncertainty comes from not knowing who especially wants what and from the random partner selection when multiple players accept a proposal. Proposers want to structure offers so that exactly one counterparty finds them attractive enough to accept, while still giving the proposer a clear value increase given their own valuations. Because there are only three rounds and three turns per round, players are unlikely to converge to fully efficient allocations; they will experiment early, seek obviously profitable trades (e.g., iguana trying to buy RED with BLUE, cat and zebra trying to buy BLUE with RED), and gradually update about who values which colors. Some mutually beneficial trades are likely, but fear of leaving value on the table, the risk of the “wrong” partner winning a multi-acceptance lottery, and incomplete learning will prevent perfect efficiency.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, no one has direct information about others’ valuations. Each player starts with symmetric endowments, so early proposals function both as attempts at profit and as signals about what the proposer might want. Given their own valuations, iguana is strongly motivated to acquire RED (and shed BLUE), cat wants more BLUE and is happy to give up RED or GREEN, and zebra wants BLUE and is relatively happier to give up RED. However, none of them know these patterns yet. Proposers in round 0 will likely make conservative, slightly favorable offers, hoping that at least one counterpart finds them acceptable while using responses to infer who likes which colors. Players will be wary of giving up too many units of a chip that might be valuable to them, especially in the first few trades.

After round 0, everyone has some evidence: cat has shown willingness to trade RED for BLUE and to accept BLUE-for-RED trades, revealing a relatively high value for BLUE and relatively low value for RED. Iguana has revealed a high value for RED and low value for BLUE by paying BLUE for RED. Zebra has revealed willingness to part with RED for BLUE. The public history indicates a pattern: RED tends to flow toward iguana, BLUE toward cat and zebra. Players still do not know exact values, but they can infer rough rankings. In round 1, proposals are more targeted: iguana will try again to buy RED with BLUE or possibly with GREEN, cat will continue to accumulate BLUE if possible while offloading RED or even GREEN, and zebra will try to exploit its comparative disadvantage in RED by trading away some RED for BLUE. Because cat now holds relatively fewer RED chips, attempts to purchase RED from cat become more expensive or smaller in scale, while iguana may consider alternative sources or prices. Players also start to anticipate that overly generous trades will be snapped up immediately, so they adjust offers to be only modestly attractive.

By round 2, the pattern is clear: iguana persistently tries to buy RED with BLUE; cat persistently tries to buy BLUE using RED or GREEN; zebra is often willing to give up RED for BLUE but has had fewer successful trades. Everyone can reasonably infer that iguana’s top priority is RED, that cat and zebra both prize BLUE, and that GREEN is relatively neutral. Because this is the final round, the value of keeping chips for future bargaining is gone: players should now be willing to accept any trade that strictly improves their monetary payoff, even if it slightly helps a rival. Proposers will try to squeeze out last small gains: iguana may still aim to capture spare RED from either opponent if they are indifferent or undervalue RED; cat will seek a final chance to convert non-BLUE chips into BLUE; zebra may either try one more time to offload RED for BLUE or simply decline unattractive offers to lock in its current payoff. Strategic uncertainty about exact values remains, but incentives are time-pressured: if a trade is not done now, it can never be done.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 each, so it’s a natural safe asset and benchmark. Private values differ for RED and BLUE, and each player only knows their own values while inferring others’ values from observed behavior. Bear values BLUE the most ($1.00) and RED second ($0.70), Swan also values BLUE highly ($0.90) but RED the least ($0.50), while Peacock values both RED ($0.60) and BLUE ($0.40) relatively low and symmetrically compared to the others. This makes Bear and Swan relatively BLUE-hungry and somewhat RED-tolerant, whereas Peacock is comparatively indifferent across RED/BLUE and may be more willing to give up BLUE or RED for GREEN if the rate is favorable. However, because everyone knows only that chips are somewhere in a public range, they cannot be sure exactly how others value them; they must infer from acceptance/decline patterns. The proposer schedule is fixed: Bear, then Peacock, then Swan in each of three rounds. Proposers must offer one color and request another, with a strictly positive quantity of each. Since partners are chosen randomly when multiple accept, proposers have to think not only about making an attractive offer but also about which counterpart is likely to accept and how risky it is to attract multiple acceptances. With only three rounds, there’s limited time to build detailed beliefs or reputations; players will try to quickly capture gains from trade by moving away from low-value chips toward high-value ones. Bear and Swan will search for ways to acquire BLUE or, to a lesser degree, RED, using GREEN or their least-valued color as payment. Peacock, being less extreme in valuations, can profit by trading away the color that’s especially valuable to someone else at a premium, but may misestimate just how desperate others are for specific colors. Overall, we expect small, relatively conservative trades early (probing others’ willingness to swap) and modest rebalancing of portfolios rather than extreme specialization, especially given uncertainty about others’ values and the risk of offers being declined.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone holds 10 of each color and has no information about others’ specific values beyond public ranges. Strategic uncertainty is highest now. Bear and Swan both privately value BLUE highly and might initially try low-risk, slightly favorable trades to test if others are willing to part with BLUE. Peacock, not knowing this, may be cautious in giving up BLUE or RED until seeing what kinds of terms are offered. Because this is the first round, small, nearly fair trades are plausible as players probe without revealing too much or risking obvious exploitation. Responses will be guided mostly by simple personal profitability checks and rough inferences: any trade that clearly reduces one’s own value will be rejected, while modest profit trades may be accepted even if players are unsure what this reveals about their preferences.

By the start of round 1, only one trade has occurred: Peacock traded 3 GREEN for 2 RED with Swan. Holdings are now: Bear unchanged (10R,10B,10G); Peacock (12R,10B,7G); Swan (8R,10B,13G). From Bear’s perspective, Peacock’s willingness to give up 3 GREEN for 2 RED suggests Peacock doesn’t value GREEN especially highly relative to RED; Swan’s acceptance implies Swan is comfortable giving up RED for GREEN, indicating lower RED value for Swan. Swan now holds extra GREEN and fewer RED, which subtly hints that RED may be less attractive to Swan than GREEN. Players still haven’t seen any BLUE trades, so valuations for BLUE remain quite uncertain. With a bit more information, proposers will still seek individually profitable trades but might slightly adjust their expectations: Bear might suspect Swan is more willing to part with RED, and Peacock might see Swan as GREEN-loving. Yet the absence of any accepted BLUE trades may make players wary about trying aggressive BLUE-for-other-color offers without sweetening terms.

Entering round 2, several trades have rebalanced portfolios. After the prior accepted trades, holdings are: Bear (12R,10B,7G), Peacock (11R,8B,10G), Swan (7R,12B,11G). The pattern shows Swan has been consistently willing to give up RED for GREEN or BLUE, and has accumulated BLUE, signaling a strong private value for BLUE and a relatively low value for RED. Peacock has traded away some BLUE and at times gained GREEN, suggesting moderate or lower BLUE valuation and no special aversion to GREEN. Bear’s only move so far was to acquire RED by giving up GREEN, implying Bear values RED above GREEN but has not yet signaled anything about BLUE in executed trades. Now with only one round left, players face a closing window to improve portfolios. They will look for clearly profitable trades given current holdings and inferred preferences: Bear may try to use remaining GREEN to acquire either RED or maybe BLUE if an attractive opportunity appears; Peacock might continue to arbitrage between RED and BLUE against GREEN if terms are slightly favorable; Swan, with a heavy BLUE stack, may be willing to part with a bit of BLUE for GREEN or possibly RED, but only at a significant premium given Swan’s high BLUE valuation. Because this is the final round, players may accept marginally risky but still privately profitable trades, but still reject offers that clearly reduce their total value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Everyone starts with an equal, symmetric endowment of 10 chips of each color, but their incentives differ sharply: gorilla and monkey both value red at $1.00 (highest for them) and blue very differently ($0.10 vs $0.90), while camel values blue at $0.60 and red at only $0.30. Green is worth $0.50 to all and thus behaves like cash. Because players only know their own valuations, they must infer others’ preferences from who tries to buy or sell which colors and at what rates. This creates strategic uncertainty: a player cannot be sure if an apparently generous deal is actually very favorable to the partner or if the partner is misvaluing chips. In equilibrium-like reasoning, players seek trades where the chip they give up has low value to them relative to the chip they get. That suggests surplus-generating exchanges should move red from camel to gorilla/monkey and move blue from gorilla to camel/monkey, while green may be used sparingly as a balancing medium. However, because partners are determined randomly when multiple players accept a proposal, proposers must design offers that are individually attractive to any intended recipient yet not so generous that others free-ride on the same terms. Over three short rounds, players have limited time to learn from others’ behavior. Early in the game, they are likely to make more exploratory, slightly conservative offers and tighten their proposals later as they infer relative values (for example, that monkey is eager for blue and red is cheap for camel). The resulting path of play will plausibly show a progression from probing offers to more directed, high-surplus bilateral trades, especially shifting blue toward higher-blue-valuers and red away from low-red-valuers, while avoiding trades that leak surplus (such as gorilla giving up red for green).

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has yet observed any trades, so beliefs about others’ values are very diffuse. Each player knows that all chip colors are roughly between $0.10 and $1.00 in value for others, and that green is common-value but not that red/blue are heterogeneous. Gorilla (low blue, high red) should be wary of trading away red for green and indifferent or favorable to selling blue. Camel (high blue, low red) sees red as an appealing asset to sell and green as neutral. Monkey (high blue, high red) strongly wants both red and blue relative to green. This round is likely to feature probing offers: gorilla may test whether others overvalue green or red; camel may test demand for red; and monkey may probe willingness to part with blue or red in exchange for green. Since there is still high uncertainty, offers will often be slightly conservative but still allow for surplus creation when a natural bilateral complementarity is discovered.

By the second round, the players have some information from round 0: monkey revealed substantial willingness to trade for blue (accepting blue-for-green and red-for-blue), camel sees that monkey is ready to exchange blue for red at a favorable rate, and gorilla sees that selling green for blue is feasible at modest terms. They still do not know each other’s exact valuations, but they can infer that monkey seems relatively bullish on blue and that camel is not extremely protective of blue but is willing to acquire red. Gorilla recognizes that monkey is an active counterparty for blue trades but will want to avoid giving away high-value red or taking in low-value green. Camel may now more confidently sell blue for red to monkey, and monkey will try to continue turning green into blue or red as long as the implied exchange rate is favorable. This round’s proposals are likely to be more targeted and more aggressively exploit emerging complementarities while still respecting individual value thresholds.

Entering the final round, patterns are clearer. Monkey has persistently accepted trades that increase red and blue holdings at the expense of green, signaling strong preferences for those colors. Camel has repeatedly agreed to trade blue for red or green when terms are not too unfavorable, revealing that blue is relatively abundant and red is less valued. Gorilla has managed to shift some green into blue and has observed that both others are willing to trade blue at reasonable rates. With only one proposal per player remaining, they will focus on high-impact, clearly beneficial trades and avoid experimental offers that risk being rejected. Gorilla can still profitably sell green for blue; camel can profit by selling blue for red or green; and monkey will accept almost any deal that trades away green for red or blue as long as the implied value loss is small. The deals here should be cleaner, more surplus-enhancing, and more one-sidedly beneficial than earlier probing offers, as players exploit their now-better-understood comparative advantages.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a known common value for green chips. Each player starts with an equal endowment of 10 chips of each color, but they only know their own payoffs per chip; they must infer others’ valuations from observed offers and acceptances. Greens are common-value at $0.50 to everyone, so green-for-red or green-for-blue trades are relatively transparent and lower-risk. By contrast, red and blue chips are likely to have heterogeneous private values: alligator strongly prefers blue and green over red (blue $0.60, green $0.50, red $0.10), otter strongly prefers blue and modestly values red (blue $1.00, red $0.40), and tiger values both colors but especially red (blue $0.90, red $0.70). From an analyst’s perspective, the efficient pattern would shift red chips toward tiger, blue chips toward otter (and secondarily tiger), and green chips could be traded flexibly as a medium of exchange. However, in play, each agent sees only their own values and a coarse public range, so they are uncertain about whether the counterparties value a given chip more or less than they do. This leads to conservative, incremental trades early (using green as a safe benchmark), followed by more targeted red–blue trades once players update beliefs about who values what. The proposer schedule repeats across rounds, giving early movers a chance to set price anchors and respond to revealed information. Because multiple acceptances trigger random partner assignment, proposers must design offers that are at least weakly acceptable to more than one counterpart but not overly generous, while responders must balance the value of an immediate, slightly favorable trade against the option value of waiting for better terms in later turns. Overall, we expect a modest number of executed trades that move red chips toward tiger and away from alligator, blue chips mainly toward otter and tiger, and greens out of the hands of those who urgently need to buy their favored color, with some surplus left unrealized due to asymmetric information and strategic caution.

rounds.round_explanation: At the beginning of the game, all players have symmetric holdings (10 of each chip) but different private valuations. No one has yet revealed information through trades. Alligator knows red is nearly worthless to them and expects others might value it more within the public range; otter suspects blue is likely valuable to others but uniquely high for themselves; tiger expects red and blue are both good but is unsure who values which more. In this first round, proposals are likely to be conservative and framed around green chips as a neutral currency, allowing players to test how eager others are to part with or acquire red/blue without exposing too much private information. Responders will be cautious, accepting only clearly positive-value trades, since they have two more rounds and many upcoming proposal slots ahead.

By the start of round 1, some information has been revealed: alligator attempted to buy blue with green and was refused, suggesting others value blue at least as much as green; otter bought red from tiger at a 1:1 green price, implying tiger is at least indifferent between red and green and otter is willing to pay green for red. This nudges beliefs that tiger has moderate red value and that otter sees red as worthwhile but not more valuable than green per chip. No trades have occurred involving alligator directly, so others still have weak beliefs about alligator’s value profile, though the opening attempt to trade green for blue hints that alligator might value blue more than green. In this round, players begin to refine offers: otter now tries to accumulate even more blue using red/green, tiger seeks to recover some green while keeping high-value chips, and alligator attempts a more generous offer to actually move unwanted red away, using green conservatively as a payment medium.

Going into the final round, the trade history suggests clear patterns: otter has revealed a strong appetite for both red and blue, having paid green and then red to acquire them; tiger has shown willingness to trade red away for green once but then declined a less favorable green-for-red offer, signaling red is relatively valuable; alligator has demonstrated a desire to acquire blue and spend green. Beliefs converge: everyone now suspects otter’s blue value is very high, tiger’s red value is high, and alligator’s red value is low with moderate blue value. Because this is the final round, there is no benefit to holding chips for future bargaining; players will accept any strictly profitable trade and may make more aggressive proposals. However, the random matching when multiple players accept still matters, so proposers tailor offers to target a likely partner while avoiding giving too-good deals to both. Remaining trades focus on moving low-value chips (for the holder) against high-value chips from others, with green again acting as a fallback medium when direct red–blue swaps look too risky.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game is driven by privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players start symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color), but their valuations differ. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so trades in green mainly serve as a convenient, transparent medium of exchange. The key asymmetries are: Dolphin values both red and blue very low ($0.10 each), so they are generally happy to give up red or blue in exchange for green, and will be reluctant to pay green for red/blue. Frog values red very highly ($0.90) and blue somewhat high ($0.50), so they want to accumulate red from others, ideally by paying in blue or green at terms that still give them profit. Pig has intermediate values (red $0.40, blue $0.30); compared to Dolphin, Pig values red and blue more, but compared to Frog, Pig values them less. This creates classic double-sided bargaining incentives and uncertainty: Dolphin knows only their own low red/blue values and green’s common value, and must guess how much others value colors; they might undercharge for red and blue. Frog knows they value red a lot and will try to extract red cheaply, but must be careful not to offer so much that others infer their strong preference. Pig sits in the middle and can profit by buying undervalued chips from Dolphin and potentially selling at a markup to Frog. Uncertainty about the others’ values means players will likely test prices with small trades early on—especially in Round 0—then adjust offers as they infer who is willing to accept which exchanges. Because responders move simultaneously and a random recipient is chosen if multiple accept, proposers cannot perfectly pick partners; that risk pushes them to make offers that are still profitable no matter which responder accepts. Over three rounds, the likely pattern is: (1) early exploratory offers revealing that Dolphin is eager to sell red/blue for green; (2) Frog attempting to accumulate red, often paying with green; (3) Pig sometimes taking advantageous trades with Dolphin and occasionally with Frog when terms are good. Some mutually beneficial trades may fail because proposers misprice offers under uncertainty, but overall, trades that move red chips from Dolphin or Pig toward Frog, and blue chips from Frog toward Dolphin or Pig, are most likely to be accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone has 10 chips of each color, and no one has observed any trade behavior yet. Each player only knows their own private values and the common value of green. Dolphin, valuing red and blue very low, is likely to test offers that sell red or blue for green at a favorable rate for themselves, unsure how others value those colors. Frog, who privately values red very highly, will likely probe for trades that acquire red using green or blue, while trying not to reveal just how valuable red is to them. Pig, with middle valuations, may be opportunistic, looking for clearly profitable deals (especially trading green away only when they get significant value back). This first round is thus characterized by exploratory and somewhat conservative offers, as players begin to form beliefs about how much the others will pay for each color.

After Round 0, some information has been revealed. Dolphin’s initial trade showed they were willing to give up multiple red chips for a modest amount of green; Frog gained red cheaply, confirming for Frog that others undervalue red relative to their own valuation. Pig saw that red can be purchased cheaply from Dolphin, but also noticed that neither Dolphin nor Frog were willing to overpay for blue or green in Pig’s first offer. Entering Round 1, Dolphin is even more inclined to convert red and perhaps blue into green, Frog aims to continue accumulating red but realizes that others will not accept extremely costly offers in green, and Pig remains cautious, looking for trades where they give up relatively low-value chips (for them, blue) in exchange for higher-value assets (especially green or, at a good price, red). Offers now adjust slightly based on what each player has inferred about others’ willingness to trade.

By Round 2, chip holdings and observed behavior have clarified each player’s likely priorities. Dolphin has repeatedly turned low-value red and blue into green and now strongly prefers to use their remaining red and blue as trade currency only if they can get more green. Frog has successfully accumulated some red and is now more selective, aware that pushing prices too high in terms of green reduces acceptance. Pig has learned that both counterparts are somewhat reluctant to sacrifice green unless they clearly gain value, and that there is competition for red (especially from Frog). With only one proposal left for each player, they have incentives either to finalize clearly profitable trades or to refrain from trades that might reduce their payoff. This tends to produce sharper, more value-driven offers—Dolphin posts generous terms to entice one last green-gaining trade, Frog tries a final red-acquiring offer but won’t overpay, and Pig balances between a last attempt to arbitrage and the safety of holding the current portfolio if terms are unattractive.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip counts (10 of each color) but asymmetric private values for red and blue. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Owl highly values red ($1.00) and blue ($0.60) compared to the others; squid also values red and blue more than iguana, but less than owl. Iguana has the lowest values, especially for blue ($0.20), making iguana a likely net seller of red and blue in exchange for green. Because green is worth $0.50 to all, it serves as a natural numeraire: trades that convert someone’s low-value chips into green while giving a counterparty higher-value chips are efficient. However, each player knows only their own values and only a rough public value range for each chip type, so they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances across the three rounds. Bargaining incentives push players to: (1) move chips from low-value to high-value holders, (2) ask for favorable terms given uncertainty, and (3) update beliefs as they see who is eager to buy or sell particular colors. Random matching among multiple acceptors means proposers cannot target a specific partner once more than one is willing to accept, which encourages slightly more conservative offers that are acceptable to at least one counterparty but may be generous enough that both sometimes try to accept. Over three rounds, early moves are more exploratory, later moves condition on revealed behavior (e.g., owl persistently buying red), and players balance immediate surplus with incomplete information and the risk that attractive offers may be snapped up by the "wrong" counterparty from their perspective.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, no trade history exists, so each player relies mainly on their own valuations and the common public ranges. Iguana, with relatively low values for red and especially blue, is naturally inclined to sell those colors for green, but must guess how attractive red or blue is to others. Owl knows red is extremely valuable privately and will likely try to buy red cheaply, framing early offers to probe how eager others are to sell. Squid, with moderately high values for red and blue, may both buy from iguana and sell to owl if terms are favorable. Round 0 is mostly about establishing initial price-like ratios (e.g., how many green for one red) and learning who seems most interested in each color. Because everyone still has all 10 of each color, constraints on chip availability are not yet important, and players can experiment with slightly exploratory trades.

After round 0, iguana has signaled a willingness to sell blue for green, and squid has revealed that buying blue at a modest discount (3 blue for 2 green) is acceptable, hinting that squid’s blue value is comfortably above $0.50 and above iguana’s. Owl’s attempt to buy red for green was rejected, indicating that at least one of iguana or squid values red enough not to part with it at that ratio, suggesting to owl that red may be relatively important to others as well. Squid’s declined attempt to swap blue for red also shows that neither iguana nor owl wants to give up red cheaply for blue. In this second round, players begin to refine offers: iguana continues to try to turn low-value blue into green, possibly improving terms to attract owl as well; owl adjusts red-buying offers, making them more generous in green; squid may pivot toward using green to obtain red rather than blue-for-red trades, given previous rejections. Beliefs are still forming, but initial price boundaries have been revealed.

By round 2, trade history has clarified roles. Iguana has repeatedly sold blue for green, reinforcing that iguana’s blue valuation is low relative to green. Both owl and squid have revealed a willingness to spend green to acquire more valuable red and blue. Owl’s successful purchase of red from squid at a generous green price indicates that owl values red highest and is willing to sacrifice substantial green to obtain it, while squid is comfortable converting some red into green at that premium. Now holdings are slightly skewed: iguana has more green and fewer blue; owl has fewer green but more blue and red; squid has more green and somewhat fewer red. The final round is about fine-tuning portfolios given these positions. Iguana may continue small blue-for-green trades, but remaining blue is lower; owl can no longer be as generous with green and might offer blue for red or aim for value-neutral tweaks; squid might seek an extra gain by buying undervalued blue from iguana or selling a bit more red to owl if the price is right. Because this is the last round, players do not worry about future signaling and focus purely on immediate surplus from any remaining mutually beneficial trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player bargaining game, each player privately values red and blue chips differently, while green chips have a common known value of $0.50 to everyone. Gorilla-purple-8501 (Gorilla) values red the most ($0.80) and blue at $0.50; Llama-pink-7039 (Llama) values blue the most ($0.80) and red at $0.60; Whale-orange-533 (Whale) values both red and blue relatively low ($0.60 and $0.30 respectively). Everyone values green at $0.50 and knows that others also value green at $0.50. This creates natural comparative advantages: Gorilla wants to accumulate red from others, Llama wants to accumulate blue, and Whale is the weakest bidder for both colored chips and will be relatively price-sensitive as a buyer but relatively willing to sell if compensated with greens. Because players only know their own private values, they must infer others’ preferences from which colors they try to buy or sell and at what quantities. Given symmetric initial endowments (10 of each color), early rounds are used to probe prices and reveal preferences while still leaving room for more efficient trades later. Strategic uncertainty arises from (1) incomplete information about opponents’ values, (2) the rule that if multiple players accept a proposal, a partner is chosen at random, and (3) the limited three-round horizon which can push players to accept moderately profitable trades rather than waiting for ideal ones. Rational proposals tend to offer counterparties at least weakly positive value in their own terms while moving the allocation toward more red for Gorilla and more blue for Llama. Greens are often used as the neutral ‘currency’ to compensate trade partners. Across the three rounds, we anticipate modest, incremental trades that gradually reveal that Gorilla is a strong demander of red, Llama is a strong demander of blue, and Whale selectively trades when it can turn relatively low-valued blue into green or, occasionally, into red or blue bundles that are at least value-neutral or slightly profitable given its lower valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has observed any trading behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ values are very diffuse. Players are likely to make relatively conservative, simple offers that are easy to interpret. Since greens are common-value, they are natural candidates to use as a medium of exchange. Gorilla, moving first, may test whether anyone is eager to give up green for red or blue, implicitly probing who values which color more. Llama, valuing blue the most, will likely try to pick up blue or offload some red or green if it can do so cheaply. Whale, with low blue value, has the strongest incentive to sell blue if it can obtain at least $0.50 per blue in its own terms, especially if it can receive greens. Rejections early in this round teach proposers that their terms were too harsh or misaligned with counterparties’ likely values.

By the start of round 1, some structure has emerged. Gorilla has signaled a willingness to pay green for red, revealing that red is likely relatively valuable to them. Llama has shown a willingness to pay green for blue, revealing that blue is relatively valuable. Whale has twice accepted trades where it gave up higher private value (red at $0.60, blue at $0.30) for green at $0.50, indicating a strong revealed preference for stabilizing into greens and a relatively low valuation for both red and blue. After experiencing a rejected attempt to sell blue for greens, Whale learns that others will not heavily overpay for blue, while Gorilla and Llama update that Whale is fairly price-sensitive as a buyer. In this round, players refine their strategies: Gorilla will still try to accumulate red but must keep offers attractive enough after Whale’s cautious last response; Llama will keep building blue stacks when possible; Whale will be more selective in its offers, focusing on at least break-even value in terms of greens or better.

Entering the final round, all players understand more about each other’s likely valuations: Gorilla is a consistent red buyer and is willing to trade away blue or green; Llama is a consistent blue buyer, comfortable paying with red or green; Whale routinely converts both red and blue into greens when the rate is at least value-neutral in its own $0.50 benchmark. With only three proposal turns remaining, the opportunity cost of rejecting a modestly profitable deal increases because there will be no later rounds to improve positions. Gorilla and Llama aim to fine-tune their inventories toward their favorite colors, while Whale tries for a last chance to rebalance into greens or at least secure non-negative value trades. Because players have now seen multiple rejections of Whale’s attempts to buy green too cheaply, Whale is unlikely to get very favorable prices, and counterparties will only accept offers that clearly improve their own payoffs given the effectively known common value of greens and inferred private preferences for red and blue.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players start with symmetric chip counts (10 of each color), but asymmetric values create gains from trade: duck and tiger both value red at 1.00, lion values red at 0.90; duck values blue at 1.00, lion at 0.50, tiger at 0.30; everyone values green at 0.50. Efficient trade would move blue chips toward duck and away from lion and tiger, and red chips toward duck and tiger (slightly away from lion). However, each player only knows their own values and sees only others’ behavior. This generates uncertainty about which colors others like or dislike and what exchange rates they might accept. Proposers must offer positive quantities of a chip they give up in exchange for a different color they request, and they face the risk that a very attractive proposal will be accepted by multiple others, with the partner chosen at random. This encourages moderate, not extreme, generosity. Over just three rounds (nine proposal turns total), players are unlikely to fully learn each other’s value structures. Early rounds are used to probe others’ tastes with slightly favorable offers that still test how cheaply counterparts will trade away certain colors. Middle and late rounds condition on observed acceptance patterns: a player who eagerly trades away blue likely dislikes blue, while someone who readily parts with red may reveal a low red valuation. Because all chips have positive value and the horizon is short, players will typically avoid very unfavorable trades and reserve highly favorable trades for times when they believe the other side is desperate for a particular color. Overall, we expect modest, roughly value-improving trades, with a focus on moving blue toward duck and red away from lion when possible, but without the players ever fully coordinating on the theoretically efficient allocation.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip. No one has yet revealed preferences by accepting or rejecting offers, so beliefs about others’ chip values are very diffuse. This round is mainly exploratory: each proposer is likely to make offers that slightly favor themselves while probing whether others are willing to give up particular colors. Because blue is risky for lion and tiger to hold if others might value it more, and because red is slightly less valuable to lion than to duck or tiger, trades involving red and blue are particularly salient. Proposers will keep offers mild to avoid being exploited and to limit the chance that both potential recipients simultaneously accept a clearly generous deal, which would transfer surplus away from the proposer regardless of which recipient is randomly chosen.

After round 0, a key signal has emerged: duck accepted an offer exchanging three blue for two red from lion. From duck’s perspective, this was roughly value-neutral or slightly favorable given duck’s equal valuation of red and blue, but it reveals to lion and tiger that duck is at least somewhat interested in red and not strongly averse to surrendering blue. Lion has successfully offloaded some blue in exchange for red, consistent with lion having relatively weak preferences over these colors. Tiger has not traded yet and has learned mainly that duck is an active trader in red-blue exchanges and that lion is willing to part with blue. In round 1, proposers will update: lion expects duck to respond to red/blue deals, while duck sees lion as a possible blue seller and tiger as still unknown. Offers become slightly more targeted, but uncertainty about exact valuations remains high.

By round 2, some patterns are clearer. Duck has twice accepted trades that give up more blue than red received, suggesting duck is relatively tolerant of losing blue or strongly values red, while lion and tiger each successfully offloaded blue in one deal. This is consistent with lion and tiger seeing blue as relatively weak and red as attractive, and with duck being the main buyer of both colors when the terms are only slightly unfavorable. With only one round left, players anticipate no future opportunities to correct bad trades, so they become more conservative. Proposers will try to make last-minute, modestly favorable trades that exploit their beliefs: lion and tiger see duck as a likely counterparty for blue-for-red or blue-for-green deals, while duck may either try for one more red gain or refrain from trades that could worsen their final bundle. Because remaining opportunities are so limited and offers must still be acceptable to others, many marginal trades are rejected if they are even slightly disadvantageous under each player’s private values.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip-bargaining game features common-value green chips and privately valued red/blue chips. All players start with symmetric chip counts (10 of each color), but their values differ: Bear highly values blue (1.00) and dislikes red (0.20); Goose values both red and blue at 0.80; Ram values both at 0.80 and 0.50 respectively, with moderate red and blue values. Everyone values green at 0.50. Because only private values are known individually, proposals also serve as signals about preferences. Rational players attempt to exchange low-value chips for higher-value chips while keeping offers just attractive enough that at least one counterpart might accept. However, uncertainty arises because each player sees only their own payoffs and must infer others’ valuations from observed trades. The random resolution when multiple acceptors exist further complicates strategic targeting: senders cannot pick their trading partner, so they must design offers that are profitable regardless of which acceptor is matched. Over three rounds, Bear (always first) is likely to try to swap red for blue or green at aggressive terms, Goose may seek red from Bear or Ram in exchange for green or blue, and Ram will often exploit being a symmetric intermediate type, accepting profitable trades when they appear and occasionally making offers that convert green into higher marginal value colors. As the game progresses, the public trade history—who accepts what—helps players refine beliefs about others’ private values, slightly improving the efficiency of later trades while still leaving some mutually beneficial trades unrealized due to misaligned beliefs and strategic shading.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no trade history exists, so players rely heavily on their own values and prior expectations. They will test the waters with relatively conservative offers that clearly benefit themselves and that might plausibly appeal to at least one counterpart who may value colors differently. Bear, as first mover, will likely probe demand for red by offering it for blue or green on unfavorable terms from Bear’s perspective but possibly acceptable to others. Goose, moving second, will be trying to infer whether Bear and Ram value red or blue more highly, while Ram, moving last, can react to observed acceptances or rejections to refine beliefs. Overall, round 0 tends to feature exploratory offers and few extremely generous deals.

Entering round 1, players have observed one successful trade: Goose trading away green for red with Ram. This publicly reveals that both Goose and Ram are at least somewhat willing to convert green into red, hinting that red is not extremely low-valued for them. Bear learns that others may be willing to part with green only for valuable colors. Proposers will now slightly adjust offers: Bear might still try to offload red but must consider that at least one counterparty already holds more red and less green, potentially reducing demand. Goose, having gained red, may focus on acquiring more blue or green if favorable, while Ram, having lost green and gained red, is less eager to give up additional green cheaply. Belief updates from round 0 narrow the plausible value profiles of counterparts, but uncertainty remains, so trades are still somewhat exploratory.

By round 2, two successful trades have occurred, both involving Ram as the accepting partner: first green-for-red, then red-for-blue with Goose. This pattern suggests to Bear and Goose that Ram has moderate valuations for all colors and is willing to accept modest but positive gains. Goose’s prior trades indicate a strong willingness to rearrange her portfolio toward red and blue when the terms are roughly even in chip counts, hinting at relatively balanced valuations for these two colors. Bear still has not traded and faces the final opportunity to convert low-value red into higher-value blue or green, but must now confront more informed and possibly less flexible counterparts.
