With limited time left, players may slightly relax their bargaining positions to realize remaining surplus, but they remain constrained by uncertainty and the random matching rule when multiple acceptances occur, leading to a mix of one or two final trades and some still-declined offers.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 of each chip color and know only their own private per-chip values. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it is neutral relative to value but still needed for trade structure. The main underlying tensions are in red and blue: lion-blue-472 values blue highly (0.60) and red/green moderately (0.50); octopus-green-4361 values everything very low (blue 0.10, red 0.20, green 0.50) and is generally poorest in value; ram-purple-2824 values blue the most (1.00), green moderately (0.50), and red lowest (0.30). Social surplus is maximized when blue chips end up with ram and red chips tend to move away from octopus to players who value them more. But none of the players know each other's exact values; they only see that all chip colors are tradable within a public value range. Players must therefore infer others' preferences from which colors they request or are willing to offer, and from whether they accept or decline given offers. Lion and ram both plausibly see blue as likely high-value and red as moderate, but cannot be sure how others rank them. Octopus, with consistently low valuations, will be willing to trade away red or blue if offered enough in some other color, but will try to avoid trades that clearly reduce nominal chip count by a lot because that looks risky under uncertainty. Because acceptance decisions are simultaneous and if both non-senders accept only one is randomly chosen, proposers must design offers that are attractive to at least one likely partner while avoiding offers so generous that both rivals accept and the proposer risks empowering the wrong counterparty. Over three short rounds, players have limited time to learn from behavior. Lion moves first each round and is likely to test small, somewhat one-sided offers to see who appears eager for which color. Octopus is likely to be relatively conservative early, then more willing to accept mid-range offers once it infers being behind. Ram, with the strongest valuation for blue, has great potential gain from trades that bring in blue even when they appear almost value-neutral to others. Overall, we should expect: (1) early probing offers by lion that may be declined as too demanding; (2) at least one attempted trade between octopus and ram where ram sacrifices some red or green to acquire blue or improve mix; and (3) gradual convergence toward trades that move red away from octopus and blue toward ram, subject to miscoordination and occasional declined offers when perceived as unfair or too risky under incomplete information.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has symmetric holdings (10 of each chip) but asymmetric private values. Lion moves first and will likely probe the market with a small, value-favorable trade to test others' willingness to part with a color lion wants. Octopus moves second and, not knowing that others value blue more, may initially try to obtain more red, thinking of it as potentially useful or slightly attractive compared to blue. Ram, moving last, will already have observed two offers and can start forming beliefs: if others offer red and demand blue, ram infers blue is competitive; if others avoid offering blue, ram learns it is widely valued. All three are cautious; declined trades in this first round are likely if an offer appears too one-sided or unclear in benefit.

Going into round 1, the only executed trade so far has been between octopus and ram, where octopus gave up more blue than the red received. From ram's perspective, this confirmed that octopus is relatively willing to part with blue and may not value it as much; from octopus's perspective, the trade was likely seen as roughly balanced in the absence of precise knowledge. Lion has learned only from others' revealed willingness to trade: octopus demanded red and offered blue, suggesting octopus likes red or dislikes blue; ram accepted an offer that increased ram's blue, suggesting ram values blue. With these hints, lion can now target ram more effectively by offering red or green to extract blue, but will still protect against overpaying. Octopus, having completed a trade that seemed acceptable, may be somewhat more open to further blue-for-red or blue-for-green exchanges. Ram, now with extra blue and less red, will focus on high-return opportunities to gain even more blue or to undo any unfavorable red losses if cheap.

By round 2, several patterns are clearer to the players. Ram has consistently accepted opportunities to acquire blue at the cost of red or green, signaling a strong preference for blue. Octopus has been willing to trade away blue and even to accept small net losses in chip count if the composition looked better, indicating lower valuations across the board and a desire to rebalance or experiment. Lion has successfully traded at least once with ram to gain blue, reinforcing lion's belief that blue is generally desirable and tradable but contested. With only one proposal left for each player and no future rounds, strategic emphasis shifts toward immediate value gains rather than learning. Players now propose more directly favorable offers: lion looks for a final blue-acquisition opportunity; octopus may try one last time to improve its bundle even if modest; and ram seeks a final blue gain if the terms look favorable, though both lion and ram must also guard against being exploited in this last chance window.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game revolves around reallocating privately valued red and blue chips plus a common-value green chip. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it serves as a natural medium of exchange and focal reference point: no one wants to move too far away from 10 greens unless compensated in more valuable private chips. Buffalo values red highly ($0.90) and blue poorly ($0.10); Cat values blue modestly ($0.20) and red modestly ($0.40); Duck values blue very highly ($0.90) and red poorly ($0.40). Socially efficient trades would move red toward Buffalo and blue toward Duck, potentially away from Cat, with green facilitating side payments. However, players only see their own values, so they must infer others’ valuations from observed offers and acceptances. Incentives push Buffalo to seek red and shed blue, Duck to seek blue and shed red, and Cat to arbitrage between them if possible (e.g., trading somewhat undervalued red or somewhat undervalued blue for green or for the color that others overpay for). Uncertainty arises because each player does not know whether others value red or blue more, so early rounds are likely to feature small, exploratory offers that are nearly value-neutral from the sender’s perspective but informative about counterpart preferences. As players observe who accepts which trades, they can update beliefs and shift toward more targeted, value-creating proposals. Simultaneous, private acceptance with random tie-breaking makes offers to multiple potential partners risky: if more than one player finds an offer acceptable, the realized partner is random, so proposers will typically design offers that are appealing primarily to one likely counterparty. Over three short rounds, players must balance experimentation (to learn about values) with immediate value extraction, so the path of play plausibly includes a few modestly positive-sum trades that gradually concentrate red with Buffalo and blue with Duck, while keeping most green near its initial distribution.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone has symmetric chip holdings but unknown opponent values. Rational players often begin with conservative, low-stakes trades that are close to value-neutral according to their own private valuations, using green as a relatively safe payment token. Buffalo has a strong preference for red over blue and may probe others’ willingness to give up red or green. Cat, with balanced but moderate preferences, is likely to test small trades involving red and blue to see where demand lies. Duck, who values blue most, may tentatively try to acquire blue while shedding red. Because no one yet knows who values what, many early offers will be exploratory, and at least one initial proposal is likely to be declined as players feel out acceptable exchange rates.

By the start of round 1, players have seen who was willing to trade blue for green and blue for red. Duck’s quick acceptance of Cat’s green-for-blue trade and Duck’s own attempt to acquire blue in exchange for red signal that Duck values blue relatively highly and is willing to give up both green and red for it. Cat, by accepting Duck’s red-for-blue trade, indicates that blue is not extremely precious and that red has at least comparable value. Buffalo’s initial red-for-green offer was too aggressive and drew no takers, revealing that others are not eager to part with red at a 1:1 rate for green. Now, Buffalo may soften red acquisition terms or try to unload blue; Cat may try to arbitrage between Buffalo and Duck; Duck may double down on acquiring blue but must be careful not to sacrifice too much green or red relative to private values.

Entering round 2, patterns are clearer: Duck consistently accepts opportunities to gain blue, even when paying with green or red, strongly signaling a very high private value for blue. Buffalo has shown willingness to trade away multiple blue chips for a few red chips, implying low private value for blue and high value for red. Cat has repeatedly positioned in between, sometimes giving blue for red or green when terms look favorable. With only one round left, players pivot from learning to consolidation: Buffalo will try to pick up any remaining red at reasonable blue costs, Duck will grab additional blue if prices are acceptable, and Cat will seek final small gains by reshuffling blue and red while maintaining a comfortable green balance. Trades now are more targeted, often modest in size, and reflect updated beliefs about who values what.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips, with common-value green chips. Everyone starts with 10 of each color. Green is known by all to be worth $0.50 each to everyone. Red and blue values differ by player but are privately known: butterfly (B) values BLUE=$0.10, RED=$0.30; kangaroo (K) values BLUE=$0.20, RED=$0.70; squid (S) values BLUE=$0.80, RED=$0.40. Privately, each player sees that green is medium value and symmetric, red and blue have asymmetric value structures. Aggregating true values: red is intrinsically most valuable to K, blue is intrinsically most valuable to S, and B is generally lowest-value across colored chips. This structure creates natural trading directions: K tries to buy red and sell blue/green, S tries to buy blue and sell red/green, and B can profit by selling red/blue that are less valuable to them in exchange for green (safe value) or occasionally higher-valued colors from others. However, players do not know others’ valuations; they only infer from behavior and the stated public ranges that heterogeneous preferences are likely. Incentives: each player wants to convert low-value colors (to them) into higher-value colors without overpaying given their private valuations and uncertainty over others’ values. Because multiple recipients can accept and the realized partner is random, proposals that are attractive to more than one counterpart risk being ‘mis-matched’: you might intend to target the high-value holder but trade instead with the lower-value holder. Strategically, proposers must design offers that are: (1) profitable under their own values, (2) likely to be accepted by at least one other player, but (3) not so generous that both others always accept (unless the proposer is indifferent to which partner executes). As rounds progress, observed accept/reject behavior reveals partial information: systematic eagerness to trade away a color suggests low private value for that color; persistent attempts to buy a color reveal higher value. Players may shade early offers conservatively to learn, then adjust in later rounds as they infer who values which colors. Since each player has one proposal per round for three rounds, there is limited time but enough to make a few exploratory trades then refine. Uncertainty remains throughout because no explicit values are disclosed and because rejection can mean either that the offer is unprofitable or that the responder is strategically waiting for better terms. The forecasted game below assumes boundedly rational but value-sensitive behavior: players propose trades that are mildly beneficial for counterparts (to induce acceptance) but more beneficial for themselves, with some experimentation in round 0 and more targeted bargaining once initial signals are observed.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players are effectively in the dark about others’ valuations and treat this as an exploration phase. They know green is commonly valued and safe, while red and blue likely differ, so they make conservative offers that are clearly profitable for themselves but plausibly acceptable to at least one partner. Rejections and acceptances here are especially informative: early willingness to buy or sell a color helps others infer who values what. Because chips are plentiful (10 of each) and no prior trades have occurred, risk from a single small exchange is low, favoring small-stakes probing offers rather than large aggressive trades.

After round 0, moderate information is revealed. Butterfly has shown willingness to trade away blue for green, hinting that blue is relatively low-value to them. Kangaroo accepted blue for green and traded blue for red, suggesting that they like red and are at least somewhat willing to hold blue. Squid willingly exchanged red for blue, signaling that they strongly prefer blue over red. Players update priors: K infers that S values blue highly; S infers that K values red; both infer B treats blue as relatively cheap. In round 1 they move to more targeted trades: K tries to accumulate more red from S on favorable terms; S tries to buy blue from B; B continues to move out of blue and slightly out of red to build up safe green or better colors. Offers become slightly more aggressive but still small in size to avoid large mispricing under remaining uncertainty.

By round 2, the pattern is clear: squid is a strong buyer of blue and seller of red; kangaroo is a strong buyer of red and moderate seller of blue; butterfly is consistently moving out of blue toward green and occasionally trades red away if compensated. Players view this as the final chance to lock in advantageous reallocations. Uncertainty is reduced but not eliminated; each still lacks precise numerical values for others, but they have directional beliefs. Hence, proposers in this round make more pronounced but still safe trades, aiming for deals that look quite attractive from inferred counterpart preferences while preserving a clear gain under their own valuations. They also recognize that it is the last round: there is no value to signaling or holding back for better future terms. As a result, acceptance thresholds are lower for modestly profitable trades, and almost all reasonable, directionally correct offers get accepted.

strict_validation_errors: rounds[2].turns[2].sender_id does not hold enough `BLUE` chips to sell 3.
repair_notes: rounds[2].turns[2]: clipped sell quantity to sender inventory.
rounds[2].turns[2].responses[bat-pink-1751]: removed impossible acceptance.
rounds[2].turns[2]: downgraded accepted trade to decline because no feasible accepter remained.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features one common-value chip (GREEN at $0.50 to everyone) and two privately valued chip types (RED and BLUE). Each player starts with 10 of each chip, but values differ: bat strongly prefers BLUE (0.60) over GREEN (0.50) and dislikes RED (0.20); fox strongly prefers BLUE (1.00) and moderately likes RED (0.60); frog strongly prefers RED (0.70) and dislikes BLUE (0.30). From an outside observer’s perspective, substantial gains from trade are possible by moving BLUE chips toward fox and away from frog, and moving RED chips toward frog and away from bat. GREEN chips are neutral and can be used as a safe medium of exchange because everyone values them equally. However, each player only knows their own values, so they must infer others’ preferences from which colors they try to buy or sell and what they accept. Early proposals will tend to be conservative, using GREEN as currency, or offering colors the proposer likely values relatively less (e.g., bat selling RED, frog selling BLUE). Over time, trades are likely to push holdings toward more efficient allocations, but because acceptances are simultaneous and random tie-breaking may allocate to an unintended partner, players will sometimes shade offers to ensure at least one counterpart sees a private gain. Risk of overpaying (offering too good a deal) is weighed against the risk of not trading at all. With only three rounds and fixed proposer order (bat, then fox, then frog each round), early moves will be exploratory and later moves will exploit inferred patterns, focusing especially on RED-for-BLUE or RED/BLUE-for-GREEN trades that yield mutual gains given the underlying valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, no one has observed any behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ values are largely uninformed. Players will tend to offer trades that are personally profitable given a wide range of possible opponent values. Bat is likely to offload disfavored RED to acquire GREEN (neutral) or BLUE (favored). Fox, who loves BLUE, may already experiment with acquiring BLUE using GREEN, which is safe to give up. Frog, who strongly prefers RED and dislikes BLUE, will likely try to trade away BLUE for RED or GREEN. All players know GREEN is common-value, so using GREEN as a medium of exchange is relatively safe and low-risk; they can be more aggressive with GREEN offers than with RED/BLUE until they learn others’ priorities.

After round 0, the history reveals patterns: bat willingly sold RED for GREEN and later accepted GREEN-for-BLUE, signaling that bat seems to dislike RED and value BLUE at least as much as GREEN. Fox showed a strong willingness to pay GREEN for BLUE, reinforcing that fox highly values BLUE and is comfortable parting with GREEN. Frog paid RED for GREEN and then offered BLUE for RED, revealing that frog strongly values RED and dislikes BLUE. With these signals, players can refine strategies: fox will keep hunting for BLUE but must be careful not to overpay now that others likely know fox’s preference; frog will try to buy more RED using BLUE or possibly GREEN; bat, who now holds extra BLUE and some RED again, can try to sell RED to frog or GREEN to fox at favorable terms. Round 1 trades will be more targeted, pushing chips toward their high-value holders while maintaining personal profit margins.

By round 2, everyone has strong evidence about relative preferences: fox has consistently tried to buy BLUE and is willing to give up GREEN and even RED; frog repeatedly buys RED and sells BLUE; bat tends to sell RED and accept reasonable terms for BLUE. The remaining opportunities for profit come from marginally improving each player’s portfolio before the game ends. Because this is the final round and each player has one last proposal, they might offer slightly more generous trades to ensure acceptance, especially when exchanging strongly mismatched preferences like frog’s BLUE for bat’s RED, or fox’s RED or GREEN for additional BLUE. However, players still avoid trades that would reduce their own total value, so they calibrate quantities to leave themselves a positive gain in expectation, even if multiple counterparts might accept. Final trades tend to be more aggressive and focused, with few purely exploratory moves and more direct attempts to shift remaining low-value chips to someone who values them more.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with 10 chips of each color and linear private values. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Dog and Turtle have identical values: RED=$0.50, BLUE=$1.00, GREEN=$0.50. Parrot values RED=$0.60, BLUE=$0.30, GREEN=$0.50. From an analyst’s perspective, Dog and Turtle want to trade away RED (low) for BLUE (high), and Parrot wants to trade away BLUE (low) in exchange for RED (high). GREEN is neutral at $0.50 to everyone, so it is naturally used as a low-risk medium of exchange when players are unsure of others’ values. However, each player knows only their own values, so early offers are exploratory and based on intuitive fairness (e.g., 1-for-1 swaps or slightly tilted rates) rather than full surplus-maximizing trades. Dog and Turtle suspect that at least one counterpart might value BLUE less or RED more, but they do not know which one, and any time more than one partner is willing to accept, the executed partner is random. This uncertainty pushes them to design offers that are attractive to at least one other player but not so generous that they risk overpaying. Parrot, valuing RED the most, is specifically motivated to acquire RED even at the cost of BLUE, but without knowing that others also like BLUE more than RED, Parrot’s earliest offers will likely be relatively modest. Across three rounds, there is limited horizon: players may wait for better terms, but they also face the risk that no highly favorable trade will materialize before the game ends. The expected pattern is: Round 0 features cautious, near-1:1 or slightly imbalanced trades using GREEN or moderate RED↔BLUE swaps; by Rounds 1–2, players infer (from acceptances/declines) that BLUE is in demand from at least two players and that Parrot is willing to pay for RED, so offers become more targeted and slightly more exploitative (e.g., charging a mild premium for high-value BLUE). Because Dog and Turtle share the same values, they often end up competing as suppliers of BLUE or demanders of GREEN/RED, with the random tie-breaking among multiple acceptances dampening their ability to coordinate; Parrot’s distinctive value profile makes Parrot the natural counterparty in the most surplus-enhancing trades, especially trades where Parrot gives up BLUE to receive RED.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has yet observed others’ valuation patterns. Players have symmetric starting inventories and only know that values lie in broad public ranges. They will tend to start with conservative, small-sized trades that look appealing under a wide range of possible opponent values: exchanging a few low-salience chips for GREEN or making relatively even-looking RED↔BLUE trades. Dog and Turtle, whose internal priorities favor BLUE, may initially try to obtain a bit more BLUE without offering extreme rates, while Parrot will gingerly seek more RED. All three are still mapping out whether anyone is willing to trade GREEN, and who is responsive to RED vs. BLUE offers. This round serves primarily as information-gathering with modest value gains.

By the start of Round 1, one trade has executed: Parrot traded 2 BLUE to Turtle for 2 RED. Publicly, everyone now knows Parrot was willing to give up BLUE for RED at a 1:1 rate, and Turtle accepted BLUE for RED at that same rate. This strongly suggests, from Dog’s and Turtle’s perspective, that Parrot values RED at least as much as BLUE, and from Parrot’s perspective, that at least one other player is comfortable receiving BLUE for RED. Dog has failed to move GREEN for BLUE, revealing that neither partner is desperate to obtain GREEN at a disadvantageous rate. This information encourages players to propose more value-directed trades: Dog and Turtle now suspect BLUE is attractive to at least one partner and may try to sell BLUE at a premium or acquire it more cheaply, while Parrot is more willing to pay with BLUE for additional RED, but will still anchor offers near apparent fair 1:1 rates to ensure acceptance.

By Round 2, multiple executed trades have shown a consistent pattern: Parrot repeatedly trades away BLUE to gain RED at roughly 1:1, while Turtle has twice taken BLUE in exchange for RED and Dog has once done the same. Publicly, everyone now sees that BLUE is widely acceptable and that Parrot, in particular, is keen to accumulate RED. Dog and Turtle are increasingly aware that their own values (high BLUE, lower RED) align with each other and are somewhat opposed to Parrot’s (higher RED, lower BLUE). However, the game is ending this round with only one more proposal per player. With limited time, each proposer is motivated to seek a final, modestly favorable trade that is still attractive enough to be accepted almost for sure. This leads to offers that nudge the exchange rate slightly in the sender’s favor but not so far from 1:1 that they are rejected; some still fail if they push too much, while trades that are close to previously revealed acceptable terms tend to go through.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start symmetric in chip holdings (10 of each color) and nearly symmetric in values, except unicorn-blue-7096 values red chips more ($0.50 vs. $0.30 for the others). Green is common value at $0.50 and thus strictly more valuable than blue ($0.10) for everyone, and at least as valuable as red for all; for badger and peacock, green dominates red, while for unicorn, green and red are tied. Theoretically, no mutually profitable trades in green should occur because everyone values green weakly more than the other colors. Profitable trade is most likely to center on red: unicorn is the high-value buyer of red, while badger and peacock may be willing to give up some red for bundles of blue if they believe others undervalue blue or if they misestimate unicorn’s valuation. However, with incomplete information, each only knows their own values and the public range, so they cannot be sure how others rank red vs. blue. The simultaneous response rule and random recipient selection if multiple accept create strategic uncertainty: a proposer cannot target a specific partner and must choose terms that are attractive enough to at least one other player yet not so generous that they overpay. Given the short, 3-round horizon and symmetric proposal rights, players are likely to make simple, one-sided, self-serving offers early, then adjust slightly as they observe what kinds of trades are accepted or rejected. Because green is strictly valuable and publicly so, the most realistic bargaining involves offering blue (low value) to buy red (potentially high value) from others. Even then, tension remains: unicorn wants to buy red cheaply, while badger and peacock would only sell if they either underestimate their own red value, misinfer others’ preferences, or are induced by a large enough blue transfer. The likely outcome is sparse trade with at most a modest transfer of red from badger or peacock to unicorn, and many proposals—especially those trying to acquire green—being declined.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have 10 of each chip. No trade history exists, so beliefs are driven mainly by the public ranges and each player’s own values. A rational pattern is that no one should trade away green, and unicorn might try to acquire red cheaply. Badger and peacock, with equal valuations, may test the waters by proposing trades that try to pick up greens or extra reds using blue, assuming some others may undervalue those colors. The first round thus serves as an information-gathering phase: players observe which directions of trade (e.g., green-for-blue, red-for-blue) are acceptable and begin to infer how others rank the colors.

After round 0, a key piece of information is revealed: badger accepted unicorn’s offer of blue for red, signaling that badger is relatively willing to trade away red for enough blue. Peacock declined both earlier offers, including buying red with blue, suggesting more caution or a stronger subjective valuation of red. Unicorn now holds more red and less blue and has learned that at least one counterpart is willing to sell red at a modest blue premium. Players still see no one is willing to part with green, reinforcing green’s status as a quasi-untouchable asset. In this round, unicorn is unlikely to offer red away cheaply after just buying it, while badger might attempt to leverage extra blue to buy back either green or red, and peacock may try a more moderate red-for-blue trade having seen that badger was willing to accept a similar structure.

By the final round, trade history shows that only red-for-blue trades have been accepted, always with blue flowing to badger in return for red. No one has ever given up green, and unicorn has successfully purchased some red while refusing to sell it back, revealing a strong private value for red. Badger has repeatedly shown willingness to exchange red for sufficient blue, while peacock has made a single modest purchase of red with blue. Given the finality of this round, each proposer now has one last chance to adjust their portfolio: unicorn still wants red but has less blue to pay with; badger may seek to offload remaining red if paid generously in blue; and peacock may either try one more small red purchase or simply abstain from risky trades. Uncertainty remains about exact valuations, but players now have directional signals from revealed behavior, making last proposals more targeted yet still constrained by the need for at least one counterpart to find them privately profitable.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip bargaining game features privately known values for red and blue chips and a common, publicly known value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color, and their goal is to maximize their own monetary value. Since green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone and this is common knowledge, green serves as a natural "currency" or benchmark for exchanging red and blue chips whose values vary across players.

Dog and Owl both value blue chips highly ($1.00) and red moderately ($0.80 for Dog, $0.30 for Owl). Mouse values blue moderately ($0.60) and red very low ($0.20). This makes Mouse a natural seller of red chips and a relatively weaker competitor for blue chips. Dog sees blue as more valuable than red, so Dog is willing to trade away some red (and possibly green) to acquire blue from Mouse. Owl shares Dog's high value on blue but values red much less, so Owl is a natural buyer of blue and seller of red, but Owl does not know others’ exact values.

Because players know only their own valuations and the public green value, they are uncertain about how much others value red vs. blue. They can infer some information from which offers are proposed and which are accepted. However, this game is short—only three rounds with one turn each per round—so there is limited time to learn. Strategic uncertainty also arises because multiple players can accept a trade, and the partner is then chosen randomly; proposers cannot target a specific responder, which discourages offers that are just barely favorable for one specific partner but not robustly good for others.

We should expect early rounds to feature relatively conservative, self-favorable offers using green as a medium, probing others’ willingness to trade red or blue. Dog, as the first mover each round and a strong blue lover, will likely try to convert red or possibly green into blue at an advantageous rate. Mouse may attempt to sell red above Mouse's private valuation, often for green or blue, while being cautious about giving away blue too cheaply. Owl may test the market to either accumulate blue or offload low-valued red for green or blue. Over the three rounds, some trades that clearly increase at least one responder’s payoff relative to holding greens (and do not look obviously terrible for their unknown valuations) are likely to be accepted, while skewed or ambiguous offers are likely to be declined.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color. No one has observed any previous behavior, so beliefs about others’ valuations are broad. Dog moves first and, valuing blue at $1.00 and red at $0.80, is inclined to start by using relatively less-preferred red to try to acquire either blue or green. Since green is common-value at $0.50, Dog can safely think that modest red-for-green offers might attract interest. Mouse, moving second, knows red is personally weak ($0.20) and will likely test selling red for green or blue at favorable rates, while keeping blue if possible. Owl, moving third, likes blue a lot ($1.00) and red significantly less ($0.30), and may be willing to swap red for green or blue if an attractive offer appears. In this opening round, all players are mostly probing: they offer strongly self-favorable terms, expecting low acceptance rates but hoping to learn something if a trade is taken.

At the start of round 1, Dog still has the original 10 of each color because Dog’s first offer was declined. Mouse has traded away some red to Owl in exchange for blue, confirming to Mouse that Owl is at least somewhat willing to give up red for blue and likely values blue highly. Owl now holds extra red and fewer blue chips, revealing that Owl accepted a deal favorable in terms of Owl’s private values (cheap red for valuable blue). Observers can infer that Owl either values blue strongly or red weakly, or both. In this round, Dog may cautiously adjust offers, recognizing that someone out there seems to like blue less than Dog does (since Mouse got blue). Mouse, having successfully sold low-valued red, may now aim to either lock in gains or slightly rebalance by selling or buying at milder terms. Owl, having just paid blue for red, may be hesitant to further reduce blue holdings unless it is clearly profitable. The bargaining remains tentative, but players now have a bit of evidence about blue’s relative scarcity and desirability around the table.

By round 2, the final round, some patterns are visible. Mouse has repeatedly used red as a selling chip and has been willing to accept trades that improve on Mouse’s low $0.20 valuation of red. Owl has once traded away blue for red and once traded red for green, hinting that Owl sees red as weak and blue as strong but is flexible if green is offered at good rates. Dog has successfully obtained blue by trading red with Mouse, confirming that Mouse’s private value for blue is likely lower than Dog’s. Because this is the last round, there is no future learning benefit; each proposal is evaluated purely on immediate value. Players are more willing to accept any trade that yields a clear value gain given their own valuations, even if it slightly helps a rival, since there is no tomorrow. The remaining offers tend to be more balanced or slightly favorable but not extreme, making acceptance more likely when they clearly beat holding the status quo portfolio.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features common-value green chips and privately valued red and blue chips. Each player starts with an equal quantity of each chip color (10 each) but has different dollar values for red and blue, which creates gains from trade. All players know the common value of green (always $0.50), and they know only the public range for red and blue values, plus their own exact values. Thus, they are uncertain about others’ exact valuations but can infer that other players may value some colors differently based on observed behavior.

Given the priors: alligator-orange-6315 values RED=$0.60, BLUE=$0.50, GREEN=$0.50; buffalo-purple-4069 values RED=$0.50, BLUE=$0.20, GREEN=$0.50; rabbit-yellow-6681 values RED=$1.00, BLUE=$0.30, GREEN=$0.50. This implies: (1) rabbit has an especially high marginal value for red and is willing to trade away blue or even green to acquire red as long as the trade is favorable; (2) buffalo strongly dislikes blue relative to red/green and is a natural seller of blue and buyer of red/green, but must be careful not to give away too much surplus; (3) alligator slightly prefers red to blue/green and is also a likely buyer of red while being more neutral between blue and green.

Strategically, proposers must make offers good enough that at least one other player expects to gain, but not so generous that the proposer gives away most of the surplus. Because responses are simultaneous and multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, proposers cannot target a specific partner: they must design offers that they are comfortable executing with any responder. Early in the game (round 0), players are still learning about one another’s preferences, so initial offers may be more exploratory and might be rejected if perceived as too one-sided or unclear in benefit to the responders. As history unfolds, players update beliefs about others’ valuations based on which trades are proposed and accepted, shifting toward more targeted and efficient trades—especially directing red toward rabbit and away from buffalo’s low-valued blue. Since there are only three rounds and each player proposes once per round, proposers face a limited window to realize gains from trade and may become more aggressive or more concessionary in later rounds depending on what has already been traded and remaining chip inventories.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players still have their initial endowments (10 of each color) and only know their own red/blue valuations. Proposals are likely to be relatively simple and symmetric in quantities, often using green (the common-value chip) as a safe medium or using small numbers of red/blue to probe others’ willingness to trade. Players will be tentative about giving up their high-value chips without clear compensation, so early offers perceived as lopsided are likely to be declined. Alligator, moving first, has a mild preference for red over blue/green and may try to cheaply acquire green or test whether someone is willing to give up red for green. Buffalo, moving second, wants to unload low-value blue and gain red/green. Rabbit, moving last, highly values red and may start signaling a willingness to pay with blue or green to attract red trades in future rounds.

At the start of round 1, the only executed trade so far is buffalo’s sale of 3 BLUE for 2 RED to rabbit. Holdings are now: alligator (10R,10B,10G), buffalo (12R,7B,10G), rabbit (8R,13B,10G). Observers can infer that buffalo was eager to offload blue and that rabbit was keen to acquire red, hinting that buffalo’s blue value is low and rabbit’s red value is high. Alligator saw rabbit accept a trade that is roughly value-neutral for alligator’s own preferences, suggesting rabbit’s underlying valuation of red might exceed alligator’s. In this round, alligator may adjust by trying either to buy some red cautiously or to exploit others’ apparent willingness to trade blue. Buffalo, now with more red, may try to transform some red into green or further reduce blue holdings. Rabbit, having revealed interest in red, might face more expensive offers but still seeks red if gains remain, or may pivot to trades that exploit buffalo’s low blue valuation by offering red at a premium.

Entering round 2, executed trades have shifted holdings and revealed patterns: buffalo has twice sold blue, both times to players who seem comfortable holding blue, and both buffalo and rabbit have repeatedly engaged in red-for-blue trades favorable to rabbit. Holdings now are: alligator (10R,9B,12G), buffalo (14R,4B,10G), rabbit (6R,17B,10G). The revealed behavior suggests buffalo’s blue valuation is indeed low, and rabbit’s red valuation is very high. Alligator now has extra green and slightly fewer blue chips, buffalo is red-rich and blue-poor, and rabbit is very blue-heavy and red-poor.

This final round is the last chance to adjust portfolios. Proposers will consider that any remaining trade must yield a clear private gain because there is no future signaling benefit. Alligator, with surplus green, may seek red or blue if profitable; buffalo may try to dump remaining blue or sell a bit of red if the price is attractive; rabbit may still seek red but must pay more, as others know rabbit values red strongly. With limited turns left (one proposal each), players are likely to propose more one-sided trades hoping to capture surplus quickly, but such offers are often rejected if they leave the responder worse off. Given the now-clear patterns, trades that continue the buffalo–rabbit red/blue exchange at slightly more aggressive terms are plausible, while marginal or exploratory offers that do not clearly help the responder are likely to be declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip bargaining game features incomplete information about private chip values for red, blue, and purple, with green as a known common-value chip at $0.50. Each player starts with an equal endowment of all colors, but their private values create strong comparative advantages: Badger values BLUE (0.60) and RED (0.40) more than PURPLE (0.10); Cat strongly values PURPLE (0.90) and somewhat BLUE (0.50) while disliking RED (0.30); Dog highly values RED (0.80) and moderately values PURPLE and BLUE (0.40). Because they only know their own valuations, each player must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and responses. Efficient trades would move PURPLE chips toward Cat, RED chips toward Dog, and BLUE chips toward Badger, while potentially using the common GREEN as a relatively safe medium of exchange early on. However, strategic uncertainty and the random resolution when multiple players accept create risk: proposers cannot target a specific partner when more than one gains from the trade, so they may design offers that are attractive enough to at least one player but not so obviously beneficial to multiple players. Over three rounds, Badger consistently has the first-mover role, which incentivizes her to set initial price anchors and probe others’ preferences, often using GREEN or low-value-to-her PURPLE to acquire higher-valued colors. Cat is likely to try to accumulate PURPLE and shed some RED, while Dog is inclined to accumulate RED and trade away PURPLE or BLUE if the rate is favorable. Early moves are exploratory, with modestly unbalanced but not extreme exchange rates; later moves adjust based on revealed behavior—who accepted or rejected which terms—and on updated beliefs about others’ valuations. Since each player maximizes only their own payoff, coalition-like patterns (e.g., repeated mutually beneficial trades between a particular pair) may arise but remain contingent on immediate gains and perceived bargaining power rather than explicit coordination.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players still only know their own chip values and the common value of GREEN. The likely focus is on exploratory offers that use GREEN as a relatively transparent benchmark and that slightly favor the proposer while still leaving obvious gains for at least one responder. Badger, as the first mover, may test demand for GREEN or PURPLE relative to RED or BLUE; Cat then reacts by probing the market for PURPLE and shedding some low-valued RED, while Dog, moving last, can already condition on two turns of observed behavior to refine his inferences and aim for favorable RED-centric trades. Offers are expected to be cautious, with modest quantities and conservative exchange rates that allow players to gather information without risking large losses.

By round 1, several informative trades have occurred. Cat has revealed strong willingness to buy PURPLE and to part with RED; Dog has revealed a willingness to give up RED for PURPLE, suggesting he values RED but is not completely averse to losing a few units at favorable rates; Badger has signaled interest in acquiring BLUE and GREEN while using low-valued-to-her PURPLE as a bargaining chip. Each player now has updated beliefs: they can guess that Cat values PURPLE highly, Dog places significant value on RED, and Badger is relatively PURPLE-poor in valuation. With this information, more targeted proposals emerge: Badger can aim to offload remaining PURPLE or surplus BLUE for GREEN or RED; Cat can structure offers that trade away disliked RED or possibly some BLUE in order to accumulate even more PURPLE, while being careful not to overpay if she now suspects others understand her PURPLE preference; Dog can attempt to acquire additional RED from Badger or Cat, using BLUE or PURPLE as payment at exchange rates that still benefit him privately. Despite better information, offers remain modest in size to manage risk from the random partner selection in cases where multiple responders would benefit.

In the final round, players know this is their last chance to improve payoffs, so they may accept somewhat less favorable but still positive-value trades and make bolder proposals that more aggressively exploit perceived comparative advantages. Cat is now likely heavily invested in PURPLE and still relatively unconcerned about ending with less RED, while Dog aims to finish with a strong RED position and is willing to shed PURPLE or possibly BLUE. Badger, having accumulated value in BLUE and GREEN and traded away low-valued PURPLE, may now look for opportunistic final trades that marginally increase her total value, especially by exchanging any remaining PURPLE or excess GREEN for BLUE or RED, or by offering attractive bundles to either Cat or Dog that just barely leave them better off. Information is now relatively rich, but each move is constrained by current holdings and the one-turn-per-player limit, so players prioritize high-leverage trades in small quantities that exploit their clearest edge without overexposing themselves to the randomness of multi-acceptor outcomes.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game features privately valued red, blue, and purple chips plus a common-value green chip. All players start with 10 of each color, and they know only their own valuations. Buffalo strongly prefers blue and purple over red, Goose strongly prefers red and purple over blue, and Bear has uniformly low values except for green, which is equally valuable to all. From an analyst perspective, large potential gains come from moving red chips toward Goose and blue chips toward Buffalo, often mediated by green or purple as a common currency. However, players do not know each other’s exact values and must infer them from observed offers and acceptances. Early in the game, proposals will likely be conservative, probing what others might value while trying to secure clearly positive expected value given worst-case opponent valuations. Because multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient, proposers cannot fully target partners, which weakens their ability to exploit specific bilateral gains and encourages them to offer trades that could plausibly benefit more than one counterpart. Over the three rounds, we should see Buffalo try to convert red into blue or purple or even green; Goose try to acquire red while shedding blue or sometimes green; and Bear, with weaker private values, act opportunistically, accepting trades that slightly improve value in obvious ways (especially involving green) even if the gains are modest. Strategic uncertainty centers on unknown valuations, risk of being undercut by a more eager acceptor, and the limited horizon of just three rounds, which discourages overly patient waiting for perfect trades and leads to moderately generous but not extreme offers.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no direct evidence of others’ valuations. Rational, risk-averse behavior implies that proposers will craft trades that are clearly profitable for themselves given their own values while leaving enough potential surplus that at least one counterpart might consider the deal plausible. Expect offers to use green as a neutral currency and to move obviously low-value chips away from their holders in exchange for possibly higher-value ones. Responses will be cautious; players are unlikely to accept trades that reduce their payoff under any plausible belief about opponents’ values.

By the second round, some valuation hints have emerged: Goose’s early willingness to trade away blue for red suggests red is relatively valuable to Goose, while Bear’s acceptance of blue-for-red and green-for-blue trades suggests Bear sees modest, similar value in those colors. Buffalo has not yet traded and remains opaque. Proposers now adjust: Goose will likely keep trying to acquire red, Buffalo will probe for blue or purple at favorable rates, and Bear may aim to pick up small surpluses by offering slightly favorable terms that others still perceive as acceptable. The limited horizon begins to matter, nudging players to be more willing to accept mildly positive trades rather than hold out.

In the final round, all players know this is their last chance to improve payoffs. Goose has gathered more red at the expense of blue and some green; Bear has modestly reshaped holdings but remains low value overall; Buffalo has not yet completed a trade and faces strong incentives to secure at least one profitable exchange, particularly to gain blue or purple for red. With no future rounds, there is no reason to preserve chips for later bargaining, so proposers are more willing to offer generous terms that still yield them positive value, and responders are more inclined to accept any deal that gives a clear immediate gain. This often produces more imbalanced but mutually beneficial trades, especially moving remaining red toward Goose and blue toward Buffalo, provided holdings and earlier trades still allow such exchanges.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-player, 3-round bargaining game features common-value green chips and privately valued red, blue, and purple chips. All players value green at $0.50, but their valuations diverge on the other colors, generating gains from trade. Dog and Buffalo both value red and blue relatively highly (Dog: $0.70 red/blue, Buffalo: $0.60 red/blue), while Bear values them much less (red $0.30, blue $0.40, purple $0.40). Dog highly values purple ($0.60) while Buffalo nearly ignores it ($0.10), and Bear is middling on purple ($0.40). This structure creates clear surplus opportunities: Dog and Buffalo want to buy red/blue (and purple, for Dog), especially from Bear, who is comparatively low-value on those colors, in exchange for green or low-value colors from their own perspective. However, each player only knows their own valuations; they must infer others’ values from offers and acceptances across the short horizon of nine total proposal turns. Strategic uncertainty is high because: (1) Proposers cannot choose among multiple acceptors—accepting partners are randomly selected; (2) each proposal targets exactly one chip type to buy and one to sell, limiting flexibility; and (3) the game ends after three rounds, so there is pressure to secure profitable trades early without fully revealing one’s private values. Dog, moving first each round, is likely to be relatively aggressive early in trying to convert lower-valued green into higher-valued red/blue/purple while still being cautious not to overpay given the unknown valuations of Buffalo and Bear. Buffalo sits in the middle, with moderate-to-high values on red/blue and a very low value on purple; Buffalo will likely seek red/blue and be willing to part with purple or sometimes green, but will be selective in trades that reduce their green stock. Bear is relatively low on red/purple and moderate on blue; Bear can profitably sell red or purple to Dog/Buffalo in exchange for green or sometimes blue, but may hold out if unsure others value red or purple much more. Across rounds, we should see some early feeling-out offers that are declined or only marginally profitable, followed by more directed trades as players infer where others are willing to move. Because surplus is larger in trades where high-value holders buy from low-value holders (especially Dog/Buffalo buying red or purple from Bear), we expect the accepted trades to cluster around those channels once revealed by behavior, with later rounds reflecting updated beliefs about others’ willingness to trade green for red/blue/purple.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, players have symmetric chip counts but asymmetric, privately known values. No one yet knows the others’ valuations, so Round 0 is largely exploratory. Dog, who moves first, is motivated to turn common-value green into higher-valued colored chips, but must avoid making such generous offers that they tip off their strong preferences or invite random acceptance from the wrong partner. Buffalo wants red/blue more than purple and will hesitate to give up green without a clear edge. Bear is the most likely low-value seller of red and purple, but doesn’t know that others value them more, so may test by asking a bit too much initially. We should see at least one early overreaching offer that gets declined, followed by a first successful transaction that partially reveals who is willing to move which colors for green.

After Round 0, everyone has observed that Bear is willing to sell red for green and that Dog and Buffalo both refrained from accepting Dog’s initial red-for-green offer. This suggests to Bear that red is somewhat more valuable to others than it is to them, but also that neither rival is desperate enough to accept a neutral or slightly unfavorable exchange ratio. Buffalo now holds extra red and slightly fewer green chips, while Bear has more green and fewer red. Dog has unchanged holdings but has learned that Bear will trade red for green around a 1:1 or 1:1-ish ratio. In Round 1, Dog can exploit this by trying to buy red at a modest premium in green, or by attempting to convert purple (which Buffalo undervalues) into red or blue. Buffalo, now possessing more red, might try to offload surplus red to Dog or even to Bear for green or blue, while Bear will test whether they can leverage their increased green stack to pick up blue or purple on favorable terms. We expect at least one accepted trade involving Bear selling a lower-valued color for green or blue, and potentially a cautious but profitable trade between Dog and Buffalo around purple.

By Round 2, trade patterns are clearer: Bear has shown willingness to trade red for green and to acquire purple cheaply; Buffalo has signaled that purple is low-value and can be exchanged for green or red; Dog has revealed a relatively strong appetite for red and a willingness to part with purple. The remaining horizon is short, so players focus on clearly positive expected-value moves rather than subtle signaling. Dog, having already improved their portfolio toward higher-valued reds, may now try to adjust marginally by swapping remaining purple or even some green for blue or additional red if available. Buffalo, now holding fewer purple and some extra red, might attempt to convert any residual low-value chips into green or blue but will reject trades that give away too much value, especially with green. Bear, with sizeable green and some purple, is looking for final opportunities to buy blue cheaply or sell off any lingering red or purple at a gain. Because beliefs are somewhat refined and offers must clearly benefit both sides to be accepted, we expect one or two small, clearly profitable trades and at least one final declined proposal where a proposer slightly overreaches, misjudging the tight end-of-game thresholds.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but different private values for red, blue, and purple; green is common-value at $0.50. This creates classic three-player bargaining with hidden values and competing bilateral opportunities. Because everyone knows only their own values, each player must infer others’ preferences from what they propose and what they accept. Strategically, each player wants to trade away colors they value relatively less in exchange for colors they value more, but they must propose deals that are attractive enough for at least one counterpart (given unknown valuations) while still improving their own payoff. Peacock-red-7894 values blue and purple at $0.80, red at $0.70, green at $0.50, so they prefer accumulating blue/purple and are relatively happy to give green, somewhat willing to give red, and least happy to give blue/purple. Squid-pink-8454 values purple highest at $1.00, then red at $0.60, and blue at only $0.50 (equal to green), so they are strongly motivated to buy purple and may be willing to trade away blue and possibly green. Otter-orange-7286 values blue highest at $0.90, then purple at $0.70, with red lowest at $0.50 (equal to green), so they want to accumulate blue and possibly purple while using red/green as funding. Efficient trades, from an analyst’s perspective, shift blue toward otter, purple toward squid (and somewhat peacock), and red toward peacock/squid, while green is neutral. But because players don’t know each other’s values, early-round offers are likely exploratory and somewhat conservative, sometimes mixing green (known to be low for everyone) with other colors to test relative valuations without risking large value transfers. Simultaneous responses with random selection among multiple accepters mean each proposer cannot target a specific partner ex ante; instead, they must craft offers that at least one other player finds strictly beneficial. Over three rounds, players update their beliefs from observed acceptances/declines, but with only nine total proposal turns, there is limited time for full learning, so some trades will remain mildly inefficient from the omniscient analyst’s point of view.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has observed any prior behavior, so all players act under maximal uncertainty about others’ private values. Proposals are likely to be simple and low-stakes, often anchoring on green (whose common value is known) in combination with one other color to probe whether someone especially likes or dislikes that color. Each proposer wants to secure an early gain without overpaying, and responders are cautious about accepting offers that might reveal they prize a color too highly. The key strategic tension is between exploration (learning about others’ preferences) and immediate exploitation (locking in small, safe gains).

After round 0, some structure has emerged. Otter’s acceptance of green-for-blue signaled that otter values blue more than green, while squid’s refusal of that same offer hinted that squid does not especially prize blue. Squid’s blue-for-purple trade being accepted by peacock reveals that both squid and peacock like purple more than blue, and that peacock is at least moderately willing to hold purple. Otter’s failed attempt to buy purple for red shows that, at that price, neither peacock nor squid is eager to give up purple for red. In this second round, each proposer adjusts: peacock should lean into selling green or possibly red to the two others in exchange for blue or purple; squid should try to acquire more purple by offering blue/green, possibly sweetening terms; otter must recognize that red is not a very attractive asset to others and may need to use green or offer better ratios to access more purple or even blue.

By the final round, players have clearer signals: everyone has seen that green is frequently used as a funding chip and that purple tends to be in high demand, while blue is especially attractive to otter and somewhat attractive to peacock. Peaock has repeatedly used green to buy higher-value colors; squid has bid aggressively for purple; otter’s attempts to buy using red or green have often failed unless the price strongly favors the seller. With only three proposal turns left, players are more willing to seek last small improvements rather than cautious learning. Proposers now aim to squeeze incremental value out of any remaining mispriced holdings, while responders are opportunistic but choosier, rejecting marginal or symmetric trades that don’t clearly improve their final payoff.
