parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, multi-round bargaining game features privately valued red, blue, and purple chips plus a common-value green chip. All players start symmetric in chip holdings (10 of each color) but strongly asymmetric in how much they privately value each color. Cat highly values red (1.00), then blue (0.90), then purple (0.80), with green as a modest constant (0.50). Otter values red (0.50) more than any colored chip except green (0.50), but cares very little about blue (0.10) and purple (0.30). Badger values purple (0.40) slightly more than blue (0.20), and both more than red (0.10), while green is again 0.50. Because players only know their own values, each must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances over just three rounds. Public ranges for non-green chips are wide ($0.10–$1.00), creating substantial uncertainty: a chip that is nearly worthless to one player could be extremely valuable to another. Strategically, cat wants to accumulate red and blue while offloading surplus green or less-valued purple; otter wants red and perhaps green while happily dumping blue and sometimes purple; badger wants purple and green, and will be eager to trade away red, and possibly blue, for them. Each proposer must balance asking for chips they suspect are cheap to others with offering chips they are willing to give up, while being mindful that any acceptable offer might be taken by either non-sender if both accept and the recipient is randomly chosen. Because there are only three rounds with one proposal per player per round, dynamics are fast and path-dependent: early trades quickly reshape portfolios and reveal hints about value rankings, influencing later proposals. Players may experiment with relatively safe, mildly favorable exchanges at first and then adjust as they learn whose behavior signals a strong or weak valuation for specific colors. The random resolution among multiple acceptors also encourages proposers to design offers that are attractive primarily to their intended partner, reducing the risk that value leaks to an unintended counterpart. Given the incentives, we expect: (1) early trades where otter and badger shed low-value reds in exchange for more valued colors; (2) cat gradually trying to convert green and purple into red and blue; and (3) occasional speculative offers probing whether others over- or undervalue certain colors, with some proposals rejected as players hold out for better terms or remain uncertain about others’ valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold identical portfolios (10 of each color) but only know their own private valuations. There is no price history, so offers serve both as trades and as signals. Each proposer is likely to begin with conservative, slightly favorable trades, testing what others might accept. Cat, who values red and blue highly, will probe for ways to acquire them without paying too much in terms of her own high-value chips. Otter will want to offload blue and possibly purple in exchange for red or green. Badger will look for opportunities to trade away low-value red for purple or green. With no firm beliefs yet about others’ preferences, some offers will be too lopsided to attract acceptance, while others will succeed when they align with the counterparties’ incentives.

By round 1, two trades have already occurred. From round 0, everyone has seen that otter was willing to trade away a large amount of blue for red, suggesting blue is relatively low for otter and red relatively higher. They also saw badger eager to convert red into purple, indicating red is low and purple higher for badger. Cat’s initial attempt to exchange purple for green failed, hinting her initial terms for green were unattractive to the others. Armed with these signals, players can now propose more targeted deals: otter can press her advantage by trading blue for red and perhaps purple; badger can continue unloading low-value red; cat can attempt to acquire red or blue from counterparts who seem eager to part with them, likely by offering green or purple. However, the random matching among multiple acceptors and limited time horizon still create tension: offers must be profitable even if the “wrong” partner ends up matched, and players start to worry about over-trading away their key high-value chips.

Entering the final round, players have a clearer revealed-preference picture: otter aggressively trades away blue and red when she can gain in green or purple; badger repeatedly discards red for purple or green; cat is willing to part with green to obtain red and possibly blue. With only three turns left, players face a closing window to reshape their portfolios toward their private values. This typically makes them more willing to accept moderate gains and less likely to propose purely exploratory trades. Each sender now tries to lock in improvements that are clearly positive given what they’ve observed: cat seeks a last chance to turn green or purple into red or blue; otter tries to dump remaining blue or perhaps marginal reds; badger looks for any remaining opportunities to transform low-value red into purple or green. Some proposals still fail if they ask too much relative to what has already been revealed, while other offers succeed when they align cleanly with the counterparty’s now-evident valuations.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip-bargaining game features privately known values for red, blue, and purple chips, and a common known value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color, and the only way to increase payoff is to trade away chips they value less for chips they value more, subject to the uncertainty of others’ unknown valuations.

From an analyst’s perspective using the true values:
- camel-yellow-4406 (Camel) values all non-green chips at $0.50 except purple at $0.80. Camel is moderately balanced with a mild premium on purple.
- badger-purple-8825 (Badger) has uniformly low values for red, blue, and purple (0.20, 0.10, 0.10) and values green at $0.50, making Badger strongly green-loving and relatively indifferent (and low-value) toward other colors.
- otter-orange-6095 (Otter) values purple very highly at $0.90, and blue at $0.50, red at $0.40. Otter is strongly purple-loving with reasonable value for blue and modest for red.

Common-value greens at $0.50 are a safe baseline: players know everyone values green the same, so green-for-other-color trades must offer at least equal green value to be attractive. However, red/blue/purple values are private; each player initially has only the publicly known range ($0.10–$1.00) and must infer others’ preferences from observed behavior.

Strategic dynamics:
- Badger, if roughly rational, wants to convert red/blue/purple into green whenever possible, ideally at ratios that give more than $0.50 of low-value chips per green surrendered. However, Badger doesn’t know that others value purple much more; Badger just knows their own low valuations and suspects that at least some colors might be more important to others.
- Otter strongly wants purple and is willing to give up some red or perhaps green if they believe counterparties do not value purple much. However, Otter also worries that others might value purple too, so overly generous offers risk being exploited.
- Camel likes purple more than other non-green colors but is far less skewed than Otter. Camel can profit by moving from blue/red into purple, but will still demand reasonable compensation in any trade involving green.

Uncertainty and inference:
- Players cannot directly price the other players’ chips; they infer through what is offered and accepted. For example, if someone offers many purples cheaply, observers may infer that player has low purple value.
- Simultaneous accept decisions and random selection among multiple accepters add strategic risk: a player might accept a somewhat marginal deal to avoid missing out if another player also accepts, but could also end up getting a trade that’s not exactly tailored to them.

Bargaining trajectory over three rounds:
- Early in Round 0, proposals are likely exploratory, with conservative terms because players don’t yet know others’ preferences. Some trades may fail because offers are too one-sided or mistargeted.
- As trades occur, accepted ratios reveal partial information (e.g., someone trading away purple cheaply implies low purple valuation), allowing others to refine expectations and adjust offers in later rounds.
- Given only three rounds and one proposal each per round, there is limited time to fully learn others’ types. Players may remain cautious, using small quantities (1–3 chips) to experiment while preserving flexibility.

In this forecasted full game, we assume players behave in a boundedly rational but valuation-consistent way: they won’t knowingly accept negative-value trades from their own perspective, they tentatively shade offers in their favor, and they update qualitatively based on observed behavior. The realized path shows modest but not fully efficient gains: Badger manages to turn some low-value colored chips into green, Otter accumulates additional high-value purple at the cost of green and some red, and Camel opportunistically rebalances slightly toward purple while avoiding obvious losses. Several proposed trades are declined because they don’t offer enough surplus to the potential recipients or come at a time when inferred valuations make them unattractive.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players have symmetric endowments (10 of each color) but very asymmetric valuations that only they themselves know. No trades have occurred yet, so everyone faces maximum uncertainty about others’ preferences. Proposals this round are likely to be small and somewhat conservative, aiming to feel out whether others value certain colors more or less. Because greens are known to be worth $0.50 to everyone, trades involving green set a clear benchmark: rational players will resist giving up more than roughly equal value in their own terms. Red/blue/purple ratios are more speculative, and early offers may be rejected if they misjudge the other players’ private values.

By Round 1, one trade has occurred: Badger traded 5 red to Camel for 2 green. This signals that Badger is willing to pay quite a bit of red for green, suggesting Badger values green relatively more than red and is eager to convert. Camel’s willingness to accept only 2 green for 5 red implies Camel is not highly attached to red and is comfortable boosting green. Otter learned from this that Badger is green-seeking and that Camel is somewhat flexible on red. Players now adjust: Badger may continue to offer large bundles of low-value red/blue/purple for green, Camel may probe for more purple, and Otter, having had a purple-for-red offer refused, might shade offers to be slightly more generous or shift to using green as currency. However, uncertainty remains high, especially about Otter’s strong purple preference and Badger’s very low non-green valuations.

Heading into Round 2, more information has emerged. Camel has twice accepted trades that exchange red/blue for green or purple, showing a willingness to convert lower-valued chips into slightly higher-valued ones, especially purple and green. Badger has now repeatedly given up large amounts of red, blue, and purple in exchange for green, revealing a strong green preference and low valuation of other colors. Otter has just traded green for purple, signaling that purple is highly valued relative to green. Each player now has only three remaining proposal opportunities, so they aim to lock in final beneficial exchanges. Badger continues trying to buy green with surplus low-valued colors, Otter looks to top up on purple while not sacrificing too much of other valuable colors, and Camel seeks moderate improvements without taking on too much risk. Some offers are now finely tuned; rejections tend to occur when proposed ratios are close to indifference or worsened posteriors make the terms look exploitative.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game is driven by privately heterogeneous values for red, blue, and purple chips, with green as a common-value currency ($0.50 for everyone). Lion highly values red and blue (both $0.80) and dislikes purple ($0.10). Butterfly highly values purple ($0.80), moderately values blue ($0.40), and dislikes red ($0.20). Camel values blue relatively highly ($0.70) and sees red and purple as low value ($0.20 each). Because only own values are known to each player, they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptance behavior. The core surplus opportunities arise from moving chips from low-value holders to high-value holders: red and blue from butterfly and camel toward lion, purple from lion and camel toward butterfly, and low-value chips away from their current owners in exchange for green or more valued colors. Strategic uncertainty comes from: (1) incomplete information about others’ valuations, so players must guess which chips others will trade cheaply; (2) simultaneous acceptance where multiple acceptors create random matching risk, so players must design offers attractive but not so generous that multiple players compete; and (3) a finite three-round horizon, which incentivizes more aggressive, exploitative moves in early rounds and more concessionary, deal-closing behavior in the last round. Efficient patterns tend to feature lion trying to sell purple to butterfly or camel while buying red/blue, butterfly trying to offload red in exchange for purple/green, and camel trying to trade red and purple for blue/green. The forecast below assumes moderately strategic but risk-averse players, who seek strictly positive expected value on each trade given their beliefs, gradually update from early rejections/acceptances, and become more willing to accept small gains by round 2 before the game ends.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip type and know only their own payoffs. They will likely begin with conservative, simple trades that use green (common value) as a safe medium or probe others’ valuations of a single color. No one yet has clear information about who values which chip, so early proposals serve as both value extraction attempts and signals. Lion is inclined to test the market for purple (which they dislike) or to cautiously seek extra red/blue. Butterfly is likely to experiment with improving her red holdings only if she can do so cheaply, or to offer red if she guesses it is valued more by others. Camel will want to move low-value red or purple for blue/green but with little knowledge of others’ preferences. Rejections are common in this first round as players calibrate what offers are acceptable.

By round 1, players have a bit more information. Lion’s failed attempt to trade away purple for green revealed that at least at that price, others did not strongly want purple versus sure $0.50 green. Butterfly’s accepted trade where she swapped low-value red for green at a 2:1 rate suggests to observers that butterfly is happy to offload red cheaply, while camel is willing to pay green for red—hinting that camel might believe someone else values red more than $0.50 per chip. Camel’s declined purple-for-blue offer indicates that neither lion nor butterfly finds a 2:1 purple-for-blue trade appealing, hinting that both may value blue somewhat highly or purple relatively low. With this updated picture, lion will lean more heavily into acquiring red/blue from butterfly or camel if the terms are favorable, butterfly will try to offload more red or perhaps pick up purple if she learns it is undervalued, and camel will refine offers that secure blue on better terms while still shedding low-value chips.

In the final round, the game’s finite horizon becomes salient: this is each player’s last chance to improve their portfolio. The earlier accepted trades revealed patterns: butterfly is consistently willing to part with red; lion is willing to spend green to buy red; camel is willing to exchange red for green and for purple, and has shown interest in blue but faced resistance. Players now know that lion seems to value red highly, butterfly values purple more than red, and camel values blue but not purple or red much. Strategic behavior now shifts toward closing clearly profitable trades, even if small, since there is no future bargaining to preserve chips for. Risk aversion to random matching (multiple acceptances) encourages somewhat targeted offers that one specific counterpart is likely to accept. Lion will focus on acquiring high-value red/blue from players who see them as weak, butterfly will try to convert any leftover red into purple/green, and camel will seek a last improvement by upgrading red/purple into blue/green if terms allow.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip-bargaining game has privately known values for red, blue, and purple chips, with green chips common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Each player starts symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color) but asymmetric in values, so gains come from trading low-value chips away for high-value chips. However, players only know their own values and must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances. This generates both bargaining power and uncertainty: a player who can signal strong demand for a color risks revealing private value, while a player who appears to treat all chips similarly may be hiding a steep value difference.

Incentives by player, given their own private values:
- squid-orange-6967: BLUE and GREEN are high ($0.50); PURPLE and RED are low ($0.10). Squid wants to trade away red/purple to acquire blue/green, but must appear selective enough to avoid overpaying for chips that might be high value to others.
- camel-blue-7414: BLUE=$0.60, PURPLE=$0.50, GREEN=$0.50, RED=$0.40. Camel values all colors moderately, with blue slightly highest and red lowest but still significant. Camel is flexible, willing to do many trades that create small private gains, and can often profit by intermediary-style deals that move chips from very high-value owners to lower-value owners for a margin.
- lion-green-7491: BLUE=$0.90, RED=$0.90, PURPLE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50. Lion has very strong preferences for blue and red, moderate for purple, and baseline for green. Lion’s best gains come from buying blue and red and selling green (and occasionally purple) when possible.

Publicly, everyone knows green’s value is the same for all. This makes green an efficient medium of exchange: trading green for a color you value more is profitable only if the counterparty values that color less than green. However, since valuations are private, players initially treat green conservatively. Over only three rounds (nine proposal turns total), proposals will likely be relatively simple and myopic: single-color-for-single-color trades with small quantities that clearly improve the proposer’s own payoff under plausible beliefs about others’ values.

Because acceptances, not counteroffers, are the only possible responses, proposers must build in enough surplus for the recipient while still improving their own position. Multi-acceptor uncertainty (random recipient if more than one accepts) makes it risky to propose highly favorable terms that more than one player might find attractive, especially when the proposer would rather target a specific partner. Early in the game, expect cautiously generous exchanges using green as the flexible side of the trade to test others’ interest in different colors. As history accumulates, players can infer who likes or dislikes specific colors and adjust offers. With only three rounds, there is limited time for sophisticated reputation building, so most deals will be straightforward, surplus-creating trades where one player sells a chip color that is relatively low for them but plausibly higher for others, often using green or the proposer’s own low-value colors as payment.

Overall, the likely path is: early probing offers featuring green versus non-green colors; gradual revelation that lion strongly values blue/red, squid strongly values blue/green and dislikes red/purple, and camel is moderate on everything; and late-game attempts to finalize one or two mutually beneficial exchanges before the game ends, with uncertainty about exact cutoffs driving some declined offers that are only modestly beneficial to recipients.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is the learning and probing phase. All players start with 10 of each color and no trade history, so beliefs about others’ preferences are nearly uniform over the public ranges. Proposers are likely to attempt small, simple trades that (a) strictly increase their own value under many plausible opponent valuations and (b) are easy for at least one counterpart to view as weakly beneficial. Given green’s common value, trades involving green versus a non-green color are especially attractive as early tests: if someone eagerly buys or sells a color against green, that reveals comparative preferences. Because of the random resolution when multiple players accept, proposers will design offers that are attractive to exactly one likely partner when possible, or at least not too costly if the wrong partner accepts. Expect small-quantity offers to avoid overcommitting chips before values are inferred.

By Round 1, players have observed that squid traded away red for blue (signaling squid dislikes red relative to blue), that camel tried to offer purple for green and found no takers (suggesting others may not value purple more than green at the proposed rate), and that lion tried, unsuccessfully, to buy red with green (indicating lion may value red more than green). These observations start to differentiate preferences: squid looks blue-oriented, lion appears to like red, and camel’s first move shows some willingness to part with purple. Proposers now have slightly more targeted beliefs. We expect offers that exploit perceived mismatches: squid will keep pushing to swap low-valued red/purple for higher-valued blue/green; camel may attempt modest arbitrage deals, and lion may refine offers for red or blue while trimming the generosity on green after the previous rejection.

Entering Round 2, several patterns are clearer. Squid has repeatedly sold low-valued red/purple for blue/green, revealing that squid treats blue/green as high-value and red/purple as low-value. Camel has accepted trades that bring in red and green in exchange for blue or purple at modest rates, indicating relatively balanced preferences and a willingness to take small gains. Lion has shown strong interest in both red and blue, paying green and modestly favorable terms to obtain them. With only one round left, players have fewer opportunities to correct mistakes, so proposers focus on clearly positive-margin trades rather than long-shot experiments. We expect targeted offers: squid will continue to unload remaining low-value chips for blue/green if possible; camel will seek any remaining small arbitrage gains; lion will try to fine-tune one last acquisition of blue or red if the pricing remains acceptable to a counterpart given their now more refined beliefs.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued colored chips (red, blue, purple) plus a common-value green chip. Every player begins with 10 of each chip type. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it can safely be used as a numeraire, but rational players will quickly realize that their non-green chip values differ from others’ because some trades that look bad in their own value scale will be accepted by others. Each proposer must offer one color and request a different color in strictly positive quantities, and responders decide simultaneously and privately. If multiple players accept a proposal, the realized partner is determined randomly, so proposals must be attractive enough that at least one responder is willing to accept but not so generous that both are extremely likely to accept if the proposer fears random partner selection. Strategic uncertainty comes from two main sources: (1) incomplete information about others’ valuations for red, blue, and purple, and (2) stochastic matching when multiple players accept. Because there are only three rounds (with each player proposing once per round) and everyone observes all executed trades, players can use the early rounds to probe others’ valuations and then refine later offers. Using the analyst’s knowledge of true valuations, the clear efficient pattern is for high-value holders of a chip to accumulate more of it while low-value holders shed it in exchange for colors they value more. In this configuration, green is moderate for everyone, red is especially valuable for alligator, blue is especially valuable for peacock (and moderately valuable for squid and alligator), and purple is valuable for peacock, moderate for squid, and low for alligator. One likely dynamic is that peacock and alligator both try to pull chips in their high-value colors (blue for peacock, red for alligator), while squid opportunistically trades away low-value purple for higher-valued red/blue and occasionally uses green as a safe bridge currency. Because each player knows only their own payoffs, they will infer others’ preferences partly from what they offer and what they accept, leading to exploratory trades in early rounds and more targeted trades later. The simulation below reflects a plausible, self-consistent path where each player gradually reveals and exploits comparative advantages without having full common knowledge of values.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color and have no information about others’ private values. They know only that green is common-value at $0.50 and that red/blue/purple are somewhere in $0.10–$1.00 for everyone. A reasonable early strategy is to use green in small trades to probe willingness to exchange, and to make modest offers in one’s own lower-valued colors for potentially higher-valued colors. Proposers are likely to choose simple 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 trades to test the market without revealing too much or risking large losses. Responses are cautious: players will accept trades that are clearly favorable under their own valuations and might tentatively accept slight losses in low-stakes quantities if they suspect learning value, but with only three rounds in total, learning is still relatively expensive.

Going into Round 1, players have observed two accepted trades and one declined trade. From squid’s first offer (trading away purple for blue) and peacock’s acceptance, everyone can infer that squid does not highly value purple relative to blue and that peacock is at least mildly willing to give up blue for purple—hinting that purple might be more valuable for peacock than for squid. Alligator’s trade of multiple purple chips for a single blue chip, accepted by squid, reveals that alligator values blue more than purple and that squid is still comfortable accumulating purple at a big numerical discount, likely because squid values purple only moderately. The declined peacock offer of blue-for-red suggests neither squid nor alligator found that exchange favorable at that rate. Now proposers can adjust: squid may continue to offload low-value purple if she can get red/blue; peacock will seek more blue and purple while trying not to overpay for red; alligator, having revealed very low purple value, will push aggressively for red or blue while dumping purple. Small-step trades using public signals dominate, but each proposer also recognizes that the game is now halfway over, raising the incentive to secure clearly profitable value-improving trades rather than purely exploratory ones.

By Round 2, substantial information about relative valuations has been revealed through accepted trades. Squid has repeatedly traded away purple for red and blue, showing purple is her lowest-valued non-green color. Peacock has once traded blue for purple and once traded red for blue, consistent with blue and purple both being relatively valuable to her and red being weakest. Alligator has consistently offered large quantities of purple for small quantities of blue or red, clearly signaling that purple is his least valued color and that red and blue are significantly better for him, with red likely top. With only one proposal left for each player, the focus shifts from exploration to squeezing out final value gains consistent with what has been learned. Squid will try to flip any surplus purple into red/blue one last time; peacock will try to upgrade from red or possibly green into blue or purple; alligator will aim to convert any remaining purple into red, ideally, or blue as a fallback. Since everyone knows the game ends after this round, time pressure makes players somewhat more willing to accept borderline-profitable trades if they are on the favorable side, and less willing to gamble on uncertain or learning-motivated moves.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red, blue, and purple chips, plus a common-value green chip. Every player begins with 10 of each color. Green chips are worth $0.50 to everyone, so they act as a natural "currency" that all players are relatively willing to trade. Red chips are very valuable to Alligator and Ram ($1.00) and moderately valuable to Owl ($0.60). Blue chips are very cheap for Owl ($0.10) but quite valuable for Alligator ($0.90) and moderately valuable for Ram ($0.50). Purple chips are best for Owl ($0.70), moderate for Ram ($0.50), and least valuable for Alligator ($0.30). Because players know only their own private valuations (plus the public ranges), they must infer others' preferences from observed offers and acceptances. The main efficiency gains come from moving red chips toward Alligator and Ram, blue chips away from Owl toward Alligator and Ram, and purple chips toward Owl and Ram and away from Alligator. Early in the game, players are uncertain about each others' exact valuations and will test the waters with conservative offers that improve their own payoff while being plausibly acceptable across a wide range of opponent types. As public trade history accumulates, players update beliefs: for example, if someone eagerly accepts red-for-green trades, they likely value red highly; if someone is willing to part with purple cheaply, they likely value it less. Strategic tension arises from the simultaneous response rule and random selection among multiple accepters: proposers cannot target a specific partner once both are willing to accept. This encourages designs of offers that are profitable regardless of which responder is matched. Over three rounds (nine total proposals), we expect incremental, value-improving trades centered on green as a medium of exchange and red/blue/purple flowing toward the players who value them more, with most proposals conservative enough to be acceptable to at least one counterpart while still strictly improving the proposer's total value.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, all players have symmetric endowments and highly incomplete information about others' valuations. Owl is likely to explore trades that convert their very cheap blue chips into more valuable colors (especially purple or red) or green currency. Alligator, knowing they value red and blue highly and purple relatively little, will tentatively try to acquire more red and blue using purple or green, but must keep offers modest because they do not know whether others share these preferences. Ram, whose valuations are more balanced but with red as the top chip, is inclined to pick up red when possible, paying with green or blue at near-fair rates. Offers this round are likely small and exploratory, testing whether others are willing to exchange at modest markups over what a risk-neutral, uninformed trader might consider roughly fair given public ranges.

After Round 0, public history has revealed some information. Owl's willingness to give up multiple blue chips for only a couple of green suggests blue is relatively low value to them and green is standard currency. Alligator's eagerness to trade away several purple chips for red signals that red is quite important and purple is less so. Ram's acceptance of purple-for-red also shows they value red at least as much as purple. These signals encourage future proposals that use purple as a funding source for red acquisitions and treat blue chips as cheaper, especially when trading with Owl. However, uncertainty remains about exact valuations, so players continue to make small, profitable trades that would still be acceptable under a variety of possible opponent value structures. This round likely sees fine-tuning: Owl trying to increase their holdings of purple and red, Alligator continuing to seek red (and possibly blue) while offloading purple, and Ram opportunistically trading into red while avoiding giving up too much value unnecessarily.

By the final round, players have several data points about who values which colors. It is clearer that Alligator values red quite highly and is ready to pay with purple or green; Owl seems to value purple highly and blue weakly; and Ram values red strongly but is somewhat more balanced overall. With only three proposal turns left, players weigh the immediate gains from trade against the risk of offering deals that are too generous. There is less reason to hold back inventories for future trades, which can make final-round offers slightly more aggressive but still self-serving. We expect Owl to keep unloading remaining blue in exchange for higher-valued colors, Alligator to make at least one more red-acquisition attempt paying with purple or green, and Ram to seek a last improvement if a clearly favorable deal presents itself. Some offers will be declined as players recognize they cannot profitably accept given their private valuations and the limited remaining time.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value green chips equally at $0.50, so green is a stable numeraire with no private-information wedge. Strategic gains must come from trading red, blue, and purple chips, where valuations differ. Butterfly strongly values red and purple ($0.80 each) and is indifferent between blue and green ($0.50). Badger strongly values purple ($0.90), moderately values red ($0.40), and dislikes blue ($0.20). Cat strongly values blue ($0.60), moderately values red ($0.40), and dislikes purple ($0.20). This creates clear comparative advantages: badger wants to accumulate purple and unload blue, cat wants to accumulate blue and unload purple, and butterfly wants to accumulate red/purple and is willing to give up blue/green. Because each player knows only their own values, they infer others’ preferences from offers and acceptances. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be exploratory and somewhat conservative, using green as a safe payment because its value is common knowledge. Later, players may attempt more targeted swaps among red, blue, and purple that exploit suspected value differences. However, the rule that any accepting responder is chosen at random discourages highly tailored offers to a single partner; proposers will often post deals they are comfortable executing with either opponent, or else structure offers so that only one specific opponent is likely to find them attractive. Across three rounds, we expect some mutually beneficial color reallocation, especially trading away low-value colors (e.g., cat’s purple, badger’s blue) in exchange for higher-valued colors or green, but also some failed trades due to uncertainty and misaligned expectations.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds an even bundle of chips and has no information about others’ private values. Proposers in round 0 are likely to use green as a safe, common-value unit and to make relatively small offers to probe who is willing to trade what. Butterfly moves first and may experiment with buying a color that is privately valuable (red or purple) using green or blue. Badger, moving second, will likely try to trade away low-value blue or perhaps buy purple. Cat, moving third, will be inclined to trade away purple or buy blue. Because acceptances are simultaneous and random among accepters, offers will be structured so that the proposer is not harmed regardless of which responder accepts, but may still be slightly mispriced due to incomplete information.

After round 0, only one trade executed: badger bought a purple from butterfly in exchange for green. Publicly, everyone sees that butterfly was willing to give up purple for green, and badger was willing to give up green for purple. This suggests to observers that both might value purple and green somewhat similarly, or at least that the trade was not obviously dominated. Cat sees that no one accepted her attempt to buy blue for green, hinting that others may value blue at least as much as green or are cautious about selling it cheaply. In round 1, butterfly now has slightly more green and fewer purple chips, badger has more purple and fewer green, and cat remains at her initial holdings. Proposers can use these signals to refine beliefs: badger is revealed as a likely heavy purple demander, and butterfly as at least somewhat flexible on purple. Offers may become slightly more targeted: badger may try to acquire more purple or trade away blue, butterfly may again seek red or purple using green or blue, and cat may try again to reduce purple or obtain blue, but will be wary of offering pure green-for-blue trades that were previously rejected.

By round 2, several informative trades have occurred. Butterfly has shown willingness to trade both purple and blue; badger has clearly revealed a strong appetite for purple and some willingness to part with green and blue; cat has demonstrated a willingness to trade purple for blue and to acquire red for blue. Publicly, it is evident that purple has been moving toward badger, blue has been moving toward cat, and butterfly has been partly a facilitator by exchanging colors at roughly one-for-one rates. With only one final proposal turn each, players face a trade-off between trying to extract additional surplus and avoiding unfavorable last-minute trades that reduce their private value. Since acceptances are still random among accepters, proposers design offers that help them regardless of whether butterfly or cat, or butterfly or badger, responds. Given limited remaining time and some revealed pattern of comparative advantage (badger–purple, cat–blue, butterfly–red/purple), round 2 proposals are likely to be more directional but also somewhat conservative: marginal one-for-one swaps that slightly improve private portfolios if accepted but are not disastrous if beliefs about others’ valuations are a bit off.
