parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 of each color. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so it serves as a natural, transparent benchmark. Red/blue/purple are heterogeneous: iguana values blue most and red least (BLUE $0.60 > GREEN $0.50 > PURPLE $0.40 > RED $0.30); whale values red most (RED $0.50 = GREEN $0.50 > BLUE $0.40 = PURPLE $0.40); duck values blue and red highly and purple very little (BLUE $0.90 > RED $0.50 = GREEN $0.50 > PURPLE $0.20). Efficiently, trades should move blue toward duck, red toward whale and duck, and purple toward iguana and whale, with green used as a neutral currency. However, each player only knows their own values and must infer others’ preferences from proposals and acceptances. Early in the game, proposals are likely to be conservative, test prices against the known green benchmark, and avoid giving away obviously high-value chips cheaply. Because acceptance is simultaneous and, when multiple accept, the recipient is randomly drawn, proposers face uncertainty both about who will accept and whether a too-generous offer might inadvertently benefit the wrong partner. Over three short rounds, players have limited time to learn, so we expect a small number of clearly positive-sum trades (especially involving blue flowing to duck and red flowing to whale/duck) and some declined offers that are either too aggressive or misaligned with others’ hidden values. Iguana, who likes blue, will try to acquire blue without overpaying and may be willing to dump red. Whale will try to accumulate red using green or lower-valued blue/purple. Duck is willing to pay strongly for blue (even in green or purple), but will be reluctant to give up red unless getting a lot of blue or green. The play below forecasts a plausible, moderately rational path with simple, one-color-for-one-color trades, incremental price discovery, and some inefficiencies from incomplete information.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone is symmetric in holdings and only knows their own values plus the public range. Green is obviously a safe anchor at $0.50. Players will test the market with simple trades pegged loosely to green: offers that look roughly even in the proposer’s own values but might exploit unknown differences in others’ valuations. Given the complete symmetry of holdings, no one is pressured to trade quickly, so they may reject thin-margin offers. The main hidden structure is that duck loves blue, whale loves red, and iguana dislikes red; if early offers hint at these, later turns can become more targeted. In this forecast, the first round features exploratory trades priced around green that start to reveal who wants what.

After round 0, some structure has emerged. Iguana traded away low-value red and gained blue, suggesting to others that iguana somewhat prefers blue to red. Whale’s red-for-blue swap with duck hints that whale likes red more than blue and that duck at least doesn’t hate blue. Duck’s purple-for-blue trade with iguana at a 2:1 rate strongly signals that duck values blue highly and purple weakly, and that iguana is willing to hold some purple if the price is good. Beliefs are still noisy, but all three can now reasonably suspect: duck loves blue, whale likes red, iguana dislikes red. This round, they are more willing to make multi-chip-margin trades where both sides gain in their own values, particularly moving blue toward duck and red toward whale/duck, often mediated by green or low-valued purple.

By the final round, patterns are clear: duck has repeatedly paid a premium for blue using purple and sometimes green; iguana has been a willing counterparty, loading up on cheap purple to get blue; whale has consistently accumulated red using assets roughly equivalent to green or blue in their own valuations. Everyone now has a rough map of others’ preferences, but with only three turns left, they must decide whether further trades are worth the risk and whether they can still get good prices. Margins narrow as some arbitrage opportunities are exhausted. Proposers favor straightforward, clearly positive-sum trades rather than highly speculative ones. A couple of final exchanges occur that push the allocation a bit closer to efficiency—more blue to duck, more red consolidated at whale and duck, more purple at iguana—before the game ends, and at least one moderate but not obviously great offer is declined as players guard their now well-understood high-value chips.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip bargaining game features privately known values for red, blue, and purple chips and a common value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color and wants to maximize their own monetary value, not total group surplus. Because players know only their own values, they must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances. This creates uncertainty and scope for signaling and experimentation, especially early on.

In this configuration, ram-purple-8899 values red and blue the highest (0.70 each), purple slightly less (0.60), and green at 0.50. Lion-yellow-9203 values purple highest (0.60), then green (0.50), then red (0.40), and dislikes blue the most (0.30). Owl-yellow-9160 values blue (0.60) and green (0.50) relatively highly, is indifferent or moderate on purple (0.50), and strongly dislikes red (0.10). Efficient trades generally move red chips away from owl and toward ram or lion, move blue chips away from lion toward ram or owl, and can move purple away from owl toward ram or lion if good terms are offered. Green is neutral for all, so green trades are mostly used as a medium of exchange for revealing preferences or structuring slightly profitable deals.

Strategically, each proposer must request a color they privately value more than what they give up, expecting that at least one other player values the requested color less and the offered color more. However, because others’ values are unknown, players will start with modest, somewhat exploratory offers that are at least weakly profitable given any plausible beliefs about others’ valuations within the public range. Over time, accepted trades shift chip holdings and give information about relative valuations: e.g., if owl readily trades away red but fights to keep blue, that reveals owl’s pattern. The risk for proposers is that if both non-senders accept a good deal, a random recipient is chosen, meaning the proposer cannot target their preferred partner; this discourages extremely generous offers that would be attractive to everyone. Conversely, responders must decide whether the offered deal is clearly profitable in terms of their own values or whether to decline in hopes of better terms later, under the constraint that there are only three rounds and three turns per round.

Given this is the first negotiation game, players are unlikely to be highly sophisticated about equilibrium reasoning. They will more likely behave in line with simple heuristics: avoid giving away chips they privately consider very valuable, be willing to part with chips they like least, and demand more of the chips they suspect others might undervalue. Early turns are exploratory and cautious; later turns build on newly revealed patterns and the dwindling opportunity to trade before the game ends. As the final round approaches, responders may accept smaller gains they would have rejected earlier, because there is little time left to find a better offer. Overall, we should expect a few mutually beneficial trades that move red away from owl and blue away from lion, balanced against a number of declined offers that were either too one-sided or misaligned with others’ hidden preferences.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players have no direct evidence about each other’s private values and will tend to make simple, safe, and mildly self-serving offers. Ram, who highly values red and blue, will likely try to acquire more of these colors while giving up green or purple, which are relatively less valuable to him. Lion strongly dislikes blue and somewhat dislikes red, so he may try to offload blue chips in exchange for purple or green, and he may be a bit less protective of red than ram. Owl is likely to be very willing to give away red (which is almost worthless to him) and more defensive about blue and green. Because everyone is still uncertain, some offers will be declined simply because responders are not sure whether they are giving away something too valuable. Round 0 serves mainly as an information-gathering and probing stage.

By round 1, each player has observed at least one trade and can update their beliefs. Ram knows that lion was willing to trade green for purple and that owl was willing to give up blue for purple, suggesting both lion and owl value purple more than green and are not extremely protective of blue. Owl’s generous trade of red for blue with ram reveals owl’s strong dislike of red and his high valuation of blue. Lion sees that others are clearly willing to shed green and that owl is ready to offload red at good terms. With this information, players can start crafting more targeted offers: ram focuses more on accumulating blue and red, owl continues to dump red and avoid losing blue, and lion looks for improved purple holdings while trying to rid himself of cheap blue. However, they still do not know exact numbers, so offers remain somewhat conservative but better aligned with inferred patterns.

Round 2 is the final opportunity to trade, so players face a now-or-never decision environment. By this point, it is relatively clear that owl dislikes red and loves blue, lion wants purple and is comfortable giving up blue or some red, and ram values red and blue strongly and is willing to trade away green and (to a lesser extent) purple. With limited turns remaining, proposers are more willing to offer deals that leave a visible surplus for at least one responder to ensure acceptance, sacrificing some bargaining power to avoid ending with unexploited gains from trade. Responders, knowing that there will be no more rounds, are more inclined to accept modestly profitable or even roughly break-even trades rather than hold chips they do not particularly value. Still, some offers can be rejected if they appear too unfavorable relative to expectations formed from earlier trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color), but very different private valuations over the non-green chips. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so trades involving green are relatively easy to reason about, while red/blue/purple create conflicting private incentives and asymmetric information.

Privately, owl-yellow-8687 values blue and purple especially highly (BLUE=$1.00, PURPLE=$0.70) and has the strongest valuations overall, so owl is willing to overpay in green or lower-valued chips to accumulate blue and purple—especially blue. Ram-green-9746 values purple the most (PURPLE=$0.60) and red second (RED=$0.30), with blue being relatively weak (BLUE=$0.20), so ram is a natural seller of blue and a buyer of purple and possibly red if the price is sufficiently low in terms of green or blue. Butterfly-purple-4350 values blue moderately (BLUE=$0.60) and is largely indifferent between red and purple (both $0.20), so butterfly is a natural buyer of blue and seller of red/purple, but has a weaker willingness to pay for blue than owl.

Strategically, every proposer must balance two key issues: (1) since multiple responders can accept and a random one is chosen, each offer must be attractive enough to at least one opponent while not being too generous, and (2) each player only knows their own values, so they infer others’ valuations indirectly from observed acceptances and rejections. Because green is common-value, offers involving green are more transparent, but they also risk revealing how much someone is willing to give up for a privately valued color. Over three rounds, players are likely to start with relatively small, slightly favorable offers that test demand (who is eager for which color), before potentially moving to more targeted trades once they see who accepts what. Given that blue is cheap for ram and very valuable for owl and moderately valuable for butterfly, the most likely mutually beneficial trades involve ram selling blue for green (or occasionally purple/red) to either owl or butterfly. Owl, being the strongest overall valuer, can profitably pay more in common-value green to acquire blue without fear of being exploited in the final payoff.

Given limited time (three rounds, nine total offers), we forecast modest but non-trivial reallocation: owl will successfully buy blue from ram at a favorable but not extreme price, possibly after one or two exploratory offers by butterfly and owl that get declined because they are too one-sided. Butterfly will likely try to gain green at a discount or trade low-valued purple/red for blue, but may struggle to find trades that both ram and owl like, especially once they see how high owl bids for blue. As the game progresses, successful offers become more tailored: ram shifts to being a blue seller to owl; owl shifts to buying blue (and sometimes purple) for green; butterfly tests but may end up with fewer successful deals, learning that their competitors place higher values on some colors than butterfly expected.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players have no information about others’ private chip values beyond the common-value green. They will typically make small, exploratory offers. Butterfly moves first and is likely to probe whether anyone is willing to give up green (safe benchmark) for a chip that butterfly values less, such as red or purple. Ram, moving second, can experiment with selling blue (which ram values poorly) for green or another color, without yet knowing that owl values blue especially highly. Owl, moving last, may already suspect that paying green for someone else’s blue is attractive and will test a relatively generous but still favorable blue-for-green offer to see who bites.

By the second round, some information has been revealed. Ram has just sold blue for green to owl, indicating that ram is at least somewhat willing to part with blue for green, and that owl is at least willing to pay that price. Butterfly learns that blue might be more valuable to at least one opponent than to ram, and that owl was willing to spend green to acquire blue. Now, players begin to refine their strategies: butterfly may try to buy blue from ram or owl using low-valued purple or red; ram, having seen owl’s eagerness for blue, might consider slightly worse terms for owl on any future blue sales; and owl may test whether butterfly is also willing to sell blue for green or whether butterfly prefers to hold blue.

In the final round, players know that owl is repeatedly buying blue from ram for green, revealing a strong relative valuation of blue versus green for owl and a weak valuation of blue for ram. Butterfly has seen little interest in trades involving purple or red, suggesting that others may not value these as highly as what butterfly has asked. With only three proposal turns left, players adjust: butterfly might either lower demands or leave values as they are, ram has almost exhausted profitable blue-for-green trades with owl, and owl focuses on squeezing out any last beneficial exchanges in blue if possible. Because the game ends after this round, there is no reason to hold back; agents will only accept trades that immediately increase their final payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red, blue, and purple chips plus a common-value green chip. Each player begins symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color), but their dollar values per chip differ: whale-purple-6500 values red and purple highly (red especially), dolphin-purple-5083 values blue relatively highly but purple weakly, and owl-red-4188 has moderate, fairly balanced values. Because only green is common-value, players must infer others’ preferences by watching what they offer and request. Any trade that is privately profitable for both sides requires that the buyer value the purchased chip more than the seller does, and/or value the chip they give up less than the counterparty. However, players do not see others’ values, so they face uncertainty about which colors are attractive to whom and about how aggressive they can be with markups. Strategic tensions arise because: (1) each proposer wants terms with positive surplus to themselves, (2) recipients will only accept if the deal is (weakly) profitable or at least strategically informative, and (3) if more than one recipient accepts, the partner is randomly chosen, which can discourage finely targeted offers. Over three rounds, there is limited time to learn, so early turns are likely to feature conservative “test” offers around green or blue (perceived as safer) and visible reluctance to part with highly valued chips like whale’s red or purple. As offers and acceptances occur, players update beliefs about others’ likely valuations and adjust proposals in later rounds to exploit inferred comparative advantages, though some profitable trades may still fail due to misaligned expectations or fear of giving too much surplus to an opponent.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have 10 of each color and only green’s value is common knowledge ($0.50 for everyone). Each player knows only their own red/blue/purple values, so initial offers are likely exploratory, using green as a relatively safe medium and avoiding large concessions in their most-valued colors. Proposers will try small marked-up trades to test others’ willingness to move particular chips, while recipients may decline offers that appear too one-sided until they gain more information.

After round 0, one trade has occurred: owl-red-4188 sold 2 red to whale-purple-6500 for 2 green. Publicly, others see that whale was willing to give up green for red, suggesting whale may value red relatively highly, and that owl was willing to part with red, hinting that owl may not see red as especially precious. No one has traded purple yet, signaling some reluctance or uncertainty over its worth. In this round, dolphin-purple-5083 and owl-red-4188 may probe more around red and blue to exploit inferred comparative advantages, while whale will try to accumulate more high-value red or purple without overpaying in green.

Going into the final round, some patterns are clearer. Whale-purple-6500 has repeatedly paid green for red, reinforcing others’ belief that whale prizes red. Dolphin-purple-5083 and owl-red-4188 have traded purple for blue in both directions, revealing that both sides find blue at least moderately attractive relative to purple. With only one proposal left for each player, they face a last chance to improve holdings: proposers are more willing to make slightly generous offers to secure profitable trades, while recipients may still reject if a deal obviously sacrifices too much value. Remaining uncertainty about exact valuations, especially for purple, still limits fully efficient reallocation, but final offers are more targeted thanks to the trading history.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player, multi-round chip-bargaining game features private values over red, blue, and purple chips and a common value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color, so all early trades are about improving the composition of their portfolio rather than just acquiring chips. From an analyst perspective, duck-green-5428 has the strongest valuations overall and especially loves red ($1.00) and both blue and purple ($0.80). Dolphin-pink-1017 values red ($0.80) and blue ($0.40) moderately but places very low value on purple ($0.10). Alligator-orange-1621 strongly values purple and blue ($0.80 and $0.60) and is middling on red ($0.70). Green is neutral at $0.50 to everyone.

The primary surplus-creating opportunities come from trading away low-valued colors for higher-valued ones, exploiting value gaps: dolphin is happy to dump purple; duck is happy to buy almost any non-green color if the price is right; alligator can gain from trading away red for more purple or blue, and from picking up cheap blue. However, players only know their own values, so they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. Since all know that green is identical for everyone, green trades provide no inherent value arbitrage; they are more useful as a safe medium of exchange or as a signaling/price-setting tool early on. Rational players will try to avoid giving away high-value chips for low-value ones unless compensated by quantity (e.g., trading one very valuable chip for multiple moderately valuable chips).

Strategically, the main tension is between making offers attractive enough to be accepted, while not revealing too much about one’s own valuations or giving away surplus. Each sender must anticipate how both potential recipients might value each color. Rejections provide information, and accepted trades reveal at least some directional preference (e.g., someone accepting a trade giving up red for purple suggests they don’t value red as much). Another layer of uncertainty is that if multiple recipients accept a proposal, the realized partner is chosen at random, so the proposer cannot precisely target their preferred trading partner; this encourages offers that are acceptable no matter which of the two others accepts.

Over three rounds with three sequential turns per round, early moves are often exploratory and moderate in size, while later moves (particularly in Rounds 1 and 2) can exploit what has been learned. Dolphin, moving first each round, is likely to test small trades that shed purple or green in exchange for red or blue. Duck, with very high private values, can profitably buy strong colors even at seemingly expensive prices if they believe others undervalue them. Alligator, moving last each round, observes the most history before acting and can opportunistically arbitrage when others reveal their preferences. We forecast a game where some modest surplus-improving trades occur, but not all possible efficiency gains are realized due to private information, caution, and the randomness over which acceptor is matched when multiple players accept the same offer.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 begins with completely symmetric holdings (10 of each color per player) and only private valuations differ. Players know only their own value structure and the common value of green. Proposals this round are likely to be cautious and somewhat exploratory. Dolphin, as the first mover, will try to shape the portfolio toward more red (high value) and away from purple (low value) or possibly green (neutral) but must guess others’ preferences. Duck, with the strongest valuations overall, can afford to be more aggressive in picking up red, blue, and purple if the price seems favorable. Alligator, moving last, will have slightly more information after observing two turns and can respond by either accepting profitable offers or proposing trades that exploit any emerging patterns of acceptance or rejection.

Round 1 begins with some informative trades already executed in Round 0. Dolphin successfully traded away low-value purple for high-value red with duck, learning that duck is at least somewhat willing to part with red if the quantity of purple is large. Duck attempted to buy blue with green but was refused by both others, suggesting that the implicit price was not attractive or that others value blue reasonably highly. Alligator traded away red for blue with duck, indicating that alligator likely values blue more than red. Going into this round, dolphin should feel more confident that duck appreciates purple and is flexible on red, while alligator is a candidate partner for red-for-blue swaps. Duck has learned that others are reluctant to give up blue cheaply and that both dolphin and alligator will accept trades that shift them toward their favored colors at good rates.

By Round 2, several patterns have emerged. Dolphin has repeatedly been willing to trade away purple and, more recently, green when the rate is acceptable, confirming that dolphin is focused on accumulating red and perhaps blue. Duck has accumulated more purple and blue while giving up some red and green, signaling strong private values for non-green colors. Alligator has shifted from red toward blue and purple, reinforcing the idea that red is comparatively less attractive for alligator. With only one round left, players have less incentive to partially hide their preferences and more incentive to execute any remaining value-improving trades they can identify from the history. However, caution remains, because no one has exact knowledge of others’ value functions, and an unattractive last-round trade cannot be undone.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player chip bargaining game features privately valued red, blue, and purple chips plus common-value green chips. Each player has 10 of each color initially, and they know only their own values. Trades must be one-for-one across colors (one color bought, a different color sold) with an integer quantity for each side. Because everyone values green at $0.50, green functions as a safe anchor asset. However, each player's private values for red, blue, and purple differ, which creates hidden gains from trade: some players should be willing to give up chips they value relatively little in exchange for chips they value more. Players must forecast others' valuations from observed offers and acceptances while facing simultaneous and private response decisions. If more than one player accepts a trade, the realized partner is random, so proposers must consider that any sufficiently attractive offer may be snapped up by the player who benefits most. Over three short rounds with fixed proposer order, players balance experimentation (to learn who likes which colors) against exploitation (locking in high-margin trades). Uncertainty about others' values and the risk of overpaying or revealing too much structure bargains into relatively simple, conservative offers early, with more targeted and assertive proposals later as participants infer each other's preferences from the trade history.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric chip holdings and no direct evidence about others' private values. The strategic focus is on exploratory trades that are plausibly beneficial to at least one counterparty while not being too costly if misunderstood. Because green is common-value and moderately priced, and each player privately ranks the non-green colors differently, early trades are likely to use green as one side of the deal or to propose modest, low-risk color-for-color exchanges. Responses will be cautious: players will accept clear value gains based on their own valuations but may decline trades that are close to break-even out of fear of being exploited by someone with very different preferences.

After round 0, players have limited but informative signals. Duck successfully traded away low-value purple for higher-value blue, revealing a likely preference for blue over purple. Whale accepted that deal, signaling that blue is very valuable to them as well and that purple is not too costly to give up; this hints that whale may have relatively high blue value. Swan’s declined offer involving green suggests they will not weaken their green position just to marginally adjust their blue holdings. With this partial information, players in round 1 will refine strategies: duck and whale should seek more blue; swan may try to monetize either purple or green, but cautiously, knowing others might value blue strongly. Trades are more targeted, but residual uncertainty about red values and cross-player rankings still constrains how aggressive proposals can be.

By round 2, the emerging pattern is that purple is generally weak relative to blue and red for both duck and whale, and that swan is willing to trade purple for red at parity. Duck has repeatedly tried to dump purple and accumulate blue and red; whale has tried to upgrade from purple into blue; swan is cautious with green and has only engaged when trades are clearly favorable or neutral on their valuations. With only one proposal left per player, there is a premium on locking in any remaining win-win trades without overreaching. Players may avoid green trades to keep the common-value anchor steady and instead focus on exchanging low-valued purple for higher-valued blue or red where counterparties remain willing. However, as inventories of purple shrink for the most eager sellers, opportunities for further large gains diminish, and some final proposals may be declined if they ask too much from already well-positioned opponents.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but different private values for red, blue, and purple chips. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 to everyone, so green behaves like money or a neutral medium of exchange. Pig values blue the most ($1.00) and is relatively indifferent between red and green ($0.50 each) while disliking purple ($0.10). Zebra strongly values red ($1.00) and purple ($0.80), and cares much less about blue ($0.40). Swan values red highly ($0.80), is middling on blue and purple (both $0.40 and $0.20 respectively), and—like everyone—values green at $0.50.

Given these differences, mutually beneficial trades exist: Pig has strong demand for blue and is willing to give up purple, red, or even some green; Zebra wants to acquire red and purple and is relatively willing to part with blue; Swan wants red most, is moderately okay with blue, and least with purple. However, players know only their own private valuations and see only the history of offers and who accepts. They must infer others’ values from observed behavior: which colors people try to buy or sell, and what trades they accept or reject.

In this one-shot 3-round game with exactly one proposal per player per round, bargaining power is limited by time and by the random recipient rule when multiple players accept. Proposers want to design offers that at least one other player will accept but that do not give away too much surplus. Because acceptance is simultaneous and the recipient is randomly chosen if multiple accept, proposers cannot target a specific partner; they must choose offers that are attractive to at least one counterpart while controlling downside risk if a less-desired partner accepts. Early in the game, offers will likely be exploratory and coarse (e.g., small trades exchanging green or low-valued colors from the sender’s perspective for colors they suspect others undervalue). As rounds progress, players can adjust based on what was previously accepted or declined, but uncertainty about others’ exact chip values remains, so observed trades are plausible but not fully optimized from the omniscient observer’s point of view.

Below is one plausible complete trajectory of proposals, responses, and realized trades, given the incentive structure and incomplete information.

rounds.round_explanation: At the very start, no one has information about the others’ private values beyond the public ranges. Each proposer is likely to make relatively small, safe offers that move chips they themselves value less in exchange for chips they suspect might be under-valued by others. Because green is known to be worth $0.50 to everyone, using green in offers can help anchor perceived value without giving away clear information about private valuations of red, blue, or purple. This round is mostly exploratory: players test whether others are willing to part with certain colors for modest green payments or swaps involving clearly low-value colors for themselves.

After round 0, some information has leaked. Zebra’s willingness to give up blue for red suggests Zebra values red more than blue. Swan’s acceptance of that trade confirms Swan also values red enough to swap away blue at close to value parity. Pig’s acceptance of purple-for-green shows Pig is relatively eager to offload purple for stable green, hinting that purple is low value for Pig. Players still do not know exact numbers but they can infer ranking patterns. In this middle round, offers become a bit more targeted: Zebra and Swan attempt to accumulate more of their favored colors (especially red and purple for Zebra, red for Swan), while Pig tries to convert low-value colors (especially purple) into higher-value ones or green, but must remain cautious not to reveal too much or offer overly generous terms.

By the final round, patterns are clearer. Pig has signaled a strong dislike for purple and a willingness to pay with purple and sometimes green to get blue or, secondarily, red. Zebra appears to prize both red and purple and is prepared to give blue and some green away for them. Swan wants red and is relatively more willing to move purple and, to a lesser extent, blue. With only one proposal opportunity left for each player, they focus on final, more aggressive but still plausible trades that nudge their portfolios toward their highest-value colors. However, they must keep offers just attractive enough to induce at least one acceptance without overpaying in colors they also value, and their beliefs are still only approximate. The realized trades in this round are therefore somewhat lopsided from the omniscient observer’s perspective but remain sensible given the incomplete information each player faces.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player chip-bargaining game, each participant starts with an identical bundle of chips (10 red, 10 blue, 10 purple, 10 green) but privately values the non-green colors differently. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so they act as a safe, transparent medium for comparison: any trade that converts a chip someone values below $0.50 into green is clearly attractive, and any trade that gives up green for a chip someone values below $0.50 is clearly unattractive. The main bargaining friction is that each player only knows their own valuation vector, so they must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances. Zebra and Turtle both highly value blue chips ($1.00) and above-average red (Zebra $0.60, Turtle $0.90), while Swan has more balanced but weaker valuations (all non-green at $0.40–$0.50). Zebra’s clear dominance is blue, with red somewhat valuable and purple and green tied as low types; Turtle strongly prefers red and blue, and is weak on purple; Swan is more indifferent across colors but should be willing to part with blue if they can reliably gain value in red or purple or in green. Publicly, each sees the history of trades and current holdings but not each other’s valuations, so proposals that clearly exploit the common green chip (e.g., offering green to buy a chip type the proposer must value even more than green) may credibly signal priorities. Given randomization when multiple players accept, proposers must design offers that are attractive to at least one likely counterparty but not so generous that the wrong partner is equally willing to accept, since the proposer cannot choose among multiple acceptors. Over three short rounds, players face a tension between extracting immediate gains from clearly dominated chips (e.g., trading away low-value colors for green or for obviously desired colors of others) and experimenting to learn opponents’ valuations. The likely pattern is cautious, small trades that leverage green as a benchmark, with Zebra and Turtle trying to consolidate blue and red, and Swan playing a middle role, often supplying blue to the others in exchange for value gains in red, purple, or green. Some mutually beneficial trades will be missed because players misjudge how much their partners value specific colors, especially in the early rounds; by the final round, observed acceptances give some evidence about who craves which colors, leading to more targeted but still incomplete reallocation.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, players are effectively learning and testing the waters. No one yet knows who values which colors, beyond the common knowledge that green is worth exactly $0.50 to everyone and that all non-green colors lie between $0.10 and $1.00. Zebra and Turtle internally know they strongly like blue (and Turtle also strongly likes red), but they do not know that the other also likes blue or how Swan compares. The typical early strategy is to make modest, low-risk offers that use green as a reference, inviting counterparties to reveal their preferences by accepting or rejecting. Proposers will likely start with conservative trades they expect to be mildly favorable based on their own valuations while leaving room to adjust later behavior once they see who is willing to give up which colors.

Entering round 1, there has been one executed trade: Swan gave up blue in exchange for red to Turtle. From Zebra’s perspective, this suggests that at least one opponent is willing to trade away blue, implying they might not value blue especially highly; from Turtle’s perspective, the acceptance reveals that Swan is at least somewhat open to parting with blue to gain in red, confirming that red has comparable or higher value for Swan than blue. This begins to separate players’ reputations: Turtle appears to like red and blue; Swan appears relatively indifferent but slightly more eager to collect red; Zebra has not yet signaled any clear preferences. Players now adjust: Zebra may cautiously try to acquire blue without paying too much, while Swan and Turtle consider further value-improving trades that respect what has just been revealed. However, uncertainty remains high, and each proposer still must worry about overpaying or unintentionally giving a windfall to a competitor who also accepts.

By the final round, the limited history has revealed some structure: Swan was willing to swap blue for red (benefiting Turtle) and later to exchange purple for green (benefiting Zebra). This suggests to observant players that Swan does not place a strong premium on blue or purple relative to red and green, and that Zebra is comfortable disposing of purple to gain green, hinting that purple is among Zebra’s least-valued colors. Turtle has mainly attempted to consolidate blue and red but failed to secure additional blue beyond the first swap due to unattractive terms for others. With only three proposal turns left and no future games to influence, players will focus on concrete, immediate value gains. Proposals will target known or suspected weakly valued colors of others (e.g., Swan’s blue or Zebra’s purple) in exchange for chip types that the proposer values highly, but only if the proposer can still improve relative to keeping their current bundle. Some rational win–win trades still exist in theory (like Turtle paying a modest amount of purple or green to acquire more blue from Swan), but given simultaneous responses, fear of overpaying, and incomplete learning, at least some potential surplus will remain unrealized when the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red, blue, and purple chips, plus a common-value green chip at $0.50 each. Each player starts with an equal, symmetric endowment (10 of each chip), but their private valuations create gains from trade. The key asymmetries: kangaroo values PURPLE highly ($0.90) and BLUE least ($0.30); frog values BLUE highest ($0.80) and PURPLE low ($0.20); unicorn values only RED ($0.50) and BLUE ($0.30) moderately and PURPLE very low ($0.10). All value GREEN identically at $0.50, making it a natural “currency” and safe medium of exchange. Incentives: kangaroo wants to acquire PURPLE (especially from frog and unicorn, who value PURPLE poorly) and is happy to give up BLUE; frog wants BLUE (especially from kangaroo and unicorn, who both value BLUE only $0.30) and can cheaply give PURPLE; unicorn has no strong specialty but benefits from trading away PURPLE at very low value for higher-value RED or GREEN. Strategic uncertainty arises because nobody observes others’ exact values; they only infer from offers and acceptances. Proposers must design trades attractive to at least one counterpart, knowing that multiple acceptances trigger random partner selection. This pushes them toward generous but not too costly offers that are still strictly beneficial ex ante even under random matching. Over three rounds, we should see early exploratory offers in common-value GREEN and moderately valued RED, followed by more targeted exploitation of cheap PURPLE from frog and unicorn and cheap BLUE from kangaroo/unicorn to frog. Because each player has only three proposal turns in total, they balance safe, slightly generous trades against the risk of overpaying when others’ valuations are unknown. The forecast below reconstructs a plausible, value-consistent path where players gradually reveal their preferences through which offers they make and which trades they accept, with GREEN often used to make offers simultaneously appealing to multiple potential partners.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players have symmetric chip holdings and only know their own values. They treat GREEN as a safe $0.50 anchor and tentatively test the market for RED/BLUE/PURPLE. Kangaroo is likely to probe for PURPLE (which kangaroo values highly) using safe, mid-value payment (e.g., GREEN or BLUE), while frog will probe for BLUE and unicorn will try to offload low-value PURPLE for safer value like GREEN or RED. Because valuations are unknown, first-round offers are relatively conservative and use GREEN to increase the chance at least one counterpart finds the deal attractive.

By round 1, the first few exchanges have revealed structure: kangaroo appears eager for PURPLE (having just bought and then accepted PURPLE-for-BLUE trades), frog reveals a strong desire for BLUE (willing to trade multiple PURPLE for a couple of BLUE), and unicorn shows willingness to sell PURPLE for GREEN but learned that exchanging too much PURPLE for GREEN at a low rate may not be appealing to others. Now, proposers can refine offers: kangaroo can leverage cheap BLUE from frog in exchange for PURPLE that kangaroo still values highly; frog tries to secure more BLUE and possibly some RED, and unicorn will search for fairer rates to convert remaining low-value PURPLE into GREEN or RED. Players also begin to anticipate that overly one-sided trades may be rejected now that some preferences are partially revealed.

In the final round, each player gets one last chance to adjust their portfolio toward chips they value most. Information is richer: frog’s repeated eagerness to acquire BLUE and RED, kangaroo’s strong pursuit of PURPLE (even when paying in BLUE), and unicorn’s acceptance of trades that give more RED/GREEN and shed PURPLE are all visible patterns. Now proposers focus on clearly profitable marginal trades: kangaroo aims to convert remaining low-value BLUE/RED into PURPLE or GREEN; frog aims to finish by acquiring high-value BLUE while shedding PURPLE or slightly less-valued RED; unicorn tries small, safe improvements, such as trading away any remaining PURPLE or swapping between RED and GREEN when terms are favorable. Because it is the last round, players are less concerned with future reputation and more willing to propose sharply one-sided but still just-acceptable trades that exploit inferred preferences.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This is a three-player, incomplete-information bargaining game over four chip colors. All players start with 10 of each color and share a common value of $0.50 for green chips, but differ in how much they privately value red, blue, and purple.

Private values:
- dolphin-green-2899 (dolphin): RED 0.80, PURPLE 0.70, BLUE 0.30, GREEN 0.50
- zebra-pink-6926 (zebra): BLUE 1.00, GREEN 0.50, PURPLE 0.40, RED 0.30
- swan-green-4000 (swan): PURPLE 1.00, GREEN 0.50, RED 0.50, BLUE 0.40

Each player wants to trade away chips they value relatively less for chips they value more, increasing their own total monetary payoff. However, they only know their own private valuations and must infer others’ preferences from the proposals and acceptances they observe. Because multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, each proposer must design offers that (a) are attractive enough to induce at least one acceptance, but (b) still produce a clear surplus for themselves even if the highest-value counterparty ends up receiving the trade.

Key incentive patterns based on true values:
- dolphin strongly values red and purple, is indifferent between blue and green in value (0.30 vs 0.50, but green is also 0.50). Dolphin’s best gains come from acquiring red and purple in exchange for green and especially blue.
- zebra highly values blue and undervalues red. Zebra is naturally a net buyer of blue and net seller of red and possibly purple.
- swan highly values purple, moderately values red, and is least excited about blue (though still above green). Swan is a natural net buyer of purple and seller of green and possibly blue.

Socially efficient trades (from an analyst perspective) move chips toward their highest valuers: blue chips toward zebra, purple toward swan (and dolphin somewhat), red toward dolphin, and green is just a neutral token at 0.50 for all. But with incomplete information, players will test the waters with conservative, slightly favorable rates (e.g., offering 1-for-1 in nominal chip counts while hoping the counterparty values the incoming color more). Over three rounds, we expect:
- Early: cautious, simple 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 offers using green as a neutral medium, probing others’ demand for blue, red, and purple.
- Middle: proposals that increasingly target visible demand (e.g., zebra aggressively trying to buy blue, swan or dolphin seeking purple) as inferred from acceptance behavior.
- Late: either more aggressive value-extracting offers if someone seems desperate to rebalance, or very selective acceptance as each player has fewer remaining turns and less need to reshuffle if they are already in a good position.

Because every round has only three turns and the schedule is fixed (dolphin, then zebra, then swan), dolphin repeatedly sets the tone each round, zebra can respond or counterposition in the middle, and swan often reacts based on the updated state. This leads to asymmetric bargaining power in timing, with dolphin having repeated early-mover opportunities to create surplus trades, while zebra and swan decide whether to accept or hold out for better offers on their own proposal turns.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip color and have no direct evidence about others’ private values. Round 0 should feature exploratory, low-risk trades. Green, being common-value and widely understood, is likely used as a neutral medium to probe the demand for red, blue, and purple. Dolphin moves first and may float a simple, modest offer that favors red (which dolphin loves) in exchange for green or blue, hoping at least one counterpart reveals interest patterns. Zebra, knowing their own strong blue valuation, is likely to try to accumulate blue without giving up too much, while swan may cautiously test purple trades. Because this is the first round, all three players will be conservative on rates and keenly watch who accepts what, as those early signals inform subsequent strategy.

After Round 0, each player has some evidence: dolphin saw zebra accept a 1 green for 1 red trade (hinting red was not highly valued by zebra) and saw zebra’s later effort to buy blue from swan with green, which strongly suggests zebra values blue a lot. Swan saw that zebra is willing to part with green for blue, hinting that zebra might overvalue blue or undervalue green. Both dolphin and swan also see that zebra parted with red easily. With this information, dolphin is more confident treating zebra as a natural counterparty for red and blue trades; zebra wants to keep pressing their blue advantage but must be careful about depleting green; swan wants to find profitable ways to exchange green or blue for purple but will also be sensitive to not getting exploited now that others are learning. Round 1 likely features more targeted proposals toward known or suspected weaknesses, but still within modest, mostly 1-for-1 or 2-for-1 ranges to avoid overplaying hands.

Going into the final round, the earlier trade patterns have clarified preferences considerably. Dolphin has clearly signaled a strong taste for red (and now some willingness to part with green or blue for purple), zebra has visibly chased blue while dumping red and green, and swan has started to move green for purple. Each player now faces the last three proposal opportunities total, so their focus shifts from exploration to final portfolio optimization. Dolphin will try to make one or two last value-increasing swaps, likely trading away remaining blue or green for red or purple. Zebra must decide whether additional blue accumulation is worth sacrificing red or purple, given the limited remaining chances and possibly dwindling counterparty interest. Swan, seeing purple’s value and past acceptance behavior, will try to secure extra purple if a favorable rate appears, but may reject marginal offers that don’t clearly add value. We should expect more selective acceptance and slightly more aggressive attempts to improve terms, tempered by the risk that a too-greedy offer goes unaccepted in this final opportunity.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game involves privately valued red, blue, and purple chips and a common-value green chip. Each player starts with 10 of each chip, so nobody begins with a strong endowment advantage. However, their private values create clear comparative advantages: Turtle values red chips the most (0.70) and blue the least (0.20); Elephant values blue and red fairly highly (0.60 and 0.50) and purple moderately (0.30); Hamster values all non-green chips the least, with a slight tilt toward blue and red (0.40) over purple (0.20). Green is worth 0.50 to everyone and thus serves as a natural numeraire: it is both safe to accept and a convenient chip to use when trying to induce acceptance. Because players only know their own values, they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. Proposers face a tension between exploiting perceived mispricing (getting more subjective value from a trade than the partner does) and keeping offers attractive enough for at least one other player to accept. The random selection among multiple accepters adds additional strategic uncertainty: a proposer cannot target a specific partner, so they may structure offers that are acceptable to both potential partners while still being slightly more attractive for the partner they would most like to trade with. Over three rounds of proposals (nine turns total), the likely pattern is that players gradually learn that green is safe, that Turtle is strongly interested in red, and that Hamster is more indifferent across colors. Early turns will be more exploratory with symmetric or slightly generous offers for green; later turns will likely feature more targeted trades where Elephant and Turtle reshuffle red and blue chips to better match their values, often paying with green. Some profitable-looking trades from each proposer’s perspective will be declined because the responders, using their own valuations, see the terms as unfavorable or too revealing of their private information. Overall, we can expect a modest reallocation: Turtle accumulating some extra red chips (possibly surrendering blue or purple), Elephant improving their mix of blue versus lower-valued colors, and Hamster ending near their initial bundle, having occasionally sold off colors that seem slightly less valuable to them in exchange for guaranteed 0.50 green payments.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is the initial exploratory phase. All players still have symmetric holdings (10 of each chip), and nobody has yet revealed information about their valuations. Offers are likely to be conservative and anchored on green, because green’s value is common knowledge and safe. Proposers may test the waters with nearly fair trades that slightly favor themselves according to their own valuations, while responders compare offers to their private values and acceptance thresholds. Declines this round are especially informative, as they help each player infer which colors others might value more or less.

By Round 1, some information has been revealed through the universal rejections in Round 0. Everyone learns that modestly favorable trades for the proposer, even involving common-value green, can still be rejected if they are only slightly beneficial or ambiguous for responders. Proposers now have incentives to sweeten their offers toward green or structure them so that at least one potential partner clearly gains in their own valuation. At the same time, responders remain cautious, inferring that others will only propose if they expect to benefit. This pushes offers toward near-balanced subjective value or toward more targeted attempts to exploit perceived differences, for example, Turtle’s willingness to trade blue for red or others’ potential willingness to give up purple.

Round 2 is the final opportunity to trade, and holdings are now asymmetric due to the accepted trades in Round 1. Elephant has slightly fewer purple and more green, Hamster has given up some green for both purple and red, and Turtle has increased their red holdings at the cost of some green. Players update their beliefs: they can reasonably infer that Turtle is willing to buy red, Hamster is not strongly attached to green, and Elephant is comfortable parting with purple. With only one turn left for each player, proposals become more pointed: each proposer attempts a last improvement in their portfolio, often by offering what they now hold in relative surplus for what they value more. Responders weigh the final trade-offs knowing there is no future retaliation or adjustment, so they accept only if the offer strictly raises their final payoff.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip holdings but asymmetric private values for red, blue, and purple. Green is common-value at $0.50, making it a safe medium of exchange and a salient benchmark: trades that give up more than $0.50 of private value per green chip look unattractive. Dog strongly values red and blue ($0.90 each) and is indifferent between them, while thinking purple is low ($0.10). Badger values red and green at $0.50, blue moderately ($0.30), and purple low ($0.10). Cat values purple highest ($0.30), with red and blue low ($0.20 each). From an analyst’s perspective, efficient reallocation would move red and blue chips toward Dog, purple toward Cat, and use green or low-value colors as payment from high-value holders to lower-value holders. However, each player only knows their own values, so they infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances. Because multiple responders can accept and the realized partner is then random, proposers must design deals that remain acceptable even if the ‘wrong’ partner ends up matched. Over three rounds (nine total turns), we expect: (1) early cautious trades as players test counterpart valuations, often around green; (2) Dog trying to acquire red/blue while offloading purple or sometimes green; (3) Cat trying to acquire purple while offloading red/blue; (4) Badger willing to trade away purple and sometimes blue for red/green, but avoiding losing too many greens. Uncertainty about others’ valuations and short time horizon mean not all mutually beneficial trades will occur, and some generous offers will be declined because they do not look safe from the responders’ private perspective.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, everyone holds 10 of each color and knows only their own values. Players have no data yet about others’ valuations, so round 0 is mostly about exploratory, conservative trades. Proposers are likely to use green as a stable yardstick and avoid very lopsided-looking exchanges that might be rejected. Each accepted trade in this round will strongly shape beliefs about others’ relative values, guiding more targeted proposals in later rounds.

By round 1, players have observed that Cat was willing to give up a green for purple and that Badger was comfortable trading purple away and even trading red for purple. Dog’s attempt to swap purple for red was rejected, suggesting to Dog that at least one counterpart either values purple relatively more or red relatively less than he does. This round, proposals start to reflect tentative hypotheses: Dog pushes a bit harder for red, Cat continues to seek purple, and Badger looks to rebalance toward more green and some red while unloading low-value purple and possibly blue. The history now helps players target likely partners, but the random-recipient rule still discourages overly tailored offers.

Going into the final round, some structure has emerged: Cat has persistently sought purple; Badger has repeatedly been willing to surrender purple (and sometimes blue) for small gains; and Dog has succeeded once in acquiring red from Cat using green, while a purple-for-red attempt failed. Players infer that Dog likely values red more than others, and that Cat values purple relatively highly. Because this is the last chance to trade, players may accept modestly favorable or even roughly break-even deals if they seem unlikely to get better opportunities later. Proposals now focus on solidifying each player’s preferred portfolio: Dog tries to lock in more red/blue, Cat to get a final boost in purple, and Badger to hold onto green and red while dumping remaining low-value purple when compensated.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with symmetric chip endowments (10 of each color) but asymmetric private values for red, blue, and purple. Green is common-value at $0.50, so it naturally becomes the focal medium of exchange and a safe baseline for evaluating trades. Goose has the strongest preference for red and purple (both $1.00), bear also likes purple and red but slightly less, while buffalo finds red weak ($0.20) and values blue and purple at $0.80. From an analyst perspective, efficient trades shift red and purple chips toward goose (and to a lesser extent bear) and shift some blue away from goose toward buffalo and bear. However, players only know their own payoffs, so they must infer others’ valuations from observed behavior. Because recipients are chosen randomly when multiple accept, proposers face strategic uncertainty: they cannot target a specific partner and must design offers that (1) are attractive enough to at least one other player, (2) do not overpay given their own values, and (3) manage the risk of an unintended partner accepting. Over three short rounds, players are unlikely to reach full efficiency; instead, we expect cautious trades around green as a reference, gradual revelation of who values what, and some missed opportunities when offers are either too stingy to attract acceptance or too generous relative to alternative partners and future rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no trade history is available, so each player is guessing about others’ preferences. Proposers are likely to start with relatively conservative, simple exchanges using green as a stable benchmark. Buffalo will probably try to shed low-value red in exchange for green or a more favored color, testing how much others value red. Goose, who highly values red and purple, may cautiously bid for those colors using green, trying not to overpay before learning rivals’ valuations. Bear will also be feeling out the market, possibly attempting mild improvements using green as well. Given the uncertainty, some initially reasonable-seeming offers may be declined if they do not clearly beat the safe value of holding onto green chips.

By the start of round 1, one small trade has executed: bear managed to obtain a purple chip in exchange for green from goose. This reveals that at least one counterparty is willing to trade away purple for green at a 1:1 rate, which is surprisingly cheap from an analyst’s standpoint given purple’s high value to both goose and bear. Buffalo remains uninvolved and thus relatively uninformed about specific opponent values, but everyone now knows that some players may undervalue purple or overvalue green. Proposers may adjust by making slightly bolder attempts to acquire preferred colors. At the same time, the awareness of potential mispricing could make players more selective, especially with purple and red, increasing the chance that aggressive but uneven proposals are declined if they appear too one-sided against green’s safe value.

Going into the final round, a pattern has emerged: goose and bear have repeatedly traded purple and then red with each other for green at or near 1:1 rates, revealing that both are willing to use green as a near-equal substitute for high-value colors, even though from an analyst perspective that leaves substantial surplus for the recipient of the high-value chip. Buffalo still has not completed a trade, suggesting their offers may have been viewed as unattractive or unbalanced. With only one proposal left per player, urgency increases. Buffalo may either continue to seek a favorable multi-chip exchange that others still find too demanding, or pivot to a more modest but acceptable green-based trade. Goose, knowing they value red and purple the most, may attempt one last acquisition using green, while bear might try to convert more green into purple or red. However, because time is almost up and some players may suspect they have been underpricing valuable chips, proposals that appear even slightly unfavorable relative to simply keeping green are more likely to be rejected, constraining the final set of accepted trades.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately known values for red, blue, and purple chips and a common known value for green chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color and maximizes their own payoff given only their own valuations and the public trade history. Because green chips are worth $0.50 for everyone, they are natural low-risk bargaining currency; players can safely trade green if they perceive an opportunity to gain higher private value in red, blue, or purple. However, since no one knows others’ exact values, each proposal both tests the market and reveals partial information. Bear-red-1079 and dog-orange-6132 both highly value red (0.90 and 1.00), while buffalo-green-3116 values blue highly (0.90) and red very low (0.10). Buffalo also values purple very little (0.20), whereas bear and dog value purple more (0.70 and 0.80). As a result, efficient gains from trade exist: buffalo is an ideal seller of red and purple and buyer of blue; bear and dog are ideal buyers of red and purple and sellers of blue (and possibly green if needed as currency). But with uncertainty, players will tentatively propose trades that seem advantageous under many possible opponent values, adjust based on which offers get accepted, and avoid trades that obviously sacrifice too much of their own value. The fixed proposer schedule (bear, buffalo, dog each round) gives everyone equal formal opportunity, but players will anticipate future chances and may hold out for better terms. Random recipient selection when multiple accept introduces additional risk: proposers cannot target a specific partner and must structure offers that are acceptable but not too generous to any potential responder. Over three rounds, we expect early, conservative proposals that use green or disliked colors as payment and later, more targeted offers once some information is revealed by acceptances and rejections. The forecast below assumes moderately rational, value-sensitive players who test attractive cross-value trades while avoiding strictly dominated deals.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip. Nobody knows others’ exact valuations, but all know the common green value and the possible value ranges. Bear goes first and is likely to probe for trades that increase high-value colors (red, purple) in exchange for lower-value colors (from bear’s perspective, blue or green). Buffalo, who values blue a lot and red very little, will be inclined to trade away red and purple for blue or green. Dog, with very high red and purple values, is also a natural buyer of red and purple and a seller of blue or green. Given this, early proposals will likely involve exchanging green or blue for red or purple, but in small quantities as everyone tests how responsive others are and tries not to reveal too much about their valuations.

Entering Round 1, holdings have shifted. From Round 0: Bear traded 3 blue for 2 red with Buffalo and then observed Buffalo trade 4 red for 2 blue with Dog. Buffalo’s acceptance of both red-for-blue trades signals that Buffalo likely values blue more and red less, while Dog’s acceptance of red for blue signals that Dog values red highly compared to blue. Dog’s declined attempt to buy purple for green (2-for-2) suggests that this ratio was not attractive to bear or buffalo, either because they value purple more than $0.50 or because they want to preserve flexibility. With more information, Bear and Dog now know Buffalo is a natural seller of red and purple and buyer of blue; Buffalo knows both Bear and Dog are willing to trade away blue for red at some rates. The second round should see more confident attempts to exploit these patterns, still mostly using blue and green as payment for red and possibly purple.

By Round 2, the pattern is clear: Buffalo consistently trades away red and purple for blue, while Bear and Dog trade away blue for red and purple. Multiple accepted trades have reinforced beliefs: Buffalo’s marginal value for blue is clearly high and for red/purple clearly low; Dog’s and Bear’s behavior show they view red and purple as relatively valuable and blue as a natural give-away chip. Green has barely moved, consistent with its common value of $0.50 and the presence of better arbitrage opportunities involving private-value chips. With only one round left (three final turns), players now aim to fine-tune their portfolios, trying to convert remaining low-value holdings into high-value colors before the game ends. Offers will be relatively aggressive but still need to be acceptable to at least one counterpart, and proposers will lean on established patterns—Buffalo offering red/purple for blue, Bear and Dog offering blue for red/purple—while avoiding trades that clearly reduce their own total value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player chip bargaining game, each participant privately values red, blue, and purple chips differently, while green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each color, and they trade to convert low-value colors into higher-value ones from their own perspective. Because values differ, there are many potential surplus-creating trades, but players do not know others’ exact valuations; they only see public histories and current holdings. This creates strategic uncertainty: a proposer must guess which directions of trade will be attractive to at least one partner, while responders must judge whether an offer is favorable given only their own values. Red is most valuable to squid and otter, purple is especially valuable to peacock, and blue is moderately valuable to both peacock and otter but low for squid. Green is neutral for all and thus is a natural medium of exchange: those with high-value colors may be willing to swap them for green in small quantities if they suspect others undervalue them, and vice versa. Because prospects for future trades exist but the game is short (3 rounds, with each player proposing once per round), players face a tension between holding out for better deals and securing gains early. Proposers will tend to offer trades that are strictly profitable by their own values, but that they hope are still weakly attractive for at least one responder; responders will accept only when they see clear or at least plausible gains. The randomness in partner selection when multiple responders accept further complicates strategy, as proposers cannot target a specific counterpart and may shade offers to be attractive to more than one player at once. Across three rounds, we expect early exploratory offers testing where demand lies, followed by more targeted trades as the public history reveals which colors others appear eager to gain or shed. However, given private values and the risk of overpaying, some potentially efficient trades may never occur, especially near the end when players are wary of being exploited on the final proposals.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, all players still hold 10 of each chip and have no information about others’ valuations beyond the public value ranges. Proposals are likely to be exploratory, using green chips as a safe middle value and testing demand for red, blue, and purple. Peacock especially wants to accumulate purple and is moderately keen on blue; otter and squid both highly value red, but this is not yet common knowledge. Because players expect others to treat green as a neutral currency, early trades may involve swapping a target color for green at seemingly modest rates. Responders will be cautious but may accept trades that appear plausibly favorable under a range of opponent valuations, particularly when they receive a color they already suspect is strong (like red or purple) in exchange for green.

By round 1, the initial trades have revealed some weak signals. Squid’s acceptance of green-for-purple suggests squid is not strongly attached to purple, and the red-for-blue trade between squid and otter hints that both may like red more than blue. Peacock, having paid green for purple, appears to value purple highly and might continue to try to accumulate it, but must also watch their green balance. Otter’s failed attempt to buy red with green indicates that others may not find a simple 1:1 red-for-green trade attractive. Players now adjust by targeting colors that counterparts seem willing to give up (purple from squid, blue from squid) and exploit apparent demand (otter and squid for red). However, with limited remaining rounds, every proposal must balance extracting surplus with keeping it just good enough that at least one counterpart will accept.

Entering the final round, patterns are clearer: squid has repeatedly given up purple and blue and consistently bought red, signaling very high value on red and relatively low value on those other colors. Otter has twice accepted trades where they gain red, confirming a strong interest in red as well, and has been unwilling to sacrifice green for red at a 1:1 rate, suggesting green is also viewed as solid. Peacock has focused on acquiring purple, indicating that purple is especially valuable to them, and has not expressed clear interest in red. With only one proposal left per player, each will try to secure any remaining obvious gains: trades that convert what they now confidently believe is a low-value color (for them) into a color widely revealed to be high-value for someone else, while still keeping the rate profitable. Responders, knowing this is the last chance, will accept anything that clearly improves their final portfolio and reject marginal or ambiguous offers, as there are no future rounds to compensate for errors.

parsed_target.game_explanation: In this three-player, three-round bargaining game, each player starts with an equal endowment (10 of each chip color) but has different private values for red, blue, and purple; green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Privately, dog-red-9959 values purple and red highly (PURPLE=0.90, RED=0.60), bear-blue-8468 is moderately balanced (BLUE=0.60, RED=0.50, GREEN=0.50, PURPLE=0.30), and otter-yellow-9944 strongly prefers purple (0.60) and green (0.50) while disliking red and blue (0.30 each). Because only own values are known, each player must infer others’ preferences from which colors they try to buy or sell. Trades must involve swapping one color for another, and any responding player can accept; if more than one accepts, the recipient is chosen randomly, so proposers cannot fully direct trades. Strategic incentives: players want to move out of colors they likely value less and into colors they privately value more, but they must offer counterparties at least weakly beneficial deals given the counterparties’ (unknown) values. Green is a safe benchmark at $0.50 for all, so trading away green for colors that are personally worth ≤$0.50 is unattractive. With only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, there is pressure to focus on simple, clearly positive-sum trades rather than long signaling sequences. We should expect: (1) early cautious offers that give responders a small but obvious gain (e.g., swapping a 0.30–0.40-valued chip for a 0.50 or 0.60-valued chip for the responder), (2) a few accepted trades where someone with low value for a color offloads it to someone who probably values it more, and (3) limited, noisy learning about others’ preferences from observing which colors they try to acquire or shed. Because dog-red-9959 privately has the strongest taste for purple and decent value for red, and both others have at least one color at 0.30, efficient gains mainly come from dog acquiring purple, and otter and bear shifting away from their 0.30 colors when they suspect a counterparty likes them more. However, incomplete information and random matching among acceptors will keep behavior somewhat conservative and symmetric across partners.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players still hold 10 of each color and have no behavioral information about the others’ valuations. Rational players will tend to make simple, symmetric offers that would be at least mildly attractive to any counterpart who happens to value the traded-for color more than the traded-away color. Given that green is known to be worth $0.50 to everyone, nobody wants to give up green cheaply. Each proposer is likely to experiment with trades that offload a color they personally suspect might be relatively weak (e.g., purple for bear, red or blue for otter) in exchange for a color they personally like more, while trying not to be so demanding that nobody accepts. Rejections in this round mainly reflect that a proposed trade is clearly worse than keeping one’s current bundle, given private values.

Going into round 1, players have observed a few revealing choices. Bear-blue-8468 has publicly signaled that blue is more attractive than purple by trading away purple for blue, while otter-yellow-9944 has traded away red for purple, hinting that otter prefers purple over red. Dog-red-9959 attempted to buy more purple by offering blue, suggesting dog values purple more than blue. Even though players do not see each other’s exact payoffs, they can infer that both otter and dog somewhat covet purple, and that bear is willing to dispose of it. This creates scope for further reallocation of purple from bear (and possibly otter) toward dog, and for otter to continue shedding red and blue. However, because players must propose a single offer to both potential counterparts at once, they cannot precisely target the identified best partner. Proposals are still likely to be somewhat conservative but more focused on continuing favorable directions discovered in round 0.

By round 2, the public history shows a clear pattern: bear has repeatedly been willing to trade away purple for blue, otter has repeatedly traded away red for purple, and dog has accepted several purple-for-blue or purple-for-other-color trades. This implies a rough ranking: dog values purple most, otter also values purple strongly and dislikes red, and bear is relatively cool on purple compared with blue. The remaining opportunities for gains lie mostly in marginally shifting more purple toward dog (and perhaps otter) and moving red away from otter, but chip constraints and earlier trades have already captured many of the biggest gains. Because this is the final round, players have no reason to leave beneficial trades on the table; they will propose relatively aggressive but still individually rational exchanges. Still, some offers will be declined because they fail to compensate the responder enough relative to the responder’s private valuations, and the randomization among multiple accepters can deter extremely skewed, partner-specific proposals.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip-bargaining game features overlapping but distinct private values over three colored chips (RED, BLUE, PURPLE) plus a common-value GREEN chip. Each player starts with 10 of each color and privately knows their own per-chip values. Badger and Otter both have relatively high values for BLUE (0.60, 0.70) and differ on RED (0.60 vs. 0.30) and PURPLE (0.20 vs. 0.40). Cat has perfectly flat values (0.50) for all non-GREEN colors and thus is relatively indifferent across red/blue/purple mixes, as long as total value is maintained. GREEN is common-value at 0.50 for everyone.

Strategically, Cat is the natural middleman: because Cat values all non-GREEN chips at 0.50, it can sometimes profit from trades that move chips toward the players who value them more (e.g., BLUE toward Otter, RED toward Badger) while extracting a small surplus for itself. Badger wants to accumulate RED and BLUE and is willing to give up lower-valued PURPLE; Otter especially wants BLUE but is relatively less keen on RED and only moderately values PURPLE. GREEN is a convenient, risk-free numéraire: everyone values it at 0.50, so transfers of GREEN primarily serve to compensate perceived differences in private values of other colors.

However, each player only knows their own payoffs, so they must infer others’ values from observed offers and acceptances. This uncertainty limits how finely they can exploit comparative advantages. Because proposals must be simple (requesting one color and offering another, with positive quantities) and recipients are randomly chosen if multiple accept, proposers will try to craft deals that are attractive to at most one likely counterpart or that they believe both potential partners value similarly. Over three short rounds, players face a trade-off between exploration (probing others’ preferences) and exploitation (locking in favorable trades). The most likely dynamic is that early offers are conservative and anchored on GREEN as a known 0.50 baseline, with modest adjustments that all parties can plausibly accept given incomplete information. As public history accumulates, later rounds may feature slightly more targeted trades—often involving exchanging PURPLE or RED for BLUE or GREEN—aimed at moving chips toward higher valuers without being so aggressive that everyone declines.
