repair_notes: rounds[1].turns[1]: clipped sell quantity to sender inventory.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red, blue, and purple chips plus a common-value green chip. Each player begins with 10 of each color and will negotiate over 3 rounds, each round containing one proposal from each player. Because offers are broadcast and either or both opponents can accept (with random resolution if both accept), proposers must balance value extraction with making deals attractive enough for at least one opponent, while facing uncertainty about others’ private values. Zebra strongly values red ($1.00) and purple/green ($0.50) and weakly values blue ($0.20). Pig values blue and red moderately ($0.40 and $0.50), green at $0.50, and purple very little ($0.10). Swan values blue highly ($0.90), purple fairly well ($0.60), green at $0.50, and red weakly ($0.30). Efficient patterns would shift red toward Zebra, blue toward Swan, and purple away from Pig toward Zebra or Swan, with green neutral for all. However, players only know their own valuations and infer others’ preferences from observed behavior, leading to incremental, exploratory trades early and more targeted trades later. Strategic uncertainty comes from not knowing others’ values or how they will respond, plus the random resolution when both opponents accept an offer. Proposers often craft deals that are clearly profitable for any counterparty (based on their own values as a rough benchmark), while skewing surplus in their own favor and avoiding overpaying in less-valued colors. As the game progresses and trade history reveals hints about who values what, offers are likely to become more tailored: Zebra will try to offload blue for red or purple, Swan will trade away red/green for blue/purple, and Pig will try to turn purple into blue/red/green, while everyone remains cautious not to give up high-value chips for low-gain swaps this late in a short, finite game.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, everyone starts with symmetric endowments and no direct information about others’ private values beyond the public value ranges. Players are likely to make relatively simple, symmetrical offers that they see as clearly beneficial given their own values, hoping they will also look attractive to at least one opponent. The goal is to test the waters, reveal a bit about preferences, and secure early gains without risking large amounts of high-value chips. Because it is round 0 of 3, players can afford exploratory offers that might be slightly generous to attract acceptance and gather information.

By the second round, prior trades have revealed partial information: Zebra was willing to give up blue for green and to buy red for purple, suggesting blue is relatively low value and red relatively high. Pig accepted blue-for-green and paid red for purple, indicating at least some appetite for blue and some tolerance for purple, albeit possibly as a sweetener. Swan showed a strong willingness to swap red for blue, signaling high value for blue and relatively low attachment to red. With these patterns, players now refine their beliefs: Zebra will more confidently pursue red and green while unloading blue and possibly some purple; Pig will continue to seek blue and red while dumping purple when possible; Swan will try to accumulate blue and purple while trading away red or green. However, endowments have shifted from round 0, constraining what each can offer and making them more cautious about parting with their now-scarcer high-value chips.

In the final round, players are closer to their privately desired portfolios. Zebra has likely accumulated a strong position in red and green while shedding much of their low-valued blue and some purple. Pig has moved toward more blue and red while unloading purple, although possibly now constrained in purple supply. Swan has acquired more blue and purple and given up some red, aligning with their values. With no future rounds, bargaining becomes myopic: players will accept only clearly profitable last-minute trades and will propose deals that create immediate value without worrying about signaling. Random resolution of competing acceptances still matters, but the short horizon and updated beliefs encourage tighter, more demanding offers, often leading to declined trades when counterparties’ marginal valuations are closer. The last few proposals thus tend to be either small, very favorable tweaks or ambitious but overly one-sided offers that get rejected.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This is a three-player, three-round bargaining game with privately-known values for red, blue, and purple chips and a common value for green chips. Each player starts with a symmetric endowment of 10 of each color, but they disagree about how much the non-green chips are worth to them. Red is relatively valuable to everyone, especially zebra-blue-2700 and swan-orange-6532 ($0.70) and somewhat less for turtle-purple-273 ($0.60). Blue is most valuable to swan-orange-6532 ($0.50), moderately valuable to turtle-purple-273 ($0.40), and low for zebra-blue-2700 ($0.20). Purple is highest for zebra-blue-2700 and swan-orange-6532 ($0.30) and lowest for turtle-purple-273 ($0.20). Green is common-value at $0.50 to everyone.

These differences create gains from trade: zebra-blue-2700 can profit by giving up blue or purple to acquire more red; swan-orange-6532 is happy to trade away purple or perhaps red for blue; turtle-purple-273 would like to transform low-value purple into higher-value red or blue. But uncertainty is substantial: each player only sees their own values and must infer others’ preferences from what they offer and request. Strong proposals that create clear surplus for both sides are more likely early, while later proposals may become more speculative or adversarial as players update beliefs from past acceptances and rejections.

The random resolution of multiple acceptances adds another strategic wrinkle: a proposer cannot target a specific partner if both non-senders find the deal attractive. This may push proposers toward offers that are especially attractive to one likely counterparty (based on inferred values) but less appealing to the other, to avoid randomness. As the game progresses over the three rounds, players who have successfully improved their portfolios may become pickier, while those who have not made good trades may be more willing to accept modest gains. Because there are only nine proposal turns and no carryover beyond this one game, players have incentives to be somewhat aggressive in finding surplus-creating trades rather than waiting for a future stage.

Given the valuation structure, we expect: (1) many proposals where red is demanded in exchange for blue, purple, or green; (2) zebra-blue-2700 often trying to acquire red while shedding blue; (3) swan-orange-6532 seeking blue and sometimes willing to part with red or purple; and (4) turtle-purple-273 looking to trade away purple, and occasionally green, to get red and blue, while being cautious with red. Some proposals will fail because they attempt to appropriate almost all of the surplus or misjudge others’ values; others will succeed when they clearly benefit the focal counterparty even under very different possible valuations.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, all players still have their initial symmetric chip holdings and no direct information about others’ valuations. Proposals are likely to be somewhat conservative and symmetric-looking (e.g., 2-for-1 or 1-for-1 in nearby colors) while players test which directions of trade might be acceptable. Each player is also trying to signal or mask their preferences: asking too aggressively for a color might reveal that it is very valuable to them, inviting tougher future deals. Rejections here are especially informative, as they help players update beliefs about whether, for example, others also value red very highly or whether someone is unusually eager to dump purple or blue.

By the second round, players have observed which colors others were willing to give up or acquire in round 0, providing some Bayesian evidence about private valuations. Turtle-purple-273 just improved by trading away low-value purple for higher-value red, revealing that turtle likely values red quite a bit more than purple and suggesting to others that purple may be relatively cheap for turtle. Zebra-blue-2700 managed to get red via trading away low-value blue, confirming that zebra strongly prefers red to blue. Swan-orange-6532 accepted blue in exchange for red, indicating that swan values blue very highly relative to red, or at least is happy to diversify into blue. These patterns make future red-for-blue or red-for-purple proposals more targeted: turtle might now seek more red or blue and be willing to sell purple, zebra will attempt to keep accumulating red and perhaps unload blue and purple, and swan will eagerly seek blue but may be more protective of red after giving some up cheaply.

Entering the final round, chip holdings have diverged, and players have a clearer picture of each other’s likely priorities. Turtle-purple-273 has repeatedly been willing to part with purple and now holds more red and some extra blue, cementing a portfolio tilted toward high-value colors from turtle’s perspective. Zebra-blue-2700 has systematically converted low-value blue and purple into high-value red and some extra green, and now has a very red- and green-intensive set of holdings. Swan-orange-6532 has concentrated more on blue while giving up some red and green when the price was right. With only three proposals left, players have strong incentives to look for any remaining win-win trades, but they have also become more demanding: someone who already greatly improved might reject thin-margin offers. Because this is the last chance to trade, each accepted proposal is likely to be clearly surplus-creating, while marginal or lopsided offers will be rejected outright.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player 4-chip bargaining game features heterogeneous private values over red, blue, and purple chips and a common value green chip. All players start with 10 of each color and have three rounds (nine total proposal turns) to trade. Red is most valuable to Kangaroo (0.90), moderately valuable to Unicorn (0.70), and low value to Frog (0.10). Purple is highly valuable to Frog (0.90), moderately to Unicorn (0.60), and somewhat to Kangaroo (0.40). Blue is modestly valuable to Unicorn and Frog (0.40) and low value to Kangaroo (0.20). Green is common value (0.50) and serves as a natural “currency” benchmark that everyone knows is symmetric. However, each player only knows their own private chip values, and must infer others’ preferences from observed trades and which offers are accepted or rejected. This creates strategic uncertainty about what exchanges will be mutually profitable and whether competing acceptances will randomize the trade partner.

Kangaroo is strongly red-loving and blue-averse; Unicorn is relatively balanced but likes purple more than red and both more than blue; Frog is purple-loving and red-averse. Socially efficient trades would move red chips from Frog to Kangaroo and Unicorn, purple chips from Kangaroo toward Frog and Unicorn, and blue chips from Kangaroo toward Unicorn and Frog, often with green acting as a medium of exchange or as a safe fallback value. But each agent is myopically self-interested in value terms, cannot directly negotiate with a specific counterpart (since multiple acceptances are randomized), and must worry about giving away too much surplus. Proposers will typically start by testing obviously favorable directions (e.g., Kangaroo offering purple or blue to acquire red or green, Frog offering red or blue to get purple, Unicorn trying to arbitrage between them) while shading terms enough that at least one responder finds the deal profitable but not both so attractive that randomization risk becomes costly. Early rounds provide information: acceptance of a cheap red-for-purple or blue-for-purple trade quickly reveals who values what, tightening later bargaining. Because the horizon is short (three rounds) and all players act each round, there is limited benefit to long-term signaling; instead, we expect mostly straightforward, value-improving trades, with occasional declined offers when proposers misestimate others’ valuations or demand too much surplus. Over the full game, we expect a net flow of red toward Kangaroo and Unicorn, purple toward Frog and Unicorn, and blue away from Kangaroo, with green used sparingly but credibly as a neutral payment chip.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has yet revealed their private values, so proposals mainly serve as exploratory tests of the market. Each player starts with 10 of each color, so all trades are about improving portfolio composition rather than resolving scarcities. Kangaroo is likely to probe whether others are willing to give up green or red (which Kangaroo likes) in exchange for low-value blue. Unicorn may try to trade less-liked blue for more-valued purple or red while keeping terms conservative to avoid overpaying before learning who cares most about what. Frog, valuing purple highly and red very little, will plausibly attempt to dump red or blue for purple at favorable terms. Declines are common this round as players are still guessing about others’ values and are wary of making lopsided trades. Any acceptance begins to reveal which counterpart likes or dislikes a particular color, informing the second round.

By the second round, some information has been revealed. Frog’s acceptance of green for blue at a 2:3 ratio suggests Frog is at least indifferent between green and blue at moderate rates, while Unicorn’s willingness to take red-for-purple at 3:2 indicates Unicorn values purple but is quite happy to unload some purple for cheap red. Kangaroo’s initial move to buy green with blue indicates that Kangaroo treats green as safer value than blue. Players now update beliefs: (1) Frog probably values purple highly (given their later demand in round 0) and is comfortable parting with red, (2) Unicorn is flexible but not desperate for purple at unfavorable rates, and (3) Kangaroo is likely blue-averse. In this round, Kangaroo can try to convert surplus green or purple into red; Unicorn may arbitrage between Frog’s red dump and Kangaroo’s red appetite; Frog may keep pushing red-for-purple trades. However, the looming finite horizon makes players less willing to leave easy gains on the table, so we expect somewhat more aggressive—but still individually profitable—offers, with occasional stalls if terms are too one-sided.

The final round is the last chance to reshape portfolios, so players become more willing to accept moderately favorable trades and have less incentive to hold back for future opportunities. By now, patterns are clearer: Kangaroo has repeatedly tried to improve away from blue toward green and red; Unicorn has positioned between Frog and Kangaroo, trading among red, purple, and green; Frog has consistently traded away red and blue for purple and green. With only one proposal left per player, they will try to execute straightforward, low-friction deals that add value given their inferred counterpart preferences. Kangaroo will focus on acquiring any remaining red or green at tolerable prices, likely offering purple or blue; Unicorn may fine-tune its mix of red vs. purple, and Frog will still chase purple while dumping remaining red or blue. Randomization risk from multiple acceptances remains, so proposers still shade terms to likely attract just one responder. Overall, the last round should tighten the emergent allocation: red concentrated with Kangaroo and Unicorn, purple heavily with Frog and some with Unicorn, blue away from Kangaroo, and green spread but slightly tilted toward those who repeatedly traded into it.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip-bargaining game features privately known, player-specific values for red, blue, and purple chips, with a common value for green chips. Every player starts symmetric in quantities (10 of each color) but asymmetric in values, which creates many potential gains from trade. However, players do not know others’ valuations, only their own, and must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. This incomplete information generates uncertainty about whether a given offer is attractive to others and about how aggressively to push for surplus.

Incentives:
- zebra-pink-6926 (zebra) strongly values BLUE ($1.00) and PURPLE ($0.90), views GREEN as moderate ($0.50), and RED as almost worthless ($0.10). Zebra is strongly motivated to trade away RED for BLUE or PURPLE, and is willing to use GREEN as a flexible currency when needed.
- swan-green-4000 (swan) has relatively flat preferences: RED and PURPLE are both $0.40, BLUE is low at $0.20, and GREEN is $0.50. Swan is happiest accumulating GREEN, somewhat indifferent between RED and PURPLE, and least interested in BLUE. Swan’s advantage is being able to accept a broader class of trades that modestly raise value.
- dolphin-green-2899 (dolphin) moderately values all non-green colors, with RED=$0.60, PURPLE=$0.70, BLUE=$0.50, and GREEN=$0.50. Dolphin sees all colors as at least moderately valuable and will tend to accept trades that increase total chip value even if they shift composition, but will pay a small premium for PURPLE and RED over BLUE/GREEN.

Strategic dynamics:
- Because recipients are chosen randomly when multiple players accept, proposers face a coordination and competition problem: an attractive offer might be over-subscribed, making the realized partner uncertain. This discourages extremely generous offers that are appealing to both others unless the proposer is indifferent between partners.
- Players must balance learning and earning: early in the game they may propose conservative, self-serving trades to explore others’ likely preferences; later turns can be more targeted based on observed behavior.
- Zebra’s low value on RED vs. others’ higher values creates a natural pattern: zebra offering RED in exchange for BLUE or PURPLE is potentially very profitable for both sides, but zebra must pitch prices carefully to appeal to at least one partner under uncertainty.
- Swan’s slight preference for GREEN and relative indifference between RED and PURPLE makes swan a natural buyer of GREEN alternatives (willing to give up low-value BLUE) and a flexible counterparty in multi-color trades, especially if they can move from BLUE into GREEN or from BLUE into a mix of RED/PURPLE when price is favorable.
- Dolphin is a good candidate to accept many modestly favorable trades, especially those that swap BLUE or GREEN into PURPLE or RED at decent rates. However, dolphin must avoid accepting offers that slowly leak value if they misjudge others’ valuations.

We forecast a game where zebra tries to unload RED, swan tries to convert BLUE into GREEN or other slightly higher valued chips, and dolphin opportunistically upgrades composition. Over three rounds, players progressively refine beliefs about who values what (e.g., deducing that zebra dislikes RED, that swan is not excited by BLUE, and that dolphin is relatively flexible), leading to somewhat more efficient trades by the final round, though still imperfect due to the random recipient rule and limited time.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, no one has direct information about others’ values. Zebra knows RED is almost worthless personally and will try a conservative, value-improving offer that uses RED as the payment. Swan and dolphin each evaluate whether the proposed price is good given their own, still-private values. Subsequent turns in this round will reflect initial reactions: swan will tentatively test swaps that move out of BLUE into more preferred colors, and dolphin will probe whether others are willing to pay a premium for PURPLE or RED. Overall, round 0 is dominated by cautious, exploratory trades with modest surplus to limit the risk of badly mispricing counterparts under uncertainty.

After a fully declined first round, each player has weak but useful information: no one was willing to trade GREEN at the prices offered, implying that others either also value GREEN reasonably highly or objected to the rate. Zebra learns that pricing RED at 1.5 per GREEN is too stingy for at least one counterpart; swan learns that 1.5 BLUE per GREEN is also unattractive; dolphin infers that neither counterpart is eager to give up PURPLE cheaply. In round 1, players modestly sweeten their offers and shift toward colors that, by their own logic, might be more appealing to others: zebra will offer more generous terms on RED for GREEN, swan will consider selling BLUE or PURPLE for GREEN at a better rate, and dolphin will propose trades that allow counterparts to shed colors they plausibly undervalue (e.g., BLUE for zebra, PURPLE for swan). We expect at least one trade to succeed as prices improve and beliefs update.

By the final round, players have observed two successful trades involving GREEN and BLUE/RED, and several rejections around PURPLE and symmetric color swaps. Zebra now knows that at least one other player is willing to acquire RED at a sufficiently favorable rate and that GREEN is actively demanded; swan has demonstrated a willingness to buy GREEN with BLUE and to sell GREEN for RED at the right rate; dolphin has revealed a readiness to convert BLUE into GREEN when it is close to value-neutral. With only three proposal turns left, players shift from exploration to exploitation: zebra tries one more favorable RED-for-BLUE trade, swan seeks a small improvement in composition without risking big losses, and dolphin aims for a final small surplus trade. Given the remaining holdings inferred from prior trades, offers become more targeted and smaller, and we forecast at least one more marginally profitable deal before the game ends.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player chip bargaining game features privately known values for red, blue, and purple chips, with green chips having a common known value of $0.50 to everyone. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Privately, turtle strongly prefers purple (0.60) and green (0.50), is mildly interested in blue (0.20), and nearly indifferent to red (0.10). Hamster strongly values purple (0.90) and blue (0.80), moderately values red (0.30), and is neutral on green (0.50). Elephant highly values red (0.90) and blue (0.60), is neutral on green (0.50), and dislikes purple (0.20). From an omniscient perspective, the biggest surplus opportunities lie in transferring purple chips from elephant to hamster or turtle, and transferring red chips from turtle or hamster to elephant, as well as trading blue from turtle to hamster or elephant. However, players do not know each others' exact values, only their own and the common green value, so offers will be based on guesses and conservative improvements rather than perfectly efficient trades.

Strategically, everyone knows green is worth $0.50 to all, so green-for-non-green trades must at least preserve $0.50 per chip on both sides to be attractive. Players will be reluctant to give away their highest-valued colors without good compensation in other colors. Because recipients are selected randomly if more than one accepts, a proposer faces partner-choice uncertainty: they cannot target one specific trading partner, and must design offers that remain profitable regardless of which opponent accepts. This discourages proposals that would be good with one player but potentially bad with another.

Given the short, three-round structure and one proposal per player per round, players are pressured to seek clearly beneficial trades early while still learning a bit from who accepts or declines. With private values, we expect modest, safe trades around green chips (the only common-value chip) and attempts to unload low-value colors in exchange for higher-value ones from the proposer’s perspective. Turtle will try to trade away red and maybe blue for purple or green; hamster will aim to acquire purple and blue while possibly giving up some green or red; elephant will try to collect red and blue while shedding purple. Not all surplus-improving trades will actually occur, because each player’s uncertainty and risk aversion about others’ valuations leads them to propose deals that are clearly beneficial in value terms for themselves but not obviously generous enough to induce acceptance from both possible counterparties.

The following forecast traces one plausible complete trajectory of play that respects individual incentives and the informational constraints, including conservative early offers around green, a mid-game high-value purple-for-red trade between hamster and elephant, and later attempts to refine portfolios before the game ends.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players still have their initial holdings and no information about others’ private values beyond the common green value. They are likely to make conservative, simple offers that use green (the safely priced chip) to test counterparties’ willingness to trade, or try small attempts to offload their lowest-value color. Rejections are cheap information, and any accepted trade reveals weak hints about others’ preferences, shaping future proposals.

By round 1, two trades have already occurred: hamster and elephant have traded purple for green, and then purple for red, revealing that elephant is willing to part with purple easily and likes red, while hamster values purple highly. Turtle has learned that elephant is shedding purple and that hamster wants it, but has not directly traded yet. With slightly better information, players now pursue more targeted improvements: hamster continues to consolidate purple while trimming green and low-value reds; elephant focuses further on red and blue while offloading purple; turtle still seeks to exchange away low-value red for chips that are at least as good as green.

Entering the final round, hamster has heavily concentrated in purple at the cost of red and green, elephant has accumulated additional red while dramatically reducing purple, and turtle is still stuck with many low-value reds and no successful trades yet. With only three proposal opportunities left, players focus on clearly profitable but simple trades. Turtle tries one last time to unload red for better colors; hamster, now rich in high-value purple, cautiously tries to fine-tune its holdings without giving away too much surplus; elephant makes a final attempt to pick up more valuable colors in exchange for its still-unwanted purple. Risk of ending with current portfolios makes players reluctant to agree to marginal trades that could backfire given their uncertainty about others’ values.
